Example:

```text
Software Engineering
```

---

## Dimension 2 — Tags

Answers:

```text
What concepts appear here?
```

Example:

```text
Next.js
React
Architecture
```

---

## Dimension 3 — Journey

Answers:

```text
What larger story is this part of?
```

Example:

```text
Building TWN
```

---

## Dimension 4 — Author

Answers:

```text
Who created this?
```

Example:

```text
Vanessa
```

---

# Lifecycle

Articles follow a strict editorial workflow.

```text
Draft
   ↓
Review
   ↓
Scheduled
   ↓
Published
   ↓
Archived
```

---

## Draft

Private.

Editable.

Incomplete.

Not indexed.

---

## Review

Ready for editorial verification.

Checks include:

* accuracy
* grammar
* accessibility
* metadata
* references

---

## Scheduled

Approved.

Waiting for publication date.

---

## Published

Public.

Indexable.

Searchable.

Shareable.

---

## Archived

Retained.

No longer promoted.

Still accessible.

---

# Relationship Model

The revised Article relationship model becomes:

```text
Author
   ↓
Articles
   ↓
Category
   ↓
Tags

Articles
   ↓
Margin Notes

Articles
   ↔
Journeys

Articles
   ↔
Journey Chapters

Articles
   ↓
Media

Articles
   ↓
Distribution Records (future)
```

---

# Journey Integration

Articles do not contain:

```sql
journey_id
```

This is intentionally prohibited.

---

## Why?

Because one article may participate in multiple journeys.

Instead:

```text
journey_articles
```

acts as a junction entity.

---

Example

```text
Article
How I Built Authentication

↓

Journey A
Building TWN

↓

Journey B
Learning Architecture
```

No duplication required.

---

# Chapter Participation

Within a Journey, Articles appear inside Chapters.

Example:

```text
Journey
Building TWN

↓

Chapter
Architecture

↓

Article
Why I Chose Next.js

↓

Article
Authentication Decisions

↓

Notebook Entry
The Week Everything Broke
```

This creates narrative flow without changing Article ownership.

---

# Canonical Source Principle

Every piece of knowledge should have one canonical source.

For Articles:

```text
Article
```

is the canonical source.

Everything else derives from it.

Examples:

```text
LinkedIn Post

Newsletter Summary

Podcast Discussion

YouTube Script

Twitter Thread
```

These are derivatives.

The Article remains authoritative.

---

# Article Metadata

Every Article should contain:

Core Metadata

```text
Title
Slug
Excerpt
Author
Category
Status
Published Date
```

Editorial Metadata

```text
Reading Time
Word Count
SEO Title
SEO Description
Canonical URL
```

Narrative Metadata

```text
Journey Participation
Chapter Participation
Featured Status
```

Media Metadata

```text
Cover Image
Cover Alt Text
```

---

# Future Evolution

The Article entity is expected to support:

* Multiple authors
* Revision history
* Citations
* Footnotes
* Cross-references
* Version comparison
* AI-assisted summaries
* Audio narration
* EPUB export
* PDF export
* Reading difficulty scores
* Knowledge graph relationships

None of these should change the core responsibility of the Article.

---

# Article Engineering Principles

Every engineer working with Articles must remember:

1. Articles are knowledge assets.
2. Articles are independent entities.
3. Articles may participate in multiple Journeys.
4. Narrative context must never overwrite editorial context.
5. Categories classify.
6. Tags describe.
7. Journeys contextualize.
8. Authors create.
9. Media enhances.
10. Distribution amplifies.

---

## End of Section 7.4.10.1

This revised specification makes Articles compatible with the Journey architecture while preserving normalization, editorial independence, and future support for books, podcasts, videos, courses, and other content types.

Next, we should document **7.4.10.2 — Notebook Entries**, because Notebook Entries are the second half of TWN's identity and will participate in Journeys differently from Articles. They are not educational publications; they are the reflective voice of the platform. That distinction needs to be formalized before we move to Authors and Journeys.

# 7.4.10.2 Domain Specification — Notebook Entries

---

# Purpose

Notebook Entries are the reflective layer of TWN.

If Articles represent structured knowledge,

Notebook Entries represent lived experience.

They capture:

* observations
* reflections
* lessons
* questions
* doubts
* realizations
* moments of growth
* personal discoveries

Notebook Entries are what transform TWN from a publication into a notebook.

Without Notebook Entries, TWN becomes another content platform.

With them, TWN gains a voice.

---

# Philosophy

Articles answer:

> What do I know?

Notebook Entries answer:

> What am I learning?

Articles communicate conclusions.

Notebook Entries preserve the process.

Articles are polished.

Notebook Entries are human.

Articles teach.

Notebook Entries reveal.

Both are equally important.

---

# What Makes Notebook Entries Different

Notebook Entries are not mini articles.

They are not social media posts.

They are not diary entries.

They are not status updates.

They are editorial reflections.

---

## Example

Article

```text
How I Designed TWN's Architecture
```

Notebook Entry

```text
Today I realized I was spending more time
thinking about scalability than readers.

The architecture was improving.

The experience wasn't.
```

Both discuss architecture.

But they serve completely different purposes.

---

# Core Responsibilities

Notebook Entries exist to:

* capture reflections
* preserve lessons learned
* document growth
* share uncertainty
* record observations
* provide emotional context
* humanize technical journeys
* create continuity between articles

---

# What Notebook Entries Do NOT Do

Notebook Entries are not responsible for:

* comprehensive teaching
* deep tutorials
* SEO-driven content
* structured educational material
* official announcements
* community submissions

Those belong elsewhere.

---

# The Notebook Principle

Every Notebook Entry should feel like a page torn from a real notebook.

When reading one, the reader should feel:

> Someone paused long enough to write this down.

If the entry feels like marketing,

it has failed.

If it feels like content optimization,

it has failed.

If it feels like a genuine observation,

it succeeds.

---

# Relationship to Articles

Notebook Entries may exist completely independently.

However,

they may also complement Articles.

---

Example

Article

```text
Designing the Editorial CMS
```

Notebook Entry

```text
The more features I added to the CMS,
the less it felt like a notebook.

That scared me.
```

The Notebook Entry adds emotional context.

The Article provides technical context.

Together they create a richer narrative.

---

# Relationship to Journeys

Notebook Entries participate naturally in Journeys.

In fact,

Journeys are where Notebook Entries become most powerful.

---

Example

Journey

```text
Building TWN
```

Chapter

```text
Architecture
```

Contents

```text
Article:
Why I Chose Next.js

Notebook Entry:
The Week Everything Broke

Article:
Designing the CMS

Notebook Entry:
I Almost Overengineered This
```

Notice how the reader experiences both:

* knowledge
* reflection

This creates narrative depth.

---

# Notebook Entry Dimensions

Every Notebook Entry exists in multiple dimensions.

---

## Reflection Dimension

Answers:

```text
What did I learn?
```

---

## Narrative Dimension

Answers:

```text
Where does this fit in my journey?
```

---

## Author Dimension

Answers:

```text
Who experienced this?
```

---

## Context Dimension

Answers:

```text
What was happening when this was written?
```

---

# Lifecycle

Notebook Entries follow a simpler workflow than Articles.

```text
Draft
   ↓
Review
   ↓
Published
   ↓
Archived
```

---

## Draft

Private.

Work in progress.

---

## Review

Optional editorial review.

Primarily focused on:

* clarity
* privacy
* tone
* quality

---

## Published

Visible to readers.

Eligible for:

* Hero rotation
* Today's Page
* Journeys
* Search

---

## Archived

Preserved.

Not actively surfaced.

Still accessible.

---

# Hero Participation

One unique responsibility of Notebook Entries is supporting the Living Hero.

---

Example

```text
Curiosity scales better than certainty.
```

or

```text
There are questions no search engine can answer.
```

These are Notebook Entries.

Not Articles.

---

## Rule

The Hero should only use Notebook Entries.

Never Articles.

The Hero represents the voice of the notebook.

---

# Today's Page Participation

Notebook Entries may appear as:

```text
Today's Page
```

on the homepage.

This creates a living editorial rhythm.

---

Example

```text
TODAY'S PAGE

July 14

The problem wasn't the database.

It was the assumptions behind it.
```

---

# Canonical Structure

A Notebook Entry should contain:

```text
Title (optional)

Thought

Author

Status

Created Date

Published Date

Journey Participation

Chapter Participation
```

Unlike Articles,

many fields remain intentionally minimal.

---

# Recommended Database Structure

```sql
notebook_entries
----------------
id
title
thought
slug
author_id
status
is_featured
display_date
published_at
created_at
updated_at
deleted_at
```

---

# Field Specifications

## title

Optional.

Many Notebook Entries may not require titles.

Example

```text
Today I Learned...
```

---

## thought

Required.

The core content.

May contain:

* reflection
* lesson
* observation
* insight

Must never be empty.

---

## slug

Optional initially.

Required if the entry receives its own public page.

---

## author_id

Required.

Every Notebook Entry has an owner.

---

## display_date

Used for:

```text
Today's Page
```

feature.

---

## is_featured

Used for:

* Hero rotation
* Featured notebook sections

---

# Validation Rules

Required:

✓ Thought

✓ Author

✓ Status

Optional:

Title

Journey Participation

Chapter Participation

Display Date

---

# Length Guidelines

Unlike Articles,

Notebook Entries should remain concise.

Suggested ranges:

| Type                |   Words |
| ------------------- | ------: |
| Hero Thought        |    5–20 |
| Reflection          |  20–200 |
| Long Notebook Entry | 200–800 |

These are editorial guidelines.

Not hard technical limits.

---

# Search Behavior

Notebook Entries should appear in search.

However,

results should clearly distinguish them from Articles.

Example

```text
[Notebook Entry]

The Week Everything Broke
```

instead of

```text
[Article]

Designing the CMS
```

Readers should understand the content type immediately.

---

# SEO Philosophy

Notebook Entries are not created for SEO.

They are created for authenticity.

Search visibility is a benefit,

not the objective.

---

# Future Evolution

Notebook Entries are expected to evolve into:

* Daily Pages
* Reflection Collections
* Personal Journals
* Journey Commentary
* Audio Reflections
* Voice Notes
* Illustrated Notes
* Book Draft Fragments

The architecture should support these expansions without changing the core identity of Notebook Entries.

---

# Relationship Model

```text
Author
   ↓
Notebook Entries

Notebook Entries
   ↔
Journeys

Notebook Entries
   ↔
Journey Chapters

Notebook Entries
   ↓
Hero

Notebook Entries
   ↓
Today's Page
```

---

# Engineering Principles

Every engineer working with Notebook Entries must remember:

1. Notebook Entries are reflections, not articles.
2. They preserve learning, not conclusions.
3. They may participate in Journeys.
4. They power the Living Hero.
5. They power Today's Page.
6. Authenticity is more important than optimization.
7. Simplicity is more important than feature richness.
8. The notebook voice must remain distinct from the article voice.
9. Notebook Entries should feel personal, even when public.
10. They are a defining part of TWN's identity.

---

## End of Section 7.4.10.2

This specification establishes Notebook Entries as the reflective counterpart to Articles.

Together:

```text
Articles
    ↓
Knowledge

Notebook Entries
    ↓
Reflection
```

form the dual foundation of TWN.

The next entity should be **7.4.10.3 — Authors**, because Authors own Articles, Notebook Entries, and future Journeys. Once Authors are defined, we can formally model ownership throughout the editorial domain before documenting Journeys themselves.

# 7.4.10.3 Domain Specification — Authors

---

# Purpose

The Author entity represents the creator and steward of knowledge within TWN.

Authors are responsible for creating, maintaining, evolving, and preserving editorial content.

Without Authors, content becomes disconnected from its source.

Without ownership, accountability disappears.

The Author domain exists to ensure every piece of knowledge has a clear creator, custodian, and historical context.

---

# Philosophy

In many publishing systems, authors are treated as metadata.

A name attached to an article.

TWN rejects this approach.

An Author is not a field.

An Author is a first-class domain entity.

Authors are the source of:

* Articles
* Notebook Entries
* Journeys
* Future Books
* Future Podcasts
* Future Courses

The Author domain preserves the relationship between people and knowledge.

---

# Core Principle

Every piece of editorial content must have a creator.

Readers should always be able to answer:

> Who wrote this?

> Who experienced this?

> Who is responsible for this knowledge?

---

# What an Author Is

An Author represents a person who creates editorial content.

Examples:

```text
Vanessa

Guest Contributor

Future TWN Editor

Future Community Writer
```

An Author may create:

* Articles
* Notebook Entries
* Journeys
* Books
* Podcasts
* Courses

---

# What an Author Is Not

An Author is not:

* a login account
* a role
* a permission set
* a session

These belong to the Identity domain.

---

Important distinction:

```text
Identity
   ↓
Who can log in?

Author
   ↓
Who creates knowledge?
```

These are different concepts.

---

# Why Separate Author from User?

Because not every Author needs an account.

Example:

Guest contributor.

```text
Guest Author
      ↓
Published Article
```

No login required.

No dashboard required.

Still an Author.

---

Similarly:

An administrator may have an account but never publish anything.

```text
Administrator
      ↓
User

NOT

Author
```

This separation protects the domain model.

---

# Core Responsibilities

The Author entity is responsible for:

* Editorial ownership
* Public attribution
* Author biography
* Author profile pages
* Content relationships
* Journey ownership
* Future contributor identities

---

It is NOT responsible for:

* Authentication
* Sessions
* Roles
* Permissions
* Billing
* Analytics

---

# Author Types

TWN should support multiple author types.

---

## Founder Author

Example:

```text
Vanessa
```

Creates and owns TWN content.

---

## Editorial Author

Future staff writers.

Example:

```text
TWN Editor
```

---

## Guest Author

Invited contributor.

Example:

```text
Open Source Community Member
```

---

## Organization Author (Future)

Example:

```text
She Code Africa
```

or

```text
Wikimedia Cameroon
```

This should be supported later without redesigning the system.

---

# Author Lifecycle

Authors progress through states.

```text
Draft
   ↓
Active
   ↓
Inactive
   ↓
Archived
```

---

## Draft

Profile being created.

Not public.

---

## Active

Visible publicly.

Can own content.

---

## Inactive

No longer creating content.

Existing content remains published.

---

## Archived

Historical preservation.

Author profile retained.

No new content.

---

# Ownership Model

Authors own content.

Ownership is one-directional.

```text
Author
   ↓
Content
```

Content never owns Authors.

---

# Relationships

---

## One Author

↓

Many Articles

```text
Author
   ↓
Articles
```

---

## One Author

↓

Many Notebook Entries

```text
Author
   ↓
Notebook Entries
```

---

## One Author

↓

Many Journeys

```text
Author
   ↓
Journeys
```

---

## Future

One Author

↓

Many Books

Many Podcasts

Many Courses

Many Videos

---

# Canonical Database Structure

```sql
authors
-------
id
slug
display_name
bio
avatar_media_id
website_url
location
status
created_at
updated_at
deleted_at
```

---

# Field Specifications

## id

UUID.

Internal identifier.

Never exposed publicly.

Immutable.

---

## slug

Example:

```text
vanessa
```

Used for:

```text
/authors/vanessa
```

Must be unique.

---

## display_name

Example:

```text
Vanessa Wah
```

Public-facing identity.

Required.

---

## bio

Short biography.

Example:

```text
Developer, writer, educator, and builder of TWN.
```

Optional initially.

Recommended later.

---

## avatar_media_id

Reference to Media domain.

Never store image URLs directly.

Allows future image replacement.

---

## website_url

Optional.

May point to:

* personal site
* portfolio
* organization page

---

## location

Optional.

Not required.

Should remain broad.

Example:

```text
Cameroon
```

not

```text
Street address
```

---

## status

Enum:

```text
draft
active
inactive
archived
```

---

# Public Author Pages

Every active Author should eventually have a profile page.

Example:

```text
/authors/vanessa
```

Containing:

* biography
* journeys
* articles
* notebook entries
* future books
* future podcasts

---

# Author and Journeys

Journeys belong to Authors.

This relationship is important.

Example:

```text
Author

↓

Building TWN

↓

Architecture

↓

Articles

↓

Notebook Entries
```

The Journey reflects the Author's experience.

Therefore ownership remains with the Author.

---

# Multiple Authors (Future)

Version 1:

```text
One Author
↓
One Article
```

Future:

```text
Many Authors
↔
Many Articles
```

Implemented through:

```text
article_authors
```

junction table.

---

Do not build this initially.

Design for it.

---

# Search Behavior

Authors should be searchable.

Searching:

```text
Vanessa
```

should return:

* author profile
* journeys
* articles
* notebook entries

---

# SEO Considerations

Author pages provide:

* credibility
* expertise signals
* content discoverability

Author metadata should contribute to structured data and search visibility.

---

# Media Relationships

Authors may own:

```text
Avatar

Cover Image (future)

Profile Banner (future)
```

Media should remain independent.

---

# Future Evolution

The Author domain should support:

* Contributor profiles
* Organization profiles
* Author verification
* Author social links
* Author newsletters
* Author reading lists
* Author recommendations
* Author interviews
* Author podcasts
* Author books

without redesigning the core model.

---

# Security Principles

Only authorized editors may:

* modify authors
* archive authors
* merge authors

Public readers may:

* view active authors

Nothing more.

---

# Engineering Principles

Every engineer working with Authors must remember:

1. Authors are domain entities, not login accounts.
2. Authors own knowledge.
3. Authors may exist without user accounts.
4. User accounts may exist without authors.
5. Ownership flows from Author to Content.
6. Journeys belong to Authors.
7. Public attribution is mandatory.
8. Historical authors should be preserved.
9. Author pages strengthen credibility.
10. Future growth must not require redesigning the Author model.

---

# Relationship Summary

```text
Author
│
├── Articles
├── Notebook Entries
├── Journeys
├── Books (future)
├── Podcasts (future)
├── Courses (future)
└── Videos (future)
```

---

## End of Section 7.4.10.3

The Author entity establishes ownership across the entire editorial ecosystem.

We now have the three foundational pillars of TWN:

```text
Authors
    ↓
Create

Articles
    ↓
Knowledge

Notebook Entries
    ↓
Reflection
```

The next section should be **7.4.10.4 — Journeys**, because Journeys are now the narrative layer that connects Articles and Notebook Entries into meaningful long-term stories such as *Building TWN*, *Learning Architecture*, or *My Open Source Journey*. This is where TWN becomes significantly different from a traditional blog or CMS.

# 7.4.10.4 Domain Specification — Journeys

---

# Purpose

Journeys are the narrative layer of TWN.

They exist to connect individual pieces of content into a larger story.

Without Journeys, TWN is a collection of independent Articles and Notebook Entries.

With Journeys, TWN becomes a living body of connected knowledge.

Journeys answer a question that Categories and Tags cannot answer:

> What larger story does this belong to?

---

# Philosophy

Categories organize topics.

Tags organize concepts.

Journeys organize experiences.

This distinction is fundamental.

Consider:

```text
Article:
Why I Chose Next.js

Category:
Software Engineering

Tags:
Next.js
React
Architecture

Journey:
Building TWN
```

The Category explains the subject.

The Tags explain the concepts.

The Journey explains the story.

---

# What Is A Journey?

A Journey is a long-running narrative container.

It groups related content together over time.

A Journey may contain:

* Articles
* Notebook Entries
* Future Podcasts
* Future Videos
* Future Books
* Future Courses
* Future Resources

The Journey itself is not content.

It is structure.

---

# Real Examples

---

## Journey

```text
Building TWN
```

Contains:

```text
Chapter 1
The Idea

Chapter 2
Research

Chapter 3
Architecture

Chapter 4
Launch

Chapter 5
Growth
```

---

## Journey

```text
Learning Software Architecture
```

Contains:

```text
Chapter 1
Foundations

Chapter 2
System Design

Chapter 3
Scaling

Chapter 4
Leadership
```

---

## Journey

```text
My Open Source Journey
```

Contains:

```text
Chapter 1
Discovering Open Source

Chapter 2
First Contributions

Chapter 3
Community Leadership
```

---

# Why Journeys Exist

People rarely learn in isolated events.

Growth happens through sequences.

Knowledge evolves.

Understanding deepens.

Mistakes create lessons.

Journeys preserve that progression.

Instead of:

```text
Article
Article
Article
Article
```

Readers experience:

```text
Beginning

↓

Learning

↓

Failure

↓

Growth

↓

Mastery
```

This is a fundamentally different reading experience.

---

# Core Responsibilities

Journeys are responsible for:

* Narrative organization
* Long-term storytelling
* Context preservation
* Reader progression
* Knowledge continuity
* Connecting Articles and Notebook Entries
* Future multimedia organization

---

Journeys are NOT responsible for:

* Content creation
* Categories
* Search ranking
* SEO metadata
* Reader comments
* Analytics

---

# Journey Ownership

Every Journey belongs to one Author.

Example:

```text
Vanessa
   ↓
Building TWN
```

This ownership is required.

Journeys represent experiences.

Experiences belong to someone.

---

# Journey Independence Principle

A Journey is not dependent on any single content type.

This is a core architectural rule.

Bad:

```text
Journey
   ↓
Articles
```

Good:

```text
Journey
   ↓
Editorial Content
```

Current content types:

```text
Articles

Notebook Entries
```

Future content types:

```text
Books

Podcasts

Videos

Courses

Resources
```

The Journey architecture must not change when new content types are introduced.

---

# Journey Lifecycle

Journeys evolve over time.

Lifecycle:

```text
Draft
   ↓
Active
   ↓
Completed
   ↓
Archived
```

---

## Draft

Planning stage.

Not publicly visible.

May contain no Chapters.

---

## Active

Publicly visible.

Receiving new content.

Still evolving.

---

## Completed

Narrative has reached a conclusion.

Still public.

No longer growing.

---

## Archived

Historical preservation.

Retained for reference.

No longer promoted.

---

# Journey Structure

Every Journey contains Chapters.

```text
Journey
   ↓
Chapters
   ↓
Content
```

A Journey cannot directly contain content.

Chapters are mandatory.

This ensures structure.

---

# Why Chapters Are Required

Without Chapters:

```text
Journey
   ↓
50 Articles
```

becomes chaotic.

With Chapters:

```text
Journey
   ↓
Chapter 1
The Beginning

Chapter 2
Research

Chapter 3
Architecture

Chapter 4
Launch
```

the narrative remains understandable.

---

# Canonical Database Structure

```sql
journeys
--------
id
slug
title
description
author_id
cover_media_id
status
started_at
completed_at
created_at
updated_at
deleted_at
```

---

# Field Specifications

## id

UUID.

Primary key.

Never exposed publicly.

---

## slug

Example:

```text
building-twn
```

Used in URLs.

Example:

```text
/journeys/building-twn
```

Must be unique.

Immutable after publication.

---

## title

Required.

Examples:

```text
Building TWN

Learning Architecture

My Open Source Journey
```

Maximum:

150 characters.

---

## description

Required.

Explains the purpose of the Journey.

Example:

> A long-term documentation of the decisions, mistakes, lessons, and architecture behind building The Notebook of a Tech Woman.

---

## author_id

Required.

Journey owner.

References:

```text
authors
```

---

## cover_media_id

Optional.

References:

```text
media
```

Used for Journey cover art.

---

## status

Enum:

```text
draft
active
completed
archived
```

---

## started_at

Records when the Journey began.

---

## completed_at

Used when a Journey reaches its conclusion.

May remain NULL indefinitely.

---

# Validation Rules

Required:

✓ Title

✓ Description

✓ Author

✓ Status

Optional:

Cover Image

Completion Date

---

# Search Behavior

Searching:

```text
Building TWN
```

should return:

```text
Journey

Building TWN

42 Pages

6 Chapters
```

before returning individual Articles.

The Journey acts as a portal.

---

# Journey Pages

Every Journey should eventually have a dedicated page.

Example:

```text
/journeys/building-twn
```

Containing:

* Journey description
* Author
* Progress
* Chapters
* Related content
* Timeline

---

# Reader Experience

A reader entering a Journey should understand:

```text
What is this?

Why does it exist?

Where should I start?

What comes next?
```

These questions must always be answerable.

---

# Journey Navigation

Future Journey pages should support:

```text
Start Journey

Continue Journey

Previous Chapter

Next Chapter

Progress Tracking
```

These are reader experience features.

Not domain responsibilities.

---

# Relationship Model

```text
Author
   ↓
Journeys

Journeys
   ↓
Journey Chapters

Journey Chapters
   ↓
Articles

Journey Chapters
   ↓
Notebook Entries
```

Future:

```text
Journey Chapters
   ↓
Books

Journey Chapters
   ↓
Podcasts

Journey Chapters
   ↓
Videos
```

---

# Media Relationships

Journeys may have:

```text
Cover Image

Banner Image

Illustration
```

All media references belong in the Media domain.

---

# Future Evolution

Journeys should support:

* Progress indicators
* Journey timelines
* Reading paths
* Learning maps
* Multi-author journeys
* Journey collaborations
* Journey collections
* Interactive journeys
* Educational tracks
* Multimedia narratives

without changing the core Journey model.

---

# Engineering Principles

Every engineer working with Journeys must remember:

1. Journeys are narratives.
2. Journeys are not categories.
3. Journeys are not tags.
4. Journeys organize experiences.
5. Journeys own Chapters.
6. Journeys never own content directly.
7. Content remains independent.
8. New media types must fit into Journeys without redesign.
9. Journeys should improve reader understanding.
10. Every Journey should feel like a meaningful story.

---

# Relationship Summary

```text
Author
   ↓
Journey
   ↓
Chapter
   ↓
Article

Author
   ↓
Journey
   ↓
Chapter
   ↓
Notebook Entry
```

---

## End of Section 7.4.10.4

Journeys become the narrative backbone of TWN.

Articles provide knowledge.

Notebook Entries provide reflection.

Journeys provide meaning and progression.

The next section is **7.4.10.5 — Journey Chapters**, which defines how large narratives are broken into meaningful phases and how content is ordered within those phases. This section is critical because it determines how readers move through a Journey.

# 7.4.10.5 Domain Specification — Journey Chapters

---

# Purpose

Journey Chapters are the structural units of a Journey.

If a Journey is the story,

a Chapter is a major phase within that story.

Chapters exist to transform a large narrative into understandable segments.

Without Chapters, Journeys become long lists of unrelated content.

With Chapters, Journeys become readable, navigable, and meaningful.

---

# Philosophy

Humans understand stories through phases.

Not through endless timelines.

For example:

```text
Building TWN
```

is too large to understand as a single narrative.

Breaking it into phases creates clarity.

```text
Building TWN

↓

The Idea

↓

Research

↓

Design

↓

Architecture

↓

Launch

↓

Growth
```

Each phase becomes a Chapter.

---

# What Is A Chapter?

A Chapter is a narrative container inside a Journey.

Its purpose is to group related content that belongs to the same stage of a larger story.

A Chapter does not create content.

A Chapter organizes content.

---

# What A Chapter Is Not

A Chapter is not:

* a Category
* a Tag
* a Folder
* a Collection
* a Playlist

A Chapter exists only within the context of a Journey.

Without a Journey, a Chapter has no meaning.

---

# Example

Journey

```text
Building TWN
```

Chapter

```text
Architecture
```

Contains

```text
Why I Chose Next.js

Designing The CMS

Authentication Decisions

The Week Everything Broke
```

The Chapter provides context.

---

# Core Responsibilities

Journey Chapters are responsible for:

* Narrative segmentation
* Content ordering
* Reader progression
* Context preservation
* Structural organization

---

Journey Chapters are NOT responsible for:

* Content creation
* Publishing
* Search ranking
* Categorization
* Ownership

---

# Chapter Ownership

Ownership hierarchy:

```text
Author
   ↓
Journey
   ↓
Chapter
```

A Chapter belongs to exactly one Journey.

A Chapter cannot exist independently.

---

# Chapter Lifecycle

Lifecycle:

```text
Draft
   ↓
Active
   ↓
Completed
   ↓
Archived
```

---

## Draft

Planning phase.

Not visible publicly.

---

## Active

Publicly visible.

May continue receiving content.

---

## Completed

Narrative phase is finished.

No new content expected.

Still public.

---

## Archived

Historical preservation.

No longer promoted.

---

# The Narrative Principle

A Chapter should represent a meaningful phase of growth.

Good examples:

```text
Research

Architecture

Launch

Scaling

Leadership
```

Poor examples:

```text
Miscellaneous

Random Thoughts

Other Stuff

General
```

Every Chapter should answer:

> What happened during this stage?

---

# Content Organization

A Chapter contains ordered content.

Order matters.

Readers should experience content intentionally.

Example:

```text
Chapter

Architecture

↓

Notebook Entry
Why I Started Redesigning

↓

Article
Designing The CMS

↓

Notebook Entry
The Trade-Offs Were Hard

↓

Article
Authentication Decisions

↓

Notebook Entry
What I Learned
```

This sequence tells a story.

Changing the order changes the experience.

---

# Ordered Reading Principle

Content inside a Chapter should be ordered deliberately.

Readers should never be forced to guess:

```text
What should I read next?
```

The system should answer that automatically.

---

# Content Types Supported

Version 1

```text
Articles

Notebook Entries
```

Future Versions

```text
Books

Podcast Episodes

Videos

Courses

Resources

Interactive Experiences
```

The Chapter architecture must support new content types without redesign.

---

# Canonical Database Structure

```sql
journey_chapters
----------------
id
journey_id
slug
title
description
order_index
status
created_at
updated_at
deleted_at
```

---

# Field Specifications

## id

UUID.

Primary key.

Immutable.

---

## journey_id

Required.

References:

```text
journeys
```

Every Chapter belongs to one Journey.

---

## slug

Example:

```text
architecture
```

Used in URLs.

Example:

```text
/journeys/building-twn/architecture
```

Must be unique within a Journey.

---

## title

Required.

Examples:

```text
Research

Architecture

Launch
```

Maximum:

120 characters.

---

## description

Required.

Provides context.

Example:

> This chapter explores the major architectural decisions behind TWN, including technology selection, content modelling, and system design.

---

## order_index

Required.

Determines sequence.

Example:

```text
1

2

3

4
```

Order must remain explicit.

Never depend on creation date.

---

## status

Enum:

```text
draft

active

completed

archived
```

---

# Validation Rules

Required:

✓ Journey

✓ Title

✓ Description

✓ Order

✓ Status

Optional:

Slug

---

# Reader Experience

A Chapter page should help readers answer:

```text
Where am I?

What happened before?

What happens next?
```

The system should always provide context.

---

# Chapter Navigation

Future navigation should support:

```text
Previous Chapter

Next Chapter

Chapter Progress

Journey Overview

Continue Reading
```

---

# Chapter Overview Page

Example:

```text
Journey

Building TWN

Chapter 3

Architecture

--------------------

Introduction

4 Articles

3 Notebook Entries

Estimated Reading Time

Continue Reading →
```

The Chapter becomes a reading hub.

---

# Content Ordering Model

To support future growth, Chapters should not directly own Articles.

Instead, Chapters own ordered content relationships.

Conceptually:

```text
Chapter
   ↓
Ordered Content
      ↓
Article

Chapter
   ↓
Ordered Content
      ↓
Notebook Entry
```

This allows future content types without redesigning the Chapter itself.

---

# Why Not Direct Article References?

Bad:

```text
Chapter
   ↓
Articles Only
```

This prevents future growth.

Good:

```text
Chapter
   ↓
Ordered Content
      ↓
Article

      ↓
Notebook Entry

      ↓
Podcast
```

The Chapter remains stable.

---

# Search Behavior

Searching:

```text
Architecture
```

may return:

```text
Journey Chapter

Building TWN

Chapter 3

Architecture
```

alongside individual Articles.

This helps readers understand context.

---

# SEO Considerations

Chapter pages are valuable landing pages.

A Chapter may become:

```text
The Architecture Behind TWN
```

containing multiple related resources.

Search engines often prefer well-organized thematic collections.

---

# Media Relationships

A Chapter may have:

```text
Cover Image

Illustration

Timeline Graphic
```

All media remains within the Media domain.

---

# Future Evolution

Journey Chapters should support:

* Reading paths
* Learning tracks
* Progress indicators
* Timelines
* Milestones
* Multimedia chapters
* Interactive chapters
* Educational modules
* Collaborative chapters

without changing the core model.

---

# Engineering Principles

Every engineer working with Journey Chapters must remember:

1. Chapters organize phases, not topics.
2. Chapters belong to Journeys.
3. Chapters do not create content.
4. Chapters provide structure.
5. Order matters.
6. Readers should always know what comes next.
7. Chapters should remain media-agnostic.
8. New content types should fit without redesign.
9. Chapters should strengthen narrative clarity.
10. A Chapter should feel like a meaningful stage in a larger story.

---

# Relationship Summary

```text
Author
   ↓
Journey
   ↓
Chapter
   ↓
Ordered Content
        ↓
Articles

        ↓
Notebook Entries

        ↓
Future Content Types
```

---

## End of Section 7.4.10.5

At this point, the core editorial architecture of TWN is fully defined:

```text
Author
   ↓
Journey
   ↓
Chapter
   ↓
Content

Content
├── Articles
└── Notebook Entries
```

The next section should be **7.4.10.6 — Categories**, where we formally define topical classification and clearly separate it from Journeys. This distinction is extremely important because Categories classify knowledge while Journeys contextualize knowledge.

# 7.4.10.6 Domain Specification — Categories

---

# Purpose

Categories provide the primary topical classification system within TWN.

Their purpose is to answer a simple but critical question:

> What is this content about?

Categories help readers discover knowledge based on subject matter.

Unlike Journeys, which organize stories,

Categories organize topics.

This distinction must remain clear throughout the entire platform.

---

# Philosophy

Categories classify knowledge.

Journeys contextualize knowledge.

Tags describe knowledge.

These three systems work together but solve different problems.

---

## Example

Article:

```text
Why I Chose Next.js
```

Category:

```text
Software Engineering
```

Tags:

```text
Next.js
React
Architecture
```

Journey:

```text
Building TWN
```

---

Each answers a different question.

Category:

```text
What field does this belong to?
```

Tag:

```text
What concepts are discussed?
```

Journey:

```text
Why does this exist?
```

---

# What Is A Category?

A Category is a high-level topical grouping.

Categories should represent enduring knowledge domains.

Examples:

```text
Software Engineering

Leadership

Career

Open Source

Design

AI

Product

Community

Entrepreneurship
```

Categories are stable.

They should not change frequently.

---

# What Categories Are NOT

Categories are not:

* Journeys
* Tags
* Projects
* Campaigns
* Technologies
* Personal experiences

Bad categories:

```text
Building TWN

Next.js

My Thoughts

July Notes

Community Event
```

These belong elsewhere.

---

# The 80-Year Rule

Every Category should pass the 80-Year Rule.

Ask:

> Will this still be meaningful in 80 years?

Good:

```text
Leadership

Education

Engineering
```

Bad:

```text
ChatGPT

Vercel

TikTok Strategy
```

Technologies evolve.

Domains endure.

---

# Why Categories Exist

Without Categories:

```text
1000 Articles
```

becomes impossible to browse.

Categories provide:

* discoverability
* organization
* editorial structure
* reader orientation

---

# Core Responsibilities

Categories are responsible for:

* topical classification
* content discovery
* navigation
* editorial organization
* archive structure

---

Categories are NOT responsible for:

* narrative structure
* content ownership
* chronology
* publishing workflows
* reader engagement

---

# Category Ownership

Categories belong to the Editorial Domain.

Editorial leadership controls:

* creation
* modification
* archival

Readers never create categories.

---

# Category Lifecycle

Categories evolve slowly.

Lifecycle:

```text
Draft
   ↓
Active
   ↓
Archived
```

---

## Draft

Being planned.

Not visible publicly.

---

## Active

Available for content assignment.

Visible publicly.

---

## Archived

No longer assigned to new content.

Existing content remains associated.

---

# Category Structure

TWN should begin with a flat category structure.

Version 1:

```text
Software Engineering

Leadership

Career

Open Source

Design
```

---

# Why Flat Categories?

Deep hierarchies create complexity.

Bad:

```text
Technology
    ↓
Software
    ↓
Frontend
    ↓
React
```

Difficult to maintain.

Difficult to understand.

---

Good:

```text
Software Engineering
```

Simple.

Clear.

Scalable.

---

# Future Category Hierarchies

If TWN grows significantly,

parent-child relationships may be introduced.

Example:

```text
Engineering
    ↓
Software Engineering

Engineering
    ↓
DevOps

Engineering
    ↓
Architecture
```

Do not implement this initially.

Design for it.

---

# Content Assignment Rules

Every Article must have exactly one primary Category.

Example:

```text
Article
    ↓
Software Engineering
```

---

# Why Only One?

Multiple Categories create ambiguity.

Example:

```text
Article

↓

Software Engineering

Leadership

Career

Product
```

Readers no longer know the primary focus.

One Category forces clarity.

---

# Notebook Entries

Notebook Entries may optionally have Categories.

Example:

```text
Reflection

↓

Leadership
```

However, Categories are less important for Notebook Entries than for Articles.

---

# Category Metadata

Each Category should include:

```text
Name

Slug

Description

Status

Display Order

Created Date
```

---

# Canonical Database Structure

```sql
categories
----------
id
slug
name
description
status
display_order
created_at
updated_at
deleted_at
```

---

# Field Specifications

## id

UUID.

Primary key.

Immutable.

---

## slug

Example:

```text
software-engineering
```

Used in URLs.

Example:

```text
/categories/software-engineering
```

Must be unique.

---

## name

Required.

Examples:

```text
Software Engineering

Leadership

Career
```

Maximum:

100 characters.

---

## description

Required.

Provides editorial context.

Example:

> Articles and reflections about software architecture, development, engineering practices, and technical decision-making.

---

## status

Enum:

```text
draft

active

archived
```

---

## display_order

Controls category presentation.

Never depend on creation dates.

---

# Category Pages

Every Category should eventually have its own page.

Example:

```text
/categories/software-engineering
```

Containing:

* category description
* featured content
* latest articles
* latest notebook entries
* related tags

---

# Search Behavior

Searching:

```text
Architecture
```

may return:

```text
Category

Software Engineering
```

alongside content.

Categories should be searchable.

---

# Category Navigation

Categories should power:

```text
Browse by Topic
```

on the homepage.

Example:

```text
Software Engineering

Leadership

Career

Design

Open Source
```

This becomes a major discovery mechanism.

---

# Relationship Model

```text
Category
    ↓
Articles

Category
    ↓
Notebook Entries (optional)
```

Future:

```text
Category
    ↓
Books

Category
    ↓
Podcasts

Category
    ↓
Videos
```

---

# Category Analytics

Categories should eventually track:

* total content count
* total reads
* growth trends
* reader interest

Analytics remain outside the Category domain.

---

# Media Relationships

Categories may eventually have:

```text
Icon

Cover Illustration

Banner Image
```

These remain references to Media.

---

# Future Evolution

Categories should support:

* parent-child relationships
* category collections
* category landing pages
* category recommendations
* learning paths
* category newsletters

without changing the core model.

---

# Engineering Principles

Every engineer working with Categories must remember:

1. Categories classify topics.
2. Categories are not Journeys.
3. Categories are not Tags.
4. Categories should remain stable.
5. Categories should be broad.
6. Every Article must have one primary Category.
7. Categories should aid discovery.
8. Categories should pass the 80-Year Rule.
9. Categories belong to Editorial.
10. Categories should remain simple.

---

# Relationship Summary

```text
Category
    ↓
Articles

Category
    ↓
Notebook Entries (optional)

Category
    ↓
Future Content Types
```

---

## End of Section 7.4.10.6

Categories establish the topical structure of TWN.

At this point we have:

```text
Author
   ↓
Journey
   ↓
Chapter
   ↓
Content

Content
   ↓
Category
```

The next section is **7.4.10.7 — Tags**, which will define fine-grained knowledge classification and explain why Tags describe content while Categories classify content and Journeys contextualize content. This distinction becomes increasingly important as TWN grows into hundreds or thousands of pieces of content.

# 7.4.10.7 Domain Specification — Tags

---

# Purpose

Tags provide fine-grained knowledge classification within TWN.

If Categories answer:

> What broad domain does this belong to?

Tags answer:

> What specific concepts are discussed here?

Tags exist to improve discoverability, navigation, search, and knowledge connections across the platform.

They create a semantic layer that Categories alone cannot provide.

---

# Philosophy

Categories are broad.

Tags are precise.

Journeys are narrative.

These three systems must never be confused.

---

## Example

Article:

```text
Why I Chose Next.js
```

Category:

```text
Software Engineering
```

Journey:

```text
Building TWN
```

Tags:

```text
Next.js

React

Architecture

SSR

Performance
```

Each serves a different purpose.

---

# Categories vs Tags vs Journeys

This distinction should be documented everywhere.

---

## Categories Answer

```text
What field is this in?
```

Examples:

```text
Software Engineering

Leadership

Career
```

---

## Tags Answer

```text
What concepts appear here?
```

Examples:

```text
Next.js

TypeScript

System Design

Open Source
```

---

## Journeys Answer

```text
What story does this belong to?
```

Examples:

```text
Building TWN

Learning Architecture
```

---

# What Is A Tag?

A Tag is a semantic descriptor.

It identifies important concepts discussed within content.

A Tag should represent:

* ideas
* technologies
* frameworks
* methodologies
* people
* organizations
* disciplines
* patterns

---

# Examples

Good Tags:

```text
React

Next.js

TypeScript

Accessibility

Open Source

Leadership

Documentation

System Design

DevOps

Mentorship
```

---

Bad Tags:

```text
Article

Interesting

Cool

My Thoughts

Stuff

General
```

These provide no discovery value.

---

# Why Tags Exist

Without Tags:

```text
1000 Articles
```

must be discovered primarily through Categories.

This becomes limiting.

Tags create:

* semantic relationships
* search relevance
* topic exploration
* knowledge discovery
* content recommendations

---

# Example

Reader opens:

```text
Designing TWN's CMS
```

Tags:

```text
Next.js

CMS

Architecture

Editorial Systems
```

Reader clicks:

```text
Architecture
```

and discovers:

```text
Designing TWN's CMS

Authentication Decisions

Building Scalable APIs

The Cost of Overengineering
```

This creates learning pathways.

---

# Tag Ownership

Tags belong to the Editorial Domain.

Editorial staff controls:

* creation
* modification
* archival

Readers do not create Tags.

---

# Tag Lifecycle

Tags evolve slowly.

Lifecycle:

```text
Draft
   ↓
Active
   ↓
Archived
```

---

## Draft

Being defined.

Not publicly visible.

---

## Active

Available for assignment.

Visible publicly.

---

## Archived

No longer assigned to new content.

Existing relationships remain.

---

# Tag Assignment Rules

Unlike Categories:

Tags are many-to-many.

---

## Article

Can have:

```text
1 Category

Many Tags
```

---

Example:

```text
Category

Software Engineering

Tags

Next.js
React
Architecture
Performance
```

---

# Recommended Limits

To avoid abuse:

Minimum:

```text
0
```

Maximum:

```text
10
```

Recommended:

```text
3–7
```

Tags.

Too many Tags reduce quality.

---

# Tag Quality Principle

Every Tag should improve discovery.

Before creating a Tag ask:

> Will a reader benefit from browsing this concept?

If the answer is no,

the Tag should not exist.

---

# The 80-Year Rule

Categories must pass the 80-Year Rule.

Tags do not necessarily have to.

---

Example

Valid Tag:

```text
Next.js
```

Next.js may disappear someday.

That's acceptable.

The Tag reflects the content.

---

Categories should be timeless.

Tags may evolve with knowledge.

---

# Canonical Database Structure

```sql
tags
----
id
slug
name
description
status
created_at
updated_at
deleted_at
```

---

# Relationship Table

Tags require a junction table.

```sql
content_tags
------------
id
tag_id
content_type
content_id
created_at
```

---

However, for Version 1, I would recommend:

```sql
article_tags
------------
article_id
tag_id
```

because only Articles and Notebook Entries exist.

---

Future evolution can introduce:

```text
Books

Podcasts

Videos

Courses
```

when needed.

Do not overengineer early.

---

# Field Specifications

## id

UUID.

Primary key.

Immutable.

---

## slug

Example:

```text
next-js
```

Used in URLs.

Example:

```text
/tags/next-js
```

Must be unique.

---

## name

Required.

Example:

```text
Next.js
```

Maximum:

100 characters.

---

## description

Optional.

Provides context.

Example:

> Articles discussing the Next.js framework and its ecosystem.

---

## status

Enum:

```text
draft

active

archived
```

---

# Tag Pages

Every Tag should eventually have a dedicated page.

Example:

```text
/tags/system-design
```

Containing:

* description
* latest content
* related tags
* related categories

---

# Tag Discovery

Tags should support:

```text
Related Topics
```

throughout the platform.

---

Example

Reader viewing:

```text
Architecture
```

may see:

```text
Related Tags

Scalability

System Design

Performance

DevOps
```

---

# Search Behavior

Searching:

```text
Next.js
```

should return:

```text
Tag

Next.js

142 Articles
```

alongside matching content.

---

# Relationship Model

```text
Tag
    ↔
Articles

Tag
    ↔
Notebook Entries (future)

Tag
    ↔
Books (future)

Tag
    ↔
Podcasts (future)

Tag
    ↔
Videos (future)
```

---

# Tag Analytics

Future metrics:

* content count
* reader interest
* growth trends
* related tag networks

These belong to Analytics, not Tags.

---

# Anti-Patterns

Avoid:

```text
Creating tags for every article.

Creating tags with one use.

Duplicating category names as tags.

Creating vague tags.
```

---

Bad Example:

```text
Tag

Software Engineering
```

when Software Engineering already exists as a Category.

---

# Future Evolution

Tags should support:

* related tag graphs
* semantic relationships
* tag recommendations
* learning pathways
* knowledge maps
* AI-assisted discovery

without changing the core model.

---

# Engineering Principles

Every engineer working with Tags must remember:

1. Tags describe concepts.
2. Tags are not Categories.
3. Tags are not Journeys.
4. Tags improve discovery.
5. Tags are many-to-many.
6. Tags should remain meaningful.
7. Tags should not duplicate Categories.
8. Tags should evolve with knowledge.
9. Tags should strengthen search.
10. Every Tag should justify its existence.

---

# Relationship Summary

```text
Category
    ↓
Classifies

Article

Tag
    ↔
Describes

Article

Journey
    ↓
Contextualizes

Article
```

This is one of the most important distinctions in the entire TWN architecture.

---

## End of Section 7.4.10.7

At this point, the complete Editorial Domain is defined:

```text
Author
   ↓
Journey
   ↓
Chapter
   ↓
Content

Content
├── Articles
├── Notebook Entries
│
├── Category
│
└── Tags
```

The next section should be **7.4.10.8 — Shared Pages**, where we begin documenting the Community Domain and define how community-generated content differs from editorial content while preserving TWN's premium, notebook-like identity.

# 7.4.10.8 Domain Specification — Shared Pages

---

# Purpose

Shared Pages are the primary community contribution mechanism within TWN.

They allow readers and members of the community to contribute their own reflections, experiences, lessons, and stories while preserving the editorial quality and notebook identity of the platform.

Shared Pages exist to answer:

> What are other women learning, experiencing, questioning, and becoming?

They create a bridge between TWN's editorial voice and its community.

---

# Philosophy

Shared Pages are not Articles.

Shared Pages are not Notebook Entries.

Shared Pages are not Comments.

Shared Pages are not Social Posts.

Shared Pages are community reflections.

---

# Why Shared Pages Exist

TWN is not intended to be a one-way publication.

Knowledge grows through conversation.

Experience becomes richer when shared.

The goal is not merely:

```text
Author
   ↓
Reader
```

The goal is:

```text
Author
   ↓
Community
   ↓
Shared Learning
```

Shared Pages create this second layer.

---

# The Notebook Principle

A Shared Page should feel like:

> A page another woman slipped into the notebook.

Not:

> A blog post.

Not:

> A social media update.

Not:

> A comment thread.

Not:

> User-generated content spam.

The tone should remain thoughtful, reflective, and personal.

---

# Examples

Good Shared Page

```text
Last year I almost quit software engineering.

Not because I couldn't code.

Because I convinced myself everyone else was progressing faster than I was.

Looking back, comparison was doing more damage than failure ever did.
```

---

Good Shared Page

```text
Today I realized mentorship is not always advice.

Sometimes it is simply seeing someone survive what you are currently facing.
```

---

Bad Shared Page

```text
Check out my portfolio.

Like and subscribe.

Follow me here.
```

---

Bad Shared Page

```text
Who else loves JavaScript?
```

Too shallow.

---

# Core Responsibilities

Shared Pages exist to:

* amplify community voices
* preserve personal reflections
* encourage learning in public
* build belonging
* document growth
* create human connection

---

Shared Pages are NOT responsible for:

* tutorials
* long-form teaching
* editorial essays
* platform announcements
* reader discussions

Those belong elsewhere.

---

# Editorial Standard

Every Shared Page must pass one test:

> Does this add meaningful value to another reader?

If not,

it should not be published.

---

# Moderation Philosophy

Unlike Articles,

Shared Pages are community-generated.

Therefore moderation is mandatory.

There is no direct publishing.

Workflow:

```text
Submission
   ↓
Review
   ↓
Approved
   ↓
Published
```

---

# Why Moderation Exists

Without moderation:

```text
Community
   ↓
Noise
```

With moderation:

```text
Community
   ↓
Quality
```

The goal is not censorship.

The goal is maintaining signal.

---

# Ownership Model

Every Shared Page belongs to its contributor.

However,

TWN retains editorial control over publication.

Example:

```text
Contributor
   ↓
Shared Page
```

---

# Contributor Identity

Contributors may choose:

```text
Real Name
```

or

```text
Anonymous
```

Both should be supported.

---

Examples

```text
Vanessa
```

```text
Anonymous
```

```text
A Junior Developer
```

---

# Lifecycle

Shared Pages follow this workflow:

```text
Draft
   ↓
Pending Review
   ↓
Approved
   ↓
Published
   ↓
Archived
```

---

## Draft

Being written.

Not yet submitted.

---

## Pending Review

Awaiting moderation.

Not publicly visible.

---

## Approved

Accepted by editors.

Ready for publication.

---

## Published

Visible on the site.

Searchable.

Discoverable.

---

## Archived

Preserved.

No longer actively surfaced.

---

# Length Philosophy

Shared Pages should remain concise.

Target:

```text
150–300 words
```

Minimum:

```text
100 words
```

Maximum:

```text
500 words
```

These are editorial limits.

Not technical limitations.

---

# Why Keep Them Short?

Shared Pages are reflections.

Not essays.

Readers should be able to consume multiple reflections in a single sitting.

---

# Homepage Integration

Shared Pages appear within:

```text
Pages From The Community
```

section.

Example:

```text
Pages From The Community

─────────────────────

Sofia T.

Today I almost quit software engineering.

I didn't.

July 14, 2026

─────────────────────

Anonymous

Comparison was stealing my joy.

July 13, 2026
```

---

# Relationship To Articles

Shared Pages should remain independent.

A Shared Page does not belong to an Article.

A Shared Page does not belong to a Journey.

A Shared Page does not belong to a Category.

This is intentional.

---

# Why?

The moment Shared Pages become deeply attached to editorial structures,

they stop feeling like community voices.

They become editorial content.

---

# Search Behavior

Shared Pages should appear in search.

Results should clearly identify them.

Example:

```text
[Shared Page]

The Day I Almost Quit
```

---

# Canonical Database Structure

```sql
shared_pages
------------
id
author_name
content
word_count
status
submitted_at
reviewed_at
published_at
archived_at
created_at
updated_at
deleted_at
```

---

# Field Specifications

## id

UUID.

Primary key.

Immutable.

---

## author_name

Required.

Defaults to:

```text
Anonymous
```

if omitted.

---

## content

Required.

The reflection itself.

Plain text.

No rich formatting initially.

---

## word_count

Automatically generated.

Used for moderation and editorial quality.

---

## status

Enum:

```text
draft

pending

approved

published

archived
```

---

## submitted_at

Records submission.

---

## reviewed_at

Records moderation decision.

---

## published_at

Records publication.

---

# Validation Rules

Required:

✓ Author Name (or Anonymous)

✓ Content

✓ Status

---

Content must:

* be original
* be respectful
* be meaningful
* avoid spam
* avoid self-promotion

---

# Editorial Guidelines

Moderators should prioritize:

* authenticity
* insight
* vulnerability
* usefulness
* reflection

Not:

* popularity
* status
* credentials

---

# Future Evolution

Shared Pages may eventually support:

* themes
* featured reflections
* audio submissions
* seasonal collections
* community anthologies
* annual reflection books

without changing the core model.

---

# Security Principles

Readers may:

* submit reflections
* view published reflections

Editors may:

* review
* approve
* archive

Readers may never:

* directly publish
* modify approved content

---

# Engineering Principles

Every engineer working with Shared Pages must remember:

1. Shared Pages are community reflections.
2. Shared Pages are not Articles.
3. Shared Pages are not Comments.
4. Moderation is mandatory.
5. Reflection is more important than engagement.
6. Quality matters more than volume.
7. Community voices should feel human.
8. Simplicity should be preserved.
9. Shared Pages should strengthen belonging.
10. The notebook identity must remain intact.

---

# Relationship Summary

```text
Contributor
    ↓
Shared Page

Shared Page
    ↓
Moderation

Moderation
    ↓
Publication
```

---

## End of Section 7.4.10.8

Shared Pages establish the first major component of the Community Domain.

They allow TWN to evolve from a personal publication into a shared space for thoughtful reflection while maintaining editorial quality and coherence.

The next section should be **7.4.10.9 — Margin Notes**, which defines the second community mechanism and one of the most unique features of TWN: allowing readers to leave short reflections directly alongside knowledge, much like writing in the margin of a physical notebook.

# 7.4.10.9 Domain Specification — Margin Notes

---

# Purpose

Margin Notes are TWN's primary reader reflection mechanism.

They allow readers to leave short, thoughtful responses directly beneath a piece of content.

Unlike comments, Margin Notes are not discussions.

Unlike Shared Pages, Margin Notes are not standalone reflections.

A Margin Note exists because a reader encountered an idea and felt compelled to write something beside it.

Just as readers write notes in the margins of physical books, Margin Notes allow readers to leave traces of thought beside TWN content.

---

# Philosophy

Most websites use comments.

TWN intentionally does not.

Comments often become:

* debates
* arguments
* promotion
* notifications
* engagement farming

None of those support TWN's mission.

Margin Notes exist for reflection.

Not conversation.

---

# The Notebook Principle

Imagine reading a notebook.

You finish a page.

At the bottom someone has written:

> "I needed this today."

or

> "This reminded me of my first internship."

or

> "I never thought about architecture this way."

That is a Margin Note.

It is small.

Personal.

Human.

Temporary.

Yet meaningful.

---

# What A Margin Note Is

A Margin Note is:

* a reflection
* a reaction
* an observation
* a realization
* a brief insight

---

Examples

Good Margin Note:

```text id="lx6fxq"
This reminded me that simplicity is a feature.
```

---

Good Margin Note:

```text id="qjhkv4"
I spent months making the same mistake.
```

---

Good Margin Note:

```text id="h9q4we"
Thank you. I needed this today.
```

---

# What A Margin Note Is NOT

Margin Notes are not:

* discussions
* replies
* threads
* debates
* questions and answers
* social media comments
* marketing

---

Bad Example:

```text id="s2w1s2"
Can you explain this more?
```

---

Bad Example:

```text id="j15k9f"
Follow me on LinkedIn.
```

---

Bad Example:

```text id="i4xtik"
You're wrong.
```

---

# Why Margin Notes Exist

Knowledge should not feel one-sided.

Readers should be able to leave evidence of learning.

Margin Notes answer:

> What did this stir in you?

---

They help create:

* reflection
* connection
* belonging
* reader participation

without turning TWN into a social network.

---

# Relationship To Content

Margin Notes always belong to a specific content item.

Version 1:

```text id="7d9wcl"
Article
    ↓
Margin Notes
```

---

Future:

```text id="ojg4z9"
Article
    ↓
Margin Notes

Notebook Entry
    ↓
Margin Notes

Book Chapter
    ↓
Margin Notes
```

---

# One-Way Communication

Margin Notes are intentionally one-way.

Example:

```text id="2sj1z2"
Reader

↓

Margin Note

↓

Published
```

---

Not:

```text id="z42sdl"
Reader A

↓

Reader B

↓

Reader C

↓

Thread
```

This is prohibited.

---

# Why No Replies?

Replies create:

* moderation complexity
* arguments
* notifications
* hierarchy

Eventually:

```text id="x1ttrg"
Comment System
```

Instead of:

```text id="y38qtw"
Notebook
```

The notebook metaphor must remain intact.

---

# Ownership Model

A Margin Note belongs to:

```text id="qkwglx"
Content
```

and

```text id="49zv7f"
Reader
```

simultaneously.

---

Example

```text id="z7mjlwm"
Article
Designing TWN

↓

Margin Note

↓

Anonymous Reader
```

---

# Reader Identity

Readers may choose:

```text id="6xyxq0"
Real Name
```

or

```text id="p0cjlwm"
Anonymous
```

---

Examples

```text id="8mbr35"
Vanessa
```

```text id="qvw5nq"
Anonymous
```

---

```text id="lb2z9x"
A Reader From Kenya
```

---

Identity should remain lightweight.

---

# Lifecycle

Margin Notes follow a moderation workflow.

```text id="7p7t9t"
Draft
   ↓
Pending Review
   ↓
Approved
   ↓
Published
   ↓
Archived
```

---

## Draft

Being written.

Not submitted.

---

## Pending Review

Awaiting moderation.

Not visible.

---

## Approved

Accepted.

Ready for publication.

---

## Published

Visible publicly.

Displayed below content.

---

## Archived

Retained historically.

No longer visible.

---

# Length Philosophy

Margin Notes should be short.

Very short.

---

Target:

```text id="v4lcc7"
20–120 characters
```

---

Maximum:

```text id="p5v4v4"
200 characters
```

---

Why?

A Margin Note should feel handwritten.

Not like a comment.

Not like a post.

---

# User Experience

At the end of content:

```text id="5v6vxz"
────────────

Leave a Margin Note

[ Your Name ]

[ What did this stir in you? ]

                Leave Note

────────────

Margin Notes

"This changed how I think about architecture."

— Anonymous

"This felt painfully familiar."

— Sofia T.
```

---

The interaction should feel calm.

Not social.

---

# Moderation Philosophy

All Margin Notes require approval.

No instant publishing.

---

Reasons:

* spam prevention
* quality preservation
* notebook identity
* reader safety

---

# Editorial Standard

Every published Margin Note should:

* add value
* show reflection
* remain respectful
* feel human

---

Reject:

* insults
* spam
* self-promotion
* trolling
* harassment

---

# Canonical Database Structure

```sql
margin_notes
------------
id
content_type
content_id
author_name
content
status
submitted_at
reviewed_at
published_at
archived_at
created_at
updated_at
deleted_at
```

---

# Field Specifications

## id

UUID.

Primary key.

Immutable.

---

## content_type

Version 1:

```text
article
```

Future:

```text
notebook_entry

book_chapter

podcast_episode
```

---

## content_id

References the parent content.

---

## author_name

Optional.

Defaults to:

```text
Anonymous
```

---

## content

Required.

The Margin Note itself.

---

## status

Enum:

```text
pending

approved

published

archived
```

---

# Validation Rules

Required:

✓ Content

✓ Parent Content

✓ Status

Optional:

Author Name

---

Content must:

* remain under character limit
* be respectful
* avoid promotion
* avoid abuse

---

# Search Behavior

Margin Notes should NOT dominate search.

They are supporting reflections.

---

Searching:

```text
architecture
```

should prioritize:

1. Articles
2. Notebook Entries
3. Journeys
4. Categories
5. Tags

Margin Notes should appear only as supplemental context.

---

# Analytics

Future metrics:

* notes per article
* reflection rate
* participation rate
* approval rate

Analytics belong to Analytics Domain.

Not Margin Notes.

---

# Future Evolution

Margin Notes may eventually support:

* highlighted notes
* editor picks
* annual reflection collections
* export to community anthologies

They should NEVER support:

* replies
* likes
* follower systems
* threading

Those features violate the notebook philosophy.

---

# Engineering Principles

Every engineer working with Margin Notes must remember:

1. Margin Notes are reflections.
2. Margin Notes are not comments.
3. Margin Notes are not discussions.
4. Replies are prohibited.
5. Simplicity is mandatory.
6. Moderation is required.
7. Reader reflection matters more than engagement.
8. The notebook metaphor must remain intact.
9. Margin Notes should feel handwritten.
10. TWN is a knowledge platform, not a social network.

---

# Relationship Summary

```text
Reader
   ↓
Margin Note
   ↓
Article

Future

Reader
   ↓
Margin Note
   ↓
Notebook Entry

Reader
   ↓
Margin Note
   ↓
Book Chapter
```

---

## End of Section 7.4.10.9

With Margin Notes completed, the Community Domain becomes fully defined:

```text
Community
│
├── Shared Pages
│
└── Margin Notes
```

Shared Pages answer:

> "What do I want to share?"

Margin Notes answer:

> "What did this make me think or feel?"

Together they create community participation without turning TWN into a traditional social platform.

The next section should be **7.4.10.10 — Newsletter Subscribers**, because newsletters become the primary communication channel between TWN and readers and eventually power distribution, retention, audience growth, and future editorial products.

# 7.4.10.10 Domain Specification — Newsletter Subscribers

---

# Purpose

The Newsletter Subscriber domain represents the relationship between TWN and its readers.

Its purpose is not merely to collect email addresses.

Its purpose is to create a direct, permission-based communication channel between the platform and the people who find value in it.

This domain exists to answer:

> Who wants to continue this journey with us?

Unlike social media platforms, where distribution is controlled by algorithms, newsletters provide a direct connection between TWN and its audience.

---

# Philosophy

The newsletter is not a marketing funnel.

The newsletter is not an advertising channel.

The newsletter is not a growth hack.

The newsletter is an extension of the notebook.

Readers subscribe because they want continued access to ideas, reflections, journeys, and knowledge.

The relationship should always feel:

```text
Reader
    ↔
Notebook
```

not

```text
Brand
    ↓
Marketing Target
```

---

# Why Newsletters Exist

TWN owns its content.

TWN does not own social media platforms.

Algorithms change.

Platforms rise and fall.

Audience ownership matters.

The newsletter creates:

* direct communication
* audience retention
* reader loyalty
* distribution independence
* long-term sustainability

---

# Core Responsibilities

The Newsletter domain is responsible for:

* subscriber management
* subscription status
* email preferences
* consent tracking
* communication permissions
* newsletter delivery metadata

---

The Newsletter domain is NOT responsible for:

* content creation
* email rendering
* analytics calculations
* audience segmentation logic
* journey ownership

Those belong elsewhere.

---

# Subscriber Philosophy

A subscriber is not a lead.

A subscriber is not a conversion.

A subscriber is not a metric.

A subscriber is a person who has trusted TWN with access to their inbox.

That trust must be respected.

---

# Types of Newsletter Content

The newsletter may eventually distribute:

```text
New Articles

Notebook Entries

Journey Updates

Community Highlights

Shared Pages

Editorial Letters

Books (future)

Podcast Releases (future)

Course Launches (future)
```

The newsletter distributes knowledge.

It does not own knowledge.

---

# Subscription Lifecycle

Every subscriber follows a lifecycle.

```text
Visitor
   ↓
Subscribed
   ↓
Confirmed
   ↓
Active
   ↓
Unsubscribed
```

---

## Visitor

Not subscribed.

Anonymous.

---

## Subscribed

Submitted email.

Awaiting confirmation.

---

## Confirmed

Email ownership verified.

---

## Active

Receiving communications.

---

## Unsubscribed

Opted out.

No further emails.

Historical record retained.

---

# Double Opt-In Principle

TWN should eventually support double opt-in.

Workflow:

```text
Subscribe
   ↓
Confirmation Email
   ↓
Confirm
   ↓
Active Subscriber
```

This improves:

* trust
* deliverability
* compliance
* audience quality

---

# Why This Matters

Without confirmation:

```text
Spam
```

With confirmation:

```text
Intentional Readers
```

The goal is quality.

Not volume.

---

# Subscriber Ownership

Subscribers belong to the Communication Domain.

Not Editorial.

Not Analytics.

Not Marketing.

Communication owns the relationship.

---

# Canonical Database Structure

```sql
newsletter_subscribers
----------------------
id
email
status
confirmed_at
unsubscribed_at
source
created_at
updated_at
deleted_at
```

---

# Field Specifications

## id

UUID.

Primary key.

Immutable.

---

## email

Required.

Unique.

Normalized.

Stored in lowercase.

Example:

```text
reader@example.com
```

---

## status

Enum:

```text
pending

active

unsubscribed
```

---

## confirmed_at

Records verification time.

May remain NULL before confirmation.

---

## unsubscribed_at

Records opt-out event.

Used for compliance and analytics.

---

## source

Tracks acquisition source.

Examples:

```text
homepage

article

journey

shared_page

direct
```

This helps understand growth channels.

---

# Validation Rules

Required:

✓ Email

✓ Status

---

Email must:

* be valid
* be unique
* be normalized

---

# Consent Philosophy

Consent is mandatory.

Subscribers must always know:

* what they are subscribing to
* how often communication occurs
* how to unsubscribe

Hidden consent is prohibited.

---

# Unsubscribe Principle

Every email must include:

```text
Unsubscribe
```

No exceptions.

Leaving should be as easy as joining.

---

# Newsletter Homepage Integration

Current location:

```text
Homepage
   ↓
Newsletter Section
```

Example:

```text
Stay Close To The Notebook

Get thoughtful reflections,
new articles,
and journey updates.

[ Email Address ]

[ Subscribe ]
```

The tone should feel personal.

Not corporate.

---

# Editorial Letter Vision

Future newsletters should feel like:

```text
A letter from the notebook
```

rather than:

```text
A marketing campaign
```

Example:

```text
This week I learned something unexpected while redesigning TWN...
```

This aligns with the brand.

---

# Search Behavior

Subscribers should never appear in public search.

Newsletter data is private.

---

# Security Requirements

Newsletter data is sensitive.

Requirements:

* encrypted transport
* secure storage
* unsubscribe support
* consent records
* access controls

Only authorized staff may access subscriber information.

---

# Privacy Principles

TWN should collect the minimum information required.

Version 1:

```text
Email Only
```

This is sufficient.

Avoid collecting:

```text
Address

Phone Number

Employer

Date of Birth
```

unless a future business requirement exists.

---

# Relationship Model

```text
Subscriber
    ↓
Receives

Articles

Notebook Entries

Journey Updates

Community Highlights
```

The subscriber receives content.

They do not own content.

---

# Distribution Domain Integration

Future architecture:

```text
Editorial
    ↓
Content

Distribution
    ↓
Newsletter

Subscriber
    ↓
Receives Content
```

This keeps responsibilities separate.

---

# Future Evolution

Newsletter Subscribers should support:

* preference management
* topic subscriptions
* journey subscriptions
* weekly digests
* editorial letters
* recommendation engines
* book release notifications
* podcast release notifications

without changing the core model.

---

# What We Will NOT Build

Avoid:

```text
Follower Counts

Subscriber Rankings

Public Subscriber Lists

Social Features
```

These violate the notebook philosophy.

---

# Engineering Principles

Every engineer working with Newsletter Subscribers must remember:

1. Subscribers are people, not metrics.
2. Consent is mandatory.
3. Unsubscribing must be easy.
4. Privacy matters.
5. Newsletter data is sensitive.
6. Communication should remain personal.
7. The newsletter distributes content; it does not create content.
8. The inbox is a privilege.
9. Trust is more important than growth.
10. The relationship should feel human.

---

# Relationship Summary

```text
Subscriber
    ↓
Newsletter

Newsletter
    ↓
Articles

Notebook Entries

Journey Updates

Community Highlights
```

---

## End of Section 7.4.10.10

With Newsletter Subscribers complete, TWN now has its first fully documented communication channel.

The next section should be:

**7.4.10.11 — Media**

This will be one of the most technically important sections because Media will eventually power:

* Article cover images
* Notebook illustrations
* Author avatars
* Journey covers
* Chapter artwork
* Future podcasts
* Future videos
* Future books
* Downloadable resources

It becomes the foundation for every visual and multimedia asset across the entire platform.

# 7.4.10.11 Domain Specification — Media

---

# Purpose

The Media domain is responsible for managing all non-text assets within TWN.

It provides a centralized system for storing, organizing, referencing, securing, and delivering media across the platform.

Media exists to support content.

Media is not content.

This distinction is important.

An Article owns knowledge.

A Media asset supports the communication of that knowledge.

---

# Philosophy

Many systems treat media as uploaded files.

TWN does not.

TWN treats media as reusable editorial assets.

Every image, illustration, audio recording, cover image, video, and downloadable resource should be managed as a first-class asset.

---

# Core Principle

A file is not the asset.

The asset is the thing the file represents.

Example:

```text
Article Cover Image
```

may exist as:

```text
original.webp

thumbnail.webp

social-preview.webp

mobile.webp
```

The file changes.

The asset remains the same.

---

# Why Media Exists

Without Media:

```text
Articles
Notebook Entries
Journeys
Authors
```

become text-only experiences.

Media enhances:

* comprehension
* emotion
* storytelling
* visual identity
* accessibility
* discoverability

---

# Core Responsibilities

Media is responsible for:

* asset storage
* asset metadata
* asset relationships
* asset delivery
* optimization
* accessibility metadata
* asset lifecycle management

---

Media is NOT responsible for:

* content ownership
* publishing workflows
* editorial classification
* user permissions

---

# Media Ownership Philosophy

Media does not own anything.

Everything references Media.

Example:

```text
Article
    ↓
Cover Image

Author
    ↓
Avatar

Journey
    ↓
Cover Art
```

Ownership remains with the parent entity.

---

# Media Types

Version 1

```text
Images
```

Supported formats:

```text
jpg
jpeg
png
webp
svg
```

---

Future Versions

```text
Audio

Video

Documents

Books

Downloads

Presentations

Templates
```

---

# Media Classification

Media should be classified by purpose.

Not by file extension.

---

Examples

```text
Avatar

Cover Image

Banner Image

Illustration

Hero Asset

Download

Audio Recording

Video Recording
```

---

This classification is more useful than:

```text
jpg

png

webp
```

---

# Media Lifecycle

Every Media asset follows a lifecycle.

```text
Uploaded
   ↓
Processed
   ↓
Active
   ↓
Archived
```

---

## Uploaded

File exists.

Metadata incomplete.

Not publicly usable.

---

## Processed

Optimization completed.

Metadata generated.

Ready for use.

---

## Active

Available throughout the platform.

---

## Archived

No longer used.

Retained for historical references.

---

# Media Reuse Principle

The same asset may be used multiple times.

Example:

```text
Author Avatar
```

appears in:

```text
Author Profile

Articles

Notebook Entries

Journey Pages
```

Only one media record should exist.

---

Never duplicate files unnecessarily.

---

# Canonical Database Structure

```sql
media
-----
id
slug
filename
mime_type
file_size
width
height
alt_text
caption
storage_key
status
uploaded_by
created_at
updated_at
deleted_at
```

---

# Field Specifications

## id

UUID.

Primary key.

Immutable.

---

## slug

Human-readable identifier.

Example:

```text
vanessa-profile-photo
```

---

## filename

Original file name.

Example:

```text
vanessa-profile.webp
```

---

## mime_type

Example:

```text
image/webp

image/jpeg

image/png
```

---

## file_size

Stored in bytes.

Example:

```text
152400
```

---

## width

Pixel width.

Example:

```text
1200
```

---

## height

Pixel height.

Example:

```text
800
```

---

## alt_text

Required for all editorial images.

Example:

```text
Notebook resting on a wooden desk beside a laptop.
```

Accessibility first.

---

## caption

Optional.

Example:

```text
Early design sketches of TWN.
```

---

## storage_key

Internal storage reference.

Example:

```text
media/2027/07/notebook-cover.webp
```

Never expose storage implementation details publicly.

---

## status

Enum:

```text
uploaded

processed

active

archived
```

---

## uploaded_by

Reference:

```text
users
```

Tracks uploader.

---

# Accessibility Philosophy

Accessibility is not optional.

Every meaningful image should contain:

```text
alt_text
```

---

Bad:

```text
Image 1
```

---

Good:

```text
Wireframe sketch of TWN's homepage notebook layout.
```

---

# Relationship Model

Version 1

```text
Media
│
├── Author Avatar
├── Article Cover
├── Journey Cover
├── Chapter Illustration
└── Homepage Assets
```

---

Future

```text
Media
│
├── Audio Files
├── Video Files
├── Book Covers
├── Downloadables
├── Templates
└── Course Assets
```

---

# Article Integration

Articles should never store:

```sql
cover_image_url
```

---

Instead:

```sql
cover_media_id
```

---

Why?

Because:

* URLs change
* storage providers change
* optimization changes

Media IDs remain stable.

---

# Author Integration

Authors reference:

```sql
avatar_media_id
```

not:

```sql
avatar_url
```

---

# Journey Integration

Journeys reference:

```sql
cover_media_id
```

---

# Chapter Integration

Chapters may reference:

```sql
cover_media_id
```

or

```sql
illustration_media_id
```

---

# Homepage Integration

The homepage may reference:

```text
Hero Notebook Image

Background Assets

Editorial Illustrations
```

through the Media domain.

---

# Storage Philosophy

Version 1:

Use object storage.

Examples:

* Cloudflare R2
* AWS S3
* Backblaze B2

Avoid storing files inside the database.

Store:

```text
metadata
```

in the database.

Store:

```text
files
```

in object storage.

---

# Image Optimization

All uploaded images should eventually generate:

