```text
Original

Large

Medium

Thumbnail
```

versions.

---

Why?

Improves:

* performance
* SEO
* bandwidth usage

---

# Search Behavior

Media should not dominate search.

Readers search for:

```text
knowledge
```

not

```text
files
```

Media appears as supporting content.

---

# Security Principles

Only authorized users may:

* upload
* replace
* archive

media assets.

Public users may only:

* view active assets

---

# Media Deletion Philosophy

Prefer soft deletion.

Example:

```text
deleted_at
```

---

Why?

An image may still be referenced by:

* Articles
* Authors
* Journeys

Hard deletion risks broken content.

---

# Future Evolution

Media should support:

```text
Audio

Video

Podcast Assets

Course Assets

Book Assets

Downloadable Resources

Version History

AI-generated Metadata

Image Focal Points

Responsive Images
```

without changing the core model.

---

# Engineering Principles

Every engineer working with Media must remember:

1. Media supports content.
2. Media is not content.
3. Files are not assets.
4. Reuse assets whenever possible.
5. Store metadata in the database.
6. Store files in object storage.
7. Accessibility is mandatory.
8. Use references, not URLs.
9. Optimize for performance.
10. Preserve media history.

---

# Relationship Summary

```text
Media
│
├── Articles
├── Notebook Entries
├── Authors
├── Journeys
├── Chapters
└── Future Content Types
```

Media becomes the shared asset layer of the entire TWN ecosystem.

---

## End of Section 7.4.10.11

At this point, the Editorial Domain, Community Domain, Communication Domain, and Media Domain are fully specified.

The final foundational section is:

**7.4.10.12 — Identity, Roles & Permissions**

This section will define:

* Users
* Authentication
* Authorization
* Editorial Roles
* Contributor Roles
* Admin Roles
* Ownership Rules
* Permission Boundaries
* Future Team Management

and becomes the security foundation for the entire platform.

# 7.4.10.12 Domain Specification — Identity, Roles & Permissions

---

# Purpose

The Identity Domain is responsible for determining:

* who someone is
* what they can access
* what they can modify
* what they can publish
* what they can administer

This domain provides the security foundation of TWN.

Without Identity, every user becomes equally powerful.

Without Permissions, content integrity cannot be guaranteed.

Without Roles, administration becomes impossible at scale.

---

# Philosophy

Identity is not about users.

Identity is about trust.

The system must always answer:

> Who performed this action?

> Were they allowed to perform it?

> Can we verify ownership?

---

# Core Principle

Authentication answers:

```text
Who are you?
```

Authorization answers:

```text
What are you allowed to do?
```

These are different problems.

---

# Example

User:

```text
Vanessa
```

Authentication:

```text
✓ Logged In
```

Authorization:

```text
✓ Can Publish Articles

✓ Can Edit Journeys

✓ Can Moderate Shared Pages
```

---

# Identity vs Author

This distinction is critical.

Many developers confuse these concepts.

---

## Author

Represents:

```text
Knowledge Creator
```

---

## User

Represents:

```text
Authenticated Account
```

---

Example:

```text
Guest Writer
```

may be:

```text
Author

WITHOUT

User Account
```

---

Example:

```text
System Administrator
```

may be:

```text
User

WITHOUT

Author Profile
```

---

Never merge these concepts.

---

# Why Separate Identity From Authors?

Because they evolve independently.

Authors own knowledge.

Users access systems.

A single person may be both.

But they are not the same entity.

---

# Identity Responsibilities

The Identity Domain manages:

* Accounts
* Authentication
* Sessions
* Roles
* Permissions
* Security Policies
* Access Control
* Team Membership

---

Identity is NOT responsible for:

* Content Ownership
* Categories
* Journeys
* Media
* Analytics

---

# Identity Architecture

```text
User
   ↓
Role
   ↓
Permissions
```

---

Example:

```text
Vanessa
   ↓
Editor
   ↓
Publish Articles
Edit Articles
Manage Journeys
```

---

# User Entity

A User represents a person with system access.

---

Examples:

```text
Administrator

Editor

Moderator

Future Contributor
```

---

# Canonical User Structure

```sql
users
-----
id
email
status
last_login_at
created_at
updated_at
deleted_at
```

---

# User Lifecycle

```text
Invited
   ↓
Active
   ↓
Suspended
   ↓
Archived
```

---

## Invited

Account created.

Not yet activated.

---

## Active

Normal access.

---

## Suspended

Temporarily blocked.

Cannot access protected areas.

---

## Archived

Historical record retained.

No access.

---

# Authentication

Version 1 Recommendation

Use:

```text
Clerk
```

or

```text
Supabase Auth
```

---

Why?

Avoid building authentication yourself.

Authentication is security-critical infrastructure.

Use mature providers.

---

# Supported Authentication Methods

Version 1

```text
Email + Password
```

---

Recommended Future

```text
Google

GitHub

Magic Links
```

---

Avoid complexity early.

---

# Session Management

Users receive authenticated sessions.

Sessions determine:

```text
Logged In

Logged Out
```

state.

---

Sessions should:

* expire automatically
* be revocable
* be secure
* support logout everywhere

---

# Roles Philosophy

Roles simplify permission management.

Instead of:

```text
User A

20 Permissions

User B

17 Permissions

User C

31 Permissions
```

Use:

```text
Role
   ↓
Permissions
```

---

# Version 1 Roles

I recommend only four.

---

## Super Admin

Full control.

Can do everything.

Typically:

```text
Founder
```

---

Permissions:

```text
All
```

---

## Editor

Responsible for editorial content.

Can:

* create articles
* edit articles
* publish articles
* manage notebook entries
* manage journeys

Cannot:

* modify system security

---

## Moderator

Responsible for community quality.

Can:

* review shared pages
* approve margin notes
* archive submissions

Cannot:

* publish articles

---

## Viewer

Read-only access.

Can:

* access admin dashboard metrics

Cannot modify anything.

---

# Why Only Four Roles?

Simple systems survive.

Complex role systems create confusion.

Start small.

Expand later.

---

# Permission Philosophy

Permissions define actions.

Examples:

```text
create_article

edit_article

publish_article

delete_article

approve_shared_page

manage_users
```

---

Roles group permissions.

---

Example

```text
Editor
```

contains:

```text
create_article

edit_article

publish_article
```

---

# Canonical Role Structure

```sql
roles
-----
id
name
description
created_at
```

---

# Canonical Permission Structure

```sql
permissions
-----------
id
name
description
created_at
```

---

# Role Permissions

Many-to-many relationship.

```sql
role_permissions
----------------
role_id
permission_id
```

---

# User Roles

Many-to-many relationship.

```sql
user_roles
----------
user_id
role_id
```

---

# Why Many-to-Many?

Future flexibility.

Example:

```text
Editor

+

Moderator
```

One user may hold both.

---

# Authorization Philosophy

Every protected action should verify permissions.

Never trust:

* UI visibility
* frontend state
* client requests

---

Bad:

```javascript
if (isAdmin) {
  showDeleteButton();
}
```

---

Good:

```text
Frontend Checks

+

Server Authorization

+

Database Policies
```

---

Security must exist at multiple layers.

---

# Row Level Security

If using Supabase:

Enable RLS.

Always.

---

Example:

```text
Editors

Can Modify Articles
```

---

Not:

```text
Any Authenticated User
```

---

The database should enforce security.

Not merely the application.

---

# Ownership Rules

Content ownership matters.

Example:

```text
Article
   ↓
Author
```

---

Editors may edit.

Authors may own.

Admins may override.

Ownership and permissions are separate concepts.

---

# Audit Logging

Every sensitive action should be tracked.

Examples:

```text
Published Article

Deleted Media

Approved Shared Page

Changed Permissions
```

---

Why?

Accountability.

---

Recommended Structure

```sql
audit_logs
----------
id
user_id
action
entity_type
entity_id
created_at
```

---

# Invitation System

Future team members should be invited.

Workflow:

```text
Invite

↓

Accept

↓

Role Assignment

↓

Access Granted
```

---

Avoid manual account creation.

---

# Security Principles

Every engineer must follow:

---

## Principle 1

Least Privilege

Give only the permissions required.

---

## Principle 2

Deny By Default

No access unless explicitly granted.

---

## Principle 3

Verify Server Side

Never trust clients.

---

## Principle 4

Log Sensitive Actions

Critical operations should leave evidence.

---

## Principle 5

Preserve Accountability

Every action should be traceable.

---

# Future Evolution

Identity should support:

```text
Teams

Organizations

Guest Contributors

External Reviewers

Author Invitations

Multi-Factor Authentication

Single Sign-On

API Keys

Service Accounts
```

without redesigning the core model.

---

# What We Will NOT Build

Avoid:

```text
Complex Permission Trees

Custom Authentication

Social Profiles

Followers

Messaging Systems
```

These are unnecessary for TWN's mission.

---

# Engineering Principles

Every engineer working with Identity must remember:

1. Identity is about trust.
2. Authentication and authorization are different.
3. Authors are not Users.
4. Roles simplify permissions.
5. Permissions control actions.
6. Security belongs at every layer.
7. RLS should be enforced.
8. Audit logs matter.
9. Simplicity improves security.
10. The safest system is the easiest to understand.

---

# Relationship Summary

```text
User
   ↓
Roles
   ↓
Permissions

User
   ↓
Audit Logs

Author
   ↓
Content

(User and Author remain separate domains)
```

---

## End of Section 7.4.10.12

With this section complete, the entire foundational domain model is now documented:

```text
Editorial
├── Authors
├── Articles
├── Notebook Entries
├── Journeys
├── Journey Chapters
├── Categories
├── Tags

Community
├── Shared Pages
├── Margin Notes

Communication
├── Newsletter Subscribers

Media
├── Media Assets

Identity
├── Users
├── Roles
├── Permissions
├── Audit Logs
```

Before moving to the next volume, I would recommend a dedicated section **7.4.11 — Database Schema Blueprint & Physical Data Model**, where we convert all these conceptual entities into actual tables, foreign keys, indexes, junction tables, constraints, RLS policies, and migration strategy. That's where architecture becomes implementation.


# 7.4.11 Database Schema Blueprint & Physical Data Model

**Status:** Foundational database specification
**Primary database:** PostgreSQL through Supabase
**Scope:** Physical tables, keys, relationships, constraints, indexes, access policies, migrations, and data-retention rules
**Current implementation stage:** Modular monolith with one PostgreSQL database

---

## 7.4.11.1 Purpose

This chapter converts TWN’s conceptual domain model into an implementable relational database design.

The earlier domain specifications define what the entities mean:

* Articles preserve structured knowledge.
* Notebook Entries preserve reflection.
* Journeys connect knowledge into narratives.
* Journey Chapters divide narratives into meaningful phases.
* Categories classify broad subjects.
* Tags describe specific concepts.
* Shared Pages and Margin Notes support moderated community participation.
* Media stores reusable asset records.
* Identity controls system access.

This chapter defines how those concepts are represented physically in PostgreSQL.

It establishes:

* database schemas and table boundaries;
* primary and foreign keys;
* junction tables;
* uniqueness rules;
* lifecycle constraints;
* delete behaviour;
* indexes;
* data ownership;
* Row Level Security;
* migration practices;
* backup boundaries;
* audit requirements;
* future evolution rules.

The physical model must preserve the domain. It must not simplify a real business concept merely because a less accurate schema would be easier to write.

---

# 7.4.11.2 Authoritative Design Principles

## Principle 1 — PostgreSQL is the source of truth

Published content, moderation decisions, ownership, lifecycle states, and relationships must be enforceable in the database.

Frontend validation improves usability but cannot guarantee integrity. PostgreSQL constraints must prevent structurally invalid states even when data is inserted through scripts, administrative tools, imports, or future applications. PostgreSQL supports primary keys, foreign keys, unique constraints, check constraints, and not-null constraints for this purpose. ([PostgreSQL][1])

---

## Principle 2 — The schema models domains, not pages

Tables must represent stable business concepts.

Correct:

```text
articles
notebook_entries
journeys
journey_chapters
shared_pages
margin_notes
```

Incorrect:

```text
homepage_cards
admin_items
landing_page_content
content_blocks_everything
```

The homepage is a presentation. It references editorial entities; it does not own them.

---

## Principle 3 — Every relationship must be explicit

Relationships must use foreign keys or dedicated junction tables.

An Article does not store the text name of its Author or Category as its authoritative relationship. It stores stable identifiers:

```text
author_id
category_id
```

Foreign keys allow PostgreSQL to prevent references to records that do not exist. ([PostgreSQL][1])

---

## Principle 4 — Content remains independent of Journeys

Articles and Notebook Entries can exist without belonging to a Journey.

A Journey provides narrative context but does not own the underlying content. The same Article may eventually participate in more than one Journey without duplication.

Therefore, the following is prohibited:

```sql
ALTER TABLE articles
ADD COLUMN journey_id uuid;
```

Journey membership belongs in relationship tables.

---

## Principle 5 — Do not create one universal content table

TWN must not store Articles, Notebook Entries, Podcasts, Books, Videos, and Courses in one large table distinguished only by `content_type`.

These entities have different:

* metadata;
* publishing rules;
* required fields;
* rendering behaviour;
* validation;
* analytics;
* media requirements;
* revision workflows.

They should remain separate domain tables.

A shared abstraction may be introduced later for discovery or Journey ordering, but it must not erase each entity’s domain-specific structure.

---

## Principle 6 — Normalize first

The default target is a normalized relational model.

Denormalization is permitted only when:

1. a real performance problem has been measured;
2. the duplicated field has an identified source of truth;
3. synchronization is defined;
4. the decision is documented in an ADR;
5. tests protect against drift.

No data should be duplicated merely to make an early query easier.

---

## Principle 7 — Constraints before application assumptions

The database must enforce rules that are always true.

Examples:

* an Article slug must be unique;
* a Journey Chapter must belong to a Journey;
* a Margin Note must reference valid parent content;
* a published Article must have a publication date;
* the same Tag must not be assigned twice to one Article;
* one Chapter cannot contain the same content record twice unless editorial policy explicitly allows it.

---

## Principle 8 — RLS is mandatory on exposed tables

Supabase recommends enabling Row Level Security on tables exposed through its Data API. RLS policies behave like additional row filters and provide defence in depth even when data is accessed through client tooling. ([Supabase][2])

For TWN:

* public readers may only read public, published records;
* community visitors may submit through tightly limited insert paths;
* editors may manage editorial records according to permission;
* moderators may review community records;
* subscriber data must never be publicly readable;
* administrative access must be denied by default.

The precise policy SQL must reflect TWN’s final authentication integration and verified JWT claims. It must not assume that a role supplied by the browser is trustworthy.

---

# 7.4.11.3 Database Namespace Strategy

TWN begins with one PostgreSQL database.

It should not begin with multiple databases or independent service databases. A single database is cheaper, simpler to operate, and capable of enforcing transactions across related editorial workflows.

Logical ownership is maintained through domains and controlled repository access.

## Recommended schemas

### `public`

Application-owned business tables exposed selectively through Supabase APIs.

Examples:

```text
authors
articles
notebook_entries
journeys
journey_chapters
categories
tags
shared_pages
margin_notes
media
newsletter_subscribers
```

### `private`

Sensitive server-only data that should not be exposed through ordinary public API access.

Potential contents:

```text
audit_logs
idempotency_keys
distribution_credentials
internal_job_metadata
security_events
```

Whether TWN creates a separate `private` schema immediately depends on current implementation complexity. At minimum, sensitive tables must have no anonymous access and must be operated through server-side code.

### Provider-managed schemas

Supabase manages schemas such as:

```text
auth
storage
```

TWN must not casually modify provider-owned schemas. Supabase notes that changes made to `auth` and `storage`, including custom triggers or policies, require separate attention during backup or migration procedures. ([Supabase][3])

---

# 7.4.11.4 Identifier Strategy

## Default identifier

For the current Supabase deployment, the compatibility-first default is:

```sql
id uuid PRIMARY KEY DEFAULT gen_random_uuid()
```

PostgreSQL has a native `uuid` data type. Current PostgreSQL 18 also supports UUIDv4 and UUIDv7 generation, but the available functions depend on the actual PostgreSQL version running the project. ([PostgreSQL][4])

Therefore:

* use `gen_random_uuid()` while that is the supported project default;
* do not claim UUIDv7 support until the deployed Supabase PostgreSQL version is verified;
* a future UUIDv7 migration must be documented separately;
* identifiers remain internal and are not used as primary public URLs.

## Public identifiers

Public content uses slugs:

```text
/articles/why-i-built-twn
/journeys/building-twn
/authors/vanessa
```

UUIDs remain useful for:

* foreign keys;
* imports;
* internal APIs;
* audit events;
* data synchronization;
* avoiding sequential enumeration.

A UUID is not itself an authorization control. Access must still be enforced.

---

# 7.4.11.5 Naming Conventions

## Tables

Plural `snake_case`:

```text
articles
notebook_entries
journey_chapters
newsletter_subscribers
```

## Columns

Singular `snake_case`:

```text
author_id
published_at
display_order
cover_media_id
```

## Primary keys

```text
id
```

## Foreign keys

```text
<referenced_entity>_id
```

Examples:

```text
article_id
journey_id
category_id
```

## Boolean fields

Must read as yes/no propositions:

```text
is_featured
is_active
is_verified
```

Avoid ambiguous names:

```text
featured
active_flag
value
```

## Timestamp fields

Use `_at`:

```text
created_at
updated_at
published_at
archived_at
deleted_at
```

## Date-only fields

Use `_date`:

```text
display_date
```

## Indexes

```text
idx_<table>_<columns>
```

Example:

```text
idx_articles_status_published_at
```

## Unique constraints

```text
uq_<table>_<columns>
```

## Check constraints

```text
chk_<table>_<rule>
```

## Foreign-key constraints

```text
fk_<table>_<referenced_table>
```

Explicit names improve migration readability and production debugging.

---

# 7.4.11.6 Lifecycle Representation

## Status values

Lifecycle entities require explicit status fields.

Examples:

```text
articles:
draft | review | scheduled | published | archived

journeys:
draft | active | completed | archived

shared_pages:
pending | approved | rejected | published | archived
```

### PostgreSQL enum versus text with check constraint

For TWN’s early stage, prefer:

```sql
status text NOT NULL
CHECK (status IN (...))
```

rather than PostgreSQL enum types for every workflow.

Reason:

* status values may evolve while the editorial workflow is still maturing;
* adding and removing enum values requires more deliberate migrations;
* check constraints remain explicit and are easy to inspect;
* TypeScript can still expose a strict union type.

Database enums remain valid for highly stable concepts, but they should not be introduced automatically.

---

# 7.4.11.7 Timestamp and Audit Standard

Most durable entities should include:

```sql
created_at timestamptz NOT NULL DEFAULT now(),
updated_at timestamptz NOT NULL DEFAULT now()
```

PostgreSQL `timestamptz` should be used for real-world instants such as publication, moderation, and account activity.

Additional timestamps are added only when they represent a meaningful lifecycle event:

```text
submitted_at
reviewed_at
scheduled_at
published_at
completed_at
archived_at
deleted_at
```

Do not add every possible timestamp to every table.

## Updated timestamp handling

TWN may use one shared trigger function to update `updated_at`.

Example design:

```sql
CREATE FUNCTION set_updated_at()
RETURNS trigger
LANGUAGE plpgsql
AS $$
BEGIN
  NEW.updated_at = now();
  RETURN NEW;
END;
$$;
```

A trigger is then attached only to editable tables.

This is acceptable because automatic modification timestamps are infrastructure behaviour, not editorial business logic.

---

# 7.4.11.8 Deletion Policy

## Editorial records

Use soft deletion or archival.

Examples:

```text
articles
notebook_entries
journeys
journey_chapters
authors
media
```

A published record should not disappear merely because it is no longer promoted.

## Community spam

Rejected spam may be hard-deleted after a retention period if there is no legal, security, or moderation reason to retain it.

## Subscriber information

Deletion and retention must follow TWN’s privacy policy and applicable law. Unsubscription and deletion are separate:

* `unsubscribed_at` records withdrawal from email delivery;
* deletion addresses removal of stored personal data where required.

## Foreign-key behaviour

Delete behaviour must be chosen deliberately.

### `ON DELETE RESTRICT`

Use when deleting the parent would invalidate durable knowledge.

Example:

```text
authors → articles
categories → articles
```

An Author with published Articles should not be deleted accidentally.

### `ON DELETE CASCADE`

Use only for records that have no independent meaning.

Example:

```text
article_tags
journey_chapter_articles
role_permissions
```

When the relationship owner disappears, the junction row may disappear.

### `ON DELETE SET NULL`

Use when an optional supporting relationship may safely disappear without destroying the child.

Example:

```text
article.cover_media_id
```

This decision depends on whether TWN prefers to block deletion of referenced media. For active editorial media, `RESTRICT` is generally safer; archival should be used instead of deletion.

---

# 7.4.11.9 Physical Domain Map

```text
authors
 ├──< articles
 ├──< notebook_entries
 └──< journeys
          └──< journey_chapters

categories
 └──< articles

articles
 ├──< article_tags >── tags
 ├──< margin_notes
 ├──< journey_chapter_articles >── journey_chapters
 └── cover_media_id ──> media

notebook_entries
 ├──< journey_chapter_notebook_entries >── journey_chapters
 └── cover_media_id ──> media

journeys
 └── cover_media_id ──> media

journey_chapters
 └── cover_media_id ──> media

shared_pages
 └── moderated_by_user_id ──> users

newsletter_subscribers

users
 ├──< user_roles >── roles
 └──< audit_logs

roles
 └──< role_permissions >── permissions
```

This is the Version 1 physical model. Future content types will add their own domain tables and Journey relationship tables without converting the schema into a universal content table.

---

# 7.4.11.10 Core Editorial Tables

## A. Authors

```sql
CREATE TABLE public.authors (
  id uuid PRIMARY KEY DEFAULT gen_random_uuid(),

  slug text NOT NULL,
  display_name text NOT NULL,
  bio text,
  avatar_media_id uuid,
  website_url text,
  location text,

  status text NOT NULL DEFAULT 'draft',
  created_at timestamptz NOT NULL DEFAULT now(),
  updated_at timestamptz NOT NULL DEFAULT now(),
  deleted_at timestamptz,

  CONSTRAINT uq_authors_slug UNIQUE (slug),
  CONSTRAINT chk_authors_status
    CHECK (status IN ('draft', 'active', 'inactive', 'archived')),
  CONSTRAINT chk_authors_display_name_not_blank
    CHECK (length(btrim(display_name)) > 0)
);
```

### Design decisions

* `display_name` is required because every editorial object must have attributable authorship.
* `bio` remains optional.
* `location` remains broad and optional.
* `avatar_media_id` is added after the `media` table exists, or introduced through a follow-up migration to avoid circular creation order.
* an Author is not automatically a system User.

### Recommended indexes

The unique slug constraint already enforces uniqueness and is backed by an index, so a duplicate slug index should not be added. PostgreSQL creates indexes to enforce primary-key and unique constraints. ([PostgreSQL][1])

Additional index:

```sql
CREATE INDEX idx_authors_status
ON public.authors (status)
WHERE deleted_at IS NULL;
```

---

## B. Categories

```sql
CREATE TABLE public.categories (
  id uuid PRIMARY KEY DEFAULT gen_random_uuid(),

  slug text NOT NULL,
  name text NOT NULL,
  description text NOT NULL,
  status text NOT NULL DEFAULT 'draft',
  display_order integer NOT NULL DEFAULT 0,

  created_at timestamptz NOT NULL DEFAULT now(),
  updated_at timestamptz NOT NULL DEFAULT now(),
  deleted_at timestamptz,

  CONSTRAINT uq_categories_slug UNIQUE (slug),
  CONSTRAINT uq_categories_name UNIQUE (name),
  CONSTRAINT chk_categories_status
    CHECK (status IN ('draft', 'active', 'archived')),
  CONSTRAINT chk_categories_name_not_blank
    CHECK (length(btrim(name)) > 0),
  CONSTRAINT chk_categories_description_not_blank
    CHECK (length(btrim(description)) > 0),
  CONSTRAINT chk_categories_display_order
    CHECK (display_order >= 0)
);
```

### Design decisions

* Version 1 remains flat.
* No `parent_id` is introduced until a verified need for hierarchical Categories exists.
* The Category description is required because Categories are editorial destinations, not merely labels.

---

## C. Tags

```sql
CREATE TABLE public.tags (
  id uuid PRIMARY KEY DEFAULT gen_random_uuid(),

  slug text NOT NULL,
  name text NOT NULL,
  description text,
  status text NOT NULL DEFAULT 'draft',

  created_at timestamptz NOT NULL DEFAULT now(),
  updated_at timestamptz NOT NULL DEFAULT now(),
  deleted_at timestamptz,

  CONSTRAINT uq_tags_slug UNIQUE (slug),
  CONSTRAINT uq_tags_name UNIQUE (name),
  CONSTRAINT chk_tags_status
    CHECK (status IN ('draft', 'active', 'archived')),
  CONSTRAINT chk_tags_name_not_blank
    CHECK (length(btrim(name)) > 0)
);
```

Tag aliases and synonyms are deferred. They require their own explicit model when search behaviour justifies them.

---

## D. Media

```sql
CREATE TABLE public.media (
  id uuid PRIMARY KEY DEFAULT gen_random_uuid(),

  slug text,
  original_filename text NOT NULL,
  mime_type text NOT NULL,
  file_size_bytes bigint NOT NULL,
  width_pixels integer,
  height_pixels integer,

  asset_kind text NOT NULL,
  alt_text text,
  caption text,
  storage_provider text NOT NULL,
  storage_key text NOT NULL,

  status text NOT NULL DEFAULT 'uploaded',
  uploaded_by_user_id uuid,

  created_at timestamptz NOT NULL DEFAULT now(),
  updated_at timestamptz NOT NULL DEFAULT now(),
  deleted_at timestamptz,

  CONSTRAINT uq_media_storage_location
    UNIQUE (storage_provider, storage_key),

  CONSTRAINT chk_media_file_size
    CHECK (file_size_bytes > 0),

  CONSTRAINT chk_media_dimensions
    CHECK (
      (width_pixels IS NULL AND height_pixels IS NULL)
      OR
      (width_pixels > 0 AND height_pixels > 0)
    ),

  CONSTRAINT chk_media_asset_kind
    CHECK (
      asset_kind IN (
        'image',
        'audio',
        'video',
        'document',
        'download'
      )
    ),

  CONSTRAINT chk_media_status
    CHECK (
      status IN (
        'uploaded',
        'processing',
        'active',
        'failed',
        'archived'
      )
    )
);
```

### Important correction

Not every image requires non-empty alt text:

* meaningful editorial images require descriptive alt text;
* decorative images should use an intentionally empty alternative;
* audio, video, and documents require different accessibility metadata.

Therefore, the database should not blindly apply `alt_text NOT NULL` to all Media records. Domain validation must enforce the appropriate accessibility requirements based on where and how the asset is used.

### Storage and backup boundary

Supabase database backups cover database records but do not automatically restore deleted Storage objects; Storage files and database metadata therefore need coordinated backup and recovery procedures. ([Supabase][5])

---

## E. Articles

```sql
CREATE TABLE public.articles (
  id uuid PRIMARY KEY DEFAULT gen_random_uuid(),

  slug text NOT NULL,
  title text NOT NULL,
  subtitle text,
  excerpt text NOT NULL,
  content_source text NOT NULL,

  author_id uuid NOT NULL,
  category_id uuid NOT NULL,
  cover_media_id uuid,

  status text NOT NULL DEFAULT 'draft',
  is_featured boolean NOT NULL DEFAULT false,

  word_count integer NOT NULL DEFAULT 0,
  reading_time_minutes integer NOT NULL DEFAULT 1,

  seo_title text,
  seo_description text,
  canonical_url text,

  scheduled_at timestamptz,
  published_at timestamptz,
  archived_at timestamptz,

  created_at timestamptz NOT NULL DEFAULT now(),
  updated_at timestamptz NOT NULL DEFAULT now(),
  deleted_at timestamptz,

  CONSTRAINT uq_articles_slug UNIQUE (slug),

  CONSTRAINT fk_articles_author
    FOREIGN KEY (author_id)
    REFERENCES public.authors(id)
    ON DELETE RESTRICT,

  CONSTRAINT fk_articles_category
    FOREIGN KEY (category_id)
    REFERENCES public.categories(id)
    ON DELETE RESTRICT,

  CONSTRAINT fk_articles_cover_media
    FOREIGN KEY (cover_media_id)
    REFERENCES public.media(id)
    ON DELETE RESTRICT,

  CONSTRAINT chk_articles_status
    CHECK (
      status IN (
        'draft',
        'review',
        'scheduled',
        'published',
        'archived'
      )
    ),

  CONSTRAINT chk_articles_title_not_blank
    CHECK (length(btrim(title)) > 0),

  CONSTRAINT chk_articles_excerpt_not_blank
    CHECK (length(btrim(excerpt)) > 0),

  CONSTRAINT chk_articles_content_not_blank
    CHECK (length(btrim(content_source)) > 0),

  CONSTRAINT chk_articles_word_count
    CHECK (word_count >= 0),

  CONSTRAINT chk_articles_reading_time
    CHECK (reading_time_minutes >= 1),

  CONSTRAINT chk_articles_publication_state
    CHECK (
      (status = 'published' AND published_at IS NOT NULL)
      OR
      (status <> 'published')
    ),

  CONSTRAINT chk_articles_schedule_state
    CHECK (
      (status = 'scheduled' AND scheduled_at IS NOT NULL)
      OR
      (status <> 'scheduled')
    )
);
```

### Content field naming

Use `content_source` rather than assuming `content` is always MDX.

The application may initially store Markdown or MDX source. However, the database should not misleadingly claim a media format that may later change. The chosen rendering pipeline must be separately documented.

### Publication constraint limitation

The check above prevents a published Article without `published_at`. It does not prevent non-published Articles from retaining a historical `published_at`.

That is intentional because:

* an archived Article should preserve its original publication time;
* a previously published Article may enter a non-public maintenance state;
* publication history should not be destroyed.

### Recommended indexes

```sql
CREATE INDEX idx_articles_public_listing
ON public.articles (published_at DESC)
WHERE status = 'published' AND deleted_at IS NULL;

CREATE INDEX idx_articles_category_published
ON public.articles (category_id, published_at DESC)
WHERE status = 'published' AND deleted_at IS NULL;

CREATE INDEX idx_articles_author_published
ON public.articles (author_id, published_at DESC)
WHERE status = 'published' AND deleted_at IS NULL;

CREATE INDEX idx_articles_scheduled
ON public.articles (scheduled_at)
WHERE status = 'scheduled' AND deleted_at IS NULL;
```

Do not add a separate index for `slug`; its unique constraint already provides one.

### Featured Article rule

The statement “maximum one featured Article” is an editorial choice, not a universal truth. If TWN formally adopts that rule, PostgreSQL can enforce it using a partial unique index:

```sql
CREATE UNIQUE INDEX uq_articles_single_featured
ON public.articles ((true))
WHERE
  is_featured = true
  AND status = 'published'
  AND deleted_at IS NULL;
```

Do not install this constraint until the product decision is confirmed, because it would prevent future homepage layouts with several featured Articles.

---

## F. Article Tags

```sql
CREATE TABLE public.article_tags (
  article_id uuid NOT NULL,
  tag_id uuid NOT NULL,
  assigned_at timestamptz NOT NULL DEFAULT now(),

  PRIMARY KEY (article_id, tag_id),

  CONSTRAINT fk_article_tags_article
    FOREIGN KEY (article_id)
    REFERENCES public.articles(id)
    ON DELETE CASCADE,

  CONSTRAINT fk_article_tags_tag
    FOREIGN KEY (tag_id)
    REFERENCES public.tags(id)
    ON DELETE RESTRICT
);
```

The composite primary key prevents the same Tag from being assigned twice to one Article.

Add this index because the primary-key order already supports lookups beginning with `article_id`, but not necessarily efficient reverse lookups beginning with `tag_id`:

```sql
CREATE INDEX idx_article_tags_tag
ON public.article_tags (tag_id, article_id);
```

---

## G. Notebook Entries

```sql
CREATE TABLE public.notebook_entries (
  id uuid PRIMARY KEY DEFAULT gen_random_uuid(),

  slug text,
  title text,
  thought text NOT NULL,

  author_id uuid NOT NULL,
  cover_media_id uuid,

  status text NOT NULL DEFAULT 'draft',
  is_hero_eligible boolean NOT NULL DEFAULT false,
  display_date date,

  published_at timestamptz,
  archived_at timestamptz,

  created_at timestamptz NOT NULL DEFAULT now(),
  updated_at timestamptz NOT NULL DEFAULT now(),
  deleted_at timestamptz,

  CONSTRAINT uq_notebook_entries_slug UNIQUE (slug),

  CONSTRAINT fk_notebook_entries_author
    FOREIGN KEY (author_id)
    REFERENCES public.authors(id)
    ON DELETE RESTRICT,

  CONSTRAINT fk_notebook_entries_cover_media
    FOREIGN KEY (cover_media_id)
    REFERENCES public.media(id)
    ON DELETE RESTRICT,

  CONSTRAINT chk_notebook_entries_status
    CHECK (
      status IN (
        'draft',
        'review',
        'published',
        'archived'
      )
    ),

  CONSTRAINT chk_notebook_entries_thought_not_blank
    CHECK (length(btrim(thought)) > 0),

  CONSTRAINT chk_notebook_entries_publication_state
    CHECK (
      (status = 'published' AND published_at IS NOT NULL)
      OR
      (status <> 'published')
    )
);
```

### Important naming change

Use:

```text
is_hero_eligible
```

instead of:

```text
is_featured
```

because Hero eligibility and editorial feature placement are different concepts.

An entry can be eligible for rotation without being “featured” everywhere.

### Today’s Page uniqueness

The product currently intends one dated Notebook Entry per day. That can be enforced with:

```sql
CREATE UNIQUE INDEX uq_notebook_entries_display_date
ON public.notebook_entries (display_date)
WHERE
  display_date IS NOT NULL
  AND deleted_at IS NULL;
```

This allows many undated entries but only one active dated record for a given date.

---

# 7.4.11.11 Journeys and Chapter Structure

## A. Journeys

```sql
CREATE TABLE public.journeys (
  id uuid PRIMARY KEY DEFAULT gen_random_uuid(),

  slug text NOT NULL,
  title text NOT NULL,
  description text NOT NULL,

  author_id uuid NOT NULL,
  cover_media_id uuid,

  status text NOT NULL DEFAULT 'draft',
  started_at date,
  completed_at date,

  published_at timestamptz,
  archived_at timestamptz,

  created_at timestamptz NOT NULL DEFAULT now(),
  updated_at timestamptz NOT NULL DEFAULT now(),
  deleted_at timestamptz,

  CONSTRAINT uq_journeys_slug UNIQUE (slug),

  CONSTRAINT fk_journeys_author
    FOREIGN KEY (author_id)
    REFERENCES public.authors(id)
    ON DELETE RESTRICT,

  CONSTRAINT fk_journeys_cover_media
    FOREIGN KEY (cover_media_id)
    REFERENCES public.media(id)
    ON DELETE RESTRICT,

  CONSTRAINT chk_journeys_status
    CHECK (
      status IN (
        'draft',
        'active',
        'completed',
        'archived'
      )
    ),

  CONSTRAINT chk_journeys_title_not_blank
    CHECK (length(btrim(title)) > 0),

  CONSTRAINT chk_journeys_description_not_blank
    CHECK (length(btrim(description)) > 0),

  CONSTRAINT chk_journeys_dates
    CHECK (
      completed_at IS NULL
      OR started_at IS NULL
      OR completed_at >= started_at
    )
);
```

### Distinguishing `active` from `published`

A Journey’s `status = 'active'` describes its narrative lifecycle. `published_at` records whether it is publicly available.

These should not be merged automatically:

* an active Journey may still be private while being prepared;
* a completed Journey remains publicly available;
* publication and narrative completion are different dimensions.

If TWN decides that `active` always means public, the separate publication field may be simplified later. For now, the distinction avoids overloading one status.

---

## B. Journey Chapters

```sql
CREATE TABLE public.journey_chapters (
  id uuid PRIMARY KEY DEFAULT gen_random_uuid(),

  journey_id uuid NOT NULL,
  slug text NOT NULL,
  title text NOT NULL,
  description text NOT NULL,
  cover_media_id uuid,

  display_order integer NOT NULL,
  status text NOT NULL DEFAULT 'draft',

  published_at timestamptz,
  completed_at timestamptz,
  archived_at timestamptz,

  created_at timestamptz NOT NULL DEFAULT now(),
  updated_at timestamptz NOT NULL DEFAULT now(),
  deleted_at timestamptz,

  CONSTRAINT fk_journey_chapters_journey
    FOREIGN KEY (journey_id)
    REFERENCES public.journeys(id)
    ON DELETE CASCADE,

  CONSTRAINT fk_journey_chapters_cover_media
    FOREIGN KEY (cover_media_id)
    REFERENCES public.media(id)
    ON DELETE RESTRICT,

  CONSTRAINT uq_journey_chapters_slug
    UNIQUE (journey_id, slug),

  CONSTRAINT uq_journey_chapters_order
    UNIQUE (journey_id, display_order),

  CONSTRAINT chk_journey_chapters_status
    CHECK (
      status IN (
        'draft',
        'active',
        'completed',
        'archived'
      )
    ),

  CONSTRAINT chk_journey_chapters_order
    CHECK (display_order >= 0),

  CONSTRAINT chk_journey_chapters_title_not_blank
    CHECK (length(btrim(title)) > 0),

  CONSTRAINT chk_journey_chapters_description_not_blank
    CHECK (length(btrim(description)) > 0)
);
```

### Delete behaviour

`ON DELETE CASCADE` is acceptable between a never-published Journey and its Chapters because a Chapter has no independent meaning outside its Journey.

However, published Journeys should normally be archived rather than deleted. Application authorization must prevent casual deletion even though the database relationship supports cleanup when deletion is genuinely required.

---

# 7.4.11.12 Ordered Journey Content

This is the most important unresolved physical-design decision in the Journey system.

TWN needs Chapters to contain a deliberate sequence such as:

```text
Notebook Entry
Article
Notebook Entry
Article
Podcast Episode
Video Essay
```

There are two technically valid approaches.

## Option A — Separate junction tables

```text
journey_chapter_articles
journey_chapter_notebook_entries
journey_chapter_podcast_episodes
journey_chapter_videos
```

Advantages:

* real foreign keys;
* clear referential integrity;
* simple domain ownership;
* straightforward queries per type.

Problem:

A single cross-type reading order becomes difficult. An Article with order `2` and a Notebook Entry with order `3` are stored in different tables, and PostgreSQL cannot easily enforce that no other content type also uses order `2` within the same Chapter.

## Option B — Generic content reference

```text
journey_chapter_items
content_type
content_id
```

Advantages:

* one ordered sequence;
* easy drag-and-drop;
* future content types can join without schema changes.

Problems:

* ordinary foreign keys cannot enforce that `content_id` points to the correct table;
* deletion can create orphan references;
* queries require type dispatch;
* integrity moves from PostgreSQL into application code or triggers.

## Recommended Version 1 decision

For the current product, use **one relationship table per implemented content type**, but introduce a shared `position_key` namespace controlled transactionally by the application.

```sql
CREATE TABLE public.journey_chapter_articles (
  chapter_id uuid NOT NULL,
  article_id uuid NOT NULL,
  display_order integer NOT NULL,
  transition_note text,

  created_at timestamptz NOT NULL DEFAULT now(),

  PRIMARY KEY (chapter_id, article_id),

  CONSTRAINT fk_journey_chapter_articles_chapter
    FOREIGN KEY (chapter_id)
    REFERENCES public.journey_chapters(id)
    ON DELETE CASCADE,

  CONSTRAINT fk_journey_chapter_articles_article
    FOREIGN KEY (article_id)
    REFERENCES public.articles(id)
    ON DELETE CASCADE,

  CONSTRAINT chk_journey_chapter_articles_order
    CHECK (display_order >= 0)
);
```

```sql
CREATE TABLE public.journey_chapter_notebook_entries (
  chapter_id uuid NOT NULL,
  notebook_entry_id uuid NOT NULL,
  display_order integer NOT NULL,
  transition_note text,

  created_at timestamptz NOT NULL DEFAULT now(),

  PRIMARY KEY (chapter_id, notebook_entry_id),

  CONSTRAINT fk_journey_chapter_notebook_chapter
    FOREIGN KEY (chapter_id)
    REFERENCES public.journey_chapters(id)
    ON DELETE CASCADE,

  CONSTRAINT fk_journey_chapter_notebook_entry
    FOREIGN KEY (notebook_entry_id)
    REFERENCES public.notebook_entries(id)
    ON DELETE CASCADE,

  CONSTRAINT chk_journey_chapter_notebook_order
    CHECK (display_order >= 0)
);
```

### Honest limitation

Separate junction tables do **not** allow a normal unique constraint to prevent an Article and Notebook Entry from sharing the same `display_order` within one Chapter.

TWN therefore has three choices:

1. validate cross-type ordering inside a transaction in the Application Service;
2. add a Chapter-owned ordering table later;
3. introduce a trigger that checks all Chapter content tables.

For Version 1, choose application-service validation inside a transaction and cover it with integration tests. Do not introduce a complex trigger until actual content volume and supported media types justify it.

When Podcasts or Videos become real TWN products, revisit this decision through an ADR. That is the correct point to evaluate a richer `journey_content_nodes` abstraction.

---

# 7.4.11.13 What Comes Next

The next part of this chapter must complete the physical model for:

1. Shared Pages;
2. Margin Notes;
3. Newsletter Subscribers;
4. Users, Roles, and Permissions;
5. Audit Logs;
6. RLS policy matrix;
7. migration and seed strategy;
8. backup and restore plan;
9. schema testing;
10. production rollout order.

These sections require their own detailed treatment because they contain sensitive personal data, anonymous writes, moderation state, and authorization boundaries. Supabase recommends testing database schemas and RLS policies, and its migration tooling records SQL-based schema evolution over time. ([Supabase][6])

[1]: https://www.postgresql.org/docs/current/ddl-constraints.html?utm_source=chatgpt.com "Documentation: 18: 5.5. Constraints"
[2]: https://supabase.com/docs/guides/database/postgres/row-level-security?utm_source=chatgpt.com "Row Level Security | Supabase Docs"
[3]: https://supabase.com/docs/guides/platform/migrating-within-supabase/backup-restore?utm_source=chatgpt.com "Backup and Restore using the CLI | Supabase Docs"
[4]: https://www.postgresql.org/docs/current/datatype-uuid.html?utm_source=chatgpt.com "Documentation: 18: 8.12. UUID Type"
[5]: https://supabase.com/docs/guides/platform/backups?utm_source=chatgpt.com "Database Backups | Supabase Docs"
[6]: https://supabase.com/docs/guides/deployment/database-migrations?utm_source=chatgpt.com "Database Migrations | Supabase Docs"


# 7.4.12 Application Boundaries, Module Interaction & Dependency Map

**Status:** Foundational architecture standard
**Applies to:** All current and future TWN modules
**Architecture style:** Modular monolith with explicit domain boundaries
**Primary objective:** Allow TWN to grow without turning the codebase into a network of hidden dependencies

---

# Purpose

This section defines how TWN’s modules communicate.

The database blueprint explains how information is stored.

This section explains:

* which module owns each capability;
* which modules may call one another;
* which modules may read or modify specific data;
* how cross-module operations are coordinated;
* where transactions begin and end;
* how future modules are introduced;
* what dependencies are forbidden;
* when a module may eventually be extracted into an independent service.

The objective is not to prevent modules from interacting.

The objective is to make every interaction:

* intentional;
* visible;
* testable;
* replaceable;
* secure;
* understandable.

---

# 7.4.12.1 Architectural Position

TWN is one application composed of distinct business modules.

```text
TWN Modular Monolith
│
├── Editorial
├── Notebook
├── Journeys
├── Community
├── Newsletter
├── Media
├── Identity
├── Search
├── Analytics
├── Distribution
└── Operations
```

These modules share:

* one repository;
* one deployment;
* one PostgreSQL database;
* one authentication boundary;
* one observability platform.

They do not share unrestricted access to one another’s implementation.

---

# 7.4.12.2 Why Module Boundaries Matter

Without explicit boundaries, the system may gradually become:

```text
Homepage
  ↓
Article queries
  ↓
Newsletter logic
  ↓
Community moderation
  ↓
Media deletion
  ↓
Authentication checks
```

One feature begins depending on unrelated features.

Eventually:

* a Newsletter change breaks the homepage;
* deleting Media breaks Journeys;
* Community code updates Article records directly;
* Search becomes embedded inside every repository;
* authorization rules are repeated everywhere;
* no module can be changed independently.

A modular monolith only remains modular when its boundaries are actively enforced.

---

# 7.4.12.3 Module Ownership Rule

Every business capability must have exactly one owning module.

Ownership means the module controls:

* business rules;
* lifecycle transitions;
* write operations;
* domain validation;
* public service contracts;
* repositories;
* related migrations;
* module-specific tests.

Other modules may consume its public capabilities.

They may not reproduce or bypass them.

---

# 7.4.12.4 Current Module Ownership Matrix

| Module       | Owns                                                      |
| ------------ | --------------------------------------------------------- |
| Editorial    | Articles, Authors, Categories, Tags, publication rules    |
| Notebook     | Notebook Entries, Hero eligibility, Today’s Page          |
| Journeys     | Journeys, Journey Chapters, Journey ordering              |
| Community    | Shared Pages, Margin Notes, moderation                    |
| Newsletter   | Subscribers, consent, confirmations, unsubscribe state    |
| Media        | Asset metadata, storage references, processing state      |
| Identity     | Users, Roles, Permissions, internal access                |
| Search       | Indexing, query processing, ranking, result normalization |
| Analytics    | Product and operational measurement                       |
| Distribution | Repurposing and publishing content to external channels   |
| Operations   | Logging, jobs, scheduled tasks, monitoring, audit support |

---

# 7.4.12.5 Public and Private Module Interfaces

Every module has two surfaces.

## Public surface

Capabilities that other modules may use.

Examples:

```text
Editorial.getPublishedArticleBySlug()
Editorial.publishArticle()
Notebook.getTodaysPage()
Journey.getJourneyReadingOrder()
Community.submitMarginNote()
Newsletter.subscribe()
Media.resolvePublicAsset()
Identity.hasPermission()
Search.indexArticle()
Distribution.createDistributionRecord()
```

## Private surface

Implementation details that belong only to the module.

Examples:

```text
ArticleRepository
ArticlePublishingValidator
SupabaseArticleMapper
JourneyOrderingRepository
NewsletterTokenHasher
MediaStorageAdapter
```

Other modules must not import private implementation files.

---

# 7.4.12.6 Recommended Folder Boundary

```text
src/
├── app/
├── modules/
│   ├── editorial/
│   │   ├── public.ts
│   │   ├── application/
│   │   ├── domain/
│   │   ├── infrastructure/
│   │   └── tests/
│   ├── notebook/
│   ├── journeys/
│   ├── community/
│   ├── newsletter/
│   ├── media/
│   ├── identity/
│   ├── search/
│   ├── analytics/
│   └── distribution/
├── shared/
├── infrastructure/
└── design-system/
```

Each module exposes one intentional entry point:

```text
modules/editorial/public.ts
```

Cross-module imports should use that entry point.

Correct:

```typescript
import { getPublishedArticleBySlug } from "@/modules/editorial/public";
```

Incorrect:

```typescript
import { ArticleRepository } from "@/modules/editorial/infrastructure/article-repository";
```

The second import bypasses the module boundary.

---

# 7.4.12.7 Dependency Direction

Dependencies should flow toward stable business contracts.

```text
Presentation
    ↓
Application Use Cases
    ↓
Domain Rules
    ↓
Repository Contracts
    ↓
Infrastructure Implementations
```

For cross-module communication:

```text
Consuming Module
    ↓
Owning Module Public Contract
```

Never:

```text
Consuming Module
    ↓
Owning Module Repository
```

---

# 7.4.12.8 Allowed Dependency Map

## Editorial may depend on

* Identity for authorization;
* Media for asset references;
* Operations for logging and audit events.

Editorial must not depend directly on:

* Community;
* Newsletter;
* Analytics;
* Distribution.

Those systems react to Editorial outcomes rather than controlling publication.

---

## Notebook may depend on

* Identity for authorization;
* Media for optional assets;
* Editorial only through an explicit linking contract;
* Operations for logging.

Notebook may link to an Article.

It must not directly update Article fields.

---

## Journeys may depend on

* Editorial public content references;
* Notebook public content references;
* Media;
* Identity;
* Operations.

Journeys organize content.

They do not own or mutate Articles or Notebook Entries.

---

## Community may depend on

* Editorial for validating an Article parent;
* Identity for moderator authorization;
* Operations for abuse logging and audit events.

Community must not update Article metadata.

---

## Newsletter may depend on

* Identity for administrative authorization;
* Distribution for campaign delivery;
* Operations for job execution and monitoring.

Newsletter must not directly query Article tables to construct messages. It should request content through Editorial’s public contract.

---

## Media may depend on

* Identity for upload authorization;
* Operations for processing jobs and logging;
* infrastructure storage providers.

Media must not know why an Article or Journey uses an asset.

---

## Search may depend on

* public read models exposed by Editorial;
* Notebook;
* Journeys;
* Community, if Shared Pages become searchable.

Search must not update source content.

---

## Analytics may consume

* module events;
* application logs;
* aggregated read models.

Analytics must not become a dependency required for publishing or reading.

If Analytics fails, core product functions must continue.

---

## Distribution may depend on

* Editorial;
* Notebook;
* Journeys;
* Newsletter;
* Media.

Distribution transforms canonical content for external channels.

It must never become the canonical source of knowledge.

---

# 7.4.12.9 Forbidden Dependencies

The following interactions are prohibited.

## Presentation directly accessing database clients

```text
React Component
    ↓
Supabase SDK
```

for business operations.

Public server-rendered reads may use server-side module services, not direct table calls scattered across pages.

---

## Community directly modifying Editorial records

```text
Community moderation
    ↓
UPDATE articles
```

Forbidden.

---

## Search writing canonical content

```text
Search index
    ↓
Article update
```

Forbidden.

Search reflects content; it does not own it.

---

## Analytics controlling product behaviour

```text
Analytics unavailable
    ↓
Publishing fails
```

Forbidden.

Analytics is optional infrastructure from the core product’s perspective.

---

## Distribution storing canonical content copies

A LinkedIn or YouTube derivative may store its own version and publication state.

It must reference the canonical TWN source and never replace it.

---

## Circular dependencies

Examples:

```text
Editorial → Journeys → Editorial
Community → Newsletter → Community
Media → Editorial → Media
```

Circular dependencies signal that ownership is unclear.

One module should own the operation, while the other reacts through a public contract or event.

---

# 7.4.12.10 Cross-Module Read Models

A module should not expose its complete database entity when another module only needs a subset.

Example:

Journeys need to display an Article inside a Chapter.

They may require:

```typescript
interface JourneyArticleReference {
  id: string;
  slug: string;
  title: string;
  excerpt: string;
  publishedAt: string | null;
  status: "draft" | "review" | "scheduled" | "published" | "archived";
}
```

They do not need:

* SEO internals;
* draft source;
* moderation notes;
* internal audit fields;
* storage implementation details.

This reduces coupling and prevents accidental dependence on private fields.

---

# 7.4.12.11 Service Contract Design

Public module contracts should express business intent.

Good:

```typescript
publishArticle(input)
getPublishedArticleBySlug(slug)
attachArticleToJourneyChapter(input)
approveSharedPage(input)
resolvePublicMediaAsset(mediaId)
```

Bad:

```typescript
getRow(table, id)
updateData(payload)
performOperation(type)
```

Generic contracts hide domain meaning and make permission checks harder to reason about.

---

# 7.4.12.12 Contract Stability

Public module contracts are internal APIs.

They should evolve carefully.

## Additive change

Safe:

```typescript
interface ArticleSummary {
  title: string;
  slug: string;
  excerpt?: string;
}
```

Adding an optional field usually preserves callers.

## Breaking change

Risky:

```text
rename slug to path
change null to exception
remove status
change ordering behaviour
```

Breaking changes require:

* impact analysis;
* updated consumers;
* migration notes;
* tests;
* an ADR if architectural behaviour changes.

---

# 7.4.12.13 Synchronous Communication

Synchronous calls are appropriate when the caller requires an immediate result.

Examples:

```text
Open article page
    ↓
Editorial.getPublishedArticleBySlug()
```

```text
Submit Margin Note
    ↓
Community.submitMarginNote()
```

```text
Attach Article to Journey
    ↓
Journeys.addArticleToChapter()
```

The operation cannot continue meaningfully without the response.

---

# 7.4.12.14 Asynchronous Communication

Asynchronous processing is appropriate when work may happen after the user receives a response.

Examples:

* send newsletter confirmation;
* update search index;
* generate image variants;
* send publication notifications;
* produce social-media derivatives;
* calculate advanced analytics;
* generate Open Graph images;
* export PDF or EPUB;
* process audio or video.

Conceptual flow:

```text
Article Published
    ↓
Commit Publication
    ↓
Emit Event
    ↓
Queue Background Work
        ├── Search Index
        ├── Newsletter Notification
        ├── Analytics Event
        └── Distribution Draft
```

Article publication must not be rolled back because an optional notification failed.

---

# 7.4.12.15 Domain Events

Domain events represent business facts that have already occurred.

Examples:

```text
ArticlePublished
ArticleArchived
NotebookEntryPublished
JourneyActivated
SharedPageApproved
MarginNotePublished
SubscriberConfirmed
MediaProcessed
UserRoleAssigned
```

Events must be named in past tense because they report completed facts.

---

# 7.4.12.16 Event Payload Rules

Events should include only what consumers need.

Example:

```typescript
interface ArticlePublishedEvent {
  eventId: string;
  articleId: string;
  slug: string;
  publishedAt: string;
  occurredAt: string;
  actorUserId: string | null;
}
```

Do not place the full Article body in every event.

Consumers can request additional data from the owning module if needed.

---

# 7.4.12.17 Event Delivery in Version 1

TWN does not initially require Kafka, RabbitMQ, or a distributed event platform.

Version 1 may use:

* an in-process dispatcher for non-critical local reactions;
* a database-backed outbox for reliable background work;
* scheduled workers or platform jobs.

The architecture should distinguish between:

## Best-effort event

Failure is acceptable.

Example:

```text
record optional analytics metric
```

## Reliable event

Must eventually be processed.

Examples:

```text
send confirmation email
update search index
record distribution task
```

Reliable events require persistent storage before the publishing transaction completes.

---

# 7.4.12.18 Transactional Outbox Pattern

A reliable event should be inserted into an outbox within the same database transaction as the domain change.

Example:

```text
BEGIN TRANSACTION

UPDATE article → published

INSERT audit log

INSERT outbox event: ArticlePublished

COMMIT
```

A worker later processes the outbox record.

This prevents a failure state where the Article is published but the event is lost before being queued.

A future physical table may look like:

```sql
CREATE TABLE private.outbox_events (
  id uuid PRIMARY KEY DEFAULT gen_random_uuid(),
  event_type text NOT NULL,
  aggregate_type text NOT NULL,
  aggregate_id uuid NOT NULL,
  payload jsonb NOT NULL,
  occurred_at timestamptz NOT NULL DEFAULT now(),
  available_at timestamptz NOT NULL DEFAULT now(),
  processed_at timestamptz,
  attempt_count integer NOT NULL DEFAULT 0,
  last_error text
);
```

This should be introduced when the first reliable background workflow is implemented.

---

# 7.4.12.19 Transaction Boundaries

A transaction should preserve one business invariant.

## Example: Publishing an Article

The transaction may include:

* validating current status;
* changing status to published;
* setting `published_at`;
* recording an audit event;
* inserting an outbox event.

It should not include:

* sending email;
* calling LinkedIn;
* uploading a video;
* waiting for analytics;
* performing slow external network calls.

External calls inside a transaction increase lock duration and make failures harder to recover from.

---

# 7.4.12.20 Cross-Module Transaction Rule

A single database transaction may touch several modules only when one business invariant genuinely spans them.

Example:

Attaching an Article to a Journey Chapter requires:

* validating the Article exists;
* validating the Chapter exists;
* inserting the Journey relationship.

Journeys owns the relationship row.

Editorial remains the owner of the Article.

The transaction should not change the Article itself.

Cross-module transactions must remain rare and documented.

---

# 7.4.12.21 Example: Publishing an Article

```text
Admin UI
    ↓
publishArticleAction()
    ↓
Editorial.publishArticle()
    ├── Identity.requirePermission("article.publish")
    ├── Editorial.validatePublication()
    ├── EditorialRepository.updateStatus()
    ├── Audit.record()
    └── Outbox.record("ArticlePublished")
            ↓
COMMIT
            ↓
Return success
            ↓
Background reactions
    ├── Search indexes Article
    ├── Analytics records publication
    ├── Distribution prepares drafts
    └── Cache revalidates relevant views
```

Newsletter delivery should only occur automatically if the editorial workflow explicitly schedules it.

Publishing an Article does not necessarily mean emailing every subscriber.

---

# 7.4.12.22 Example: Adding an Article to a Journey

```text
Journey Editor
    ↓
addArticleToChapterAction()
    ↓
Journeys.addArticleToChapter()
    ├── Identity.requirePermission("journey.manage")
    ├── Editorial.getArticleReference(articleId)
    ├── JourneyRepository.getChapter(chapterId)
    ├── JourneyOrdering.validatePosition()
    ├── JourneyRepository.insertArticleReference()
    └── Audit.record()
```

Journeys asks Editorial for a valid reference.

It does not import `ArticleRepository`.

---

# 7.4.12.23 Example: Submitting a Margin Note

```text
Reader
    ↓
Margin Note Form
    ↓
Community.submitMarginNote()
    ├── Validate content
    ├── Rate limit
    ├── Editorial.assertPublicArticleExists()
    ├── Normalize author identity
    ├── Insert as pending
    └── Log submission result
```

Community owns the Margin Note.

Editorial only confirms that the public parent Article exists.

---

# 7.4.12.24 Example: Publishing a Shared Page

```text
Moderator
    ↓
Community.publishSharedPage()
    ├── Identity.requirePermission("community.publish")
    ├── Confirm moderation status
    ├── Set published state
    ├── Audit decision
    ├── Revalidate Community views
    └── Emit SharedPagePublished
```

Analytics or Newsletter may react later.

They do not participate in the publication transaction.

---

# 7.4.12.25 Example: Newsletter Content Assembly

```text
Newsletter Editor
    ↓
Newsletter.createCampaignDraft()
    ├── Identity.requirePermission("newsletter.manage")
    ├── Editorial.getNewsletterEligibleArticles()
    ├── Notebook.getNewsletterEligibleEntries()
    ├── Journeys.getRecentUpdates()
    ├── Community.getFeaturedSharedPages()
    └── Newsletter stores campaign draft
```

The Newsletter module receives public editorial projections.

It does not query every domain’s tables directly.

---

# 7.4.12.26 Example: Search Indexing

```text
ArticlePublished Event
    ↓
Search.handleArticlePublished()
    ├── Editorial.getSearchDocument(articleId)
    ├── Search transform
    └── Search index write
```

Search stores only the information required for retrieval.

The Article remains canonical.

If the Search index is lost, it must be rebuildable from canonical domain data.

---

# 7.4.12.27 Example: Distribution

```text
Canonical Article
    ↓
Distribution.createDerivativeDrafts()
        ├── LinkedIn draft
        ├── Newsletter excerpt
        ├── Video outline
        ├── Short-form script
        └── RSS representation
```

Each derivative records:

* canonical source;
* target platform;
* format;
* editorial status;
* publication URL;
* publication time;
* failure state.

Distribution never modifies the Article’s canonical body.

---

# 7.4.12.28 Shared Kernel

Some concepts are used by several modules and may live in a small shared kernel.

Acceptable shared concepts include:

```text
EntityId
Timestamp
Pagination
Result
AppError
PermissionName
DomainEvent
AuditContext
```

The shared kernel must remain small.

It must not become a dumping ground for business logic.

Do not place these in shared code:

* Article status logic;
* Journey ordering;
* Newsletter consent;
* moderation rules;
* Media processing policy.

Those belong to their modules.

---

# 7.4.12.29 Import Boundary Enforcement

Boundary rules should be enforced automatically where practical.

Possible mechanisms include:

* TypeScript path aliases;
* ESLint restricted-import rules;
* module boundary tests;
* dependency graph checks;
* code-review requirements.

Example restriction:

```text
modules/community/**
may import
modules/editorial/public

may not import
modules/editorial/infrastructure/**
modules/editorial/domain/**
```

Automated enforcement is preferable to relying only on memory.

---

# 7.4.12.30 Module Test Boundaries

Each module requires several test levels.

## Domain tests

Test business rules without framework or database dependencies.

## Application tests

Test use-case orchestration and permission handling.

## Repository tests

Test database behaviour and mappings.

## Contract tests

Verify the module’s public API continues to satisfy consumers.

## Cross-module integration tests

Test defined workflows such as:

* Article attached to Journey;
* Article publication triggers indexing;
* Shared Page publication updates public views;
* Subscriber confirmation schedules email state correctly.

Cross-module tests should cover contracts, not private implementations.

---

# 7.4.12.31 Failure Isolation

Optional modules must not become critical dependencies.

Examples:

* Search failure must not prevent Article reading.
* Analytics failure must not prevent publication.
* Distribution failure must not alter canonical content.
* Newsletter failure must not take down the homepage.
* Media-processing failure should preserve the original upload and expose a clear processing state.
* Community moderation failure must not affect Article access.

The system should degrade around the failed module.

---

# 7.4.12.32 Timeout and Retry Ownership

The module making an external call owns:

* timeout;
* retry policy;
* backoff;
* failure mapping;
* observability.

Example:

Media’s storage adapter owns storage upload timeouts.

Editorial should not know storage-provider retry details.

Retry rules must distinguish between operations that are safe to repeat and those that require idempotency protection.

---

# 7.4.12.33 Idempotency Across Modules

Cross-module reactions may run more than once.

Consumers must tolerate duplicate delivery.

Examples:

* indexing the same Article twice should produce one current search record;
* creating a distribution draft twice should not create duplicates;
* sending a confirmation email requires an idempotency key;
* processing a Media asset twice should not generate inconsistent variants.

Use stable event IDs or business keys to detect duplicates.

---

# 7.4.12.34 Criteria for a New Module

A new module is justified when the capability has:

1. a distinct business purpose;
2. its own vocabulary;
3. its own lifecycle;
4. separate data ownership;
5. different authorization rules;
6. an independent rate of change;
7. enough complexity that placing it elsewhere weakens cohesion.

Do not create a module for every small feature.

Examples that may become modules:

```text
Books
Podcasts
Courses
Events
Organizations
Membership
Recommendations
```

Examples that likely remain features within existing modules:

```text
Article reading time
Hero rotation
Category navigation
Newsletter confirmation form
```

---

# 7.4.12.35 Criteria for Service Extraction

A module should remain inside the modular monolith unless there is evidence that independent deployment is necessary.

Possible extraction triggers:

* a separate team owns the module;
* the module has radically different scaling needs;
* failures must be isolated operationally;
* it requires a different runtime;
* deployment cadence conflicts with the main application;
* resource-heavy processing threatens core reading traffic;
* legal or data residency requirements demand separation.

Potential future extraction candidates:

* Media processing;
* Search;
* Analytics;
* AI generation;
* Email delivery;
* Video transcoding.

Articles, Notebook Entries, and Journeys should not be split merely because microservices sound more advanced.

---

# 7.4.12.36 Extraction Readiness

A module is easier to extract when it already has:

* explicit public contracts;
* private repositories;
* no cross-module table manipulation;
* events for asynchronous reactions;
* its own tests;
* clear data ownership;
* minimal shared kernel dependency;
* stable identifiers.

The modular monolith is therefore not a temporary or inferior design.

It is the preparation for responsible extraction only if extraction becomes necessary.

---

# 7.4.12.37 Architectural Anti-Patterns

## Shared database free-for-all

Every repository querying every table.

## God service

One service managing Articles, Journeys, Community, and Newsletter.

## Invisible side effects

Publishing code silently sends newsletters, changes Journeys, and posts externally.

## Bidirectional module dependency

Two modules requiring each other’s internals.

## Generic content manager

A universal service hiding all domain-specific behaviour.

## Event-driven everything

Using events where a simple direct call is clearer.

## Distributed system before scale

Introducing queues and services without operational need.

## Copying canonical data between modules

Creating conflicting sources of truth.

---

# 7.4.12.38 Architecture Review Checklist

Before approving a cross-module feature, verify:

* Which module owns the capability?
* Which module owns the data?
* Does the caller use a public contract?
* Is any repository imported across a boundary?
* Is the interaction synchronous or asynchronous for a clear reason?
* What happens if the dependency fails?
* Is a transaction required?
* Does the operation have hidden side effects?
* Is the operation idempotent?
* Is authorization checked by the owning module?
* Are logs and audit events assigned correctly?
* Can the consumer be tested without reading private implementation?
* Does this create a circular dependency?
* Would an event or direct call be clearer?
* Is the design still appropriate for one developer and a limited budget?

---

# 7.4.12.39 Architectural Decision Record

**Decision:** TWN uses explicit domain modules inside one modular monolith. Modules communicate through public application contracts and carefully selected domain events. Direct access to another module’s repositories or tables is prohibited.

**Reasons:**

* preserves domain ownership;
* supports long-term maintainability;
* avoids premature distributed infrastructure;
* makes eventual extraction possible;
* limits unintended side effects;
* improves testing;
* protects canonical content;
* aligns with KISS, YAGNI, SOLID, and Clean Architecture.

**Expected lifetime:** Long-term. Specific module boundaries may evolve, but the rules of explicit ownership and controlled interaction should remain permanent.

---

## End of 7.4.12

The next section is:

# **7.4.13 — Deployment Topology, Runtime Boundaries & Environment Architecture**

It will document:

* browser, edge, server, database, and worker boundaries;
* Node.js versus edge runtime decisions;
* Vercel deployment topology;
* Supabase connectivity;
* development, preview, staging, and production environments;
* secret separation;
* cron and background processing;
* regional deployment considerations;
* failure domains;
* portability and migration strategy.

# 7.4.13 Deployment Topology, Runtime Boundaries & Environment Architecture

**Status:** Foundational deployment specification
**Current platform:** Vercel + Supabase
**Application model:** Next.js modular monolith
**Primary runtime:** Node.js
**Primary objective:** Keep deployment simple, inexpensive, secure, observable, and portable while ensuring compute runs close to the data it depends on

---

# Purpose

This section defines where every part of TWN executes.

It establishes the boundaries between:

* the reader’s browser;
* Vercel’s global delivery network;
* Next.js rendering;
* Node.js server functions;
* middleware;
* scheduled functions;
* background work;
* Supabase PostgreSQL;
* object storage;
* third-party services;
* local development;
* preview deployments;
* production.

This chapter exists to prevent infrastructure decisions from being made independently inside features.

Without a documented deployment topology, the application may gradually accumulate:

* database calls from unsuitable runtimes;
* secrets exposed to the browser;
* preview deployments connected to production data;
* functions running far from the database;
* background work inside user-facing requests;
* inconsistent environment variables;
* long-running workloads on short-lived infrastructure;
* deployment-provider lock-in.

The deployment architecture must serve TWN’s product, not dictate it.

---

# 7.4.13.1 Deployment Philosophy

TWN should begin with the smallest topology that safely supports the product.

Version 1 should use:

```text
One repository
One Next.js application
One Vercel project
One production Supabase project
One non-production Supabase project where financially practical
One object-storage boundary
```

TWN does not initially require:

* Kubernetes;
* containers managed by the founder;
* multiple application services;
* a service mesh;
* independent microservice deployments;
* multi-region databases;
* dedicated message brokers;
* long-running worker fleets.

These may become justified later, but they would add operational work before they add reader value.

---

# 7.4.13.2 High-Level Deployment Topology

```text
Reader Browser
      │
      ▼
Vercel Global Network / CDN
      │
      ├── Static assets
      ├── Cached HTML
      ├── Optimized images
      └── Routing
      │
      ▼
Next.js Application
      │
      ├── Server Components
      ├── Server Actions
      ├── Route Handlers
      ├── Middleware
      └── Scheduled endpoints
      │
      ▼
Vercel Functions — Node.js Runtime
      │
      ├── Domain modules
      ├── Application services
      ├── Repositories
      └── Infrastructure adapters
      │
      ├──────────────┬───────────────┬──────────────┐
      ▼              ▼               ▼              ▼
Supabase         Object Storage   Email Provider   Search
PostgreSQL                                         Future
      │
      ▼
Backups, Logs, Monitoring
```

Vercel serves globally distributed content through its network, while Functions execute server-side code and scale according to demand. Function placement should be considered in relation to the database because physical distance between compute and data directly affects latency. ([Vercel][1])

---

# 7.4.13.3 Runtime Zones

TWN has five primary runtime zones.

## Zone 1 — Browser

The browser is untrusted.

It may execute:

* rendered HTML;
* CSS;
* minimal JavaScript;
* Client Components;
* browser interactions;
* accessible form behaviour;
* temporary interface state.

It must never receive:

* database passwords;
* service-role credentials;
* private API keys;
* newsletter subscriber lists;
* moderation queues;
* unrestricted storage credentials;
* internal authorization logic;
* audit data.

Client-side validation improves usability but has no security authority.

---

## Zone 2 — Global Delivery Network

The delivery network handles:

* static assets;
* cached pages;
* image delivery;
* routing;
* geographically distributed delivery;
* cache headers;
* immutable build assets.

This layer should serve as much public reading traffic as practical without invoking application compute.

Ideal candidates include:

```text
Fonts
CSS
JavaScript bundles
Icons
Public images
Static legal pages
Cached article output
Cached category pages
```

The delivery network does not own canonical data.

It only stores representations that can be regenerated.

---

## Zone 3 — Next.js Server Runtime

This is the primary application execution boundary.

It runs:

* Server Components;
* Server Actions;
* Route Handlers;
* metadata generation;
* authentication verification;
* domain services;
* repositories;
* cache invalidation;
* server-side transformations.

Vercel deploys App Router route handlers as server functions. Vercel currently supports several runtimes, but runtime selection changes the APIs and libraries available to the function. ([Vercel][2])

---

## Zone 4 — Data and Storage

This zone contains durable state:

* PostgreSQL records;
* authentication records;
* media metadata;
* object-storage files;
* audit records;
* future outbox events;
* future search indexes.

Application compute is disposable.

This zone is durable.

A failed or replaced function should not lose business data.

---

## Zone 5 — External Providers

Examples:

* authentication provider;
* transactional email provider;
* analytics provider;
* search provider;
* AI provider;
* social distribution APIs.

Every external provider must be isolated through an infrastructure adapter.

The Domain layer must not import vendor SDKs directly.

---

# 7.4.13.4 Node.js as the Default Runtime

TWN should use the Node.js runtime by default.

This applies to:

* Server Actions;
* Route Handlers;
* editorial workflows;
* database operations;
* Media orchestration;
* authentication integrations;
* newsletter actions;
* moderation;
* scheduled publishing endpoints.

## Why Node.js is the default

Node.js provides:

* broad package compatibility;
* full server APIs;
* mature database-driver support;
* predictable behaviour for the current stack;
* fewer restrictions than edge-specific runtimes;
* a simpler operational model for one developer.

Vercel’s current guidance recommends Node.js for many workloads and notes that its former standalone Edge Functions product is deprecated; Vercel recommends moving workloads to Node.js where improved compatibility, performance, and reliability are desired. ([Vercel][3])

## Architectural rule

Do not choose an Edge runtime merely because “edge” sounds faster.

A runtime is appropriate only when its execution characteristics match the workload.

---

# 7.4.13.5 Edge Runtime Policy

The Edge runtime may be considered for small, geographically sensitive operations that:

* do not require unsupported Node.js libraries;
* do not maintain direct database connections;
* execute quickly;
* require low-latency request inspection;
* do not perform heavy transformations;
* have measurable latency benefits.

Potential candidates:

```text
Simple redirects
Geographic routing
Light request normalization
Small authorization gates
A/B assignment
Header manipulation
```

The Edge runtime should not initially be used for:

* complex PostgreSQL queries;
* rich-text rendering;
* Media processing;
* email generation;
* PDF or EPUB export;
* AI generation;
* heavy dependencies;
* long-running operations;
* transactional workflows.

Vercel’s Edge runtime uses a V8-based isolated environment and supports a more limited API surface than full Node.js. ([Vercel][4])

---

# 7.4.13.6 Middleware Boundary

Middleware runs before the request reaches the route.

It may handle:

* basic authentication redirection;
* locale detection;
* request correlation IDs;
* security headers where appropriate;
* route access checks;
* simple request rewrites.

It should not contain:

* full authorization policy;
* database-heavy logic;
* Article publication rules;
* moderation logic;
* expensive API calls;
* large dependency imports.

## Why?

Middleware executes broadly.

A costly middleware operation can affect nearly every request.

## Authorization rule

Middleware may establish a coarse access boundary:

```text
Unauthenticated user
    ↓
Cannot enter /admin
```
