# 7.5 Frontend Architecture

---

# 7.5.1 Purpose and Frontend Philosophy

**Status:** Foundational frontend specification
**Applies to:** Public website, editorial dashboard, Journey reader, community interfaces, newsletter experiences, future reader accounts, multimedia pages, and all browser-facing TWN features
**Primary framework:** Next.js App Router with React and TypeScript
**Architectural position:** Server-first, content-first, progressively enhanced
**Primary objective:** Build a fast, accessible, durable reading and editorial interface without turning the browser into the application’s business-logic layer

---

# Purpose

This section defines the architectural principles governing every user interface within TWN.

It establishes:

* what responsibilities belong to the frontend;
* what responsibilities must remain on the server;
* how public reading experiences differ from administrative interfaces;
* how Server Components and Client Components should be used;
* how content is rendered;
* how interactions are progressively enhanced;
* how state is managed;
* how frontend code is organized;
* how the design system is applied;
* how accessibility, performance, security, and resilience influence implementation;
* how the interface evolves as TWN adds Journeys, Books, Podcasts, Videos, Courses, and community capabilities.

The frontend is not merely the visible layer of TWN.

It is the environment through which readers experience knowledge and editors manage it.

Its architecture must protect both experiences.

---

# Frontend Mission

The frontend exists to make TWN’s knowledge:

* readable;
* discoverable;
* navigable;
* understandable;
* accessible;
* emotionally coherent;
* responsive across devices;
* resilient under imperfect network conditions;
* safe to interact with;
* easy to maintain.

For editors, it must make content operations:

* deliberate;
* recoverable;
* traceable;
* understandable;
* resistant to accidental data loss;
* consistent with editorial permissions and workflow.

---

# The Frontend Is Not the Source of Truth

The frontend displays and gathers information.

It does not own business truth.

The following remain server-owned:

* Article publication status;
* User permissions;
* Journey ordering;
* moderation decisions;
* revision history;
* newsletter consent;
* Media ownership;
* subscriber information;
* publication timestamps;
* audit records;
* validation of sensitive operations.

The browser may temporarily represent these states, but it cannot define them.

```text
Browser state
    ↓
Temporary representation

Server and database
    ↓
Authoritative state
```

---

# Core Frontend Philosophy

TWN follows these primary frontend principles.

---

## Principle 1 — Content Comes Before Interface Complexity

TWN is primarily a knowledge and narrative platform.

The frontend should help readers engage with the content rather than compete with it.

The interface should prioritize:

* typography;
* reading rhythm;
* spacing;
* visual hierarchy;
* context;
* navigation;
* continuity between related content.

It should not prioritize:

* decorative motion without meaning;
* excessive dashboards;
* constant interruptions;
* engagement tricks;
* complex controls on public pages;
* visual density.

The reader should notice the ideas before noticing the framework.

---

## Principle 2 — Server Components Are the Default

Most TWN pages primarily read and display content.

That work belongs on the server.

Use Server Components for:

* Article pages;
* Journey pages;
* Notebook archives;
* Category pages;
* Author pages;
* Shared Page archives;
* editorial dashboard shells;
* CMS lists;
* permission-aware navigation;
* initial form data;
* metadata generation.

Client Components should be introduced only where browser interactivity is required.

```text
Default
    ↓
Server Component

Browser behaviour required
    ↓
Client Component
```

Do not place `"use client"` at the top of large page trees merely for convenience.

---

## Principle 3 — JavaScript Is Added Intentionally

Public content should remain useful even when:

* JavaScript is delayed;
* hydration is incomplete;
* a Client Component fails;
* the device is weak;
* the network is slow.

JavaScript should enhance:

* Hero thought rotation;
* search suggestions;
* form feedback;
* Journey progress;
* editor interactions;
* drag-and-drop;
* autosave;
* media controls;
* expandable navigation.

It should not be required merely to display an Article.

---

## Principle 4 — Progressive Enhancement

Every important interaction begins with a functional server-backed foundation.

Example:

```text
Newsletter form
    ↓
Semantic HTML form
    ↓
Server Action
    ↓
Server validation
```

Client-side enhancement may add:

* pending indicators;
* inline validation;
* optimistic feedback;
* smoother transitions.

But the underlying action remains functional without relying entirely on client state.

---

## Principle 5 — The Public Site and CMS Are Different Experiences

TWN contains at least two major frontend environments.

```text
Public Reading Experience
+
Editorial Management Experience
```

They share:

* brand language;
* design tokens;
* components;
* accessibility standards;
* technical foundation.

They do not share identical interaction priorities.

---

### Public Reading Experience

Optimizes for:

* clarity;
* performance;
* low cognitive load;
* storytelling;
* discovery;
* mobile reading;
* search visibility;
* accessibility.

---

### Editorial Management Experience

Optimizes for:

* accuracy;
* visibility of system state;
* safe mutation;
* efficient workflows;
* conflict prevention;
* recoverability;
* permissions;
* detailed feedback.

A minimal public page may be correct.

An overly minimal CMS may hide important state and create errors.

---

## Principle 6 — User Interface State Must Reflect Server Reality

The interface should never claim:

```text
Published
Saved
Approved
Scheduled
Uploaded
```

unless the server has confirmed the operation.

Temporary states must be labelled honestly:

```text
Saving…
Uploading…
Awaiting confirmation…
Saved locally only
Publication pending…
```

Optimistic updates are acceptable only where reversal is safe and clear.

They should not be used casually for:

* publishing;
* deleting;
* role assignment;
* subscriber export;
* moderation approval;
* permanent Media removal.

---

## Principle 7 — Accessibility Is Structural

Accessibility is not a final polishing task.

It influences:

* semantic markup;
* heading hierarchy;
* keyboard navigation;
* focus order;
* form labels;
* error announcements;
* colour contrast;
* motion preferences;
* Media descriptions;
* interactive control design;
* responsive behaviour.

A visually beautiful interface that prevents users from navigating or understanding it is incomplete.

---

## Principle 8 — Mobile Is a First-Class Reading Environment

Many TWN readers may access content primarily through mobile devices.

The interface must not treat mobile as a compressed desktop layout.

Mobile design should account for:

* readable line length;
* touch targets;
* text scaling;
* slow networks;
* image cost;
* navigation reach;
* form usability;
* long Articles;
* Journey progression;
* reduced screen width;
* device memory.

The frontend should be designed from the content outward, not from desktop screenshots downward.

---

## Principle 9 — Motion Must Communicate Meaning

Motion may support:

* page transitions;
* content reveal;
* Hero thought rotation;
* Journey progress;
* editor feedback;
* drag-and-drop clarity;
* state changes.

Motion should not:

* delay reading;
* obscure content;
* make navigation unpredictable;
* trigger constantly;
* ignore reduced-motion preferences;
* exist merely to appear premium.

Every animation should answer:

> What does this help the user understand?

---

## Principle 10 — Frontend Architecture Must Remain Replaceable

The current implementation may use:

* Next.js;
* React;
* Tailwind CSS;
* a specific component library;
* specific animation tools.

TWN’s content and business logic must not become inseparable from those tools.

The frontend may be replaced later without losing:

* Articles;
* Journeys;
* revision history;
* Media relationships;
* Authors;
* Categories;
* Tags;
* community content.

The interface consumes domain capabilities.

It does not define the domain.

---

# Frontend Responsibility Boundary

The frontend owns:

* rendering;
* layout;
* typography;
* interaction;
* user feedback;
* browser state;
* responsive presentation;
* accessibility behaviour;
* client-side enhancement;
* form composition;
* navigation;
* content display;
* error presentation.

The frontend does not own:

* authoritative validation;
* authorization;
* business workflows;
* database operations;
* publication rules;
* moderation rules;
* subscriber consent rules;
* content revision rules;
* audit history.

---

# High-Level Frontend Layers

```text
Frontend
│
├── Routing Layer
│
├── Page Composition Layer
│
├── Feature Interface Layer
│
├── Design System
│
├── Client Interaction Layer
│
└── Browser Infrastructure
```

---

## Routing Layer

Defines:

* public routes;
* CMS routes;
* layouts;
* loading boundaries;
* error boundaries;
* route groups;
* metadata;
* redirects.

---

## Page Composition Layer

Combines feature outputs into complete pages.

Examples:

* homepage;
* Article page;
* Journey overview;
* Category archive;
* editorial dashboard.

A page composes feature interfaces.

It should not directly implement all feature logic.

---

## Feature Interface Layer

Contains domain-facing UI features.

Examples:

```text
Article Reader
Journey Navigator
Notebook Entry Card
Shared Page Submission
Margin Note Form
Article Editor
Moderation Queue
Media Picker
```

Each feature owns its interface behaviour while calling the appropriate server capability.

---

## Design System

Provides shared visual and behavioural foundations.

Examples:

* typography;
* spacing;
* buttons;
* fields;
* cards;
* dialogs;
* icons;
* loading states;
* status indicators;
* empty states;
* feedback messages.

---

## Client Interaction Layer

Contains browser-specific behaviour such as:

* animation;
* drag-and-drop;
* autosave timers;
* local recovery;
* search autocomplete;
* media controls;
* keyboard shortcuts.

This layer should remain narrow.

---

## Browser Infrastructure

Provides:

* analytics adapters;
* error reporting;
* feature flags where appropriate;
* browser storage wrappers;
* focus management;
* network-state awareness.

It must not become a hidden business-logic layer.

---

# Frontend Application Surfaces

TWN should recognize its major frontend surfaces explicitly.

---

## Surface 1 — Public Homepage

Responsibilities:

* communicate the identity of TWN;
* present the Living Hero;
* surface featured and recent content;
* introduce Journeys;
* display Community contributions;
* support newsletter subscription;
* guide readers into deeper content.

The homepage is an editorial composition.

It is not the source of truth for the content it presents.

---

## Surface 2 — Article Reader

Responsibilities:

* present long-form knowledge;
* display Author and publication context;
* support Journey context;
* provide accessible Media;
* surface related content;
* allow Margin Note submission;
* maintain readable typography.

---

## Surface 3 — Notebook Experience

Responsibilities:

* display short and long reflections;
* maintain the notebook identity;
* support Today’s Page;
* support Hero thoughts;
* distinguish reflective content from formal Articles.

---

## Surface 4 — Journey Reader

Responsibilities:

* explain the larger narrative;
* show Chapters;
* preserve deliberate order;
* indicate current position;
* connect Articles, Notebook Entries, and future media;
* support starting and continuing a Journey.

---

## Surface 5 — Community Experience

Responsibilities:

* display approved Shared Pages;
* accept thoughtful submissions;
* present Margin Notes;
* communicate moderation expectations;
* protect contributors from confusing or unsafe interactions.

---

## Surface 6 — Search and Discovery

Responsibilities:

* accept search intent;
* distinguish content types;
* provide relevant results;
* connect results to Journeys;
* support filters and navigation;
* avoid dead ends.

---

## Surface 7 — Editorial CMS

Responsibilities:

* create and edit content;
* autosave safely;
* expose workflow state;
* manage revisions;
* manage Media;
* manage Journeys;
* review publication requirements;
* prevent accidental destructive actions.

---

## Surface 8 — Moderation Interface

Responsibilities:

* display pending submissions;
* present relevant moderation context;
* support approval, rejection, and publication;
* prevent duplicate review;
* preserve decision history;
* minimize exposure of unnecessary personal data.

---

## Surface 9 — Identity and Administration

Responsibilities:

* manage Users;
* manage roles;
* display permission effects;
* handle invitations;
* suspend accounts;
* inspect sensitive actions;
* protect critical operations with stronger confirmation.

---

# Frontend Quality Priorities

The frontend should optimize in this order:

```text
1. Correctness
2. Accessibility
3. Readability
4. Performance
5. Resilience
6. Maintainability
7. Visual refinement
8. Decorative sophistication
```

This does not mean visual quality is unimportant.

It means visual quality must not undermine the higher priorities.

---

# Frontend Constraints

The frontend must operate under these assumptions:

* readers may use low-powered phones;
* network quality may be inconsistent;
* JavaScript may load slowly;
* Media may fail;
* external providers may be unavailable;
* editors may lose connectivity;
* sessions may expire;
* content may become very long;
* Journeys may contain many entries;
* the product may grow without a large frontend team;
* costs must remain manageable.

Architecture must reflect these realities.

---

# Rendering Strategy Principle

TWN does not use one rendering method for every page.

It selects the appropriate strategy based on:

* freshness;
* privacy;
* personalization;
* interaction;
* publication frequency;
* cacheability.

High-level rule:

```text
Public and stable
    ↓
Static or cached server rendering

Public but frequently changing
    ↓
Cached server rendering with revalidation

Private or user-specific
    ↓
Dynamic server rendering

Browser interaction required
    ↓
Small Client Component
```

The detailed rendering matrix belongs later in this section under the frontend rendering strategy.

---

# State Management Philosophy

TWN should avoid introducing a global client-state library by default.

State should live as close as possible to where it is used.

Priority:

```text
URL and route state
    ↓
Server data
    ↓
Component state
    ↓
Feature context
    ↓
Global client state only when justified
```

Examples:

* search query belongs in the URL;
* Article content belongs on the server;
* open mobile menu belongs in local component state;
* editor draft state belongs in the editor feature;
* authenticated User authority belongs on the server.

---

# Form Philosophy

Forms are important frontend architecture, not incidental UI.

Every form should define:

* semantic labels;
* required fields;
* server validation;
* client enhancement;
* pending state;
* success state;
* field errors;
* general errors;
* retry behaviour;
* accessibility announcements;
* idempotency where required;
* protection against duplicate submission.

Critical forms must never rely solely on client validation.

---

# Error Philosophy

Errors should be:

* contained;
* understandable;
* actionable;
* honest;
* observable.

The frontend should distinguish:

```text
Validation failure
Authorization denial
Conflict
Temporary dependency failure
Not found
Unexpected system error
```

It should not display the same generic message for every problem.

It must also avoid exposing internal implementation details.

---

# Empty-State Philosophy

An empty state should explain:

* what is empty;
* why it may be empty;
* what the user can do;
* whether the condition is expected.

Examples:

Good:

```text
No Articles have been published in this Category yet.
```

Good:

```text
Your search returned no results. Try a broader phrase or explore these Journeys.
```

Bad:

```text
Nothing here.
```

A system outage must not be presented as a genuine empty state.

---

# Loading-State Philosophy

Loading indicators should preserve layout and orientation.

Prefer:

* meaningful skeletons;
* reserved Media dimensions;
* local pending states;
* progressive streaming;
* status text.

Avoid:

* full-page spinners for small operations;
* moving layouts;
* indefinite loading;
* hiding existing content during a minor refresh.

---

# Destructive-Action Philosophy

Destructive and high-impact actions require deliberate interface treatment.

Examples:

* delete;
* archive;
* unpublish;
* remove role;
* suspend User;
* replace scheduled revision;
* export subscribers.

The UI should provide:

* clear action name;
* affected resource;
* consequence;
* reversible alternative where available;
* confirmation;
* reason where required;
* progress state;
* final result.

Generic confirmations such as:

```text
Are you sure?
```

are insufficient for critical actions.

---

# Frontend Security Position

The frontend contributes to security through:

* safe rendering;
* output escaping;
* restricted rich content;
* correct form behaviour;
* origin-safe requests;
* hidden sensitive values;
* private cache separation;
* secure upload flows;
* defensive external-link rendering.

However, the frontend is never the final security authority.

Every protected action must remain secure when the frontend is bypassed.

---

# Frontend Performance Position

Performance should be achieved primarily through:

* server rendering;
* minimal client JavaScript;
* component-level code splitting;
* appropriate caching;
* optimized fonts;
* optimized Media;
* stable layouts;
* efficient data projections;
* selective hydration;
* responsive image delivery.

The frontend should not compensate for poor architecture with loading animations.

---

# Frontend Maintainability Position

A maintainable frontend should allow an engineer to answer:

* Where does this feature live?
* Is this component shared or feature-specific?
* Does this code run on the server or browser?
* Who owns this state?
* Which domain capability does it call?
* Is it safe to reuse?
* What data does it require?
* What happens when it fails?
* Is it accessible?
* Is it tested?

If those answers are unclear, the frontend boundary is probably poorly designed.

---

# Frontend Evolution

The frontend must support gradual expansion into:

* Books;
* Podcast Episodes;
* Video Essays;
* Courses;
* multilingual content;
* reader accounts;
* reading lists;
* Journey progress;
* institutional experiences;
* offline or installable reading;
* future mobile applications.

These should extend stable interface patterns rather than create a separate design language for every content type.

---

# Initial Frontend Non-Goals

Version 1 does not require:

* a micro-frontend architecture;
* a separate frontend repository;
* a browser-only single-page application;
* global client state for all data;
* real-time collaborative editing;
* offline-first synchronization;
* separate desktop and mobile applications;
* unrestricted user-created page layouts;
* a custom frontend framework;
* a public component marketplace.

These may be evaluated later if real product needs emerge.

---

# Frontend Decision Summary

TWN’s frontend should be understood as:

```text
Server-first
Content-first
Accessible
Progressively enhanced
Responsive
Modular
Domain-aware
Performance-conscious
Provider-contained
```

It is not:

```text
Client-state-first
Animation-first
API-dependent for internal rendering
Business-logic-owning
Desktop-only
JavaScript-dependent for reading
```

---

# Completion Criteria for 7.5.1

This subsection is complete when the team agrees that:

* the frontend is not the source of business truth;
* Server Components are the default;
* Client Components require an explicit browser need;
* public reading and CMS experiences have different priorities;
* progressive enhancement is required;
* accessibility and mobile support are foundational;
* frontend state must reflect confirmed server state;
* the browser does not enforce authorization;
* content must remain portable beyond the frontend stack;
* global client state and micro-frontends are not default solutions.

---

## End of 7.5.1

The next subsection is:

# **7.5.2 — Frontend Technology Stack and Framework Decisions**

It should document:

* why Next.js App Router is used;
* React and TypeScript boundaries;
* Tailwind CSS and design-token strategy;
* component-library policy;
* animation-library policy;
* form and validation tooling;
* content-rendering libraries;
* icon, typography, and Media tooling;
* dependency-selection rules;
* what will not be installed;
* how the stack may evolve without coupling TWN’s content to one framework.

# 7.5.2 Frontend Technology Stack and Framework Decisions

**Status:** Foundational frontend technology specification
**Applies to:** Public website, editorial CMS, Journey reader, community interfaces, reader accounts, future multimedia surfaces, and all browser-facing features
**Primary stack:** Next.js App Router, React, TypeScript, Tailwind CSS
**Selection principle:** Use the smallest stable stack that supports TWN’s server-first, content-first architecture
**Primary objective:** Define which frontend technologies TWN adopts, why they are used, where their responsibilities end, and which dependencies must not be introduced without evidence

---

# Purpose

This subsection defines TWN’s approved frontend technology stack.

It establishes:

* the primary application framework;
* the routing architecture;
* React usage;
* TypeScript rules;
* CSS and design-token strategy;
* component-library policy;
* forms and validation tools;
* animation policy;
* content-rendering tools;
* icon and typography choices;
* image and Media handling;
* testing tools;
* package-management expectations;
* dependency-selection rules;
* upgrade and security policy;
* technologies TWN should avoid initially;
* conditions under which the stack may evolve.

The technology stack should make TWN easier to build and maintain.

It must not become TWN’s identity.

---

# 7.5.2.1 Stack Summary

The recommended frontend stack is:

```text
Framework
└── Next.js App Router

UI Runtime
└── React

Language
└── TypeScript

Styling
└── Tailwind CSS
    +
    CSS custom properties
    +
    limited CSS Modules or global CSS where appropriate

Forms
└── Native HTML forms
    +
    React Hook Form for complex client-managed forms
    +
    Zod for boundary validation

Server Mutations
└── Server Actions

External HTTP Interfaces
└── Route Handlers

Content Rendering
└── Markdown parser
    +
    controlled typed-block renderer
    +
    sanitization pipeline

Icons
└── One consistent SVG icon library

Animation
└── CSS first
    +
    Motion library only for justified complex interaction

Images
└── Next.js Image
    +
    TWN Media domain

Fonts
└── next/font
    +
    self-hosted or framework-optimized font files

Testing
├── Vitest or Jest
├── React Testing Library
└── Playwright

Code Quality
├── ESLint
├── TypeScript compiler
└── Prettier or one controlled formatter

Package Management
└── pnpm
```

This stack is intentionally conventional.

TWN does not benefit from creating a custom frontend framework.

---

# 7.5.2.2 Next.js App Router Decision

## Decision

TWN uses the **Next.js App Router** as its primary web framework and routing system.

The App Router is Next.js’s file-system router built around React features including Server Components, Suspense, and Server Functions. It supports layouts, nested routing, loading states, error boundaries, data fetching, mutations, caching, and Route Handlers. ([Next.js][1])

---

## Why Next.js fits TWN

TWN requires several capabilities in one application:

* public content rendering;
* server-side data access;
* metadata generation;
* search-engine discoverability;
* static and dynamic rendering;
* editorial forms;
* authenticated CMS routes;
* structured error handling;
* streaming;
* caching;
* image and font optimization;
* internal server mutations;
* external integrations.

Next.js provides these capabilities inside one framework, reducing the need to assemble separate routing, server-rendering, build, API, image, and deployment systems.

---

## Why the App Router rather than the Pages Router

The Pages Router remains supported, but the App Router is the newer model designed around React Server Components and modern Next.js application architecture. ([Next.js][2])

For TWN, the App Router provides:

* nested layouts;
* route groups;
* colocated loading interfaces;
* error boundaries;
* Server Components by default;
* server mutation support;
* streaming through Suspense;
* clear route-based architecture.

TWN should not maintain both routing systems without a migration requirement.

The default structure is:

```text
src/app/
├── (public)/
├── (editorial)/
├── (auth)/
├── api/
├── layout.tsx
├── loading.tsx
├── error.tsx
└── not-found.tsx
```

---

## File-system routing benefit

Next.js maps folders and special files to routes, layouts, loading states, and error states. ([Next.js][3])

This gives TWN a visible correspondence between:

```text
Product route
        ↓
Frontend route directory
```

Example:

```text
/journeys/building-twn
```

maps conceptually to:

```text
app/(public)/journeys/[slug]/page.tsx
```

This structure is understandable without maintaining a separate route-registration system.

---

## Next.js boundary

Next.js owns:

* routing;
* rendering coordination;
* server and client component composition;
* request handling;
* build output;
* cache integration;
* metadata;
* framework-level optimization.

Next.js must not own:

* Article business rules;
* Journey ordering rules;
* permissions;
* moderation policy;
* revision history;
* Media ownership;
* subscriber consent.

Framework code calls TWN application services.

It does not replace them.

---

# 7.5.2.3 React Decision

## Decision

TWN uses React as its component and interface runtime.

React supports both Client Components and Server Components. Server Components render in a separate server environment and may execute during a build or per request, depending on the framework’s rendering strategy. ([react.dev][4])

---

## React component policy

Use functional components.

React continues to support class components, but its current documentation recommends functions for new code. ([react.dev][5])

Correct:

```tsx
type ArticleHeaderProps = {
  title: string;
  excerpt?: string;
};

export function ArticleHeader({
  title,
  excerpt,
}: ArticleHeaderProps) {
  return (
    <header>
      <h1>{title}</h1>
      {excerpt ? <p>{excerpt}</p> : null}
    </header>
  );
}
```

Do not introduce class components unless integrating legacy code that genuinely requires them.

---

## React is for interface composition

React should organize:

* layouts;
* content presentations;
* interactive controls;
* loading boundaries;
* forms;
* feature interfaces.

React components should not become:

* repositories;
* authorization engines;
* domain services;
* direct database clients;
* uncontrolled global state stores.

---

## Composition over inheritance

Frontend reuse should rely primarily on:

* props;
* children;
* slots;
* feature composition;
* hooks for browser behaviour;
* utility functions.

Avoid deep inheritance structures and large base components.

---

## Hooks policy

Hooks should encapsulate browser-facing behaviour.

Good examples:

```text
useReducedMotionPreference
useAutosaveStatus
useLocalDraftRecovery
useMediaQuery
useJourneyProgress
```

Hooks must not hide inappropriate server responsibilities.

Bad:

```text
usePublishArticle()
```

that contains direct database access, permission logic, and publication rules entirely in the browser.

A client hook may invoke a Server Action, but the server remains authoritative.

---

# 7.5.2.4 React Server Components

## Decision

Server Components are the default component type in TWN’s App Router.

Next.js distinguishes Server and Client Components and recommends composing them according to whether a component needs server-only access or browser interaction. ([Next.js][6])

Server Components should be used for:

* public page composition;
* Article content;
* Journey structure;
* Author information;
* Categories and Tags;
* CMS lists;
* permission-aware navigation;
* initial editor data;
* server-derived metadata;
* server-only secrets and service access.

---

## Benefits for TWN

Server Components allow TWN to:

* fetch data close to the server;
* avoid shipping data-access libraries to browsers;
* reduce browser JavaScript;
* protect server credentials;
* render content before client interaction;
* stream slow page sections;
* compose server and client behaviour.

---

## Server Component restrictions

Server Components must not use browser-only APIs such as:

* `window`;
* `document`;
* `localStorage`;
* browser event handlers;
* client-only state hooks.

When browser behaviour is required, isolate it inside a Client Component.

---

## Server Component data rule

A Server Component should receive or request the smallest projection required for rendering.

Avoid passing entire database records into the component tree.

Example:

```typescript
type ArticleCardView = {
  title: string;
  slug: string;
  excerpt: string;
  publishedAt: string;
  cover: {
    src: string;
    alt: string;
  } | null;
};
```

This is safer and more maintainable than exposing every Article field.

---

# 7.5.2.5 Client Components

## Decision

Client Components are introduced only when the feature requires:

* state that changes in the browser;
* event listeners;
* browser APIs;
* client-side animation;
* local recovery;
* drag-and-drop;
* interactive media;
* real-time user feedback;
* third-party browser-only widgets.

A file becomes a client boundary through the `"use client"` directive.

---

## Appropriate examples

```text
HeroThoughtRotator
MobileNavigation
SearchAutocomplete
ArticleEditor
MediaUploader
JourneyReorderPanel
AutosaveIndicator
AudioPlayer
VideoControls
```

---

## Inappropriate examples

These should ordinarily remain server-rendered:

```text
ArticleBody
JourneyOverview
AuthorBiography
CategoryHeader
Footer
StaticNavigationLinks
PublishedSharedPage
```

---

## Client-boundary rule

Place `"use client"` as low in the component tree as possible.

Bad:

```text
Entire Article Page
    ↓
Client Component
```

Good:

```text
Article Page — Server
├── Article Content — Server
├── Journey Context — Server
└── Margin Note Form — Client only where needed
```

A large client boundary can force otherwise static descendants into the browser bundle.

---

# 7.5.2.6 React and Next.js Security Upgrade Policy

React Server Components and related infrastructure received important security fixes after vulnerabilities were disclosed in late 2025. The React team advised immediate upgrades for affected versions. ([react.dev][7])

Therefore, TWN must treat framework upgrades as security maintenance rather than optional redesign work.

Rules:

1. Subscribe to React and Next.js security announcements.
2. Apply critical framework patches promptly.
3. Do not remain on unsupported framework versions.
4. Test upgrades in Preview before Production.
5. Pin versions through the lockfile.
6. Avoid casually using unstable or canary releases in Production.
7. Document upgrade decisions and breaking changes.
8. Run full authentication, Server Action, rendering, and CMS tests after framework upgrades.

AI-generated code must not introduce outdated framework patterns without review.

---

# 7.5.2.7 TypeScript Decision

## Decision

All TWN application code should use TypeScript.

TypeScript extends JavaScript with a static type system and can identify unexpected program behaviour before runtime. ([typescriptlang.org][8])

---

## Why TypeScript is required

TWN contains several complex data relationships:

* Articles and revisions;
* Authors and Users;
* Journeys and Chapters;
* content references;
* publication states;
* permissions;
* moderation states;
* Media types;
* API responses;
* Server Action results.

TypeScript helps make these contracts explicit.

Example:

```typescript
type ArticleStatus =
  | "draft"
  | "review"
  | "scheduled"
  | "published"
  | "archived";
```

This is safer than passing arbitrary status strings throughout the codebase.

---

## TypeScript responsibilities

Use TypeScript to model:

* component props;
* view models;
* application commands;
* application results;
* domain identifiers;
* lifecycle states;
* service contracts;
* adapter contracts;
* route parameters;
* test fixtures.

---

## TypeScript is not runtime validation

A TypeScript type disappears when the code runs.

External input still requires runtime validation.

This input is untrusted:

```typescript
const body = await request.json();
```

Even if the developer writes:

```typescript
const body = (await request.json()) as PublishArticleInput;
```

The cast does not validate the request.

Use a runtime schema at trust boundaries.

---

## Strict mode

TWN should enable strict TypeScript checking.

Recommended foundation:

```json
{
  "compilerOptions": {
    "strict": true,
    "noUncheckedIndexedAccess": true,
    "exactOptionalPropertyTypes": true,
    "noImplicitOverride": true,
    "noFallthroughCasesInSwitch": true
  }
}
```

Some options may initially reveal significant migration work in an existing codebase. They should be introduced deliberately rather than disabled permanently without explanation.

---

## Avoid `any`

`any` bypasses TypeScript’s safety.

Prefer:

```text
unknown
```

at untrusted boundaries, then validate or narrow it.

Acceptable temporary `any` usage must be:

* localized;
* commented;
* tracked for removal;
* necessary for a third-party type limitation.

---

## Domain types versus database types

Do not use generated database-row types as the only types throughout the interface.

Separate:

```text
Database Row
Domain Model
Application Result
Frontend View Model
```

Example:

```typescript
type ArticleCardView = {
  title: string;
  slug: string;
  excerpt: string;
  displayDate: string;
};
```

The card does not need to know about:

* deletion timestamps;
* audit data;
* draft pointers;
* database ownership fields.

---

## Identifier types

Plain strings are easy to mix accidentally.

TWN may eventually introduce branded identifier types:

```typescript
type ArticleId = string & { readonly __brand: "ArticleId" };
type JourneyId = string & { readonly __brand: "JourneyId" };
```

Do not introduce extensive type machinery if it makes ordinary development harder.

Use it where identifier confusion presents a real risk.

---

# 7.5.2.8 Tailwind CSS Decision

## Decision

TWN uses Tailwind CSS as its primary styling system.

Tailwind is a utility-first CSS framework whose classes are composed directly in templates. Its build process scans project files and generates static CSS for the classes used by the application. ([Tailwind CSS][9])

TWN already uses Tailwind CSS 4, so the blueprint should standardize the current generation rather than document an older configuration model. Tailwind CSS 4 introduced a revised configuration and customization approach. ([Tailwind CSS][10])

---

## Why Tailwind fits TWN

Tailwind supports:

* rapid layout implementation;
* consistent spacing;
* responsive styles;
* state variants;
* design-token integration;
* reduced naming disputes;
* colocated component styling;
* static CSS output;
* controlled custom design.

It is suitable for TWN’s custom notebook and editorial visual language because it does not impose a predesigned appearance.

---

## Tailwind does not replace the design system

Without standards, Tailwind can create:

```text
Random font sizes
Random margins
Random greys
Random widths
Random shadows
```

TWN must define tokens and approved patterns.

The correct model is:

```text
Design Tokens
    ↓
Tailwind Theme
    ↓
Design-System Components
    ↓
Feature Interfaces
```

Not:

```text
Developer invents utilities per screen
```

---

# 7.5.2.9 CSS Custom Properties and Design Tokens

TWN should define foundational design values as CSS custom properties.

Example:

```css
:root {
  --color-background: #ffffff;
  --color-foreground: #111111;
  --color-muted: #6b6b6b;
  --color-border: #dedede;

  --font-editorial: var(--font-serif);
  --font-interface: var(--font-sans);

  --content-width-reading: 42rem;
  --content-width-wide: 76rem;

  --radius-small: 0.25rem;
  --radius-medium: 0.5rem;

  --duration-fast: 120ms;
  --duration-normal: 220ms;
}
```

The actual colour and typography values belong to the design-system specification.

Benefits:

* consistent theme control;
* easier future dark mode;
* lower coupling to raw Tailwind colour names;
* more meaningful design vocabulary;
* easier global brand adjustment.

---

## Semantic tokens

Prefer:

```text
background
foreground
muted
border
accent
danger
success
warning
```

over interface code that depends directly on:

```text
gray-700
stone-200
red-500
```

Raw palette values may define tokens.

Feature components should usually consume semantic values.

---

# 7.5.2.10 Tailwind Usage Rules

## Rule 1 — Use utilities for component-level styling

Example:

```tsx
<article className="mx-auto max-w-reading px-5 py-12">
```

---

## Rule 2 — Extract repeated patterns

When the same long class set appears repeatedly, create:

* a design-system component;
* a shared variant;
* a semantic utility;
* a stable CSS abstraction.

Do not create a component merely to avoid six class names.

Extract when a meaningful reusable pattern exists.

---

## Rule 3 — Avoid uncontrolled arbitrary values

Bad:

```text
mt-[37px]
text-[17.4px]
max-w-[713px]
```

Occasional arbitrary values may be appropriate for precise art direction.

Repeated arbitrary values usually indicate missing tokens.

---

## Rule 4 — Preserve readable markup

Avoid components with extremely long unreadable class strings.

Use:

* logical line breaks;
* variant utilities;
* helper functions;
* semantic components.

---

## Rule 5 — Do not construct class names dynamically from unrestricted strings

Tailwind scans source files for class names.

This is unreliable:

```typescript
const className = `text-${color}-500`;
```

Use explicit variants:

```typescript
const variants = {
  neutral: "text-neutral-700",
  danger: "text-red-700",
} as const;
```

---

# 7.5.2.11 CSS Modules and Global CSS

Tailwind is primary, but it is not the only permitted CSS mechanism.

## Global CSS is appropriate for

* CSS reset or base layer;
* root design tokens;
* typography defaults;
* selection styles;
* reduced-motion defaults;
* article-content rendering;
* print styles;
* shared keyframes.

## CSS Modules are appropriate for

* complex notebook illustrations;
* unusual editorial layouts;
* deeply coordinated animations;
* styles that become unreadable as utilities;
* third-party DOM structures requiring selectors.

## Rule

Use the simplest styling mechanism that remains maintainable.

Do not rewrite understandable CSS into Tailwind solely for ideological consistency.

Do not create large global stylesheets that bypass the design system.

---

# 7.5.2.12 Component-Library Policy

## Decision

TWN should not adopt a large fully styled component library as its visual foundation.

Examples of libraries to avoid as the primary visual system:

* Material UI;
* Ant Design;
* Bootstrap component themes;
* large dashboard templates.

These can rapidly produce functional interfaces but may impose:

* a generic product appearance;
* heavy runtime or bundle cost;
* theme complexity;
* difficult customisation;
* visual conflict with TWN’s editorial identity.

---

## Headless primitives

TWN may use focused headless component primitives for difficult accessible interactions such as:

* dialogs;
* menus;
* popovers;
* tabs;
* comboboxes;
* tooltips;
* selects;
* focus management.

Suitable approaches may include Radix UI primitives or carefully selected equivalents.

The decision should be made per primitive or small package rather than installing an entire ecosystem automatically.

---

## shadcn-style component policy

Source-owned components generated from accessible primitives may be used selectively because TWN can:

* inspect the code;
* modify styling;
* remove unused parts;
* align components with its design language.

However:

* generated components are not automatically accessible after modification;
* copied components become TWN’s maintenance responsibility;
* the project must not install dozens of unused components;
* the visual defaults must not define the public brand.

---

## Public site versus CMS components

The public site should use a highly controlled editorial component set.

The CMS may use more conventional application components where clarity is more important than visual storytelling.

Shared foundations may include:

* Button;
* Link;
* FormField;
* Dialog;
* Alert;
* StatusBadge;
* Tooltip;
* VisuallyHidden.

Public-specific components may include:

* ArticleCard;
* NotebookPage;
* JourneyChapter;
* MarginNote;
* EditorialQuote.

CMS-specific components may include:

* DataTable;
* RevisionPanel;
* WorkflowStatus;
* MediaPicker;
* ModerationDecisionForm.

---

# 7.5.2.13 Variant Management

Components with several visual states should use a typed variant pattern.

Example:

```typescript
type ButtonVariant =
  | "primary"
  | "secondary"
  | "quiet"
  | "danger";
```

A lightweight utility such as `class-variance-authority` may be used if it genuinely reduces repetition.

Do not introduce a variant library merely to style a component with one appearance.

Every dependency must justify itself.

---

# 7.5.2.14 Form Technology Decision

## Default foundation

Use:

* native HTML forms;
* Server Actions;
* server-side validation;
* semantic form controls.

Next.js supports mutations through React Server Functions, including functions invoked from forms. ([Next.js][11])

---

## React Hook Form

React Hook Form may be used for forms with significant client-side interaction, including:

* Article editor metadata;
* complex conditional inputs;
* repeated field arrays;
* deeply nested form state;
* client-side previews;
* long CMS forms.

It should not be required for:

* newsletter email field;
* search form;
* simple confirmation forms;
* basic Margin Note form.

---

## Why native forms remain important

Native forms provide:

* semantic submission;
* keyboard accessibility;
* browser behaviour;
* progressive enhancement;
* less client JavaScript;
* integration with Server Actions.

A form library should solve complexity.

It should not be installed automatically on every form.

---

# 7.5.2.15 Zod Validation Decision

## Decision

TWN uses Zod for runtime validation at application boundaries.

Appropriate boundaries include:

* Server Action input;
* Route Handler input;
* environment variables;
* webhook payloads;
* typed-block attributes;
* form input;
* provider responses;
* browser-storage recovery data.

---

## One schema does not serve every layer automatically

Avoid forcing one massive schema to serve:

* database shape;
* domain entity;
* form input;
* API response;
* editor draft;
* public view.

Create focused schemas.

Example:

```typescript
const NewsletterSubscriptionSchema = z.object({
  email: z.string().trim().email(),
  consent: z.literal(true),
});
```

The server still normalizes and enforces domain rules.

---

## Validation ownership

```text
HTML attributes
    ↓
Basic browser usability

Zod input schema
    ↓
Request shape

Application service
    ↓
Business rules

Database
    ↓
Structural integrity
```

Each layer has a different responsibility.

---

# 7.5.2.16 Content Rendering Technology

TWN’s canonical editorial source is portable Markdown with controlled typed blocks.

The frontend rendering pipeline therefore requires:

```text
Markdown source
    ↓
Parse into syntax tree
    ↓
Validate TWN blocks
    ↓
Transform internal references
    ↓
Sanitize
    ↓
Render approved React components
```

---

## Recommended library approach

Use a maintained Markdown abstract-syntax-tree ecosystem, such as:

* `unified`;
* `remark`;
* `rehype`;
* focused supporting plugins.

Potential responsibilities:

* Markdown parsing;
* GitHub-flavoured Markdown where approved;
* syntax-tree transformations;
* HTML conversion;
* sanitization;
* heading extraction;
* plain-text extraction.

The exact plugins must be reviewed individually.

Do not install a large plugin bundle without understanding what syntax it enables.

---

## Raw HTML

Raw HTML inside Markdown should be disabled by default.

When a trusted migration genuinely contains HTML:

* parse through a controlled import process;
* sanitize;
* convert into supported Markdown or blocks;
* do not allow arbitrary HTML as routine editorial input.

---

## Code highlighting

Technical Articles may include code.

Syntax highlighting should preferably be generated on the server.

Possible options include:

* Shiki;
* a comparable server-compatible highlighter.

The frontend should avoid shipping a large language-highlighting runtime to every reader.

Only required languages should be supported or loaded.

---

## Heading generation

The renderer should generate:

* stable heading IDs;
* accessible heading hierarchy;
* table-of-contents metadata;
* duplicate-heading disambiguation.

The system must preserve user-facing heading text while generating safe anchors.

---

# 7.5.2.17 MDX Policy

Unrestricted database-authored MDX remains prohibited.

Trusted, code-reviewed MDX may later be used for:

* highly interactive technical essays;
* official static documentation;
* controlled product demonstrations.

Conditions:

* source stored in the trusted repository;
* imports reviewed;
* component allow-list;
* build-time validation;
* no community-authored execution;
* fallback representation.

The normal Article editor should produce Markdown and typed blocks, not arbitrary JSX.

---

# 7.5.2.18 Animation Technology Decision

## Default

Use CSS transitions and keyframes for:

* hover and focus transitions;
* opacity;
* small transforms;
* loading indicators;
* simple reveal effects;
* Hero ink or notebook effects where manageable.

CSS is appropriate when the animation is:

* simple;
* state-based;
* performant;
* expressible without complex orchestration.

---

## Motion library

A React animation library may be used for:

* coordinated layout transitions;
* gesture-driven interfaces;
* drag-and-drop feedback;
* complex Journey transitions;
* controlled spring animation;
* enter/exit orchestration.

The preferred choice should be a maintained focused library such as Motion.

---

## Motion-library boundary

Do not wrap the entire public site in animation providers unnecessarily.

Import animation code only in the components that need it.

Avoid making static Article content depend on an animation runtime.

---

## Reduced motion

All animation tools must respect:

```css
@media (prefers-reduced-motion: reduce)
```

Users who request reduced motion should receive:

* removed parallax;
* removed constant rotation;
* simpler fades or immediate state changes;
* no essential information conveyed through motion alone.

---

# 7.5.2.19 Icon Policy

TWN should use one primary SVG icon library.

Selection criteria:

* accessible SVG output;
* tree-shakeable imports;
* consistent visual style;
* broad but not excessive catalogue;
* maintained package;
* easy sizing and stroke control.

Possible candidates include Lucide or an equivalent focused library.

Rules:

* do not mix several icon styles;
* icons must not replace visible labels for unfamiliar actions;
* icon-only controls require accessible names;
* decorative icons should be hidden from assistive technology;
* custom brand icons should live in TWN’s own asset system.

Emoji should not become the standard administrative icon system.

---

# 7.5.2.20 Typography Technology

## Decision

Use `next/font` for application fonts.

Next.js includes font optimization in its application toolset. ([Next.js][12])

Benefits include:

* self-hosting or framework-controlled delivery;
* reduced external font requests;
* predictable loading;
* generated font variables;
* integration with layouts.

---

## Font roles

TWN should define semantic typography roles:

```text
Editorial display
Editorial body
Interface text
Code text
Handwritten accent — only where accessible and controlled
```

Do not assign fonts independently per component.

---

## Font-performance rules

* use the minimum number of families;
* load only necessary weights;
* prefer variable fonts where justified;
* define fallbacks;
* avoid render-blocking remote font stylesheets;
* test text rendering on low-end mobile devices;
* preserve readability if the custom font fails.

Typography details belong to the design-system volume, but the implementation technology belongs here.

---

# 7.5.2.21 Image and Media Technology

## Decision

Use Next.js Image for appropriate raster-image delivery while retaining TWN’s Media domain as the source of asset metadata.

Next.js includes image optimization capabilities in its application framework. ([Next.js][12])

---

## `next/image` is a renderer, not an asset manager

The Media domain still owns:

* asset identity;
* storage key;
* dimensions;
* alt text;
* captions;
* visibility;
* lifecycle;
* Media variants.

The frontend receives a resolved public Media projection.

---

## Image rules

Every meaningful image requires:

* known width and height, or controlled aspect ratio;
* correct `sizes`;
* appropriate priority;
* useful alt text;
* lazy loading when below the fold;
* optimized format where supported;
* fallback behaviour.

Do not mark every image as priority.

Priority should be reserved for likely above-the-fold assets such as the main Hero visual.

---

## SVG

SVG is appropriate for:

* icons;
* diagrams;
* illustrations;
* logos.

Untrusted uploaded SVG can contain unsafe or unexpected content.

Community-supplied SVG must not be served inline without a strict sanitization and security policy.

---

## Audio and video

Use native semantic media elements or carefully controlled players.

Future player components should support:

* captions and transcripts;
* keyboard operation;
* playback speed;
* visible controls;
* reduced autoplay;
* Media fallback;
* privacy-conscious embeds.

Do not introduce a large universal media-player package before the media requirements exist.

---

# 7.5.2.22 Date and Time Utilities

Use platform features first:

* `Date`;
* `Intl.DateTimeFormat`;
* server-normalized timestamps.

Introduce a date library only when TWN needs functionality such as:

* complex time-zone conversion;
* calendar arithmetic;
* editorial scheduling interfaces;
* recurring events.

Avoid shipping large date libraries to public pages for basic formatting.

Publication time should be formatted server-side or consistently through a lightweight shared utility.

---

# 7.5.2.23 Utility Libraries

A small class-name utility such as `clsx` may be used.

A merge utility for Tailwind classes may be used only where component APIs genuinely combine conflicting utilities.

Avoid creating a general `utils.ts` dumping ground.

Utilities should be grouped by purpose:

```text
lib/
├── dates/
├── formatting/
├── urls/
├── validation/
├── accessibility/
└── class-names/
```

Business utilities remain inside the owning module.

---

# 7.5.2.24 Client Data-Fetching Libraries

## Initial decision

Do not install React Query, SWR, Apollo Client, or another global client-data system by default.

Most TWN content should be fetched in Server Components.

Mutations should use Server Actions where appropriate.

---

## When a client data library may become justified

* polling a job state;
* frequently refreshed interactive dashboards;
* collaborative interfaces;
* complex optimistic updates;
* offline synchronization;
* client-side pagination in a persistent workspace;
* several Client Components sharing remote server state.

The library should be introduced for a defined feature, not because most React projects use one.

---

## GraphQL

TWN does not initially require GraphQL.

It may become useful if:

* several independent clients need flexible queries;
* a developer platform emerges;
* cross-domain public data becomes complex;
* schema tooling creates clear value.

GraphQL should not be introduced merely to replace straightforward internal service calls.

---

# 7.5.2.25 Global Client-State Libraries

## Initial decision

Do not install Redux, Zustand, MobX, or another global store for general application data.

Use:

```text
Server state
URL state
Local component state
Feature-scoped context
```

before global state.

---

## Possible future uses

A small global store may become reasonable for:

* complex editor UI state;
* multi-panel media workspace;
* offline reader state;
* persistent audio-player controls;
* cross-route draft workspace.

Even then, the store must not become the source of authoritative business data.

---

# 7.5.2.26 Drag-and-Drop

The CMS may eventually need drag-and-drop for:

* Journey Chapter ordering;
* Journey content ordering;
* Media organization.

A dedicated accessible drag-and-drop library may be used when that feature is implemented.

Requirements:

* keyboard alternative;
* screen-reader instructions;
* non-drag buttons for moving items;
* optimistic version checking;
* conflict handling;
* persistence only after server confirmation.

Do not add drag-and-drop to the public site for decoration.

---

# 7.5.2.27 Data Tables

Admin interfaces may need tables for:

* Articles;
* community submissions;
* Users;
* Media;
* subscriber operations;
* Audit records.

A headless table library may be considered when the interface requires:

* controlled sorting;
* filtering;
* column visibility;
* row selection;
* pagination;
* virtualisation.

Simple lists should not be implemented through a complex table engine.

Public Article archives should normally use semantic lists or card grids rather than administrative tables.

---

# 7.5.2.28 Virtualisation

Virtualisation renders only currently visible list items.

It may become useful for:

* very large Media libraries;
* long Audit lists;
* thousands of CMS records;
* large data tables.

It should not be used for:

* normal Article pages;
* small Journey Chapters;
* SEO-critical public content;
* accessible lists without careful testing.

Pagination should be considered before virtualisation.

---

# 7.5.2.29 Testing Stack

## Unit and component tests

Use Vitest or Jest with React Testing Library.

Purpose:

* component behaviour;
* formatting utilities;
* accessibility semantics;
* form feedback;
* client hooks;
* typed-block rendering;
* permission-driven UI.

Use one primary unit-test runner.

Do not maintain Jest and Vitest simultaneously without a migration reason.

---

## End-to-end tests

Use Playwright for browser workflows such as:

* public navigation;
* search;
* newsletter subscription;
* Shared Page submission;
* login;
* Article editing;
* autosave;
* publication;
* revision restoration;
* Journey reordering;
* moderation;
* permission denial.

---

## Visual testing

Visual regression may be introduced for:

* homepage;
* Article layout;
* Notebook Hero;
* Journey pages;
* responsive navigation;
* design-system components.

It should not replace semantic and behavioural tests.

---

## Accessibility testing

Use:

* semantic queries in React Testing Library;
* automated accessibility checks;
* Playwright accessibility tooling;
* manual keyboard testing;
* screen-reader checks for critical workflows.

Automated tools cannot prove full accessibility.

---

# 7.5.2.30 Linting and Formatting

## ESLint

Use ESLint for:

* framework rules;
* React rules;
* TypeScript rules;
* import boundaries;
* prohibited imports;
* accessibility checks where reliable;
* dependency rules.

Module boundaries should be enforceable through lint rules.

Example:

```text
community frontend
may import editorial/public

may not import
editorial/infrastructure
```

---

## Formatting

Use one formatter.

Prettier is acceptable where already configured.

Formatting should be automated and uncontroversial.

Do not spend engineering time debating individual whitespace choices.

---

## Type checking

The TypeScript compiler must run independently from the production build.

A successful development page load does not prove the project type-checks.

CI should run:

```text
lint
typecheck
test
build
```

---

# 7.5.2.31 Package Manager Decision

## Decision

Use `pnpm` as the canonical package manager.

Reasons:

* efficient dependency storage;
* strict dependency resolution;
* workspace support;
* existing project familiarity;
* reproducible lockfile.

The repository should contain only one authoritative lockfile:

```text
pnpm-lock.yaml
```

Remove conflicting lockfiles such as:

```text
package-lock.json
yarn.lock
```

unless an intentional migration is underway.

---

## Runtime version

The project should declare and pin an approved Node.js runtime range.

Use:

* `.nvmrc`, `.node-version`, or equivalent;
* `engines` in `package.json`;
* CI matching Production.

Avoid different major Node.js versions across local development, CI, and deployment.

---

# 7.5.2.32 Dependency-Selection Rules

Before adding a frontend dependency, answer:

1. What exact problem does it solve?
2. Can the platform or existing stack solve it?
3. Is the feature already required?
4. Is the package maintained?
5. Is its API stable?
6. What is its browser bundle cost?
7. Does it support Server Components where needed?
8. Does it require a large client boundary?
9. What are its transitive dependencies?
10. Is it accessible?
11. Does it have known security issues?
12. Can TWN remove it later?
13. Who will maintain the integration?
14. Can a focused smaller package solve the need?
15. Does it conflict with TWN’s architectural boundaries?

A package should be rejected when the problem is hypothetical.

---

# 7.5.2.33 Dependency Categories

## Foundation dependencies

Examples:

* Next.js;
* React;
* TypeScript;
* Tailwind CSS.

These define the main frontend platform.

---

## Capability dependencies

Examples:

* Zod;
* Markdown parser;
* code highlighter;
* accessible primitive library;
* React Hook Form.

These solve defined capabilities.

---

## Enhancement dependencies

Examples:

* animation;
* drag-and-drop;
* data tables;
* charts.

These should be loaded only when their feature requires them.

---

## Prohibited category

Do not install packages whose only purpose is to avoid understanding a small amount of straightforward code.

Examples may include:

* packages for one-line string operations;
* unmaintained UI effects;
* arbitrary wrapper packages;
* duplicate utility libraries.

---

# 7.5.2.34 Bundle-Impact Policy

Every client-side dependency affects:

* download size;
* parse time;
* execution;
* memory;
* mobile performance;
* vulnerability surface.

For every browser dependency, evaluate:

* minified size;
* tree-shaking support;
* route-level loading;
* whether it can remain server-only;
* whether native browser APIs are sufficient.

Large CMS-only packages should not enter public-page bundles.

Use dynamic imports for genuinely heavy optional interfaces.

---

# 7.5.2.35 Import Policy

Prefer direct imports.

Avoid package-wide namespace imports when they prevent tree shaking.

Example:

```typescript
import { Search } from "lucide-react";
```

rather than importing an entire icon catalogue into runtime structures.

Avoid barrel files that accidentally pull server-only code into client bundles.

Server-only modules should be explicitly protected and never imported by Client Components.

---

# 7.5.2.36 Server-Only and Client-Only Protection

Use framework and project conventions to prevent boundary mistakes.

Examples:

```text
*.server.ts
*.client.ts
server-only modules
client-only adapters
```

Sensitive infrastructure modules should fail clearly if accidentally imported into browser code.

Environment access should be separated:

```text
env.server.ts
env.client.ts
```

Client environment types may contain only explicitly public values.

---

# 7.5.2.37 Third-Party Scripts

Third-party browser scripts may include:

* Analytics;
* embeds;
* support tools;
* advertising in the distant future;
* experimentation platforms.

Each script must be reviewed for:

* privacy;
* performance;
* security;
* consent;
* failure behaviour;
* business necessity.

The public reading experience should not accumulate trackers casually.

Load scripts:

* after essential content where possible;
* only on required routes;
* with consent where required;
* with clear ownership.

---

# 7.5.2.38 Browser Support Policy

TWN should support modern browsers with active security support.

The project should not target obsolete browsers that require large compatibility layers unless audience evidence requires it.

Baseline capabilities should be selected based on:

* actual audience analytics;
* Next.js support;
* accessibility needs;
* device realities in African and global markets;
* low-cost Android browsers;
* mobile network conditions.

Progressive enhancement should preserve readable content even when advanced browser APIs are unavailable.

---

# 7.5.2.39 Progressive Web Application Policy

TWN does not initially need a full Progressive Web Application.

Potential future value includes:

* installable reader experience;
* offline saved Articles;
* Journey continuation;
* push notifications;
* background sync.

These features introduce:

* service-worker complexity;
* stale cache risks;
* offline conflict handling;
* notification permission concerns.

PWA features should be introduced independently based on reader demand.

Do not add a service worker merely to obtain an installation prompt.

---

# 7.5.2.40 Internationalisation Technology

TWN may later require:

* English;
* French;
* Nigerian Pidgin;
* indigenous-language content;
* locale-aware dates;
* translated interfaces;
* multilingual search.

The stack should not hard-code English strings throughout components.

Version 1 may use a simple structured dictionary system.

A dedicated internationalisation library should be introduced when:

* multiple interface locales are active;
* locale routing exists;
* translation fallbacks are needed;
* pluralisation and number formatting grow complex.

Content language and interface language must remain separate concepts.

---

# 7.5.2.41 Charting and Data Visualisation

Charts may appear in:

* Analytics dashboards;
* Journey timelines;
* research Articles;
* operational reports.

Do not install a chart library globally before a chart feature exists.

When selecting one, evaluate:

* accessibility;
* server-rendering compatibility;
* bundle cost;
* custom styling;
* responsive behaviour;
* print and export support.

Editorial diagrams may be better represented as controlled SVG or Media assets than interactive charts.

---

# 7.5.2.42 What TWN Should Not Install Initially

Do not add by default:

* Redux;
* GraphQL client;
* Apollo;
* React Query;
* multiple icon libraries;
* multiple form libraries;
* multiple animation libraries;
* large styled component frameworks;
* micro-frontend infrastructure;
* real-time collaboration frameworks;
* service-worker packages;
* browser database abstractions;
* complex internationalisation systems;
* several Markdown renderers;
* unrestricted MDX execution;
* client-side authentication state as the authority;
* frontend database SDK access for privileged workflows.

These may be introduced later only through a documented need.

---

# 7.5.2.43 Stack Upgrade Policy

## Patch updates

Apply regularly, especially security patches.

## Minor updates

Review release notes and test.

## Major updates

Require:

* migration plan;
* compatibility review;
* Preview deployment;
* CMS workflow tests;
* public-page tests;
* bundle comparison;
* accessibility verification;
* rollback strategy.

---

## Upgrade priorities

1. Critical security fixes.
2. Runtime compatibility.
3. Supported framework versions.
4. Serious bug fixes.
5. Performance improvements.
6. Developer convenience.

Do not upgrade Production to a major release solely because it is new.

Do not remain indefinitely on an insecure version solely because migration is inconvenient.

---

# 7.5.2.44 Dependency Removal Policy

Dependencies should be removed when:

* feature no longer exists;
* native capabilities replace it;
* package is abandoned;
* package introduces unresolved security risk;
* bundle cost outweighs value;
* duplicate functionality exists;
* simpler internal code is now clearer.

Removing dependencies is part of architecture maintenance.

---

# 7.5.2.45 Stack Portability

TWN should benefit from Next.js without storing business knowledge in framework-specific constructs.

Portable assets include:

* Markdown content;
* typed-block schemas;
* domain services;
* repository contracts;
* application commands;
* Media metadata;
* design tokens;
* public route concepts.

Framework-specific code should remain concentrated in:

```text
app/
frontend adapters/
framework cache integration/
metadata generation/
Server Actions/
Route Handlers/
```

A future frontend could reuse the domain and content without parsing React component files as the database.

---

# 7.5.2.46 Technology Decision Matrix

| Concern           | Selected technology           | Reason                                               |
| ----------------- | ----------------------------- | ---------------------------------------------------- |
| Web framework     | Next.js App Router            | Server-first routing and rendering                   |
| Component runtime | React                         | Component composition and framework integration      |
| Language          | TypeScript                    | Explicit contracts and earlier error detection       |
| Styling           | Tailwind CSS 4                | Custom utility-first styling and static CSS          |
| Tokens            | CSS custom properties         | Semantic, portable design values                     |
| Simple forms      | Native HTML + Server Actions  | Progressive enhancement and low client cost          |
| Complex forms     | React Hook Form               | Client-managed form complexity                       |
| Validation        | Zod                           | Runtime boundary validation                          |
| Content           | Markdown + typed blocks       | Portability and controlled extensibility             |
| Markdown pipeline | unified/remark/rehype family  | Syntax-tree processing and controlled rendering      |
| Code highlighting | Server-side highlighter       | Avoid public runtime cost                            |
| Animation         | CSS first; Motion selectively | Limit client JavaScript                              |
| Icons             | One SVG library               | Visual consistency                                   |
| Fonts             | next/font                     | Controlled and optimized delivery                    |
| Images            | next/image + Media service    | Delivery optimization without losing asset ownership |
| Unit testing      | Vitest or Jest + RTL          | Component and utility testing                        |
| Browser testing   | Playwright                    | Full workflow validation                             |
| Package manager   | pnpm                          | Reproducibility and dependency efficiency            |

---

# 7.5.2.47 Architecture Decision Record

**Decision:** TWN uses Next.js App Router, React, TypeScript, Tailwind CSS 4, CSS custom properties, Server Actions, Zod, and portable Markdown with controlled typed blocks as its foundational frontend stack.

**Supporting decisions:**

* App Router is the only routing architecture for new TWN code.
* Server Components are the default.
* Client Components are introduced at the lowest practical interaction boundary.
* Functional React components are used for new code.
* TypeScript strictness is required and improved progressively where existing code prevents immediate enforcement.
* Runtime input is validated even when TypeScript types exist.
* Tailwind implements the design system but does not replace design tokens or component standards.
* CSS remains valid for complex editorial and animation requirements.
* TWN does not use a heavily styled third-party UI system as its public visual foundation.
* Native forms and Server Actions are preferred for simple workflows.
* React Hook Form is reserved for complex client-managed forms.
* Unrestricted database-authored MDX is prohibited.
* Animation uses CSS first and a focused Motion library only when necessary.
* Fonts and images use Next.js optimizations while remaining governed by TWN’s design and Media domains.
* Global client-state and client-data libraries are deferred until real use cases justify them.
* One package manager, one lockfile, one primary test runner, and one primary icon system are maintained.
* Framework and dependency security updates are treated as operational requirements.
* Business logic and canonical content remain portable beyond the current frontend framework.

**Reasons:**

* supports TWN’s server-first architecture;
* limits public browser JavaScript;
* protects content portability;
* provides strong typing and runtime validation;
* preserves the distinct TWN visual identity;
* keeps early operating and maintenance costs low;
* supports an advanced CMS without burdening ordinary readers;
* avoids speculative dependencies;
* leaves a clear path for Books, Podcasts, Videos, Courses, and reader accounts.

**Expected lifetime:** Next.js, React, TypeScript, and Tailwind are expected to remain the primary frontend platform through TWN’s early and medium stages. Individual libraries may change, but the principles of server-first rendering, strict boundaries, portable content, controlled dependencies, and progressive enhancement remain permanent.

---

## End of 7.5.2

The next subsection is:

# **7.5.3 — Frontend Project Structure, Route Groups & Module Organization**

It will define:

* the complete `src/` structure;
* public, editorial, authentication, and API route groups;
* feature-owned component placement;
* shared versus domain-specific components;
* server-only and client-only files;
* layouts, templates, loading states, errors, and not-found files;
* import directions;
* naming conventions;
* avoiding giant `components`, `hooks`, and `utils` folders;
* how Articles, Notebook Entries, Journeys, Community, Media, Search, and Identity are represented in the frontend codebase.

[2]: https://nextjs.org/docs?utm_source=chatgpt.com "Next.js Docs | Next.js"
[3]: https://nextjs.org/docs/app/getting-started/layouts-and-pages?utm_source=chatgpt.com "Getting Started: Layouts and Pages"
[4]: https://react.dev/reference/rsc/server-components?utm_source=chatgpt.com "Server Components"
[5]: https://react.dev/reference/react/Component?utm_source=chatgpt.com "Component"
[6]: https://nextjs.org/docs/app/getting-started/server-and-client-components?utm_source=chatgpt.com "Getting Started: Server and Client Components"
[7]: https://react.dev/blog/2025/12/03/critical-security-vulnerability-in-react-server-components?utm_source=chatgpt.com "Critical Security Vulnerability in React Server Components"
[8]: https://www.typescriptlang.org/docs/handbook/intro.html?utm_source=chatgpt.com "The TypeScript Handbook"
[9]: https://tailwindcss.com/?utm_source=chatgpt.com "Tailwind CSS - Rapidly build modern websites without ever ..."
[10]: https://tailwindcss.com/blog/tailwindcss-v4?utm_source=chatgpt.com "Tailwind CSS v4.0"
[11]: https://nextjs.org/docs/app/api-reference/directives/use-server?utm_source=chatgpt.com "Directives: use server"
[12]: https://nextjs.org/docs/app/getting-started?utm_source=chatgpt.com "App Router: Getting Started"

# 7.5.3 Frontend Project Structure, Route Groups & Module Organization

**Status:** Foundational codebase architecture specification
**Applies to:** Entire TWN frontend codebase
**Primary objective:** Create a frontend structure that remains understandable after years of growth without devolving into a collection of disconnected components, utilities, and pages

---

# Purpose

This section defines how frontend code is organized.

It establishes:

* directory structure;
* route groups;
* module ownership;
* feature boundaries;
* component placement;
* server/client separation;
* import rules;
* naming conventions;
* shared infrastructure placement;
* testing placement;
* scalability rules.

The goal is not merely organization.

The goal is preserving engineering clarity.

A developer should be able to enter the codebase and answer:

```text
Where does this feature live?

Who owns it?

What can it depend on?

What depends on it?
```

without searching hundreds of files.

---

# Architecture Philosophy

TWN is a domain-driven frontend.

The codebase should be organized around:

```text
Business capabilities
```

not:

```text
Technical artifact types
```

Bad:

```text
components/
hooks/
utils/
services/
types/
```

Eventually everything ends up here.

The folders become meaningless.

Nobody knows ownership.

---

Good:

```text
editorial/
journeys/
community/
search/
identity/
media/
```

Each domain owns its code.

---

# Primary Organizational Principle

Organize by:

```text
Feature
    ↓
Sub-feature
        ↓
Implementation
```

Not:

```text
Implementation
    ↓
Feature
```

---

# Frontend Codebase Layout

Recommended root structure:

```text
src/
│
├── app/
│
├── domains/
│
├── components/
│
├── design-system/
│
├── infrastructure/
│
├── lib/
│
├── config/
│
├── styles/
│
├── types/
│
└── tests/
```

Each has a specific purpose.

---

# app/

Contains:

```text
Routing

Layouts

Page composition

Metadata

Route handlers

Loading boundaries

Error boundaries
```

The App Router owns navigation structure.

It does not own domain business logic.

---

Example:

```text
app/
│
├── (public)
├── (editorial)
├── (auth)
├── api
│
├── layout.tsx
├── error.tsx
├── loading.tsx
└── not-found.tsx
```

---

# Route Group Strategy

TWN should use route groups aggressively.

Route groups allow architecture separation without affecting URLs.

---

# Public Route Group

```text
app/
└── (public)
```

Contains reader-facing experiences.

Examples:

```text
/
/articles
/articles/[slug]

/notebook
/notebook/[slug]

/journeys
/journeys/[slug]

/authors
/authors/[slug]

/categories
/categories/[slug]

/search

/shared

/newsletter
```

This is the largest public surface.

---

# Editorial Route Group

```text
app/
└── (editorial)
```

Contains CMS interfaces.

Examples:

```text
/editor

/editor/articles

/editor/journeys

/editor/media

/editor/shared-pages

/editor/moderation

/editor/settings
```

Requires authorization.

Must never be statically exposed.

---

# Auth Route Group

```text
app/
└── (auth)
```

Contains:

```text
/login

/logout

/reset-password

/verify-email

/invitation
```

Auth routes remain isolated.

They should not contain CMS logic.

---

# API Route Group

```text
app/api/
```

Contains Route Handlers.

Examples:

```text
/api/webhooks
/api/health
/api/search
/api/media
```

Route Handlers exist only when:

* external systems need HTTP access;
* webhooks are required;
* browser APIs cannot use Server Actions.

Do not create APIs for internal frontend communication.

---

# Domain Layer

The most important part of the frontend.

```text
src/domains/
```

Contains business capabilities.

---

Example:

```text
domains/
│
├── editorial
├── journeys
├── notebook
├── community
├── search
├── media
├── identity
├── newsletter
└── analytics
```

---

# Why Domains Exist

Without domains:

```text
components/
hooks/
services/
types/
utils/
```

becomes:

```text
2,000 files
```

with no ownership.

Domains preserve boundaries.

---

# Editorial Domain

Owns:

```text
Articles

Categories

Tags

Authors

Publishing

Revisions
```

Example:

```text
domains/editorial/
│
├── components
├── actions
├── queries
├── types
├── validation
├── services
├── tests
└── constants
```

Everything related to Articles belongs here.

---

# Journey Domain

Owns:

```text
Journeys

Journey Chapters

Journey Discovery

Journey Navigation

Journey Relationships
```

Example:

```text
domains/journeys/
│
├── components
├── actions
├── queries
├── services
├── types
└── validation
```

---

# Notebook Domain

Owns:

```text
Notebook Entries

Notebook Hero

Reflections

Daily Notes

Notebook Discovery
```

Separated from Articles.

Notebook is not a subtype of Article.

---

# Community Domain

Owns:

```text
Shared Pages

Margin Notes

Moderation

Community Profiles (future)
```

Community logic stays here.

---

# Search Domain

Owns:

```text
Search UI

Autocomplete

Search Results

Filters

Discovery Experiences
```

Search code should not leak into Articles.

---

# Media Domain

Owns:

```text
Media Picker

Uploads

Media Browser

Media Rendering Helpers

Image Selection
```

---

# Identity Domain

Owns:

```text
Users

Roles

Permissions

Invitations

Account Settings
```

---

# Shared Components

There should be a very small:

```text
src/components/
```

folder.

Purpose:

Truly cross-domain UI.

Examples:

```text
Button

Dialog

Input

Textarea

Dropdown

Tooltip

Pagination
```

If a component contains Article knowledge,

it belongs in Editorial.

Not Components.

---

# Design System

Separate from Components.

```text
design-system/
```

Owns:

```text
Tokens

Typography

Spacing

Color System

Icons

Interaction Standards

Accessibility Standards
```

The design system defines rules.

Components implement them.

---

# Infrastructure Layer

```text
infrastructure/
```

Contains framework adapters.

Examples:

```text
authentication

analytics

logging

feature flags

error reporting

provider integrations
```

Infrastructure talks to external systems.

Domains talk to infrastructure.

Not the reverse.

---

# lib/

Contains small technical helpers.

Examples:

```text
date formatting

url builders

string utilities

number formatting
```

Must remain tiny.

---

Bad:

```text
lib/
    400 files
```

If that happens,

ownership has failed.

---

# styles/

Contains:

```text
globals.css

typography.css

print.css
```

Not feature styling.

Feature styling stays near features.

---

# config/

Contains:

```text
feature flags

constants

environment mappings

application configuration
```

---

# types/

Contains only:

```text
global shared contracts
```

Examples:

```text
ApiError

PaginationResult

EntityId
```

Most types belong inside domains.

---

# Tests Organization

Prefer colocated tests.

Good:

```text
ArticleCard.tsx
ArticleCard.test.tsx
```

Nearby ownership.

Easy maintenance.

---

Global tests folder:

```text
tests/
```

reserved for:

```text
integration

e2e

system
```

tests.

---

# Component Classification

Every component must belong to one of four classes.

---
