Alternatively, provide both machine and display forms where appropriate.

---

# 7.5.5.33 Minimum Client Payload

A Client Component should receive only the state required for interaction.

Bad:

```tsx
<ArticleEditor article={completeDatabaseArticleRecord} />
```

Preferred:

```tsx
<ArticleEditor
  initialDraft={articleEditorView.draft}
  permissions={articleEditorView.capabilities}
  revision={articleEditorView.revision}
/>
```

Even this should be carefully scoped.

Do not transfer:

* hidden subscriber data;
* internal permission tables;
* full revision history when only current revision is needed;
* service storage keys;
* audit metadata;
* server diagnostics.

---

# 7.5.5.34 Server Actions Passed to Client Components

A Server Component may pass an approved Server Action reference into a Client Component.

Example:

```tsx
<ArticleEditor
  initialDraft={view.draft}
  saveAction={saveArticleDraftAction}
/>
```

The action must still:

* authenticate;
* authorize;
* validate;
* enforce version checks;
* use server-owned IDs and rules.

Passing a Server Action into a component does not make client-provided input trustworthy.

---

# 7.5.5.35 Public Data and Sensitive Data Separation

The same route tree may contain both public and authenticated components.

The architecture must ensure:

```text
Public data query
≠
Private user query
```

Example Article page:

```text
Cached Article page
    +
Dynamic bookmark state
```

The public Article view model should not include the reader’s private bookmark state.

The personalized boundary retrieves it separately.

This preserves:

* cacheability;
* privacy;
* smaller payloads;
* clearer ownership.

---

# 7.5.5.36 Authentication Queries in Layouts

Protected layouts may resolve the authenticated principal and basic capabilities.

Example:

```tsx
export default async function EditorialLayout({
  children,
}: {
  children: React.ReactNode;
}) {
  const principal = await requireAuthenticatedPrincipal();
  const navigation = await getEditorialNavigationView(principal);

  return (
    <EditorialShell navigation={navigation}>
      {children}
    </EditorialShell>
  );
}
```

The layout may enforce broad access:

```text
Must be an active staff User.
```

The individual page or command must still enforce resource-specific permissions.

---

# 7.5.5.37 Avoiding Repeated Authentication Resolution

Several protected Server Components may require the current principal.

Use request memoization for the resolver.

Example:

```typescript
export const getAuthenticatedPrincipal = cache(
  async (): Promise<AuthenticatedPrincipal | null> => {
    return authenticationService.getCurrentPrincipal();
  },
);
```

This avoids repeatedly verifying and resolving the same session during one render.

However:

* authorization state must remain sufficiently current;
* cross-request caching is inappropriate;
* the resolver must not accidentally become globally persistent;
* session-specific data must never enter a shared public cache.

---

# 7.5.5.38 Authorization Projection

A frontend route should receive capabilities relevant to that route.

Example Article editor:

```typescript
type ArticleEditorCapabilities = {
  canEdit: boolean;
  canSubmitForReview: boolean;
  canPublish: boolean;
  canSchedule: boolean;
  canArchive: boolean;
  canRestoreRevision: boolean;
};
```

This supports interface presentation.

The underlying Server Actions must reevaluate permissions.

Do not send every role and permission assignment to the browser merely so the UI can calculate capabilities.

The server should calculate the projection.

---

# 7.5.5.39 Public Content Parsing

The Server Component pipeline should receive validated canonical content.

Example:

```text
Published Article revision
    ↓
Markdown parser
    ↓
Typed-block validator
    ↓
Safe render tree
    ↓
Server-rendered Article body
```

Parsing may happen:

* at publication time and be stored as a derived artifact;
* during cached rendering;
* through a render service.

It should not be repeated unnecessarily for every public request.

---

# 7.5.5.40 Render-Time View Composition

A frontend view composer may transform several application results.

Example:

```typescript
export async function getJourneyPageView(
  slug: string,
): Promise<JourneyPageView | null> {
  const journey = await journeyQueries.getPublishedJourney(slug);

  if (!journey) {
    return null;
  }

  const contentReferences =
    await journeyQueries.resolvePublishedContentReferences(
      journey.publishedStructure,
    );

  return mapJourneyToPageView({
    journey,
    contentReferences,
  });
}
```

The composer should not mutate the Journey or its contents.

---

# 7.5.5.41 Failure Categories

A Server Component query may produce:

```text
Success
Not Found
Forbidden
Conflict
Dependency Unavailable
Unexpected Failure
```

These should not all become `null`.

Use distinct result types where the caller needs different behaviour.

Example:

```typescript
type ArticleEditorViewResult =
  | { kind: "success"; view: ArticleEditorView }
  | { kind: "not-found" }
  | { kind: "forbidden" }
  | { kind: "unavailable"; requestId: string };
```

For simple public lookups, `null` may reasonably mean:

```text
No public resource exists for this route.
```

---

# 7.5.5.42 Not-Found Handling

Public route queries should distinguish between:

* no record;
* unpublished record;
* archived or removed record;
* database failure.

The public route may intentionally present several of the first cases as not found.

A database failure must not be treated as:

```text
This Article does not exist.
```

It should be logged and handled through the route’s error or resilience strategy.

---

# 7.5.5.43 Forbidden Handling

A private Server Component should not necessarily reveal that a resource exists.

Depending on policy:

```text
Forbidden private resource
    ↓
403
```

or:

```text
Hidden private resource
    ↓
404
```

The Application query or authorization service should determine this policy.

Do not implement it inconsistently in separate components.

---

# 7.5.5.44 Error Boundaries

Unexpected query failures should reach an appropriate route or component error boundary.

Optional sections may contain localized error boundaries.

Examples:

```text
Article body failure
    ↓
Route-level error

Related content failure
    ↓
Localized related-content fallback
```

Do not catch every error and return an empty array.

This hides infrastructure failures as genuine empty states.

---

# 7.5.5.45 Logging Query Failures

A failed query log should include:

* request ID;
* query operation;
* module;
* route;
* duration;
* error classification;
* relevant non-sensitive entity identifier;
* deployment environment.

Do not log:

* full unpublished content;
* subscriber records;
* access tokens;
* raw SQL parameters containing personal data;
* private Media URLs;
* entire provider responses.

---

# 7.5.5.46 Timeouts

Queries involving external systems should use bounded timeouts.

Primary database reads should also be monitored for excessive execution time.

A public Server Component must not wait indefinitely for:

* optional related content;
* Analytics;
* external Media metadata;
* AI-generated recommendations.

Slow optional sections should:

* stream;
* time out;
* use cached derived data;
* show a localized fallback.

---

# 7.5.5.47 Query Result Immutability

View models should be treated as immutable.

A React component should not modify a shared query result.

Bad:

```typescript
view.tags.sort();
```

This mutates the array.

Preferred:

```typescript
const sortedTags = [...view.tags].sort(compareTags);
```

Better still, the query or mapper should return the desired stable order.

---

# 7.5.5.48 Stable Ordering

Every collection returned for rendering must define its ordering.

Examples:

```text
Articles:
published_at DESC, id DESC

Journey Chapters:
display_order ASC, id ASC

Margin Notes:
published_at ASC, id ASC

Tags:
name ASC, id ASC
```

Relying on unspecified database order creates unstable pages and cache output.

---

# 7.5.5.49 Pagination in Server Components

List pages should use bounded pagination.

Example:

```typescript
type ArticleArchiveQuery = {
  cursor?: string;
  limit: number;
};
```

The server validates:

* cursor;
* maximum limit;
* filter;
* sort.

The route reflects public navigation state in the URL.

Example:

```text
/articles?after=cursor
```

The Server Component retrieves one page.

Do not render the complete archive and hide most items in the browser.

---

# 7.5.5.50 Search Parameters

Search and filter pages may use `searchParams`.

The route should parse and validate them before querying.

Example:

```typescript
const SearchPageSchema = z.object({
  q: z.string().trim().max(200).default(""),
  type: z
    .enum(["all", "article", "journey", "notebook"])
    .default("all"),
  cursor: z.string().optional(),
});
```

Do not pass raw URL parameters into SQL or Search-engine filters.

---

# 7.5.5.51 Query Projection for Lists

For archive or search pages, return:

```typescript
type PagedResult<T> = {
  items: T[];
  pageInfo: {
    nextCursor: string | null;
    hasMore: boolean;
  };
};
```

Do not return raw database pagination internals.

The frontend should not know:

* offset SQL;
* table row counts unless needed;
* internal cursor encoding;
* query-plan details.

---

# 7.5.5.52 Data Freshness and Query Contracts

The query name or documentation should make freshness expectations clear.

Examples:

```text
getPublishedArticlePageView
    Cached public query

getCurrentModerationQueue
    Dynamic private query

getCurrentUserCapabilities
    Request-scoped current query
```

Avoid a generic cache decorator around all query services.

Caching policy belongs to the specific use case.

---

# 7.5.5.53 Cross-Request Caching Compatibility

Persistent caching must be introduced at a layer that understands:

* visibility;
* dependencies;
* invalidation;
* freshness;
* framework version.

A frontend query may expose a cached wrapper around an application query.

Example conceptually:

```text
getPublishedArticlePageView
    ↓
cache by Article ID or slug
    ↓
invalidate after publication
```

Private queries must not use shared public caching.

The exact APIs depend on TWN’s pinned Next.js caching model, as documented in 7.5.4.

---

# 7.5.5.54 `fetch` Versus Direct Database Queries

Server Components may use both.

## Use `fetch` when

* calling an actual external HTTP service;
* consuming a public provider API;
* reaching a separate service;
* using framework HTTP caching intentionally.

## Use direct database or application queries when

* the data belongs to the same application;
* the service is in-process;
* no HTTP boundary is required.

Next.js officially supports data fetching in Server Components through either `fetch`, an ORM, or direct database access. ([Next.js][1])

Do not wrap direct database access in fake internal HTTP requests merely to gain `fetch` caching.

Use the appropriate caching facility for database queries.

---

# 7.5.5.55 External HTTP Response Validation

When a Server Component depends on an external provider through an adapter:

* apply timeout;
* verify status;
* validate response schema;
* limit response size;
* map provider errors;
* avoid exposing provider output directly.

The adapter should return a stable TWN contract.

The component should not understand provider-specific response fields.

---

# 7.5.5.56 Data Loading for Client Editors

An editor route may load:

* current draft;
* current version;
* selected Author;
* Categories;
* Tag suggestions;
* permissions;
* Media references;
* autosave status.

Do not load:

* every revision body;
* every Media asset;
* every Author;
* every Tag;
* all Audit Logs.

Use:

* pagination;
* search;
* lazy panels;
* streamed secondary sections;
* separate Server Actions or Route Handlers where interactive lookup is required.

---

# 7.5.5.57 Large Data Sets

A Server Component should not send thousands of records into a Client Component for local filtering.

Bad:

```text
Load every Media asset
    ↓
Pass to browser
    ↓
Filter locally
```

Preferred:

```text
Media search/filter query
    ↓
Paginated server result
    ↓
Small Client Component result set
```

This protects:

* payload size;
* memory;
* privacy;
* performance;
* authorization.

---

# 7.5.5.58 View Models for Forms

Form initial values should be explicit.

Example:

```typescript
type ArticleDraftFormView = {
  articleId: string;
  version: number;

  fields: {
    title: string;
    subtitle: string;
    excerpt: string;
    contentSource: string;
    categoryId: string;
    tagIds: string[];
    coverMediaId: string | null;
  };

  options: {
    categories: SelectOption[];
  };

  capabilities: ArticleEditorCapabilities;
};
```

The form does not need the complete Article domain object.

---

# 7.5.5.59 Derived Fields

The server should derive fields that are not authoritative client inputs.

Examples:

* word count;
* reading time;
* publication status label;
* allowed workflow actions;
* canonical URL;
* Media delivery URL;
* accessible Media description.

This prevents the browser from pretending to define server-owned facts.

---

# 7.5.5.60 Serialization Safety

Before passing a value into a Client Component, ask:

* Is it serializable?
* Is it safe for the browser?
* Is every field needed?
* Does it contain private content?
* Does it expose implementation details?
* Is the payload reasonably sized?
* Will it remain stable across framework upgrades?

A TypeScript type alone does not prove serialization or security safety.

---

# 7.5.5.61 Route Composition

Route files should remain thin.

Example:

```tsx
export default async function Page({
  params,
}: PageProps<"/articles/[slug]">) {
  const { slug } = await params;
  const view = await getArticlePageView(slug);

  if (!view) {
    notFound();
  }

  return <ArticlePage view={view} />;
}
```

The route should primarily coordinate:

* parameters;
* query;
* not-found decision;
* high-level composition.

It should not contain hundreds of lines of mapping and querying logic.

---

# 7.5.5.62 Layout Composition

Layouts provide stable shared structures.

Examples:

* public navigation;
* editorial shell;
* account navigation;
* section context.

Layouts should not fetch page-specific entities unless every child route genuinely needs them.

Bad:

```text
Public root layout loads every homepage section.
```

This would burden all public routes.

Fetch shared data only where it is truly shared.

---

# 7.5.5.63 Nested Layout Query Cost

A request may execute queries from:

* root layout;
* route-group layout;
* nested layout;
* page;
* nested Server Components;
* metadata.

This is powerful but can hide cumulative cost.

TWN should inspect complete route query counts rather than evaluating each component in isolation.

A route budget should include all layouts.

---

# 7.5.5.64 Metadata and Page Consistency

Metadata and page content must describe the same published revision.

Avoid:

```text
Metadata from current draft
+
Page from published revision
```

or:

```text
Metadata cached for Revision 3
+
Page showing Revision 5
```

The same public source revision and invalidation rules should drive both.

---

# 7.5.5.65 Structured Data Composition

Schema.org or other structured data should be derived from the same page view or public metadata projection.

Do not maintain manually duplicated Article metadata.

Example:

```typescript
const structuredData = mapArticleViewToStructuredData(view);
```

This reduces drift between:

* visible page;
* metadata;
* structured data;
* feeds.

---

# 7.5.5.66 Server Component Testing Levels

Server Components require several types of tests.

## Query-unit tests

Test:

* view-model mapping;
* visibility filtering;
* relationship composition;
* stable ordering;
* null behaviour;
* error mapping.

---

## Component-render tests

Render Server Components with controlled service dependencies or fixture query results.

Verify:

* semantic output;
* title and headings;
* public fields;
* missing optional sections;
* no private content.

---

## Integration tests

Use a test database to verify:

* published revision selection;
* Author and Category projection;
* Journey relationships;
* pagination;
* authorization;
* RLS where relevant.

---

## End-to-end tests

Verify:

* public Article route;
* not-found route;
* private editor access;
* suspended User denial;
* page metadata;
* route refresh after publication;
* streamed optional sections.

---

# 7.5.5.67 Query Contract Tests

Cross-module frontend queries should have contract tests.

Example Journey page expects Editorial to supply:

```typescript
type JourneyContentReferenceView = {
  type: "article";
  id: string;
  slug: string;
  title: string;
  excerpt: string;
  publishedAt: string;
};
```

If Editorial changes its internal entity, the public contract should continue to satisfy Journeys.

This protects module boundaries.

---

# 7.5.5.68 Performance Tests

For important routes, measure:

* number of database queries;
* total query duration;
* duplicate queries;
* response payload;
* server render duration;
* slowest dependency;
* streamed-section completion;
* Client Component payload.

A page that visually loads quickly during local development may still contain an N+1 query that becomes severe with production data.

---

# 7.5.5.69 Server Component Observability

Each important frontend query should support:

* operation name;
* request ID;
* duration;
* cache status where available;
* record count;
* error classification.

Example:

```json
{
  "operation": "editorial.getArticlePageView",
  "requestId": "req_...",
  "durationMs": 42,
  "result": "success"
}
```

Do not create one log line for every tiny mapping function.

Observe meaningful operations and dependencies.

---

# 7.5.5.70 Data-Fetching Anti-Patterns

## Database access inside arbitrary JSX

Hides ownership and query cost.

## Route Handlers as internal repositories

Adds unnecessary HTTP boundaries.

## `SELECT *` for every interface

Retrieves excessive and potentially sensitive data.

## One universal Article shape

Couples cards, editors, feeds, and pages.

## Sequential independent queries

Creates avoidable waterfalls.

## Query per list item

Creates N+1 behaviour.

## One giant join for every relationship

May create row explosion.

## Root layout loading page-specific data

Makes every route expensive.

## Passing database rows into Client Components

Leaks implementation and unnecessary fields.

## Treating `React.cache` as permanent caching

Confuses request memoization and cross-request reuse.

## Caching private User data publicly

Creates severe privacy risk.

## Catching all errors and returning empty data

Hides outages as legitimate empty states.

## Raw URL parameters in queries

Creates validation and injection risks.

## Client-side fetching canonical Article content

Weakens server-first rendering.

---

# 7.5.5.71 Implementation Standards

Every new Server Component data flow should define:

1. owning domain;
2. query contract;
3. visibility;
4. view model;
5. caching policy;
6. request memoization needs;
7. error behaviour;
8. not-found behaviour;
9. serialization boundary;
10. query count expectations;
11. tests;
12. revalidation dependencies.

---

# 7.5.5.72 Example: Article Route

```typescript
// editorial/frontend/queries/get-article-page-view.server.ts

import "server-only";
import { cache } from "react";

export const getArticlePageView = cache(
  async (slug: string): Promise<ArticlePageView | null> => {
    const article =
      await editorialQueries.getPublishedArticleBySlug(slug);

    if (!article) {
      return null;
    }

    const [journeys, related] = await Promise.all([
      journeyQueries.listPublicContextsForArticle(article.id),
      editorialQueries.listRelatedPublishedArticles({
        articleId: article.id,
        limit: 4,
      }),
    ]);

    return mapToArticlePageView({
      article,
      journeys,
      related,
    });
  },
);
```

Route:

```tsx
export default async function ArticleRoute({
  params,
}: PageProps<"/articles/[slug]">) {
  const { slug } = await params;
  const view = await getArticlePageView(slug);

  if (!view) {
    notFound();
  }

  return <ArticlePage view={view} />;
}
```

This example is conceptual.

Persistent caching and revalidation must follow the selected Next.js caching model from 7.5.4.

---

# 7.5.5.73 Example: Streamed Optional Section

```tsx
export default async function ArticleRoute({
  params,
}: PageProps<"/articles/[slug]">) {
  const { slug } = await params;
  const article = await getArticlePrimaryView(slug);

  if (!article) {
    notFound();
  }

  return (
    <ArticlePageShell>
      <ArticlePrimaryContent view={article} />

      <Suspense fallback={<MarginNotesSkeleton />}>
        <ArticleMarginNotes articleId={article.id} />
      </Suspense>
    </ArticlePageShell>
  );
}
```

The Article remains readable even if Margin Notes are delayed.

---

# 7.5.5.74 Example: Client Boundary

Server Component:

```tsx
export async function ArticleEditorScreen({
  articleId,
}: {
  articleId: string;
}) {
  const principal = await requireAuthenticatedPrincipal();

  const result = await getArticleEditorView({
    articleId,
    principal,
  });

  if (result.kind === "not-found") {
    notFound();
  }

  if (result.kind === "forbidden") {
    return <AccessDenied />;
  }

  return (
    <ArticleEditor
      initialDraft={result.view.draft}
      version={result.view.version}
      capabilities={result.view.capabilities}
    />
  );
}
```

The Client Component receives a controlled editor state rather than the entire Article record.

---

# 7.5.5.75 Completion Criteria

This subsection is complete when:

* Server Components call query services rather than repositories from routes;
* internal HTTP self-fetching is avoided;
* public and private query contracts are separate;
* each interface uses an explicit view model;
* database rows are not passed directly into Client Components;
* independent queries run concurrently where appropriate;
* N+1 query patterns are prevented;
* request memoization is distinguished from persistent caching;
* authentication resolution is request-scoped;
* metadata and page data use consistent published sources;
* not-found, forbidden, and dependency failure remain distinct;
* Server–Client data is minimal, safe, and serializable;
* important route query costs are measurable;
* tests cover visibility, mapping, rendering, and query behaviour.

---

# 7.5.5.76 Architecture Decision Record

**Decision:** TWN’s Server Components retrieve data through explicit application and frontend query contracts rather than direct route-level repositories or internal HTTP APIs. Query results are transformed into surface-specific view models before rendering or crossing into Client Components.

**Supporting decisions:**

* Server Components are trusted composition boundaries, not business-service implementations.
* Repositories remain server-only and infrastructure-oriented.
* Public and private content use distinct query contracts.
* View models expose only fields required by their interface.
* Explicit mapping is preferred over unrestricted object spreading.
* Independent requests are parallelized.
* Batching or projections prevent N+1 queries.
* Request-level memoization prevents duplicate retrieval during one render.
* Request memoization is not treated as persistent content caching.
* Metadata and page rendering share consistent memoized public sources where appropriate.
* Nested Server Components may own independent meaningful queries.
* Tiny presentation components should not hide separate database access.
* Private data and personalized state remain outside reusable public view models.
* Data passed into Client Components must be minimal, safe, and serializable.
* Query failures are not converted into false empty states.
* Route files remain thin and focused on routing, query orchestration, and page composition.
* Query count and duration are part of frontend performance review.

**Reasons:**

* protects domain and persistence boundaries;
* prevents accidental data leakage;
* reduces browser payload;
* avoids unnecessary HTTP and query overhead;
* supports Next.js Server Components without coupling business logic to them;
* improves caching and revalidation clarity;
* makes metadata and public rendering consistent;
* supports future mobile or alternative frontend clients;
* keeps the codebase understandable as TWN gains more domains.

**Expected lifetime:** The separation between repositories, application queries, frontend view models, and rendering components is permanent. Exact memoization and caching APIs may evolve with React and Next.js.

---

## End of 7.5.5

The next subsection is:

# **7.5.6 — Client Component Architecture, Interaction Boundaries & Browser State**

It will define:

* when `"use client"` is justified;
* how low client boundaries should be placed;
* local, URL, feature, and global state;
* event handling;
* hydration;
* browser APIs;
* local storage and recovery;
* interaction islands;
* avoiding unnecessary effects;
* state synchronization with Server Actions;
* optimistic interface rules;
* Client Component testing and performance.

[1]: https://nextjs.org/docs/app/getting-started/fetching-data?utm_source=chatgpt.com "Getting Started: Fetching Data"
[2]: https://nextjs.org/docs/14/app/building-your-application/data-fetching/patterns?utm_source=chatgpt.com "Data Fetching Patterns and Best Practices"
[3]: https://react.dev/reference/react/cache?utm_source=chatgpt.com "cache"
[4]: https://nextjs.org/docs/app/api-reference/functions/generate-metadata?utm_source=chatgpt.com "Functions: generateMetadata"
[5]: https://react.dev/reference/react/use?utm_source=chatgpt.com "use"

# 7.5.6 Client Component Architecture, Interaction Boundaries & Browser State

**Status:** Frontend interaction architecture specification
**Applies to:** Public experiences, Editorial CMS, Search, Community interactions, Reader personalization, Media management, future Podcasts, Books, Courses, and all browser-executed code
**Primary objective:** Minimize client-side complexity while preserving rich interactions, accessibility, responsiveness, and maintainability

---

# Purpose

This subsection defines how TWN uses Client Components.

It establishes:

* when Client Components are justified;
* where `"use client"` belongs;
* how browser state is managed;
* how URL state is used;
* when global state is appropriate;
* how forms interact with Server Actions;
* optimistic updates;
* browser persistence;
* hydration boundaries;
* client-side performance rules;
* interaction architecture.

The goal is simple:

> The browser should only execute code that truly requires the browser.

Everything else belongs on the server.

---

# 7.5.6.1 Core Philosophy

TWN is:

```text
Server-first
```

not:

```text
Client-first
```

The default assumption is:

```text
Server Component
```

until a real browser requirement exists.

A component does not become a Client Component because:

* it feels modern;
* it uses React;
* it contains a button;
* a developer is familiar with client-side patterns.

A component becomes a Client Component only when it requires browser capabilities.

---

# 7.5.6.2 What Requires a Client Component

Legitimate reasons include:

### Browser Events

```text
onClick
onChange
onInput
onKeyDown
```

---

### Browser APIs

```text
localStorage
sessionStorage
Clipboard
IntersectionObserver
Web Share API
Media APIs
Drag & Drop
Notifications
```

---

### Interactive State

```text
open/closed
expanded/collapsed
selected tab
draft editor state
upload progress
search input state
```

---

### Real-Time UI

```text
polling
websocket updates
presence indicators
typing indicators
```

---

### Animation Libraries

```text
Framer Motion
GSAP
```

when animation requires browser execution.

---

# 7.5.6.3 What Does NOT Require a Client Component

These are common mistakes.

---

## Rendering Data

Bad assumption:

```text
It displays Article content
therefore it needs use client
```

False.

Server Components display data perfectly.

---

## Conditional Rendering

```tsx
if (article.cover) {
  return <Cover />;
}
```

does not require a Client Component.

---

## Mapping Arrays

```tsx
articles.map(...)
```

does not require a Client Component.

---

## Data Fetching

Fetching public Article data is a Server Component responsibility.

---

## Formatting Dates

Usually server-side.

---

## Metadata

Always server-side.

---

# 7.5.6.4 Client Component Placement

Client boundaries should be:

```text
Small
Focused
Localized
```

Bad:

```tsx
"use client";

export default function ArticlePage() {
```

Entire Article page becomes client-rendered.

---

Good:

```tsx
<ArticlePage>
  <ArticleBody />

  <BookmarkButton />
</ArticlePage>
```

Only:

```tsx
BookmarkButton
```

requires browser execution.

Everything else remains server-rendered.

---

# 7.5.6.5 The Island Architecture Principle

TWN should adopt interaction islands.

Example:

```text
Article Page
│
├── Server
├── Server
├── Server
├── Server
│
└── Client Island
      Bookmark Button
```

Another:

```text
Journey Page
│
├── Server Content
├── Server Chapters
│
└── Client Progress Tracker
```

The page remains mostly server-rendered.

Only interaction islands hydrate.

---

# 7.5.6.6 Hydration Philosophy

Hydration is expensive.

Hydration means:

```text
HTML
+
JavaScript
+
React activation
```

Hydrate only what must become interactive.

Avoid:

```text
Hydrate entire page
```

for:

```text
One button
```

---

# 7.5.6.7 Hydration Budget

Every new Client Component should justify:

```text
Why browser execution is required
```

Questions:

* Does this need browser state?
* Does this need browser APIs?
* Can a Server Action solve this?
* Can a form solve this?
* Can CSS solve this?

If not,

avoid client-side code.

---

# 7.5.6.8 Browser State Categories

TWN recognizes four categories of state.

---

## Category 1 — URL State

State represented by the URL.

Examples:

```text
Search query
Filters
Pagination
Sort order
```

Example:

```text
/search?q=react&type=article
```

Benefits:

* shareable;
* bookmarkable;
* crawlable;
* refresh-safe.

---

## Category 2 — Local UI State

Examples:

```text
Dropdown open
Modal open
Selected tab
Accordion state
```

Stored inside component state.

Example:

```tsx
const [open, setOpen] = useState(false);
```

---

## Category 3 — Persisted Browser State

Examples:

```text
Editor recovery draft
Theme
Dismissed announcement
```

Stored in:

```text
localStorage
```

or

```text
IndexedDB
```

when appropriate.

---

## Category 4 — Server State

Examples:

```text
Articles
Journeys
Users
Permissions
Bookmarks
```

Source of truth remains the server.

Do not duplicate server truth in complex global stores.

---

# 7.5.6.9 URL State First

Many frontend applications misuse global state.

Example:

Bad:

```text
Search query
stored globally
```

Good:

```text
?q=architecture
```

URL state should be preferred whenever:

* users share links;
* browser history matters;
* refresh should preserve state.

---

# 7.5.6.10 Local State First

Before introducing:

```text
Context
Zustand
Redux
Jotai
```

ask:

```text
Can this state remain local?
```

Usually:

Yes.

Example:

```tsx
const [isOpen, setIsOpen] = useState(false);
```

No global store needed.

---

# 7.5.6.11 Global State Philosophy

Global state is expensive.

Every global store becomes:

```text
Shared dependency
```

for the entire application.

TWN should maintain extremely little global client state.

---

# Appropriate Global State

Examples:

```text
Theme
Authenticated User Snapshot
Feature Flags
Notification Queue
```

Potential future:

```text
Reader Preferences
```

---

# Inappropriate Global State

```text
Article Data
Journey Data
Search Results
Categories
```

These belong on the server.

---

# 7.5.6.12 Recommended Global Store Strategy

Initially:

```text
React Context
```

is sufficient.

Do not introduce:

```text
Redux
```

without demonstrated complexity.

For advanced client interactions:

```text
Zustand
```

is acceptable.

But only when necessary.

---

# 7.5.6.13 Forms Philosophy

Forms should be:

```text
Server Action first
```

where supported.

Traditional:

```text
Client fetch
API route
```

should not be the default.

---

Example:

```tsx
<form action={submitMarginNote}>
```

Simple.

Secure.

Server-owned.

---

# 7.5.6.14 Server Actions vs Client Fetch

Preferred:

```tsx
<form action={publishArticle}>
```

Over:

```tsx
fetch("/api/articles")
```

for internal application mutations.

Benefits:

* less boilerplate;
* fewer APIs;
* shared validation;
* type safety.

---

# 7.5.6.15 Form Validation Layers

Validation exists in multiple places.

---

## Client Validation

Purpose:

```text
Immediate feedback
```

Examples:

* required fields;
* max length;
* formatting.

---

## Server Validation

Purpose:

```text
Truth
```

Server validation always wins.

Never trust browser validation.

---

# 7.5.6.16 Optimistic UI

Optimistic updates may improve responsiveness.

Example:

```text
Bookmark
Like
Archive
```

User clicks.

UI updates immediately.

Server confirms later.

---

Use only when:

* failure is rare;
* rollback is easy.

---

Avoid optimistic updates for:

```text
Publishing
Deleting
Permissions
Payments
```

These require confirmation.

---

# 7.5.6.17 Optimistic Rollback

Every optimistic action must define rollback.

Example:

```text
Bookmark added
↓
Server fails
↓
Bookmark removed
↓
Error shown
```

Never leave UI lying to the user.

---

# 7.5.6.18 Browser Persistence

Some state should survive refresh.

Examples:

```text
Editor recovery draft
Reader preferences
Theme
```

Storage options:

```text
localStorage
IndexedDB
```

---

# 7.5.6.19 Editor Draft Recovery

Critical requirement.

Large editorial content must support:

```text
Crash recovery
```

Example:

```text
Browser crash
Power outage
Tab closed accidentally
```

Editor should recover recent unsaved work.

Recovery state:

```text
Browser only
```

until saved.

---

# 7.5.6.20 Autosave Strategy

For CMS editing.

Example:

```text
User typing
↓
Idle 10 seconds
↓
Autosave draft
```

Requirements:

* debounced;
* version-aware;
* conflict-safe.

Never save on every keystroke.

---

# 7.5.6.21 Browser State Ownership

Each state should have one owner.

Example:

```text
Modal open state
```

Owned by:

```text
Modal component
```

Not:

```text
Global store
```

---

# 7.5.6.22 Component Communication

Preferred:

```text
Parent
↓
Props
↓
Child
```

Avoid unnecessary global state.

---

Use Context only when:

many components

need the same state.

---

# 7.5.6.23 React Context Rules

Good use:

```text
Theme
Current User Snapshot
Feature Flags
```

Bad use:

```text
Every Article
Every Search Result
Entire CMS State
```

Context is not a database.

---

# 7.5.6.24 Effects Philosophy

React effects are often overused.

Question:

```text
Can this be computed during render?
```

If yes:

Avoid `useEffect`.

---

Bad:

```tsx
useEffect(() => {
  setFullName(first + last);
}, [first, last]);
```

Good:

```tsx
const fullName = first + last;
```

---

# 7.5.6.25 Legitimate Effects

Examples:

* subscriptions;
* browser events;
* timers;
* IntersectionObserver;
* WebSocket connection.

These truly synchronize with external systems.

---

# 7.5.6.26 Browser Event Handling

Handlers should remain:

```text
Small
Predictable
```

Bad:

```tsx
onClick={() => {
  // 200 lines
}}
```

Good:

```tsx
onClick={handleBookmark}
```

Logic extracted.

---

# 7.5.6.27 Search Experience

Search input requires client state.

Architecture:

```text
Input State
↓
URL Update
↓
Server Search Results
```

Avoid:

```text
Entire search engine in browser
```

---

# 7.5.6.28 Infinite Scroll Policy

TWN should prefer:

```text
Explicit pagination
```

for editorial content.

Infinite scroll may be used selectively:

* Media library;
* Search;
* Admin tools.

But not for primary reading experiences.

---

# 7.5.6.29 File Uploads

Uploads require Client Components.

Responsibilities:

* file selection;
* progress display;
* cancellation;
* retry.

Responsibilities NOT in browser:

* storage permissions;
* media processing;
* moderation.

---

# 7.5.6.30 Media Upload Flow

```text
Select File
↓
Request Upload Target
↓
Upload
↓
Server Verification
↓
Media Created
```

Client should not create Media records directly.

---

# 7.5.6.31 Clipboard Interactions

Allowed examples:

```text
Copy Link
Copy Citation
Copy Share URL
```

These are appropriate Client Components.

Keep isolated.

---

# 7.5.6.32 Keyboard Shortcuts

Future CMS may support:

```text
Cmd+S
Ctrl+S
```

for save.

Must remain:

* accessible;
* configurable;
* non-conflicting.

---

# 7.5.6.33 Accessibility Rules

Interactive components must support:

* keyboard access;
* focus states;
* screen readers;
* reduced motion;
* semantic elements.

Never replace:

```html
<button>
```

with:

```html
<div>
```

for styling convenience.

---

# 7.5.6.34 Theme Management

Theme state is global.

Appropriate storage:

```text
Cookie
or
localStorage
```

Server may read theme preference to avoid flash.

---

# 7.5.6.35 Real-Time Features

Future features:

```text
Notifications
Live collaboration
Presence
```

May require:

```text
WebSockets
SSE
```

These should be isolated domains.

Do not introduce real-time infrastructure early.

---

# 7.5.6.36 Client Error Handling

Client failures should be recoverable.

Examples:

```text
Upload failed
Save failed
Bookmark failed
```

User should receive:

* clear explanation;
* retry option.

Avoid:

```text
Something went wrong
```

without context.

---

# 7.5.6.37 Loading States

Interactive actions need loading states.

Example:

```text
Saving...
Publishing...
Uploading...
```

Loading states should:

* disable duplicate actions;
* preserve context.

---

# 7.5.6.38 Client Performance Budget

Every Client Component increases:

* bundle size;
* hydration cost;
* execution time.

Before adding one ask:

```text
Can this stay server-side?
```

---

# 7.5.6.39 Client Component Testing

Test:

* user interactions;
* accessibility;
* keyboard navigation;
* optimistic updates;
* rollback behaviour;
* loading states;
* error handling.

Not just snapshots.

---

# 7.5.6.40 Architecture Decision Record

**Decision:** TWN adopts a server-first architecture with small, localized Client Component islands used only where browser execution is required.

**Supporting decisions:**

* `"use client"` is opt-in, never default.
* Public content remains server-rendered.
* URL state is preferred over global state.
* Local state is preferred over Context.
* Context is preferred over large state-management frameworks.
* Server Actions are preferred for internal mutations.
* Optimistic updates require rollback.
* Editor recovery is mandatory.
* Autosave is debounced and version-aware.
* Browser persistence is limited to genuine browser-owned concerns.
* Global client state remains minimal.
* Interactive islands should hydrate independently.
* Accessibility is mandatory for all interactive features.

**Reasons:**

* smaller bundles;
* faster hydration;
* simpler architecture;
* easier maintenance;
* better accessibility;
* better performance on low-end devices;
* clearer ownership boundaries;
* alignment with Server Component architecture.

---

## End of 7.5.6

Next section:

# **7.5.7 Server Actions Architecture, Commands & Mutation Workflows**

This is where TWN's entire write-side architecture will be defined:

* publishing Articles;
* creating Notebook Entries;
* editing Journeys;
* autosave drafts;
* Media uploads;
* moderation actions;
* permissions;
* validation;
* idempotency;
* optimistic UI integration;
* audit logging;
* transactional consistency;
* command handlers;
* rollback and failure recovery.

This is one of the most important sections in the entire implementation volume because it defines how every change enters the system.

# 7.5.7 Server Actions Architecture, Commands & Mutation Workflows

**Status:** Write-side architecture specification
**Applies to:** Articles, Notebook Entries, Journeys, Journey Chapters, Shared Pages, Margin Notes, Media, Identity, Editorial Workflows, Community Workflows, Future Books, Podcasts, Courses, Events and all content mutations
**Primary objective:** Define how information enters, changes, publishes, archives, and moves through TWN while preserving integrity, auditability, security, and future scalability.

---

# Purpose

This section defines the write-side architecture of TWN.

It establishes:

* Server Actions;
* Commands;
* Command Handlers;
* Validation;
* Transactions;
* Concurrency Control;
* Idempotency;
* Audit Logging;
* Draft Management;
* Publication Workflows;
* Error Handling;
* Revalidation;
* Security Boundaries.

The goal is simple:

> Every change to TWN must pass through a predictable, auditable, validated workflow.

Nothing should mutate data directly.

---

# 7.5.7.1 Core Philosophy

Reads answer questions.

Writes change reality.

Therefore writes are inherently more dangerous.

TWN treats mutations as first-class business operations.

Bad:

```typescript
await db.article.update(...)
```

inside random components.

Good:

```typescript
await publishArticle(command)
```

Every write should express business intent.

---

# 7.5.7.2 CQRS Position

TWN adopts a lightweight CQRS model.

Not full enterprise CQRS.

Not event sourcing.

Simply:

```text
Queries
    ↓
Read Data

Commands
    ↓
Change Data
```

Examples:

Queries:

```text
getPublishedArticle()
getHomepageView()
getJourneyPage()
```

Commands:

```text
createArticle()
saveDraft()
publishArticle()
archiveJourney()
approveMarginNote()
```

Reads and writes have different responsibilities.

---

# 7.5.7.3 What Is A Command?

A command represents:

```text
Intent
```

not:

```text
Database operation
```

Bad:

```text
updateArticleTable
```

Good:

```text
Publish Article
```

Bad:

```text
insertNotebookEntry
```

Good:

```text
Create Notebook Entry
```

The command language should match editorial language.

---

# 7.5.7.4 Command Structure

Example:

```typescript
type PublishArticleCommand = {
  articleId: string;
  actorId: string;
  expectedVersion: number;
};
```

A command contains:

* required identifiers;
* actor information;
* business inputs;
* concurrency information.

A command does not contain:

* database clients;
* repositories;
* UI state;
* infrastructure objects.

---

# 7.5.7.5 Command Handler Structure

Every command should have exactly one handler.

Example:

```typescript
publish-article.handler.ts
```

Structure:

```text
Validate
↓
Authorize
↓
Load State
↓
Apply Rules
↓
Persist
↓
Audit
↓
Revalidate
↓
Return Result
```

---

# 7.5.7.6 Server Actions Position

Server Actions are:

```text
Transport Layer
```

They are NOT:

```text
Business Logic Layer
```

Bad:

```typescript
"use server";

export async function publishArticle() {
  // 500 lines of business logic
}
```

Good:

```typescript
"use server";

export async function publishArticleAction(input) {
  return publishArticleCommandHandler(input);
}
```

Server Actions should remain thin.

---

# 7.5.7.7 Mutation Flow

Standard TWN write flow:

```text
Client Form
↓
Server Action
↓
Validation
↓
Authorization
↓
Command Handler
↓
Transaction
↓
Audit Log
↓
Revalidation
↓
Response
```

This pattern should be consistent across the entire system.

---

# 7.5.7.8 Validation Layers

Validation exists in three layers.

---

## Layer 1 — UI Validation

Purpose:

```text
Immediate Feedback
```

Examples:

* required field;
* max length;
* file size.

Convenience only.

Never trusted.

---

## Layer 2 — Server Validation

Purpose:

```text
Truth
```

Every command validates input.

Examples:

```text
Title length
Slug format
Schedule date
Journey structure
```

---

## Layer 3 — Domain Validation

Purpose:

```text
Business Rules
```

Examples:

```text
Cannot publish without content.

Cannot archive published Journey
without replacement notice.

Cannot schedule publication in the past.
```

Most important layer.

---

# 7.5.7.9 Authorization Position

Authorization occurs before mutation.

Example:

```text
Can Publish?
Can Archive?
Can Restore?
Can Moderate?
```

Never trust:

```text
Hidden Button
```

as authorization.

Every command re-checks permissions.

---

# 7.5.7.10 Authentication Position

Commands require:

```text
Actor
```

Every mutation knows:

```text
Who performed it
```

No anonymous editorial mutations.

---

# 7.5.7.11 Transaction Boundary

Most mutations should execute within a transaction.

Example:

Publish Article

```text
Update Published Revision
↓
Create Publication Event
↓
Audit Log
```

Either:

```text
All succeed
```

or

```text
None succeed
```

Never partial publication.

---

# 7.5.7.12 Optimistic Concurrency Control

TWN should prevent accidental overwrites.

Example:

Editor A opens Article.

Editor B opens Article.

Editor B saves.

Editor A saves older version.

Without protection:

```text
Data Loss
```

Use version numbers.

Example:

```typescript
expectedVersion: 12
```

If current version is:

```text
13
```

Command fails.

Editor refreshes.

---

# 7.5.7.13 Version Field

Editorial entities should include:

```text
version
```

Examples:

* Article
* Notebook Entry
* Journey
* Shared Page

Increment after successful mutation.

---

# 7.5.7.14 Idempotency

Some actions may execute twice.

Examples:

* double click;
* browser retry;
* network retry.

Idempotent commands should produce the same result.

Example:

```text
Publish Article
```

already published.

Result:

```text
No change
```

not duplicate publication records.

---

# 7.5.7.15 Command Results

Avoid:

```typescript
true
false
```

Prefer:

```typescript
type PublishResult =
  | { kind: "success" }
  | { kind: "forbidden" }
  | { kind: "conflict" }
  | { kind: "validation-error" };
```

Explicit outcomes.

---

# 7.5.7.16 Draft Architecture

Drafts are separate from published content.

Published Article:

```text
Reader Truth
```

Draft:

```text
Work In Progress
```

A draft should never automatically become published.

Publication is explicit.

---

# 7.5.7.17 Autosave Draft Command

Example:

```text
saveArticleDraft()
```

Responsibilities:

* save content;
* update version;
* preserve history.

Should NOT:

* publish;
* notify readers;
* update homepage.

---

# 7.5.7.18 Publication Command

Example:

```text
publishArticle()
```

Responsibilities:

* validate readiness;
* create published revision;
* update publication state;
* trigger revalidation.

This is a major workflow.

---

# 7.5.7.19 Publication Readiness

Article cannot publish unless:

* title exists;
* slug exists;
* content exists;
* author exists;
* category exists;
* validation passes.

Optional fields remain optional.

---

# 7.5.7.20 Revision Creation

Publication should create or reference a revision.

Never publish mutable draft state directly.

Reader sees:

```text
Published Revision
```

not:

```text
Current Draft
```

This protects historical integrity.

---

# 7.5.7.21 Unpublish Workflow

Possible future command:

```text
unpublishArticle()
```

Effects:

* remove public visibility;
* update indexes;
* revalidate pages;
* preserve history.

No hard deletion.

---

# 7.5.7.22 Archive Workflow

Archiving differs from deletion.

Archived content:

```text
Exists
```

but:

```text
No longer active
```

History remains.

---

# 7.5.7.23 Delete Workflow

Hard deletion should be rare.

Allowed examples:

* spam uploads;
* temporary files;
* failed imports.

Editorial content prefers:

```text
Soft Delete
```

---

# 7.5.7.24 Revalidation After Mutation

Mutation completion should trigger targeted revalidation.

Example:

Publish Article

```text
Article Page
Homepage
Category
Author
Journey References
RSS
Sitemap
```

Not:

```text
Clear Entire Cache
```

---

# 7.5.7.25 Audit Logging Philosophy

Every significant mutation creates an audit event.

Examples:

```text
Article Published
Article Archived
Journey Updated
Role Changed
Media Deleted
```

---

# 7.5.7.26 Audit Event Structure

Example:

```typescript
{
  actorId,
  entityId,
  entityType,
  action,
  timestamp
}
```

Additional metadata permitted.

---

# 7.5.7.27 What Must Be Audited

Required:

* publication;
* archive;
* restore;
* permission change;
* moderation action;
* Journey restructure;
* media removal.

---

Optional:

* draft autosave.

---

# 7.5.7.28 What Should NOT Be Audited

Avoid:

```text
Every keystroke
```

Creates noise.

Audit meaningful actions.

---

# 7.5.7.29 Error Classification

Mutations may fail because of:

```text
Validation
Authorization
Conflict
Dependency Failure
Unexpected Error
```

These should be distinguished.

---

# 7.5.7.30 Validation Errors

Example:

```text
Title exceeds limit.
```

User can fix.

---

# 7.5.7.31 Authorization Errors

Example:

```text
User lacks permission.
```

User cannot fix without access.

---

# 7.5.7.32 Conflict Errors

Example:

```text
Version mismatch.
```

Another editor changed content.

Requires refresh.

---

# 7.5.7.33 Dependency Errors

Example:

```text
Storage unavailable.
```

External issue.

---

# 7.5.7.34 Unexpected Errors

Example:

```text
Bug
Infrastructure issue
```

Log heavily.

Return safe message.

---

# 7.5.7.35 Media Upload Workflow

Flow:

```text
Request Upload
↓
Upload
↓
Verification
↓
Create Media Record
↓
Audit
```

Media record creation should be server-owned.

---

# 7.5.7.36 Journey Update Workflow

Journey updates may affect:

* Journey page;
* Chapter order;
* Article references;
* Notebook references.

Should execute atomically.

---

# 7.5.7.37 Margin Note Workflow

Submission:

```text
Submit
↓
Moderation Queue
↓
Approve/Reject
```

Never auto-publish.

---

# 7.5.7.38 Shared Page Workflow

Possible flow:

```text
Create Draft
↓
Review
↓
Publish
```

Same principles as Articles.

---

# 7.5.7.39 Identity Mutations

Examples:

```text
Assign Role
Suspend User
Restore User
```

Require:

* audit;
* authorization;
* transactional consistency.

High sensitivity.

---

# 7.5.7.40 Notification Strategy

Notifications should occur:

```text
After successful transaction
```

Never before.

Example:

Bad:

```text
Send Email
↓
Transaction Fails
```

Reader receives false information.

---

# 7.5.7.41 Side Effects

Examples:

* Email;
* Search indexing;
* Feed update;
* Analytics event.

Should occur after mutation success.

Prefer asynchronous processing.

---

# 7.5.7.42 Command Testing

Every command should have:

### Validation Tests

### Authorization Tests

### Success Tests

### Conflict Tests

### Failure Tests

---

# 7.5.7.43 Architecture Decision Record

**Decision:** TWN uses command-based mutations executed through Server Actions, validated through multiple layers, protected by authorization and concurrency controls, recorded through audit logs, and followed by targeted revalidation.

**Supporting Decisions:**

* Server Actions are transport, not business logic.
* Commands express intent.
* Every command has one handler.
* Transactions protect consistency.
* Version checks prevent lost updates.
* Drafts are separate from published content.
* Publication creates immutable history.
* Audit logging is mandatory for major mutations.
* Revalidation follows successful mutations.
* Side effects execute after transaction success.

**Reasons:**

* protects editorial integrity;
* prevents accidental data loss;
* simplifies auditing;
* supports future workflows;
* aligns with revision architecture;
* scales to Books, Podcasts, Courses, and future domains.

---

## End of 7.5.7

Next:

# **7.5.8 Validation Architecture, Business Rules & Trust Boundaries**

This is where we define exactly:

* Zod strategy;
* schema ownership;
* domain invariants;
* trust boundaries;
* input sanitization;
* content validation;
* slug validation;
* markdown validation;
* Journey structure validation;
* media validation;
* security validation;
* anti-abuse rules;
* moderation validation;
* how validation evolves over time.

This section becomes the "guardian" of data quality across the entire TWN platform.

# 7.5.8 Validation Architecture, Business Rules & Trust Boundaries

**Status:** Foundational validation and data-quality specification
**Applies to:** Public forms, Server Actions, Route Handlers, CMS workflows, Articles, Notebook Entries, Journeys, Community submissions, Media, Identity, Search, Newsletter, external integrations, future Books, Podcasts, Videos, Courses, and AI-assisted features
**Primary validation library:** Zod
**Primary principle:** Validate structure at every trust boundary, enforce business meaning in the owning domain, and preserve final integrity through database constraints
**Primary objective:** Prevent malformed, unsafe, contradictory, incomplete, or unauthorized data from entering TWN while returning errors that users and engineers can understand

---

# Purpose

This subsection defines how TWN determines whether information is valid.

It establishes:

* the difference between parsing, validation, sanitization, authorization, and normalization;
* where validation must occur;
* which layer owns each rule;
* how Zod schemas should be organized;
* how browser form validation supports usability;
* how Server Actions and Route Handlers validate untrusted input;
* how domain invariants are enforced;
* how database constraints provide final structural protection;
* how Articles, Journeys, Notebook Entries, Media, community submissions, Search, Identity, and AI outputs are validated;
* how validation errors are represented;
* how validation rules evolve without corrupting existing content;
* how anti-abuse and security rules relate to ordinary validation.

Validation is not one function called before a database query.

It is a coordinated architecture.

---

# 7.5.8.1 Validation Philosophy

TWN follows this model:

```text
Untrusted Input
    ↓
Transport Parsing
    ↓
Structural Validation
    ↓
Normalization
    ↓
Authentication
    ↓
Authorization
    ↓
Domain Validation
    ↓
Persistence Validation
    ↓
Safe Output
```

Each stage answers a different question.

---

## Transport parsing asks

```text
Can this request be read?
```

Examples:

* Is the request valid JSON?
* Is the submitted form readable?
* Is the content type supported?
* Is the payload within the permitted size?
* Is the uploaded data complete?

---

## Structural validation asks

```text
Does this input have the expected shape?
```

Examples:

* Is `articleId` a UUID?
* Is `title` a string?
* Is `expectedVersion` a positive integer?
* Is `scheduledAt` a valid timestamp?
* Is the content format one of the allowed formats?

---

## Normalization asks

```text
How should equivalent valid inputs be represented consistently?
```

Examples:

* trimming surrounding whitespace;
* lowercasing an email address where appropriate;
* converting an empty optional form field to `null`;
* normalizing line endings;
* canonicalizing a slug;
* removing duplicate Tag IDs.

---

## Authentication asks

```text
Who submitted the request?
```

---

## Authorization asks

```text
May this actor attempt the operation?
```

---

## Domain validation asks

```text
Does this action make sense for TWN?
```

Examples:

* Can this Article be published?
* May this content be attached to this Journey?
* Is the selected revision publishable?
* Can a suspended User receive a role?
* Can the final Super Admin be removed?
* May a rejected Shared Page be published?

---

## Persistence validation asks

```text
Can the database preserve this state safely?
```

Examples:

* unique slug;
* foreign-key relationship;
* valid status value;
* non-null required field;
* positive revision number;
* one relationship record only.

PostgreSQL supports structural constraints including `NOT NULL`, `CHECK`, `UNIQUE`, primary-key, foreign-key, and exclusion constraints. These constraints are the last line of protection against invalid stored relationships, not a replacement for application-level business validation. ([PostgreSQL][1])

---

# 7.5.8.2 Validation Is Not Sanitization

These terms must not be used interchangeably.

## Validation

Determines whether data satisfies defined requirements.

Example:

```text
Title must be between 1 and 120 characters.
```

## Sanitization

Transforms or removes potentially unsafe content.

Example:

```text
Remove unsupported HTML elements from imported content.
```

## Normalization

Converts valid values into one consistent form.

Example:

```text
"  User@Example.com "
    ↓
"user@example.com"
```

## Escaping

Ensures data is safely represented in an output context.

Example:

```text
Display user text without interpreting it as HTML.
```

## Authorization

Determines whether the actor may perform the action.

A valid UUID does not mean the User may edit the referenced Article.

---

# 7.5.8.3 Validation Principles

## Principle 1 — Treat every boundary as untrusted

Untrusted inputs include:

* form data;
* JSON;
* query parameters;
* route parameters;
* request headers;
* cookies;
* uploaded files;
* webhook payloads;
* external API responses;
* AI output;
* browser-storage recovery data;
* imported content;
* database records created by older application versions.

OWASP recommends validating untrusted input for expected type, range, length, format, and permitted values, while rejecting unexpected data rather than trying to accept everything. ([cheatsheetseries.owasp.org][2])

---

## Principle 2 — Allow-lists are preferred

Define what is accepted.

Do not attempt to list every possible invalid value.

Good:

```text
status must be one of:
draft
review
scheduled
published
archived
```

Weak:

```text
status must not contain suspicious characters
```

---

## Principle 3 — Validate early, but enforce at every relevant layer

Early validation improves user feedback and reduces wasted work.

It does not remove the need for:

* domain rules;
* authorization;
* database constraints.

---

## Principle 4 — Reject invalid requests completely

Do not partially apply a command unless partial completion is an intentional business workflow.

Example:

```text
Journey update contains one invalid Chapter reference
    ↓
Reject the structural update
```

Do not silently skip the invalid Chapter and save the rest.

---

## Principle 5 — Validation errors must be deterministic

The same input under the same relevant state should produce the same validation result.

Avoid rules dependent on:

* random behaviour;
* unstable external calls;
* current UI state;
* unrecorded assumptions.

Time-sensitive rules should receive the relevant clock value explicitly where practical.

---

## Principle 6 — Valid format does not imply valid business meaning

A date may be syntactically valid but still be inappropriate.

Example:

```text
scheduledAt = 2020-01-01
```

is a valid date but invalid for scheduling a future publication.

OWASP distinguishes ordinary format validation from validating business meaning: input may be perfectly formed while still violating the workflow or being used abusively. ([cheatsheetseries.owasp.org][3])

---

## Principle 7 — Validation must not leak sensitive state

A public form should not reveal:

```text
This email belongs to a Super Admin.
```

A private route should not confirm the existence of a resource when policy requires concealment.

---

# 7.5.8.4 Trust Boundaries

TWN contains several input boundaries.

```text
Browser
    ↓
Server Action

External Client
    ↓
Route Handler

External Provider
    ↓
Webhook

Editor
    ↓
Content Parser

User Device
    ↓
Local Recovery Import

Third-Party Service
    ↓
Provider Adapter

AI Model
    ↓
AI Output Validator

Application
    ↓
Database
```

Each boundary requires its own validation contract.

---

# 7.5.8.5 Validation Layers

TWN uses five primary layers.

| Layer               | Main responsibility                     |
| ------------------- | --------------------------------------- |
| HTML and browser    | Immediate usability feedback            |
| Transport schema    | Input structure and basic constraints   |
| Application command | Actor-independent use-case requirements |
| Domain policy       | Business invariants and lifecycle rules |
| Database            | Structural and relational integrity     |

No single layer replaces the others.

---

# 7.5.8.6 Browser Validation

Browser validation may use:

* `required`;
* `minlength`;
* `maxlength`;
* `type="email"`;
* `min`;
* `max`;
* `pattern`;
* accepted upload types.

Example:

```tsx
<input
  name="title"
  required
  minLength={1}
  maxLength={120}
/>
```

Purpose:

* immediate feedback;
* accessibility;
* reduced accidental invalid submission;
* mobile keyboard hints.

Browser validation is bypassable.

It must never be treated as authoritative.

---

# 7.5.8.7 Server Action Validation

Every Server Action must validate its input on the server.

Next.js’s current form guidance demonstrates validating `FormData` through a server-side schema and returning early when parsing fails. Next.js also warns that Server Actions are reachable through direct requests and must perform their own authentication and authorization checks. ([Next.js][4])

Example:

```typescript
"use server";

const CreateArticleActionSchema = z.object({
  title: z.string().trim().min(1).max(120),
  authorId: z.string().uuid(),
  categoryId: z.string().uuid(),
});

export async function createArticleAction(
  _previousState: CreateArticleActionState,
  formData: FormData,
): Promise<CreateArticleActionState> {
  const parsed = CreateArticleActionSchema.safeParse({
    title: formData.get("title"),
    authorId: formData.get("authorId"),
    categoryId: formData.get("categoryId"),
  });

  if (!parsed.success) {
    return {
      status: "validation-error",
      fieldErrors: mapZodFieldErrors(parsed.error),
    };
  }

  const principal = await requireAuthenticatedPrincipal();

  return createArticleCommand({
    ...parsed.data,
    actorUserId: principal.localUserId,
  });
}
```

---

# 7.5.8.8 Route Handler Validation

Route Handlers must validate:

* HTTP method;
* content type;
* content length;
* route parameters;
* query parameters;
* headers;
* body;
* authentication information;
* provider signatures where relevant.

Example:

```typescript
const PublicSearchSchema = z.object({
  q: z.string().trim().min(1).max(200),
  type: z
    .enum(["all", "article", "journey", "notebook", "author"])
    .default("all"),
  limit: z.coerce.number().int().min(1).max(50).default(20),
  cursor: z.string().max(500).optional(),
});
```

Do not pass unvalidated query parameters into database or Search queries.

---

# 7.5.8.9 Zod Decision

TWN uses Zod as the primary TypeScript runtime-schema library.

Zod provides runtime parsing, TypeScript inference, transformations, refinements, and structured error output. Its `safeParse()` API returns a discriminated result containing either validated data or a `ZodError`, which is suitable for expected form and request-validation failures. ([Zod][5])

---

# 7.5.8.10 `parse` Versus `safeParse`

## Use `safeParse`

For expected invalid user input:

```typescript
const result = Schema.safeParse(input);

if (!result.success) {
  return validationFailure(result.error);
}
```

Examples:

* forms;
* Search input;
* public submissions;
* API requests;
* imported recovery state.

## Use `parse`

Where invalid input means a programming, configuration, or provider-contract failure.

Examples:

* required environment configuration;
* internal static configuration;
* provider response expected to conform strictly;
* migration fixture.

```typescript
const environment = EnvironmentSchema.parse(process.env);
```

## Use asynchronous parsing

Use `safeParseAsync` or `parseAsync` only where the schema contains asynchronous refinement.

Avoid unnecessary database or network operations inside generic schemas.

---

# 7.5.8.11 Schema Ownership

Schemas belong to the layer that owns the contract.

Recommended organization:

```text
domains/
└── editorial/
    ├── frontend/
    │   └── validation/
    │       └── article-form.schema.ts
    ├── application/
    │   └── commands/
    │       └── publish-article.schema.ts
    └── domain/
        └── policies/
            └── article-publication.policy.ts
```

Different schemas serve different purposes.

---

## Frontend form schema

Models submitted fields.

```typescript
const ArticleMetadataFormSchema = z.object({
  title: z.string().trim().min(1).max(120),
  subtitle: emptyStringToNull(z.string().trim().max(220).nullable()),
  excerpt: z.string().trim().min(1).max(300),
});
```

---

## Application-command schema

Models the use-case input.

```typescript
const PublishArticleCommandSchema = z.object({
  articleId: z.string().uuid(),
  expectedDraftVersion: z.number().int().positive(),
  selectedRevisionId: z.string().uuid().optional(),
});
```

---

## Provider schema

Models an external provider’s response or event.

```typescript
const EmailDeliveryWebhookSchema = z.object({
  id: z.string().min(1),
  type: z.enum(["delivered", "bounced", "complained"]),
  occurredAt: z.string().datetime(),
  recipient: z.string().email(),
});
```

---

## Content-block schema

Models typed editorial blocks.

```typescript
const MediaBlockSchema = z.object({
  type: z.literal("media"),
  version: z.literal(1),
  mediaId: z.string().uuid(),
  variant: z.enum(["inline", "wide", "full"]),
  caption: z.string().trim().max(500).nullable(),
});
```

---

# 7.5.8.12 Do Not Create One Universal Schema

Avoid:

```text
ArticleSchema
```

used for:

* database insertion;
* public API;
* editor form;
* revision snapshot;
* Article card;
* publication;
* import.

These contracts have different fields and trust levels.

Prefer:

```text
CreateArticleFormSchema
SaveArticleDraftCommandSchema
PublishArticleCommandSchema
ArticleRevisionSnapshotSchema
PublicArticleResponseSchema
ImportedArticleSchema
```

Duplication of a few field rules is sometimes safer than one over-broad schema.

Reusable field schemas may still be extracted.

---

# 7.5.8.13 Primitive Field Schemas

Shared domain primitives may be defined centrally.

Example:

```typescript
export const ArticleTitleSchema = z
  .string()
  .trim()
  .min(1, "Enter an article title.")
  .max(120, "The title cannot exceed 120 characters.");

export const SlugSchema = z
  .string()
  .trim()
  .min(1)
  .max(160)
  .regex(
    /^[a-z0-9]+(?:-[a-z0-9]+)*$/,
    "Use lowercase letters, numbers, and hyphens only.",
  );

export const EntityIdSchema = z.string().uuid();
```

These primitives reduce inconsistent limits.

Do not make a single generic `NameSchema` for unrelated concepts whose future rules may differ.

---

# 7.5.8.14 Strict Object Schemas

Transport schemas should reject unexpected properties where practical.

Example:

```typescript
const AssignRoleSchema = z
  .object({
    userId: z.string().uuid(),
    roleId: z.string().uuid(),
    expectedVersion: z.number().int().positive(),
  })
  .strict();
```

This helps prevent over-posting such as:

```json
{
  "userId": "...",
  "roleId": "...",
  "isSuperAdmin": true
}
```

The application should explicitly accept only permitted fields.

---

# 7.5.8.15 Input Coercion

HTML `FormData` values commonly arrive as strings.

Coercion should be explicit and narrow.

Example:

```typescript
const ScheduleFormSchema = z.object({
  expectedVersion: z.coerce.number().int().positive(),
  scheduledAt: z.string().datetime(),
});
```

Be careful with broad Boolean coercion.

For example, arbitrary strings can produce surprising truthy values.

Prefer explicit conversion:

```typescript
const BooleanCheckboxSchema = z
  .enum(["on"])
  .transform(() => true)
  .optional()
  .transform((value) => value ?? false);
```

---

# 7.5.8.16 Empty String Normalization

HTML optional fields often produce:

```text
""
```

while domain models may use:

```text
null
```

Normalize at the boundary.

Example:

```typescript
function emptyStringToNull<T extends z.ZodTypeAny>(schema: T) {
  return z.preprocess(
    (value) => (value === "" ? null : value),
    schema,
  );
}
```

Use one consistent policy.

Do not allow:

```text
undefined
null
""
"   "
```

to represent the same domain state unpredictably.

---

# 7.5.8.17 Trimming Policy

Trim surrounding whitespace for fields where it has no semantic meaning:

* titles;
* names;
* slugs;
* emails;
* short descriptions;
* Search terms.

Do not automatically trim or collapse content where whitespace is meaningful:

* code blocks;
* Markdown source;
* poetry;
* transcripts;
* formatted quotations.

Canonical content normalization must preserve intentional editorial formatting.

---

# 7.5.8.18 Unicode Normalization

User input may contain visually equivalent Unicode sequences.

For identifier-like fields, TWN may normalize to a defined Unicode normalization form.

Potential candidates:

* slugs;
* emails where appropriate;
* Tag keys;
* username-like identifiers.

Do not alter reader-facing prose in ways that change language or orthography.

Multilingual African names and content must not be forced into Westernized or ASCII-only display forms.

Public slugs may use a documented transliteration strategy while preserving the original title separately.

---

# 7.5.8.19 Validation Error Structure

TWN should use a stable validation-error contract.

```typescript
export type ValidationErrorResponse = {
  code: "VALIDATION_ERROR";
  message: string;
  fieldErrors: Record<string, string[]>;
  formErrors: string[];
  requestId?: string;
};
```

Zod supports structured issue paths and error-formatting utilities, allowing errors to be mapped to fields without exposing the raw schema internals directly. ([Zod][6])

---

# 7.5.8.20 Field Errors Versus Form Errors

## Field error

Belongs to one field.

```text
Title cannot exceed 120 characters.
```

## Form error

Belongs to the whole request.

```text
The selected publication date conflicts with the current workflow.
```

Do not attach every business error to a random field merely because the UI expects field errors.

---

# 7.5.8.21 User-Facing Messages

Validation messages should:

* identify what must change;
* avoid technical language;
* avoid blaming the User;
* preserve the entered data;
* be accessible;
* remain stable enough for testing.

Good:

```text
Choose a publication date in the future.
```

Weak:

```text
Invalid date.
```

Bad:

```text
Zod refinement failed at scheduledAt.
```

---

# 7.5.8.22 Internal Error Codes

Messages may evolve.

Codes should remain stable.

Examples:

```text
ARTICLE_TITLE_REQUIRED
ARTICLE_SLUG_INVALID
ARTICLE_SLUG_TAKEN
ARTICLE_NOT_READY_FOR_PUBLICATION
DRAFT_VERSION_CONFLICT
JOURNEY_STRUCTURE_INVALID
JOURNEY_CONTENT_NOT_PUBLIC
MEDIA_REFERENCE_INVALID
INVITATION_EXPIRED
FINAL_SUPER_ADMIN_REQUIRED
```

Codes support:

* UI mapping;
* analytics;
* tests;
* localization;
* support investigation.

---

# 7.5.8.23 Database Constraints

Application validation improves experience.

Database constraints preserve structural integrity under:

* concurrent requests;
* scripts;
* migrations;
* bugs;
* future services;
* direct administrative actions.

Recommended database constraints include:

* primary keys;
* foreign keys;
* unique slugs;
* unique normalized subscriber email;
* valid status checks;
* positive revision numbers;
* required timestamps;
* one relationship row per entity pair.

PostgreSQL foreign keys require the referenced columns to be backed by a primary key, unique constraint, or qualifying unique index, ensuring that each relationship points to a unique valid target. ([PostgreSQL][1])

---

# 7.5.8.24 Database Constraint Limitations

Database constraints should not attempt to encode every evolving editorial rule.

Poor `CHECK`-constraint candidate:

```text
An Article must have at least two sources before publication.
```

Reasons:

* depends on related rows;
* may evolve;
* needs meaningful error explanation;
* belongs to publication policy.

Good database constraint:

```text
revision_number > 0
```

Good unique constraint:

```text
UNIQUE(article_id, revision_number)
```

Good foreign key:

```text
category_id references categories(id)
```

---

# 7.5.8.25 Validation Race Conditions

Application checks can become stale before insertion.

Example:

```text
Check that slug is available
    ↓
Another request creates the same slug
    ↓
Current request inserts
```

The unique database constraint must resolve the race.

The application should map the constraint failure into:

```text
ARTICLE_SLUG_TAKEN
```

not expose a raw PostgreSQL error.

---

# 7.5.8.26 Article Draft Validation

Draft validation should be permissive enough to support work in progress.

A draft may temporarily allow:

* missing subtitle;
* missing SEO description;
* incomplete content;
* no cover;
* unresolved optional links;
* zero Tags.

It must still enforce:

* valid identifier;
* supported content format;
* source-size limit;
* valid typed-block syntax;
* valid version number;
* safe character encoding;
* authorized Article ownership;
* existing Category where required by the draft model.

---

# 7.5.8.27 Article Publication Validation

Publishing requires stronger validation than saving.

Publication policy may require:

* non-empty title;
* valid unique slug;
* non-empty excerpt;
* published Author;
* active Category;
* supported content format;
* valid content structure;
* no unresolved internal references;
* available public Media;
* required Media alt text;
* valid canonical URL;
* completed editorial checks;
* exact draft or revision version;
* lifecycle state permitting publication.

Example policy result:

```typescript
type ArticlePublicationReadiness =
  | { ready: true }
  | {
      ready: false;
      issues: Array<{
        code: string;
        field?: string;
        message: string;
      }>;
    };
```

---

# 7.5.8.28 Slug Validation

A slug should be:

* lowercase;
* hyphen-separated;
* human-readable;
* bounded in length;
* unique within its route namespace;
* free from leading or trailing hyphens;
* free from repeated separator noise;
* protected against reserved paths.

Example schema:

```typescript
const ArticleSlugSchema = z
  .string()
  .trim()
  .min(1)
  .max(160)
  .regex(/^[a-z0-9]+(?:-[a-z0-9]+)*$/);
```

---

# 7.5.8.29 Reserved Slugs

Reserve values that conflict with route architecture.

Potential examples:

```text
new
edit
admin
api
search
feed
rss
sitemap
preview
settings
login
logout
```

Reservation should be namespace-specific.

For example, `search` may be prohibited as an Article slug if `/articles/search` has a functional meaning.

---

# 7.5.8.30 Slug Generation

Slug generation may suggest a value from the title.

The editor must be able to review it.

Algorithm expectations:

* lowercase;
* trim;
* normalize separators;
* remove unsupported punctuation;
* preserve meaningful transliteration where possible;
* avoid empty result;
* append a collision suffix only through a controlled process.

A generated slug is a suggestion.

Uniqueness is decided by the server and database.

---

# 7.5.8.31 Slug Changes

Changing a published slug requires:

* permission;
* valid new slug;
* uniqueness;
* redirect creation;
* canonical URL update;
* cache invalidation;
* sitemap update;
* internal-link resolution;
* Audit Log.

The form should warn the editor about external link consequences.

---

# 7.5.8.32 Markdown Validation

Markdown validation should verify:

* source-size limit;
* supported encoding;
* parse success;
* valid heading structure where required;
* valid typed-block syntax;
* supported block versions;
* safe links;
* no prohibited raw HTML;
* stable internal references;
* valid code-fence structure;
* no parser-depth abuse.

Parsing and sanitization remain separate.

A document can parse successfully and still contain unsafe or invalid references.

---

# 7.5.8.33 Typed-Block Validation

Every block must validate against the registry.

Example:

```typescript
const TypedBlockSchema = z.discriminatedUnion("type", [
  MediaBlockSchema,
  JourneyLinkBlockSchema,
  CalloutBlockSchema,
  VideoBlockSchema,
]);
```

Validation should verify:

* supported block type;
* supported version;
* permitted placement;
* attribute schema;
* referenced entity existence;
* visibility compatibility;
* accessibility requirements;
* maximum nesting depth;
* output fallback availability.

---

# 7.5.8.34 Unsupported Block Versions

Do not silently reinterpret an unknown block version.

Example:

```text
type = media
version = 7
```

when only Version 1 is supported.

Response:

```text
CONTENT_BLOCK_VERSION_UNSUPPORTED
```

Editors should be shown where the unsupported block exists.

Published content migration must create a new revision rather than modify old revisions silently.

---

# 7.5.8.35 Internal Link Validation

Internal references should validate:

* content type;
* entity ID;
* entity existence;
* visibility;
* compatible route;
* no forbidden circular embedding;
* no access to unpublished content from a public revision.

A public Article may mention an unpublished future Article as plain prose.

It must not expose a clickable public internal reference that resolves to private content.

---

# 7.5.8.36 External Link Validation

External URLs should permit only approved schemes:

```text
https:
http: where deliberately supported
mailto:
```

Reject:

```text
javascript:
data: for ordinary links
file:
unknown custom schemes
```

Publication validation may warn about:

* malformed URL;
* redirect chain;
* unreachable destination;
* insecure HTTP link;
* unsupported embed provider.

External availability checks should not become an absolute requirement for every draft save because external networks fail.

---

# 7.5.8.37 Journey Validation

Journey validation exists at several levels.

## Journey metadata

Validate:

* title;
* slug;
* description;
* owner Author;
* cover Media;
* lifecycle status.

## Chapter structure

Validate:

* Chapter IDs;
* unique order;
* valid titles;
* no duplicate Chapter identity;
* no orphan Chapter;
* bounded Chapter count;
* expected structure version.

## Content membership

Validate:

* referenced content exists;
* entity type is supported;
* no duplicate membership where prohibited;
* position is valid;
* content belongs to the intended Chapter;
* public Journey references public content;
* pinned revisions belong to the referenced content entity.

---

# 7.5.8.38 Journey Publication Validation

A Journey should not publish when:

* it has no meaningful content;
* its published structure is missing;
* a Chapter contains invalid references;
* required content is private;
* its owner Author is invalid;
* its slug conflicts;
* its structure version is stale;
* required Chapter metadata is incomplete.

Policy may permit an intentionally empty future Chapter.

That must be represented explicitly rather than arising from missing data.

---

# 7.5.8.39 Journey Circularity

Future Journey links may create loops.

Example:

```text
Journey A links to Journey B
Journey B links to Journey A
```

This may be acceptable as related navigation.

However, recursive embedding must be constrained.

The renderer must not attempt:

```text
Render Journey A
    ↓
Render Journey B
        ↓
Render Journey A
            ↓
Infinite recursion
```

Typed embedded content should define maximum depth and cycle detection.

---
