# 7.5.8.40 Notebook Entry Validation

Draft validation may allow:

* untitled reflection;
* short thought;
* no cover;
* no Journey relationship.

Publication validation may require:

* non-empty thought;
* valid Author;
* valid display date where used;
* supported content format;
* safe blocks;
* exact revision;
* Hero eligibility requirements where selected.

Today’s Page scheduling must prevent conflicting selections for the same date unless the product intentionally supports several Entries.

---

# 7.5.8.41 Community Submission Validation

Shared Pages and Margin Notes are hostile-input boundaries.

Validate:

* length;
* required content;
* permitted markup;
* display-name rules;
* consent;
* rate-limit context;
* spam indicators;
* duplicate submission;
* target Article state;
* prohibited links or excessive links;
* honeypot fields;
* submission timestamp.

All public submissions enter a pending state.

The client must not submit:

```text
status = published
approvedBy = ...
publishedAt = ...
```

---

# 7.5.8.42 Community Content Rules

Community validation should distinguish:

* malformed content;
* spam;
* policy violation;
* content requiring human moderation.

Do not reject every difficult or controversial idea through technical validation.

Moderation policy belongs to human and policy workflows.

Technical validation protects structure and safety.

---

# 7.5.8.43 Honeypot Validation

A hidden honeypot field may detect simple automated submissions.

If filled:

```text
Reject or silently discard according to anti-abuse policy.
```

Do not expose:

```text
You were rejected because the hidden field was filled.
```

Anti-abuse responses should avoid revealing detection logic.

---

# 7.5.8.44 Search Validation

Search input must validate:

* maximum query length;
* permitted filter values;
* page limit;
* cursor format;
* sort option;
* requested entity type;
* normalized whitespace.

Empty Search may:

* return a discovery page;
* reject the query;
* show featured content.

It should not execute an unbounded “return everything” query accidentally.

---

# 7.5.8.45 Search Query Normalization

Potential normalization:

```text
Trim edges
Collapse repeated whitespace
Preserve quoted phrase syntax where supported
Normalize case according to Search engine
```

Do not remove punctuation blindly when it has meaning in technical content:

```text
C++
C#
Node.js
Next.js
```

Search normalization must respect the platform’s subject matter.

---

# 7.5.8.46 Identity Validation

Identity forms may validate:

* email format;
* invitation token shape;
* display-name limits;
* requested role ID;
* recovery code structure;
* account status transition;
* expected User version.

Provider validation still determines credential authenticity.

TWN does not implement password hashing through an ordinary Zod schema.

---

# 7.5.8.47 Email Validation

Email syntax validation should remain practical rather than pretending to prove deliverability.

A syntactically valid email may not exist.

A real account must still be verified through the identity or newsletter confirmation flow.

Normalize carefully:

* trim;
* lowercase domain;
* use a consistent normalized comparison value.

Do not rewrite the local part aggressively without evidence that the provider treats it case-insensitively.

---

# 7.5.8.48 Role Assignment Validation

Validate:

* target User exists;
* target User is eligible;
* role exists;
* actor may delegate it;
* assignment does not already exist;
* temporary expiry is valid;
* last-Super-Admin invariant remains satisfied;
* actor has required authentication assurance;
* expected User version matches.

A well-formed role ID is not enough.

---

# 7.5.8.49 Media Validation

Media validation occurs before, during, and after upload.

## Before upload

Validate:

* declared file name;
* declared content type;
* declared size;
* requested purpose;
* authenticated actor;
* quota;
* supported media class.

## During or after upload

Verify server-side:

* actual byte size;
* actual file signature;
* detected MIME type;
* image dimensions;
* duration where applicable;
* decoding success;
* malware or safety scan where available;
* storage object existence;
* checksum.

Never trust the browser-supplied MIME type alone.

---

# 7.5.8.50 Media File-Name Validation

Original file names may be stored as metadata.

They must not become trusted storage paths.

Generate storage keys independently.

Normalize or remove:

* path separators;
* control characters;
* reserved filesystem names;
* excessive length.

Do not execute or serve files based only on extension.

---

# 7.5.8.51 Image Validation

Validate:

* supported format;
* decode success;
* dimensions;
* pixel count;
* file size;
* orientation metadata;
* animation policy;
* transparency policy where relevant.

A small compressed file may expand into an extremely large image.

Pixel dimensions matter independently of byte size.

---

# 7.5.8.52 SVG Validation

Uploaded SVG is active XML-like content and may contain scripts, external references, or unsafe constructs.

Initial policy:

```text
Do not accept untrusted SVG uploads.
```

Trusted brand SVGs should live in reviewed source control or pass through a strict sanitization process.

---

# 7.5.8.53 Audio and Video Validation

Future validation should include:

* supported container;
* supported codec;
* duration;
* file size;
* metadata extraction;
* decoding;
* transcript/caption requirements for publication;
* intellectual-property confirmation;
* private/public storage intent.

Large media should be processed asynchronously after initial acceptance.

The upload completion response must not imply that processing or publication succeeded.

---

# 7.5.8.54 Newsletter Validation

Subscription input requires:

* valid normalized email;
* affirmative consent where required;
* honeypot and rate-limit checks;
* existing subscriber-state evaluation;
* idempotency;
* suppression-state handling.

A previously unsubscribed address must not be reactivated merely because the subscribe form was submitted again without the required confirmation policy.

---

# 7.5.8.55 Environment Validation

Server environment variables must be validated at startup or build.

Example:

```typescript
const ServerEnvironmentSchema = z.object({
  DATABASE_URL: z.string().min(1),
  SUPABASE_URL: z.string().url(),
  SUPABASE_SERVICE_ROLE_KEY: z.string().min(1),
  SITE_URL: z.string().url(),
});

export const serverEnvironment =
  ServerEnvironmentSchema.parse(process.env);
```

Public and private environment schemas must remain separate.

A secret must never be included in a client-safe schema.

---

# 7.5.8.56 External Provider Response Validation

Responses from trusted vendors are still external input.

Validate:

* response type;
* required fields;
* identifier format;
* status values;
* URLs;
* numeric ranges;
* result count;
* error shape.

If a provider changes unexpectedly:

```text
Fail through the adapter
```

rather than allowing malformed data to enter the domain.

---

# 7.5.8.57 Webhook Validation

Webhook processing requires:

```text
Raw Body
    ↓
Signature Verification
    ↓
Replay Check
    ↓
Payload Parse
    ↓
Schema Validation
    ↓
Idempotency Check
    ↓
Business Processing
```

Do not parse and trust business fields before verifying authenticity.

Validation of a valid signature does not prove that the event type is supported.

---

# 7.5.8.58 AI Output Validation

AI output is untrusted.

Even structured output may:

* omit required fields;
* invent IDs;
* produce unsupported values;
* contain unsafe URLs;
* exceed size limits;
* violate editorial rules.

Every AI response must pass through a schema and domain review.

Example:

```typescript
const SuggestedTagsSchema = z.object({
  tagIds: z.array(z.string().uuid()).max(10),
  explanation: z.string().max(1000),
});
```

The system must then verify that every Tag ID actually exists.

Schema validity is not factual correctness.

---

# 7.5.8.59 AI-Generated Citations

Validate:

* citation shape;
* URL scheme;
* claimed source metadata;
* duplicate citations;
* referenced passage where available.

Human editorial verification remains mandatory.

A syntactically valid citation may still be fabricated.

---

# 7.5.8.60 Browser Recovery Validation

Local draft recovery data must be treated as untrusted when reintroduced.

Validate:

* schema version;
* Article ID;
* User ownership;
* base draft version;
* content format;
* content size;
* typed blocks;
* recovery timestamp.

Never apply browser recovery automatically to the server draft.

Show a comparison and require deliberate restoration or merge.

---

# 7.5.8.61 Import Validation

Content imports require staged validation.

```text
Upload
    ↓
Parse
    ↓
Validate Source Format
    ↓
Transform
    ↓
Validate Canonical Format
    ↓
Preview
    ↓
Commit Import
```

Record:

* import source;
* original identifier;
* parsing warnings;
* rejected records;
* transformed fields;
* Media mapping;
* schema version.

Do not allow one invalid record to produce a silent partial import without a report.

---

# 7.5.8.62 Batch Validation

Batch commands should report:

* total records;
* valid records;
* invalid records;
* rejected reasons;
* whether operation is atomic.

For sensitive operations, prefer atomic batches.

Example:

```text
Assign 20 Articles to a Journey
```

If one reference is invalid, the default should be to reject the whole structural change unless partial attachment is explicitly requested and reported.

---

# 7.5.8.63 Validation and Authorization Order

Recommended order:

```text
1. Parse enough to identify the operation safely
2. Verify authentication
3. Verify coarse permission
4. Validate full input
5. Load required resources
6. Apply resource authorization
7. Apply domain rules
8. Persist transactionally
```

Why not always perform all validation before authentication?

An expensive parser or resource lookup could be abused by unauthenticated callers.

Why not authorize before any parsing?

The application must first determine the intended operation and safely extract required identifiers.

The exact ordering may vary by risk, but neither validation nor authorization may be omitted.

---

# 7.5.8.64 Expensive Validation

Some checks are costly:

* link verification;
* antivirus scanning;
* media transcoding;
* AI safety review;
* large-document parsing;
* plagiarism checking.

These may occur asynchronously.

The entity should remain in an intermediate state:

```text
uploaded
processing
validated
active
```

or:

```text
uploaded
processing
failed
```

Do not make the public request wait indefinitely.

---

# 7.5.8.65 Validation State

Some entities may need an explicit validation status.

Example Media:

```text
pending
processing
valid
rejected
failed
```

Example imported content:

```text
parsed
warnings
ready_for_review
rejected
committed
```

Do not overload editorial publication status with infrastructure-processing status.

---

# 7.5.8.66 Validation Warnings

Not every concern should block an operation.

Examples:

* excerpt may be too vague;
* SEO description is missing;
* external link uses HTTP;
* Article contains very long paragraphs;
* Journey Chapter has only one item;
* image dimensions are below recommendation.

Warnings should be distinguished from blockers.

```typescript
type ValidationReport = {
  blockers: ValidationIssue[];
  warnings: ValidationIssue[];
};
```

Only blockers prevent the action.

---

# 7.5.8.67 Validation Rule Severity

Suggested levels:

```text
error
warning
information
```

## Error

Operation cannot proceed.

## Warning

Operation may proceed after acknowledgement or according to policy.

## Information

Improvement suggestion.

Avoid overwhelming editors with low-value warnings.

---

# 7.5.8.68 Validation Rule Registry

Complex publication workflows may benefit from an explicit registry.

```typescript
interface ValidationRule<TContext> {
  code: string;
  severity: "error" | "warning" | "information";
  applies(context: TContext): boolean;
  evaluate(context: TContext): ValidationIssue | null;
}
```

Use only when rules become numerous.

Version 1 may use clear policy functions without building a generic rules engine.

---

# 7.5.8.69 Domain Invariants

An invariant is a condition that must always remain true.

Examples:

```text
A published Article points to one valid revision belonging to that Article.

A Journey Chapter belongs to exactly one Journey.

A scheduled Article has a valid scheduled revision.

A suspended User cannot perform administrative mutations.

A role assignment references an existing User and Role.

A public content reference does not expose a private revision.

The final active Super Admin cannot be removed through an ordinary workflow.
```

Invariants belong in domain and persistence design.

They are not optional form tips.

---

# 7.5.8.70 Cross-Entity Validation

Some rules require several entities.

Example publication:

```text
Article draft
+
Author
+
Category
+
Media
+
Revision
+
Review state
```

The Application Service should load a purpose-specific validation context.

```typescript
type ArticlePublicationContext = {
  article: Article;
  draft: ArticleDraft;
  author: Author;
  category: Category;
  media: MediaValidationSummary[];
  unresolvedReviewBlockers: number;
};
```

Avoid domain rules making arbitrary repository calls internally.

Pass the required state into the policy.

---

# 7.5.8.71 Time Validation

Time-sensitive rules should use a consistent server clock.

Examples:

* invitation expiry;
* scheduled publication;
* temporary access;
* embargo;
* subscription token expiry.

Do not trust browser time.

Consider injecting a clock abstraction into domain policies for deterministic tests.

```typescript
interface Clock {
  now(): Date;
}
```

---

# 7.5.8.72 Time-Zone Validation

Store canonical timestamps in UTC.

Preserve the intended editorial time zone separately when scheduling requires it.

Validate:

* timestamp;
* IANA time-zone identifier;
* daylight-saving ambiguity;
* future time;
* minimum scheduling lead time where required.

Do not accept vague strings such as:

```text
tomorrow morning
```

as canonical publication timestamps.

The UI may help convert human intent into an exact value.

---

# 7.5.8.73 Numeric Validation

All numeric input should define:

* integer or decimal;
* minimum;
* maximum;
* units;
* precision;
* whether zero is meaningful.

Example:

```typescript
const ReadingPositionSchema = z.number().int().min(0).max(100);
```

Do not trust client-calculated values for:

* price;
* quotas;
* word count;
* reading time;
* role level;
* upload size.

The server derives or verifies them.

---

# 7.5.8.74 Array Validation

Arrays should define:

* maximum items;
* duplicate policy;
* ordering meaning;
* item schema;
* empty-list policy.

Example:

```typescript
const ArticleTagIdsSchema = z
  .array(z.string().uuid())
  .max(20)
  .transform((ids) => [...new Set(ids)]);
```

If duplicate order carries meaning, do not silently deduplicate.

Reject instead.

---

# 7.5.8.75 Object-Key Validation

Never accept arbitrary object keys for update operations.

Bad:

```typescript
updateArticle(input.changes);
```

where `changes` is unrestricted.

Preferred:

```typescript
const EditableArticleFieldsSchema = z.object({
  title: ArticleTitleSchema.optional(),
  subtitle: ArticleSubtitleSchema.optional(),
  excerpt: ArticleExcerptSchema.optional(),
});
```

The server owns the allow-list.

---

# 7.5.8.76 Data Transformation Safety

Zod transformations should be:

* deterministic;
* side-effect free;
* inexpensive;
* easy to test.

Good:

```text
Trim title
Convert empty string to null
Parse bounded integer
```

Poor:

```text
Call database
Send network request
Modify external state
Generate random slug
```

Business and I/O checks belong outside the schema.

---

# 7.5.8.77 Async Refinement Policy

Asynchronous schema refinement may be used sparingly.

Example:

```text
Verify invitation token against a cryptographic service
```

However, common database checks should remain in Application Services because:

* transaction context matters;
* authorization matters;
* error mapping is clearer;
* batching is easier;
* schemas remain reusable.

---

# 7.5.8.78 Validation Performance

Validation should protect resources rather than become a denial-of-service vector.

Controls:

* request-body limits;
* file-size limits;
* collection-size limits;
* nesting-depth limits;
* Markdown-size limits;
* maximum schema complexity;
* timeouts for external checks;
* rate limits before expensive validation;
* asynchronous processing for large files.

Reject excessively large requests before fully parsing them where the platform permits.

---

# 7.5.8.79 Validation Logging

Log validation failures selectively.

Useful aggregate data:

* operation;
* rule code;
* route;
* actor type;
* count;
* request ID.

Do not log full sensitive input.

Routine human form mistakes do not require error-level logs.

Repeated malicious patterns may require security telemetry.

---

# 7.5.8.80 Validation Metrics

Potential metrics:

* validation failure rate per form;
* top failing fields;
* publication blockers;
* invalid upload rate;
* webhook-schema failure;
* provider-response mismatch;
* import rejection rate;
* Journey structure conflicts;
* Search-query rejection;
* AI-output schema failure.

These metrics can reveal:

* confusing UI;
* abuse;
* provider changes;
* schema drift;
* data-migration problems.

---

# 7.5.8.81 Validation and Localization

Validation codes should remain language-neutral.

Messages may be localized.

Example:

```text
Code:
ARTICLE_TITLE_REQUIRED
```

English:

```text
Enter an article title.
```

French:

```text
Saisissez un titre pour l’article.
```

Do not use English message text as application logic.

---

# 7.5.8.82 Accessibility of Validation Errors

Forms must:

* associate field errors with controls;
* use `aria-invalid` where appropriate;
* use `aria-describedby`;
* provide a summary for long forms;
* move or announce focus carefully after submission;
* preserve entered values;
* not communicate errors through colour alone.

The error should explain what must change.

---

# 7.5.8.83 Validation Rule Versioning

Rules evolve.

A draft valid in 2026 may fail a new 2028 publication rule.

TWN must distinguish:

* validating a new mutation;
* validating historical stored content;
* rendering an existing published revision;
* migrating content.

Do not make historical public content disappear automatically because a stricter new rule was introduced.

---

# 7.5.8.84 Grandfathered Content

When a new rule is introduced:

```text
All images require alt text.
```

Existing published content may need:

* migration queue;
* warning;
* editorial remediation;
* continued rendering with controlled fallback.

New publication should enforce the current rule immediately.

Historical content should be repaired deliberately.

---

# 7.5.8.85 Validation Migrations

A validation-rule migration should document:

* old rule;
* new rule;
* affected entities;
* count of invalid existing records;
* remediation method;
* enforcement date;
* database-constraint changes;
* rollback plan.

Example:

```text
Old:
Slug permitted underscores.

New:
Slug permits hyphens only.

Existing underscored slugs:
Retained with redirects until explicitly migrated.
```

---

# 7.5.8.86 Constraint Rollout

Adding a database constraint to a populated table may require staged rollout.

Possible sequence:

```text
1. Add application validation
2. Measure invalid existing data
3. Repair or quarantine invalid records
4. Add constraint
5. Validate constraint
6. Remove temporary compatibility paths
```

PostgreSQL supports adding some constraints as `NOT VALID` and validating them later, which can help staged migrations on populated tables. ([PostgreSQL][7])

---

# 7.5.8.87 Validation Testing Strategy

## Schema tests

Test:

* valid minimum;
* valid maximum;
* below minimum;
* above maximum;
* missing value;
* wrong type;
* unexpected field;
* whitespace;
* Unicode;
* duplicate values;
* malformed identifiers.

## Domain-policy tests

Test:

* valid workflow;
* invalid lifecycle transition;
* missing relationship;
* suspended actor;
* stale version;
* conflicting state;
* boundary times.

## Database tests

Test:

* unique constraint;
* foreign key;
* check constraint;
* transaction rollback;
* concurrent duplicate insertion.

## Security tests

Test:

* over-posting;
* unsupported URL schemes;
* raw HTML;
* oversized payload;
* deep nested content;
* forged status;
* malicious file metadata.

## End-to-end tests

Verify:

* errors appear beside fields;
* form data remains;
* accessibility announcements work;
* publication blockers are clear;
* direct action invocation is rejected;
* invalid browser-recovery data is not applied.

---

# 7.5.8.88 Property-Based Testing

Property-based testing may be useful for complex parsers and transformations.

Potential targets:

* slug generation;
* Markdown block parsing;
* cursor encoding;
* Unicode normalization;
* Journey ordering;
* round-trip import/export.

Introduce only where it provides meaningful coverage beyond example tests.

---

# 7.5.8.89 Validation Anti-Patterns

## Client-only validation

Can be bypassed.

## One schema for every purpose

Creates overexposure and coupling.

## Raw TypeScript casting

```typescript
input as PublishArticleInput
```

does not validate runtime input.

## Over-posting database entities

Allows clients to submit server-owned fields.

## Database errors shown directly to users

Leaks implementation and confuses users.

## Sanitizing instead of validating

Transforms malicious or invalid input unpredictably.

## Validating format but not business meaning

Allows semantically impossible actions.

## Performing network calls inside every schema

Creates hidden latency and poor transaction control.

## Accepting arbitrary HTML or MDX

Creates content-security risk.

## Treating AI output as trusted structure

Allows invented or unsafe data into the system.

## Applying new rules retroactively without migration

Breaks historical content.

## Returning one generic “invalid input” error

Prevents useful correction.

## Logging complete rejected payloads

May expose private or hostile content.

## Allowing warning fatigue

Editors begin ignoring important issues.

---

# 7.5.8.90 Implementation Phases

## Phase 1 — Core validation foundation

Implement:

* Zod;
* primitive field schemas;
* Server Action schemas;
* stable error contract;
* Article draft validation;
* publication-readiness validation;
* slug rules;
* database constraints;
* form-error accessibility.

## Phase 2 — Content validation

Add:

* Markdown parser validation;
* typed-block registry;
* internal-link checks;
* Media-reference validation;
* Notebook rules;
* Journey structural validation.

## Phase 3 — Community and Media

Add:

* public submission limits;
* spam and honeypot checks;
* upload validation;
* file-signature verification;
* asynchronous processing state;
* Media publication requirements.

## Phase 4 — Integrations and AI

Add:

* webhook schemas;
* provider-response validation;
* import pipelines;
* AI-output schemas;
* citation verification workflow;
* validation telemetry.

## Phase 5 — Governance

Add:

* rule registry where justified;
* localization;
* staged rule migrations;
* validation dashboards;
* periodic data-quality audits.

---

# 7.5.8.91 Validation Review Checklist

Before shipping a mutation or input boundary, answer:

* What is the trust boundary?
* What format is received?
* Is the request-size limit defined?
* Which schema owns the input?
* Are unexpected fields rejected?
* Which values are normalized?
* Is normalization safe for the language and content?
* Which checks are structural?
* Which checks are business rules?
* Which permission is required?
* Which database constraints protect the final state?
* Could a concurrency race bypass the application check?
* Are server-owned fields excluded?
* Are URLs allow-listed?
* Is content sanitization required?
* Is file content verified server-side?
* Is the error actionable?
* Are errors accessible?
* Is sensitive state concealed?
* What is logged?
* How does the rule affect existing records?
* Is a migration required?
* Are warnings separated from blockers?
* Is the rule covered by tests?
* Can invalid input cause excessive computation?
* Is AI or provider output revalidated?

---

# 7.5.8.92 Architecture Decision Record

**Decision:** TWN uses layered validation. Browser validation improves usability, Zod validates runtime input at transport and application boundaries, domain policies enforce business meaning and lifecycle invariants, and PostgreSQL constraints preserve final structural integrity.

**Supporting decisions:**

* Every external or browser-controlled value is untrusted.
* Validation, normalization, sanitization, escaping, authorization, and moderation remain separate concerns.
* Schemas are owned by their specific contract rather than one universal entity schema.
* `safeParse` is preferred for expected User-input failures.
* Server Actions and Route Handlers validate all submitted input server-side.
* Unexpected properties are rejected where practical.
* Server-owned fields are not accepted from clients.
* Draft validation is intentionally less strict than publication validation.
* Publication validates the exact selected revision and all required relationships.
* Slugs are validated, reserved, unique, and redirect-aware.
* Markdown is parsed, block-validated, and safely rendered.
* Typed content blocks are versioned and allow-listed.
* Journey structure and content visibility are validated before publication.
* Public community submissions are bounded, rate-limited, and always moderated.
* Media is validated using server-observed file properties rather than client claims.
* External provider, webhook, import, recovery, and AI outputs are validated as untrusted input.
* Warnings and blocking errors are represented separately.
* Stable error codes are separated from localized messages.
* Rule changes require compatibility and migration planning.
* Database constraints remain the final defence against races and invalid relationships.
* Validation failures are observable without logging sensitive payloads.

**Reasons:**

* protects editorial integrity;
* prevents malformed and unsafe content;
* improves form usability;
* supports secure Server Actions;
* reduces accidental over-posting;
* preserves database consistency;
* accommodates multilingual and multimedia content;
* supports future AI features without trusting model output;
* provides a controlled path for validation rules to evolve;
* keeps TWN’s data reliable over many years.

**Expected lifetime:** The layered validation model is permanent. Individual schemas, limits, and business rules will evolve through versioned changes and documented migrations.

---

## End of 7.5.8

The next subsection is:

# **7.5.9 — Form Architecture, Submission States & Accessible Error Handling**

It will define:

* native forms and progressive enhancement;
* `useActionState`;
* pending and disabled states;
* form-level and field-level errors;
* preserving input after failure;
* complex React Hook Form boundaries;
* autosave forms;
* multi-step CMS workflows;
* duplicate-submission protection;
* destructive-action confirmations;
* unsaved-change warnings;
* keyboard and screen-reader behaviour;
* form testing standards.

[1]: https://www.postgresql.org/docs/current/ddl-constraints.html?utm_source=chatgpt.com "Documentation: 18: 5.5. Constraints"
[2]: https://cheatsheetseries.owasp.org/cheatsheets/Input_Validation_Cheat_Sheet.html?utm_source=chatgpt.com "Input Validation - OWASP Cheat Sheet Series"
[3]: https://cheatsheetseries.owasp.org/cheatsheets/Business_Logic_Security_Cheat_Sheet.html?utm_source=chatgpt.com "Business Logic Security - OWASP Cheat Sheet Series"
[4]: https://nextjs.org/docs/app/guides/forms?utm_source=chatgpt.com "How to Create Forms with Server Actions"
[5]: https://zod.dev/basics?utm_source=chatgpt.com "Basic usage"
[6]: https://zod.dev/error-formatting?utm_source=chatgpt.com "Formatting errors"
[7]: https://www.postgresql.org/docs/current/sql-altertable.html?utm_source=chatgpt.com "Documentation: 18: ALTER TABLE"

# 7.5.9 Form Architecture, Submission States & Accessible Error Handling

**Status:** Frontend Architecture Specification
**Applies to:** Public forms, Editorial CMS, Notebook creation, Journey management, Newsletter signup, Community submissions, Search forms, Account settings, Media uploads, and future frontend workflows
**Primary objective:** Ensure every TWN form is accessible, resilient, predictable, performant, and consistent across the entire platform.

---

# Purpose

This section defines how forms behave throughout TWN.

It establishes:

* form architecture;
* submission patterns;
* progressive enhancement;
* pending states;
* success states;
* validation presentation;
* error handling;
* accessibility requirements;
* multi-step workflows;
* autosave experiences;
* destructive confirmations;
* duplicate submission prevention;
* recovery behaviour.

The goal is simple:

> Every form should feel reliable, understandable, and impossible to accidentally misuse.

---

# 7.5.9.1 Form Philosophy

Forms are one of the most important interaction surfaces in TWN.

Examples:

```text
Create Article
Create Notebook Entry
Create Journey
Newsletter Signup
Submit Margin Note
Update Profile
Upload Media
Search
```

A user should never wonder:

```text
Did my submission work?
Is it still loading?
Did I lose my data?
What went wrong?
```

The interface should always communicate clearly.

---

# 7.5.9.2 Progressive Enhancement First

Every form should function without JavaScript whenever practical.

Example:

```html
<form action="/submit">
```

should still work.

JavaScript enhances the experience.

It should not be required for basic functionality.

---

# 7.5.9.3 Native Form Preference

Prefer:

```html
<form>
<button type="submit">
```

Over:

```html
<div onClick={submit}>
```

Native forms provide:

* keyboard support;
* accessibility;
* browser validation;
* progressive enhancement;
* predictable behaviour.

---

# 7.5.9.4 One Form, One Responsibility

A form should perform one primary task.

Good:

```text
Newsletter Signup
```

Good:

```text
Article Metadata Form
```

Bad:

```text
Entire CMS inside one giant form
```

Large workflows should be split logically.

---

# 7.5.9.5 Form Categories

TWN defines five major categories.

---

## Category 1 — Public Forms

Examples:

```text
Newsletter Signup
Contact Form
Community Submission
```

Characteristics:

* simple;
* low risk;
* anonymous or semi-anonymous.

---

## Category 2 — Editorial Forms

Examples:

```text
Create Article
Edit Journey
Publish Notebook Entry
```

Characteristics:

* authenticated;
* complex;
* autosave support.

---

## Category 3 — Administrative Forms

Examples:

```text
Role Assignment
Feature Flag Update
```

Characteristics:

* high sensitivity;
* confirmation requirements.

---

## Category 4 — Search Forms

Examples:

```text
Global Search
Library Search
Journey Search
```

Characteristics:

* fast;
* URL-driven.

---

## Category 5 — Upload Forms

Examples:

```text
Image Upload
Audio Upload
Video Upload
```

Characteristics:

* progress tracking;
* retries;
* cancellation.

---

# 7.5.9.6 Submission Lifecycle

Every form follows a standard lifecycle.

```text
Idle
↓
Submitting
↓
Success
or
Failure
```

No hidden states.

No ambiguous states.

---

# 7.5.9.7 Idle State

Initial state.

User may:

* type;
* edit;
* navigate fields;
* submit.

The form should appear calm and ready.

---

# 7.5.9.8 Pending State

When submission begins:

```text
Idle
↓
Pending
```

The interface must clearly indicate work is occurring.

Examples:

```text
Saving...
Publishing...
Uploading...
Submitting...
```

Users should never guess.

---

# 7.5.9.9 Pending State Behaviour

During submission:

* disable duplicate submit buttons;
* prevent repeated clicks;
* preserve entered data;
* show progress where possible.

Avoid:

```text
Nothing changes
```

after submit.

That creates uncertainty.

---

# 7.5.9.10 Pending Button States

Example:

```text
Publish
↓
Publishing...
```

The label should reflect the action.

Avoid generic text:

```text
Loading...
```

when a more specific label is available.

---

# 7.5.9.11 Duplicate Submission Prevention

Users frequently:

```text
Double click
Tap repeatedly
Press Enter multiple times
```

The UI should prevent accidental duplicates.

Methods:

* disable submit button;
* ignore repeated submissions;
* show pending indicator.

---

# 7.5.9.12 Success States

Success should be visible.

Examples:

```text
Article saved.
Journey updated.
Newsletter subscription confirmed.
```

Do not silently reset forms.

---

# 7.5.9.13 Success State Persistence

Users should have enough time to notice success.

Avoid:

```text
Saved!
(disappears immediately)
```

Messages should remain visible long enough to be understood.

---

# 7.5.9.14 Failure States

Failures should:

* explain what happened;
* explain what can be done next;
* preserve user input.

Never erase completed work because validation failed.

---

# 7.5.9.15 Error Categories

Frontend errors generally fall into:

```text
Validation Errors
Permission Errors
Conflict Errors
Network Errors
Unexpected Errors
```

Each should be presented differently.

---

# 7.5.9.16 Field Errors

Field errors belong to a specific field.

Example:

```text
Title cannot exceed 120 characters.
```

Display:

* near field;
* visually connected;
* accessible to screen readers.

---

# 7.5.9.17 Form Errors

Form errors affect the entire submission.

Example:

```text
You no longer have permission to edit this article.
```

Display near:

* form header;
* submit section.

Not attached to random fields.

---

# 7.5.9.18 Error Message Quality

Good:

```text
Choose a future publication date.
```

Bad:

```text
Invalid date.
```

Errors should help users recover.

---

# 7.5.9.19 Preserve User Input

After failure:

```text
User Input
```

must remain intact.

Losing content is one of the worst user experiences.

Especially for:

* Articles;
* Notebook Entries;
* Community submissions.

---

# 7.5.9.20 Focus Management

After submission failure:

Focus should move to:

```text
First Error
```

or

```text
Error Summary
```

This improves accessibility and usability.

---

# 7.5.9.21 Error Summary Pattern

Long forms should include:

```text
Please fix the following issues:
```

followed by:

* linked errors;
* field references.

Especially useful for:

```text
Article Editor
Journey Editor
Settings Pages
```

---

# 7.5.9.22 Accessible Error States

Errors must:

* be announced to screen readers;
* not rely solely on colour;
* remain visible long enough to be understood.

Red borders alone are insufficient.

---

# 7.5.9.23 Required Fields

Required fields should be clearly identified.

Example:

```text
Title *
```

Users should know requirements before submission.

---

# 7.5.9.24 Optional Fields

Optional fields should not appear mandatory.

Label clearly when appropriate:

```text
Subtitle (Optional)
```

---

# 7.5.9.25 Multi-Step Forms

Complex workflows should be broken into steps.

Example:

```text
Create Journey

Step 1 Metadata
Step 2 Chapters
Step 3 Cover
Step 4 Review
```

Avoid overwhelming users.

---

# 7.5.9.26 Step Progress Indicators

Multi-step workflows should display progress.

Example:

```text
Step 2 of 4
```

Users should understand where they are.

---

# 7.5.9.27 Step Persistence

Users should not lose completed steps when navigating backwards.

---

# 7.5.9.28 Draft Forms

Long-form editorial work requires draft support.

Examples:

```text
Article Draft
Notebook Draft
Journey Draft
```

Users must be able to leave and return later.

---

# 7.5.9.29 Autosave Indicators

Autosave should communicate status.

Examples:

```text
Saving...
Saved 2 minutes ago
Offline
```

Users should trust the system.

---

# 7.5.9.30 Unsaved Changes

When changes are unsaved:

The interface should communicate clearly.

Examples:

```text
Unsaved changes
```

or

```text
Saving...
```

Avoid ambiguity.

---

# 7.5.9.31 Navigation Warning

For significant unsaved work:

Users should receive warning before leaving.

Example:

```text
You have unsaved changes.
Leave anyway?
```

Especially important in CMS experiences.

---

# 7.5.9.32 Search Form Behaviour

Search forms differ from content forms.

Goals:

* speed;
* responsiveness;
* minimal friction.

Search should not require:

```text
Click Search
```

for every interaction.

---

# 7.5.9.33 Search Input Feedback

Users should understand:

```text
Searching...
```

or

```text
No results found.
```

Avoid blank screens.

---

# 7.5.9.34 Empty States

Empty results are not errors.

Example:

```text
No journeys matched your search.
```

This differs from:

```text
Search failed.
```

---

# 7.5.9.35 Upload Forms

Uploads require additional states.

Example:

```text
Selected
Uploading
Processing
Completed
Failed
```

Each state should be visible.

---

# 7.5.9.36 Upload Progress

Users should see:

```text
Progress
```

whenever upload duration is meaningful.

Example:

```text
65% uploaded
```

---

# 7.5.9.37 Upload Failure Recovery

Users should be able to:

```text
Retry
```

without redoing unnecessary work.

---

# 7.5.9.38 Destructive Actions

Examples:

```text
Delete Article
Delete Journey
Delete Media
```

These actions require additional safeguards.

---

# 7.5.9.39 Confirmation Dialogs

Destructive actions should require explicit confirmation.

Example:

```text
Delete this journey?
```

Avoid accidental loss.

---

# 7.5.9.40 High-Risk Confirmation

For irreversible actions:

Require stronger confirmation.

Example:

```text
Type DELETE
```

before continuing.

---

# 7.5.9.41 Accessibility Requirements

All forms must support:

* keyboard navigation;
* screen readers;
* focus visibility;
* accessible labels;
* accessible errors;
* accessible success messages.

No exceptions.

---

# 7.5.9.42 Label Requirements

Every field requires a proper label.

Avoid:

```html
<input placeholder="Email">
```

without a label.

Placeholders are not labels.

---

# 7.5.9.43 Help Text

Complex fields may include:

```text
Help text
```

to clarify expectations.

Example:

```text
This appears on article cards and search results.
```

---

# 7.5.9.44 Disabled State Guidelines

Disabled controls should:

* remain readable;
* clearly explain why disabled if necessary.

Avoid mysterious disabled buttons.

---

# 7.5.9.45 Mobile Form Behaviour

Forms must work comfortably on mobile devices.

Consider:

* touch targets;
* keyboard types;
* spacing;
* scrolling.

---

# 7.5.9.46 Offline Awareness

Where appropriate:

Users should know when the application is offline.

Example:

```text
Changes cannot be saved while offline.
```

---

# 7.5.9.47 Recovery Philosophy

TWN prioritizes:

```text
Never lose user work
```

over:

```text
Perfectly clean state
```

Recovery mechanisms should support this principle.

---

# 7.5.9.48 Form Testing Standards

Every significant form should be tested for:

* success;
* validation failure;
* network failure;
* accessibility;
* keyboard navigation;
* duplicate submission prevention.

---

# 7.5.9.49 Architecture Decision Record

**Decision:** TWN adopts a progressive-enhancement form architecture focused on accessibility, reliability, clarity, and recovery.

**Supporting Decisions:**

* Native forms preferred.
* Progressive enhancement first.
* User input preserved after failure.
* Accessible errors required.
* Pending states always visible.
* Autosave supported for editorial workflows.
* Multi-step forms for complex processes.
* Destructive actions require confirmation.
* Uploads expose progress and recovery.
* Accessibility requirements are mandatory.

**Reasons:**

* improves usability;
* reduces user frustration;
* prevents accidental data loss;
* supports accessibility;
* works across devices;
* creates consistent platform behaviour.

---

## End of 7.5.9

Next:

# **7.5.10 Routing, Navigation & Layout Architecture**

This will cover:

* App Router structure;
* route groups;
* layouts;
* nested layouts;
* navigation hierarchy;
* Journey navigation;
* breadcrumbs;
* URL design;
* route ownership;
* navigation performance;
* layout composition;
* future route evolution;

strictly from the frontend architecture perspective.

# 7.5.10 Routing, Navigation & Layout Architecture

**Status:** Frontend Architecture Specification
**Applies to:** Public website, Editorial CMS, Community experiences, Search, Journeys, Notebook, Library, future Podcasts, Books, Courses, Events, and all TWN frontend navigation systems
**Primary objective:** Create a navigation architecture that is intuitive, scalable, URL-driven, accessible, search-friendly, and capable of supporting decades of content growth.

---

# Purpose

This section defines how users move through TWN.

It establishes:

* route architecture;
* URL philosophy;
* layout hierarchy;
* navigation systems;
* breadcrumbs;
* route ownership;
* Journey navigation;
* content discoverability;
* route scalability;
* accessibility requirements;
* future route evolution.

The goal is simple:

> A reader should always know where they are, how they got there, and where they can go next.

---

# 7.5.10.1 Navigation Philosophy

TWN is a knowledge platform.

Not a social feed.

Not a dashboard-first product.

Not a notification-first experience.

Navigation should encourage:

```text id="j80s4x"
Discovery
Reading
Reflection
Exploration
Learning
```

Rather than:

```text id="fuyxgk"
Infinite scrolling
Endless clicking
Addictive behaviour
```

Navigation should feel like moving through a library.

---

# 7.5.10.2 Route Philosophy

Every route represents a meaningful destination.

Bad:

```text id="0dmb7v"
/page-1
/page-2
/page-3
```

Good:

```text id="7psn5i"
/articles
/notebook
/journeys
/library
/search
```

URLs should reflect information architecture.

Not implementation details.

---

# 7.5.10.3 URL Design Principles

Every public URL should be:

* human readable;
* memorable;
* shareable;
* stable;
* predictable.

Good:

```text id="fc50sa"
/articles/designing-with-curiosity
```

Bad:

```text id="w5trl7"
/article?id=9827
```

---

# 7.5.10.4 URL Longevity

URLs should be treated as permanent identifiers.

Publishing content creates public references.

Breaking URLs damages:

* bookmarks;
* search indexing;
* citations;
* external references.

A URL should remain valid for years.

---

# 7.5.10.5 Route Ownership

Each route belongs to exactly one domain.

Examples:

```text id="vlnhza"
/articles/*
```

Owned by:

```text id="4hx91i"
Editorial
```

---

```text id="gd7n2j"
/journeys/*
```

Owned by:

```text id="7q87kk"
Journey Domain
```

---

```text id="t5d0ng"
/search
```

Owned by:

```text id="x3n3sl"
Search Domain
```

This prevents ownership confusion.

---

# 7.5.10.6 App Router Structure

Recommended structure:

```text id="xqydj7"
app/
│
├── (public)/
├── (editorial)/
├── (community)/
├── (account)/
├── (cms)/
└── (admin)/
```

Route groups improve organization.

They should reflect business domains.

---

# 7.5.10.7 Public Route Group

Contains reader-facing experiences.

Examples:

```text id="kmccj8"
/articles
/notebook
/journeys
/library
/search
/about
/newsletter
```

No authentication required.

---

# 7.5.10.8 CMS Route Group

Contains editorial workflows.

Examples:

```text id="c6igzj"
/cms/articles
/cms/journeys
/cms/notebook
/cms/media
```

Authentication required.

Separate from public experiences.

---

# 7.5.10.9 Admin Route Group

Contains operational tools.

Examples:

```text id="ihn1mh"
/admin/users
/admin/roles
/admin/settings
```

Restricted access.

---

# 7.5.10.10 Layout Philosophy

Layouts define structural consistency.

Examples:

```text id="nca8j5"
Header
Navigation
Footer
Search
```

Shared elements should live in layouts.

Not repeated across pages.

---

# 7.5.10.11 Root Layout

The root layout establishes:

* typography;
* theme;
* global metadata;
* providers;
* accessibility foundations.

Every page inherits from it.

---

# 7.5.10.12 Nested Layouts

Different sections may require specialized layouts.

Example:

```text id="1gyh9v"
Root Layout
│
├── Public Layout
│
└── CMS Layout
```

This avoids duplication.

---

# 7.5.10.13 Layout Responsibilities

Layouts should manage:

* structure;
* navigation;
* shared UI.

Layouts should not own page-specific business logic.

---

# 7.5.10.14 Navigation Hierarchy

Navigation should exist at multiple levels.

```text id="n5m0jb"
Global Navigation
↓
Section Navigation
↓
Local Navigation
```

Each level has a different purpose.

---

# 7.5.10.15 Global Navigation

Primary destinations.

Examples:

```text id="pk6jzv"
Articles
Notebook
Journeys
Library
Community
Search
About
```

Should remain stable.

Frequent changes create confusion.

---

# 7.5.10.16 Section Navigation

Specific to a domain.

Example:

```text id="l1vl79"
Articles
│
├── Latest
├── Featured
├── Categories
└── Archive
```

Provides deeper exploration.

---

# 7.5.10.17 Local Navigation

Page-level navigation.

Example:

```text id="7x8l0p"
Journey Chapters
```

or

```text id="qh5y9y"
Article Table of Contents
```

Context-specific.

---

# 7.5.10.18 Breadcrumb Philosophy

Breadcrumbs provide orientation.

Example:

```text id="2f3ttn"
Home
→ Articles
→ Leadership
→ Building with Curiosity
```

Users should always know where they are.

---

# 7.5.10.19 Breadcrumb Rules

Breadcrumbs should:

* reflect hierarchy;
* be clickable;
* avoid duplication;
* remain concise.

Do not create excessively deep chains.

---

# 7.5.10.20 Article Route Architecture

Recommended:

```text id="jl7rf8"
/articles/[slug]
```

Simple.

Stable.

Readable.

---

# 7.5.10.21 Notebook Route Architecture

Recommended:

```text id="smz33q"
/notebook/[slug]
```

Notebook remains a first-class domain.

---

# 7.5.10.22 Journey Route Architecture

Recommended:

```text id="t9azl7"
/journeys/[slug]
```

Journey becomes a navigational hub.

---

# 7.5.10.23 Journey Chapter Architecture

Recommended:

```text id="jys9m5"
/journeys/[slug]/chapters/[chapter-slug]
```

Allows direct linking.

Supports deep navigation.

---

# 7.5.10.24 Journey Navigation

Journey navigation should support:

* previous chapter;
* next chapter;
* chapter overview;
* progress visibility.

Readers should understand the broader narrative.

---

# 7.5.10.25 Journey as Knowledge Path

Journeys are not categories.

Journeys are curated paths.

Example:

```text id="muvd8i"
Journey:
Building TWN
```

Contains:

```text id="0psj4q"
Reflection
Technical Decisions
Failures
Lessons
Architecture
Leadership
```

across multiple content types.

---

# 7.5.10.26 Cross-Domain Navigation

A Journey may contain:

```text id="m8q6w8"
Article
Notebook Entry
Video
Podcast
Book Chapter
```

Navigation should remain seamless.

Readers follow the Journey.

Not the content type.

---

# 7.5.10.27 Related Content Navigation

Content should expose meaningful related items.

Examples:

```text id="4yjlwm"
Related Articles
Related Notebook Entries
Related Journeys
```

Relationships should be editorially meaningful.

Not random.

---

# 7.5.10.28 The TWN Journey Concept

A major TWN principle:

Content may belong to:

```text id="4h4mn7"
Category
```

and

```text id="ljlwm6"
Journey
```

simultaneously.

Example:

```text id="56u17t"
Technical Architecture Reflection
```

Category:

```text id="i4glx7"
Engineering
```

Journey:

```text id="n2s4jw"
Building TWN
```

Navigation should expose both contexts.

---

# 7.5.10.29 Content Context Display

Readers should see:

```text id="w9wjlwm"
Part of:
Building TWN Journey
```

when applicable.

This encourages deeper exploration.

---

# 7.5.10.30 Search Route Architecture

Recommended:

```text id="vgihdq"
/search
```

Parameters:

```text id="prhl9z"
?q=
&type=
```

URL-driven state.

Shareable results.

---

# 7.5.10.31 Filter Navigation

Filters should update URLs.

Benefits:

* bookmarking;
* sharing;
* browser history support.

State should survive refresh.

---

# 7.5.10.32 Pagination Architecture

Recommended:

```text id="8aq7sb"
?page=2
```

URL-based pagination.

Predictable.

Search-friendly.

---

# 7.5.10.33 Archive Navigation

Archive pages support historical discovery.

Examples:

```text id="p8yxy8"
/archive
/archive/2028
/archive/2028/03
```

Useful for long-term knowledge preservation.

---

# 7.5.10.34 Library Navigation

The Library acts as a knowledge index.

May contain:

```text id="y18sbh"
Articles
Notebook Entries
Journeys
Books
Podcasts
Videos
```

Navigation should prioritize discovery.

---

# 7.5.10.35 Future Content Types

Route architecture should accommodate:

```text id="uqzpnm"
Books
Podcasts
Videos
Courses
Events
```

without restructuring existing routes.

---

# 7.5.10.36 URL Stability During Growth

Future additions should create:

```text id="2k3pl0"
New Routes
```

not break:

```text id="wq8eqf"
Existing Routes
```

Backward compatibility matters.

---

# 7.5.10.37 Route Guards

Protected routes should redirect appropriately.

Examples:

```text id="yzs0g9"
/cms
/admin
/account
```

Public routes remain accessible.

Frontend handles experience.

Access control rules belong elsewhere.

---

# 7.5.10.38 Navigation Accessibility

Navigation must support:

* keyboard access;
* screen readers;
* focus indicators;
* skip links;
* semantic landmarks.

Accessibility is mandatory.

---

# 7.5.10.39 Skip Navigation

Readers should be able to skip repetitive navigation.

Example:

```text id="hl9d6q"
Skip to Content
```

Important for keyboard users.

---

# 7.5.10.40 Active Navigation States

Current location should be obvious.

Example:

```text id="vrifxf"
Articles
```

appears active when viewing articles.

Users should never lose orientation.

---

# 7.5.10.41 Mobile Navigation

Mobile navigation should prioritize:

* readability;
* discoverability;
* minimal cognitive load.

Avoid overwhelming menus.

---

# 7.5.10.42 Navigation Performance

Navigation should feel instant.

Users should not perceive:

```text id="wzz0pu"
Page delay
```

between common transitions.

---

# 7.5.10.43 Error Navigation

404 pages should help recovery.

Examples:

```text id="ivvhn2"
Search
Popular Content
Return Home
```

Do not trap users.

---

# 7.5.10.44 Redirect Philosophy

Redirects preserve continuity.

Examples:

* slug changes;
* Journey restructuring;
* route migration.

Users should arrive at intended content.

---

# 7.5.10.45 Architecture Decision Record

**Decision:** TWN adopts a library-inspired navigation architecture centered around stable URLs, hierarchical layouts, Journey-based exploration, and content discoverability.

**Supporting Decisions:**

* URLs are human-readable.
* Route ownership is domain-based.
* Navigation exists at global, section, and local levels.
* Journeys become first-class navigation structures.
* Layouts provide consistency.
* Search and filters are URL-driven.
* Breadcrumbs support orientation.
* Accessibility is mandatory.
* Future content types integrate without route restructuring.

**Reasons:**

* supports knowledge preservation;
* improves discoverability;
* enables long-term scalability;
* strengthens Journey storytelling;
* aligns with TWN's library philosophy;
* creates predictable navigation behaviour.

---

## End of 7.5.10

Next:

# **7.5.11 State Management Architecture**

This should remain strictly frontend-focused and define:

* local state;
* URL state;
* server state;
* Context usage;
* Zustand usage (if needed);
* form state;
* editor state;
* optimistic state;
* cache ownership;
* state boundaries;
* state lifecycle;
* state debugging and testing.

Yes. Staying strictly within the original blueprint.

We are still inside:

```text
Volume 7 — Technical Architecture

7.5 Frontend Architecture
```

So this section will only cover how frontend state is managed in the browser.

No database design.

No backend architecture.

No API design.

No security architecture.

No CMS architecture.

Those belong to later chapters.

---

# 7.5.11 State Management Architecture

**Status:** Frontend Architecture Specification

**Applies to:** Public website, CMS interfaces, Journeys, Notebook, Search, Community interactions, Account settings, Media management, and future frontend experiences

**Primary objective:** Establish a predictable, maintainable, and scalable approach to managing frontend state while minimizing complexity.

---

# Purpose

This section defines how state is handled inside the TWN frontend.

It establishes:

* local state;
* URL state;
* form state;
* server-derived state;
* editor state;
* temporary UI state;
* Context usage;
* state ownership;
* state boundaries;
* state lifecycles.

The goal is simple:

> Every piece of state should have a clear owner and a clear reason to exist.

---

# 7.5.11.1 State Philosophy

State is one of the largest sources of frontend complexity.

TWN follows:

```text
Minimal State
```

rather than:

```text
Store Everything
```

The best state is often:

```text
No State
```

at all.

If a value can be derived,

it should not be stored.

---

# 7.5.11.2 State Categories

TWN recognizes five major state categories.

```text
Local UI State
URL State
Form State
Server State
Persistent Browser State
```

Each category has different rules.

---

# 7.5.11.3 Local UI State

Examples:

```text
Modal open
Dropdown open
Selected tab
Expanded section
Tooltip visibility
```

Characteristics:

* temporary;
* component-scoped;
* short-lived.

Ownership:

```text
Component
```

Storage:

```tsx
useState()
```

---

# 7.5.11.4 Local State First

Before introducing:

```text
Context
Global Store
```

ask:

```text
Can this remain local?
```

Usually the answer is:

```text
Yes
```

Local state should be preferred whenever possible.

---

# 7.5.11.5 Derived State

Derived values should not be stored.

Bad:

```tsx
const [fullName, setFullName]
```

Good:

```tsx
const fullName = `${firstName} ${lastName}`
```

Derived state creates synchronization problems.

---

# 7.5.11.6 URL State

Some state belongs in the URL.

Examples:

```text
Search query
Filters
Pagination
Sorting
View mode
```

Example:

```text
/search?q=javascript&type=article
```

Benefits:

* shareable;
* bookmarkable;
* refresh-safe.

---

# 7.5.11.7 URL State Ownership

URL state belongs to:

```text
Router
```

not:

```text
Global Store
```

The URL is already a state container.

Avoid duplicating it elsewhere.

---

# 7.5.11.8 Search State

Search state should be URL-driven.

Example:

```text
/search?q=women-in-tech
```

Not:

```text
searchStore.query
```

This improves discoverability and sharing.

---

# 7.5.11.9 Filter State

Filters should also live in URLs.

Examples:

```text
Category
Author
Journey
Date
Content Type
```

Users should be able to refresh without losing filters.

---

# 7.5.11.10 Form State

Forms contain temporary input.

Examples:

```text
Title
Excerpt
Journey Description
Profile Settings
```

Form state exists only while editing.

---

# 7.5.11.11 Form State Ownership

Form state belongs to:

```text
The Form
```

not:

```text
Global State
```

A newsletter form should not affect the rest of the application.

---

# 7.5.11.12 Long Form Content State

Examples:

```text
Article Draft
Notebook Entry
Journey Draft
```

These forms are special.

They may contain:

```text
Thousands of words
```

and require recovery support.

---

# 7.5.11.13 Editor State

Editorial interfaces maintain:

```text
Selection
Cursor
Draft Content
Undo History
Panel Visibility
```

This state belongs to:

```text
Editor Boundary
```

It should not leak into unrelated pages.

---

# 7.5.11.14 Autosave State

Examples:

```text
Saving...
Saved
Offline
Conflict
```

Autosave state should remain close to the editor.

Not globally shared.

---

# 7.5.11.15 Server State

Examples:

```text
Articles
Notebook Entries
Journeys
Authors
Categories
```

Important:

These are not frontend-owned.

They originate elsewhere.

The frontend merely consumes them.

---

# 7.5.11.16 Server State Philosophy

Do not duplicate server data unnecessarily.

Bad:

```text
Fetch Article
Store Article Globally
Synchronize Article
```

Good:

```text
Render Article
```

The frontend should not become a second database.

---

# 7.5.11.17 Persistent Browser State

Some state should survive refreshes.

Examples:

```text
Theme
Draft Recovery
Dismissed Banner
Reading Preferences
```

Characteristics:

* user-owned;
* browser-owned;
* persistent.

---

# 7.5.11.18 Browser Storage Usage

Acceptable uses:

```text
Theme
Draft Recovery
UI Preferences
```

Avoid storing:

```text
Permissions
Sensitive Tokens
Server Truth
```

inside browser storage.

---

# 7.5.11.19 Reading Preferences

Future examples:

```text
Font Size
Reading Width
Theme
Reduced Motion
```

These belong to reader preferences.

Persistence improves experience.

---

# 7.5.11.20 State Ownership Rule

Every state item must answer:

```text
Who owns this?
```

Example:

```text
Modal Open
```

Owner:

```text
Modal Component
```

Example:

```text
Search Query
```

Owner:

```text
URL
```

Unowned state becomes difficult to maintain.

---

# 7.5.11.21 Context Philosophy

React Context is useful.

But should be used sparingly.

Context is not:

```text
Application Database
```

---

# 7.5.11.22 Appropriate Context Usage

Examples:

```text
Theme
Current User Snapshot
Feature Availability
```

These affect many components.

Context is appropriate.

---

# 7.5.11.23 Inappropriate Context Usage

Examples:

```text
All Articles
All Search Results
All Notebook Entries
```

These create unnecessary complexity.

---

# 7.5.11.24 Provider Hierarchy

Providers should remain shallow.

Example:

```text
Theme Provider
User Provider
```

Avoid:

```text
20 Nested Providers
```

Provider sprawl increases complexity.

---

# 7.5.11.25 Global State Philosophy

Global state should be rare.

Only use when:

```text
Many unrelated components
need the same information.
```

---

# 7.5.11.26 Zustand Position

If future complexity requires a dedicated state library:

Preferred option:

```text
Zustand
```

because:

* lightweight;
* simple;
* minimal boilerplate.

However:

Initial TWN versions should avoid introducing it prematurely.

---

# 7.5.11.27 State Synchronization

Duplicated state creates bugs.

Example:

Bad:

```text
URL Search Query
+
Global Search Query
+
Component Search Query
```

Three sources of truth.

Avoid this.

---

# 7.5.11.28 Single Source of Truth

Every state item should have:

```text
One Owner
```

Examples:

```text
Search → URL
Theme → Theme Context
Modal → Component
Draft Content → Editor
```

Never multiple authorities.

---

# 7.5.11.29 Optimistic State

Some interactions may update immediately.

Examples:

```text
Bookmark
Like
Follow
```

The UI updates before confirmation.

This creates perceived responsiveness.

---

# 7.5.11.30 Optimistic Rollback

If confirmation fails:

```text
Optimistic Update
↓
Failure
↓
Rollback
```

Users should never be shown permanently incorrect information.

---

# 7.5.11.31 Temporary State

Examples:

```text
Toast
Loading Spinner
Notification Banner
```

Characteristics:

* short lifespan;
* presentation-only.

Ownership remains local.

---

# 7.5.11.32 State Lifecycles

State should have a clear lifecycle.

Example:

```text
Created
Updated
Consumed
Destroyed
```

State that never dies becomes technical debt.

---

# 7.5.11.33 Route Transition State

Examples:

```text
Loading Route
Navigating
Prefetching
```

This state belongs to the routing layer.

Not individual pages.

---

# 7.5.11.34 Error State

Error state should remain close to the failing interaction.

Example:

```text
Journey Save Error
```

belongs to:

```text
Journey Editor
```

Not application-wide state.

---

# 7.5.11.35 Loading State

Loading state should be scoped.

Good:

```text
Loading Journey Chapters
```

Bad:

```text
Global Loading Everything
```

Users benefit from precise feedback.

---

# 7.5.11.36 Offline State

Future support may include:

```text
Offline
Reconnecting
Connected
```

Ownership belongs to a connectivity layer.

Not every component independently.

---

# 7.5.11.37 Mobile State Considerations

State architecture should work consistently across:

```text
Desktop
Tablet
Mobile
```

Behaviour should remain predictable.

---

# 7.5.11.38 State Debugging

State ownership should be obvious.

Engineers should be able to answer:

```text
Where does this value come from?
```

within seconds.

---

# 7.5.11.39 State Testing

Test:

* state transitions;
* optimistic updates;
* rollbacks;
* persistence;
* form behaviour;
* editor recovery;
* URL synchronization.

State bugs are often interaction bugs.

---

# 7.5.11.40 Architecture Decision Record

**Decision:** TWN adopts a minimal-state architecture emphasizing local ownership, URL-driven navigation state, lightweight Context usage, and clear state boundaries.

**Supporting Decisions**

* Local state first.
* URL state for navigation and discovery.
* Form state remains form-owned.
* Editor state remains editor-owned.
* Context is used sparingly.
* Global state is minimized.
* One source of truth per state item.
* Persistent browser state is limited to user preferences and recovery features.
* Optimistic updates require rollback support.

**Reasons**

* simpler architecture;
* easier maintenance;
* reduced bugs;
* improved performance;
* clearer ownership;
* easier onboarding for future contributors.

---

## End of 7.5.11

Next:

**7.5.12 — Design System Integration Architecture**

This will define how Volume 4 (Design System) is consumed by the frontend implementation, including component ownership, design-token usage, shadcn/ui integration, theming, typography implementation, spacing systems, and design-system governance—without redefining the Design System itself.

# 7.5.12 Design System Integration Architecture

**Status:** Frontend Architecture Specification

**Applies to:** Public website, CMS, Community experiences, Search, Journeys, Notebook, Library, future Books, Podcasts, Courses, Events, and all frontend interfaces

**Primary objective:** Ensure the frontend implementation consumes the Design System consistently while preserving maintainability, accessibility, scalability, and brand integrity.

---

# Purpose

This section defines how the frontend integrates with the Design System.

It establishes:

* component consumption;
* design token usage;
* theming;
* typography implementation;
* spacing implementation;
* shadcn/ui integration;
* component ownership;
* customization boundaries;
* frontend governance.

The goal is simple:

> Every interface should feel like TWN, regardless of who builds it.

---

# 7.5.12.1 Relationship to Volume 4

Volume 4 defines:

```text id="7dh31w"
Buttons
Cards
Inputs
Navigation
Typography
Spacing
Colors
Motion
Tokens
```

This section does not redefine them.

Instead it defines:

```text id="q8qjs3"
How Frontend Uses Them
```

Volume 4 remains the source of truth.

---

# 7.5.12.2 Design System Philosophy

The frontend should consume a system.

Not invent styles page by page.

Bad:

```text id="nk3n6v"
Custom button on every page
```

Good:

```text id="uowh9r"
Shared Button Component
```

Consistency creates trust.

---

# 7.5.12.3 Single Source of Truth

Visual decisions belong to:

```text id="njlwmx"
Design System
```

Implementation belongs to:

```text id="qz6mqs"
Frontend Architecture
```

Engineers should not redefine:

* colors;
* spacing;
* typography;
* component variants.

inside feature pages.

---

# 7.5.12.4 Component Consumption Model

Frontend pages should consume:

```text id="cjlwm1"
Design System Components
```

rather than constructing UI primitives repeatedly.

Example:

Bad:

```tsx id="zkt0g0"
<button className="...">
```

recreated across the codebase.

Good:

```tsx id="4o2egp"
<Button>
```

from the design system.

---

# 7.5.12.5 Component Hierarchy

Recommended hierarchy:

```text id="k2wv2l"
Design Tokens
↓
Primitives
↓
Design Components
↓
Feature Components
↓
Pages
```

Each layer builds on the previous layer.

---

# 7.5.12.6 Design Tokens

Tokens are the foundation.

Examples:

```text id="j9k0z4"
Color
Spacing
Radius
Typography
Motion
Elevation
```

Frontend should consume tokens.

Not raw values.

---

# 7.5.12.7 Token Usage Rule

Bad:

```text id="8y6q8l"
#1A2B3C
17px
11px
```

Good:

```text id="9mjv2v"
--color-primary
--space-4
--radius-md
```

Tokens provide consistency.

---

# 7.5.12.8 Hardcoded Values

Avoid arbitrary values whenever a token exists.

Example:

Bad:

```text id="5k0gkx"
margin: 13px
```

Good:

```text id="8g1ifv"
space token
```

---

# 7.5.12.9 Typography Integration

Typography should come from:

```text id="xzhp1l"
Typography System
```

not page-specific decisions.

Examples:

```text id="0iw8lq"
Heading
Body
Caption
Quote
Code
```

All should map to design-system definitions.

---

# 7.5.12.10 Semantic Typography

Frontend should prefer:

```text id="zh6l5r"
ArticleTitle
SectionHeading
BodyText
```

rather than:

```text id="g2dc4e"
text-4xl
text-sm
```

Semantic naming improves maintainability.

---

# 7.5.12.11 Color Integration

Color should be token-driven.

Avoid:

```text id="wtjlwm"
Random shades
```

introduced during development.

Every color should originate from the Design System.

---

# 7.5.12.12 Spacing Integration

Spacing should follow the spacing scale.

Benefits:

* visual rhythm;
* consistency;
* easier maintenance.

---

# 7.5.12.13 Radius Integration

Corner radius should use predefined tokens.

Avoid page-specific radius decisions.

---

# 7.5.12.14 Elevation Integration

Shadows and elevation should use design-system definitions.

Benefits:

* consistency;
* accessibility;
* predictable hierarchy.

---

# 7.5.12.15 Motion Integration

Motion should follow Volume 4.

Frontend implementation should consume:

```text id="7svr1j"
Motion Tokens
```

rather than creating arbitrary animations.

---

# 7.5.12.16 Motion Governance

Avoid:

```text id="7h48kp"
Random animation durations
```

Consistency improves perceived quality.

---

# 7.5.12.17 Theme Architecture

TWN supports:

```text id="kgvmsp"
Light Theme
Dark Theme
```

with future extensibility.

Themes should be token-driven.

Not component-specific.

---

# 7.5.12.18 Theme Switching

Theme changes should affect:

```text id="v1wz35"
Tokens
```

rather than replacing components.

This keeps implementation simple.

---

# 7.5.12.19 Component Library Position

Recommended foundation:

```text id="n4yz0x"
shadcn/ui
```

as a starting point.

Reason:

* ownership;
* flexibility;
* accessibility foundations;
* compatibility with Next.js.

---

# 7.5.12.20 shadcn/ui Philosophy

shadcn/ui is not the design system.

It is:

```text id="jtlgnh"
Implementation Infrastructure
```

The TWN Design System sits above it.

---

# 7.5.12.21 Component Ownership

Ownership:

```text id="qzv2iw"
TWN
```

not:

```text id="ylw7r7"
Third-party library
```

All components should be controlled by the repository.

---

# 7.5.12.22 Customization Boundaries

Customize:

* styling;
* variants;
* behaviour;
* accessibility improvements.

Avoid uncontrolled divergence.

---

# 7.5.12.23 Component Variants

Variants should be intentional.

Examples:

```text id="7b5fuw"
Primary
Secondary
Ghost
Danger
```

Variants belong in the Design System.

Not in feature pages.

---

# 7.5.12.24 Component Composition

Prefer composition.

Example:

```text id="k9d7d2"
Card
```

may be reused by:

```text id="myxhjz"
Article Card
Journey Card
Notebook Card
```

without duplicating foundations.

---

# 7.5.12.25 Feature Components

Feature components combine design-system pieces.

Example:

```text id="l8qz7g"
JourneyHero
```

built from:

```text id="2fym9e"
Typography
Button
Card
Media
```

---

# 7.5.12.26 Page Components

Pages should assemble feature components.

Pages should not become styling layers.

---

# 7.5.12.27 Design System Boundaries

Pages should not directly manipulate:

```text id="mkk9bc"
Core Tokens
```

Design System changes should remain centralized.

---

# 7.5.12.28 Accessibility Integration

Accessibility belongs inside components.

Not every page.

Example:

```text id="cl7quu"
Button
```

should already support:

* keyboard navigation;
* focus visibility;
* semantic markup.

---

# 7.5.12.29 Accessible Defaults

Components should ship with accessible defaults.

Consumers should not need to remember accessibility details repeatedly.

---

# 7.5.12.30 Form Component Integration

Inputs should already provide:

* labels;
* descriptions;
* error presentation;
* accessibility hooks.

This supports consistency.

---

# 7.5.12.31 Navigation Component Integration

Navigation primitives should provide:

* active states;
* keyboard support;
* screen-reader compatibility.

---

# 7.5.12.32 Responsive Integration

Components should be responsive by design.

Not through repeated page-level fixes.

---

# 7.5.12.33 Content-Type Agnostic Components

Many components should work across content types.

Example:

```text id="u4kgme"
Content Card
```

may support:

```text id="v53ff5"
Article
Notebook Entry
Journey
Book
Podcast
Video
```

through configuration.

---

# 7.5.12.34 Journey Components

Journey becomes a first-class frontend pattern.

Potential reusable components:

```text id="j53pn5"
Journey Hero
Journey Timeline
Journey Navigation
Journey Progress
Journey Chapters
```

These belong to the frontend layer.

---

# 7.5.12.35 CMS Component Integration

CMS interfaces should use the same design system.

Avoid:

```text id="bg2lwx"
Public UI
```

and

```text id="1t80hc"
CMS UI
```

feeling like separate products.

---

# 7.5.12.36 Visual Consistency

A user moving between:

```text id="w1f99q"
Articles
Notebook
Journeys
Search
CMS
```

should still recognize TWN.

---

# 7.5.12.37 Component Documentation

Every shared component should document:

* purpose;
* variants;
* accessibility notes;
* usage guidance.

Documentation improves consistency.

---

# 7.5.12.38 Design-System Evolution

The design system will evolve.

Frontend architecture should allow:

```text id="t5b02h"
New Components
New Tokens
New Variants
```

without major rewrites.

---

# 7.5.12.39 Deprecation Strategy

Old components may be retired.

Deprecation should be:

* documented;
* gradual;
* predictable.

Avoid breaking large sections of the application.

---

# 7.5.12.40 Testing Responsibilities

Design-system components should be tested for:

* accessibility;
* responsiveness;
* interaction behaviour;
* visual consistency.

Feature pages should not duplicate those tests.

---

# 7.5.12.41 Architecture Decision Record

**Decision:** TWN adopts a token-driven frontend architecture that consumes a centralized Design System built on owned components and integrated through shadcn/ui foundations.

**Supporting Decisions**

* Volume 4 remains the source of truth.
* Frontend consumes, not redefines.
* Tokens are mandatory.
* Components are owned by TWN.
* shadcn/ui is infrastructure, not the design system.
* Accessibility is built into components.
* Variants are centrally managed.
* CMS and public experiences share the same visual foundation.
* Journey-specific UI becomes a first-class pattern.

**Reasons**

* visual consistency;
* maintainability;
* accessibility;
* scalability;
* stronger brand cohesion;
* easier onboarding for contributors.

---

## End of 7.5.12

Next:

# **7.5.13 Responsive & Multi-Device Architecture**

This will define:

* mobile-first strategy;
* breakpoints;
* responsive layouts;
* touch interactions;
* tablet experiences;
* desktop experiences;
* large-screen support;
* foldables and future devices;
* responsive typography;
* responsive navigation;
* responsive testing standards;

while remaining strictly within **Frontend Architecture**.

# 7.5.13 Responsive & Multi-Device Architecture

**Status:** Frontend Architecture Specification

**Applies to:** Public website, Editorial CMS, Community experiences, Search, Journeys, Notebook, Library, Media management, future Books, Podcasts, Courses, Events, and all TWN frontend interfaces

**Primary objective:** Ensure TWN provides a high-quality reading, writing, and discovery experience across phones, tablets, laptops, desktops, and future device classes without creating separate products for each platform.

---

# Purpose

This section defines how TWN adapts across devices.

It establishes:

* responsive design philosophy;
* layout adaptation;
* breakpoint strategy;
* navigation adaptation;
* typography adaptation;
* content presentation;
* touch interactions;
* editorial workflows;
* responsive testing standards.

The goal is simple:

> TWN should feel intentionally designed for every device, not merely resized.

---

# 7.5.13.1 Responsive Philosophy

TWN is:

```text id="4vrl7n"
One Product
```

not:

```text id="u7a2q1"
Mobile Product
Desktop Product
Tablet Product
```

Users should encounter the same platform.

Only the presentation changes.

---

# 7.5.13.2 Content First

Responsive decisions should prioritize:

```text id="rx7nwh"
Reading
Writing
Discovery
Navigation
Accessibility
```

before visual decoration.

Content remains the primary concern.

---

# 7.5.13.3 Mobile-First Principle

Frontend implementation should begin from the smallest supported viewport.

Designing for constraints first encourages:

* clarity;
* simplicity;
* performance;
* accessibility.

Larger layouts progressively enhance the experience.

---

# 7.5.13.4 Device Classes

TWN recognizes the following logical device classes:

```text id="2s7q4m"
Mobile
Tablet
Laptop
Desktop
Large Desktop
```

These are experience categories.

Not specific hardware products.

---

# 7.5.13.5 Breakpoint Philosophy

Breakpoints exist to improve usability.

Not to match particular devices.

Bad:

```text id="3v8y9z"
iPhone breakpoint
Samsung breakpoint
```

Good:

```text id="7v91ho"
Layout breakpoint
```

---

# 7.5.13.6 Breakpoint Ownership

Breakpoints belong to:

```text id="9hy4bq"
Design System
```

Frontend implementation consumes them.

Frontend pages should not invent new breakpoint systems.

---

# 7.5.13.7 Layout Adaptation

Responsive behaviour should prioritize:

```text id="v63y9g"
Reflow
```

over:

```text id="3ldg5e"
Rebuild
```

Content should reorganize naturally.

Not become a different interface.

---

# 7.5.13.8 Progressive Enhancement Across Screen Sizes

As viewport size increases:

```text id="h4op8x"
More space
More context
More navigation
More tools
```

become available.

The core experience remains unchanged.

---

# 7.5.13.9 Reading Experience Priority

The reading experience is TWN's most important responsive concern.

All device decisions should support:

* readability;
* focus;
* comfort;
* accessibility.

---

# 7.5.13.10 Line Length

Text should remain readable.

Avoid:

```text id="s8wz75"
Very narrow columns
```

and

```text id="fq3nb2"
Extremely wide paragraphs
```

Optimal reading comfort should guide layout decisions.

---

# 7.5.13.11 Typography Scaling

Typography should scale gradually.

Headings, body text, and supporting content should remain visually balanced across devices.

Avoid dramatic jumps between breakpoints.

---

# 7.5.13.12 Responsive Spacing

Spacing should scale proportionally.

Larger screens may introduce:

```text id="9f3ch1"
More breathing room
```

without making content feel disconnected.

---

# 7.5.13.13 Responsive Images

Images should:

* scale naturally;
* preserve aspect ratio;
* avoid layout shifts;
* remain readable.

Images should never overflow content containers.

---

# 7.5.13.14 Responsive Tables

Tables present challenges on small screens.

Strategies may include:

* horizontal scrolling;
* simplified presentation;
* alternate layouts.

Content should remain accessible.

---

# 7.5.13.15 Responsive Code Blocks

Technical content may include code.

Code blocks should:

* preserve formatting;
* support horizontal scrolling;
* remain readable on mobile.

Content should never be truncated.

---

# 7.5.13.16 Responsive Navigation

Navigation should adapt to available space.

The navigation structure remains consistent.

Only presentation changes.

---

# 7.5.13.17 Mobile Navigation

Mobile navigation should prioritize:

```text id="h3x1vs"
Simplicity
Clarity
Reachability
```

Users should quickly access primary destinations.

---

# 7.5.13.18 Desktop Navigation

Desktop layouts may expose:

* additional links;
* contextual navigation;
* advanced discovery options.

More space enables richer navigation.

---

# 7.5.13.19 Journey Navigation Responsiveness

Journey navigation should work effectively across all devices.

Readers should always understand:

```text id="wx5z0v"
Where am I?
What chapter am I reading?
What comes next?
```

regardless of screen size.

---

# 7.5.13.20 Search Responsiveness

Search remains a primary discovery mechanism.

Search should function equally well across:

```text id="r0w4tm"
Mobile
Tablet
Desktop
```

without feature loss.

---

# 7.5.13.21 Filter Responsiveness

Filters may change presentation.

Examples:

Desktop:

```text id="egbl2w"
Sidebar Filters
```

Mobile:

```text id="8a76x0"
Drawer Filters
```

The capability remains identical.

---

# 7.5.13.22 Card Responsiveness

Cards should adapt naturally.

Examples:

```text id="83h1r6"
Article Cards
Journey Cards
Notebook Cards
```

The content hierarchy remains consistent.

---

# 7.5.13.23 Grid Responsiveness

Grid layouts should adapt gradually.

Examples:

```text id="q6zh6i"
1 column
2 columns
3 columns
```

depending on available space.

Avoid abrupt visual changes.

---

# 7.5.13.24 Sidebar Strategy

Sidebars should appear only when space justifies them.

Small screens should prioritize content.

---

# 7.5.13.25 Modal Responsiveness

Modals should remain usable across devices.

Large desktop:

```text id="z0vhny"
Centered Modal
```

Mobile:

```text id="nhmz0f"
Full-screen or sheet presentation
```

depending on context.

---

# 7.5.13.26 Form Responsiveness

Forms should remain easy to complete.

Requirements:

* touch-friendly controls;
* readable labels;
* sufficient spacing;
* accessible error messages.

---

# 7.5.13.27 Editorial Workflow Responsiveness

Editorial tools should function on smaller screens.

However:

Desktop remains the primary environment for:

```text id="f3vknr"
Long-form Writing
Journey Management
Media Organization
```

Mobile support should remain functional without sacrificing usability.

---

# 7.5.13.28 Touch Interaction Principles

Touch devices require:

* larger targets;
* adequate spacing;
* gesture awareness.

Avoid precision-dependent interactions.

---

# 7.5.13.29 Touch Target Guidelines

Interactive elements should be easy to activate.

Users should not struggle to:

```text id="4zm3po"
Tap
Select
Navigate
```

accurately.

---

# 7.5.13.30 Hover Dependence

Hover effects may enhance desktop experiences.

Critical functionality must never depend solely on hover.

Touch devices do not provide reliable hover interaction.

---

# 7.5.13.31 Gesture Usage

Gestures should be optional enhancements.

Avoid requiring:

```text id="0ut6rw"
Swipe
Pinch
Long Press
```

for core functionality.

---

# 7.5.13.32 Orientation Support

Layouts should function in:

```text id="1xk9ef"
Portrait
Landscape
```

orientations.

Content should remain accessible.

---

# 7.5.13.33 Large Screen Strategy
