Large displays should not simply stretch content.

Additional space may support:

* contextual navigation;
* related content;
* secondary panels.

Reading comfort remains primary.

---

# 7.5.13.34 Ultra-Wide Displays

Ultra-wide screens should avoid:

```text id="xtn7lc"
Extremely long reading lines
```

Content width should remain controlled.

---

# 7.5.13.35 Future Device Support

The architecture should remain adaptable to:

```text id="rzh6i9"
Foldables
Large tablets
Future form factors
```

without requiring major redesign.

---

# 7.5.13.36 Responsive Media Consumption

Future:

```text id="gpk3z4"
Podcasts
Videos
Courses
```

must integrate naturally into responsive layouts.

The architecture should anticipate this growth.

---

# 7.5.13.37 Responsive Performance

Responsiveness includes performance.

Users on mobile devices should receive:

* fast rendering;
* efficient interactions;
* reasonable resource usage.

---

# 7.5.13.38 Accessibility Across Devices

Accessibility requirements remain identical across device classes.

Responsive changes must not reduce:

* keyboard access;
* screen-reader support;
* readability.

---

# 7.5.13.39 Responsive Testing

Testing should include:

```text id="tnfktx"
Mobile
Tablet
Laptop
Desktop
Large Desktop
```

Testing one viewport is insufficient.

---

# 7.5.13.40 Content Integrity

Responsive layouts should never alter meaning.

Content should remain:

```text id="fxj6wl"
Complete
Readable
Accessible
```

on every supported device.

---

# 7.5.13.41 Architecture Decision Record

**Decision:** TWN adopts a mobile-first, content-first responsive architecture that provides one coherent product experience across all device classes while preserving readability, accessibility, and discoverability.

**Supporting Decisions**

* Mobile-first implementation.
* Content-first layouts.
* Design-system breakpoints.
* Responsive navigation.
* Touch-friendly interactions.
* Device-independent functionality.
* Controlled reading widths.
* Responsive editorial workflows.
* Future support for new content types.
* Accessibility across all viewports.

**Reasons**

* supports global audiences;
* improves maintainability;
* reduces duplicate interfaces;
* preserves reading quality;
* aligns with TWN's knowledge-first philosophy;
* enables future growth without major redesigns.

---

## End of 7.5.13

Next:

# **7.5.14 Frontend Accessibility Architecture**

This stays within Frontend Architecture and focuses on implementation responsibilities, accessibility primitives, keyboard interaction patterns, screen-reader support, focus management, semantic HTML usage, accessible component composition, and accessibility testing.

# 7.5.14 Frontend Accessibility Architecture

**Status:** Frontend Architecture Specification

**Applies to:** Public website, Editorial CMS, Community experiences, Search, Journeys, Notebook, Library, future Books, Podcasts, Courses, Events, and all TWN frontend interfaces

**Primary objective:** Ensure every user can access, understand, navigate, and interact with TWN regardless of ability, assistive technology, input method, device, or environment.

---

# Purpose

This section defines how accessibility is implemented within the frontend.

It establishes:

* semantic HTML usage;
* keyboard accessibility;
* screen reader support;
* focus management;
* accessible navigation;
* accessible forms;
* accessible media;
* accessible interactions;
* testing requirements;
* implementation responsibilities.

The goal is simple:

> Accessibility is a core quality attribute of TWN, not an enhancement added later.

---

# 7.5.14.1 Accessibility Philosophy

Accessibility should be:

```text id="d4u1rn"
Built In
```

not:

```text id="n2ylm9"
Added Later
```

Accessibility decisions should be made during implementation.

Not after launch.

---

# 7.5.14.2 Relationship to Other Volumes

This section defines:

```text id="ywhu3g"
Frontend Implementation
```

It does not redefine:

```text id="5cjlwm"
Volume 3 UX Bible
Volume 4 Design System
```

Those define:

* interaction design;
* component design;
* accessibility standards.

This chapter defines implementation responsibilities.

---

# 7.5.14.3 Accessibility Ownership

Accessibility belongs to:

```text id="qwh9zt"
Everyone
```

not:

```text id="8k5fw1"
A future audit
```

Every component owner is responsible.

---

# 7.5.14.4 Semantic HTML First

Prefer native HTML elements.

Good:

```html id="wbyj7n"
<button>
<nav>
<main>
<header>
<footer>
<form>
```

Bad:

```html id="r8owtf"
<div role="button">
```

when a button should be used.

Semantic HTML provides accessibility by default.

---

# 7.5.14.5 Native Before ARIA

Preferred order:

```text id="5yl07x"
Native HTML
↓
Semantic HTML
↓
ARIA
```

ARIA should enhance semantics.

Not replace them unnecessarily.

---

# 7.5.14.6 Landmark Structure

Every page should provide clear landmarks.

Examples:

```html id="jyh4hv"
<header>
<nav>
<main>
<aside>
<footer>
```

These help navigation technologies understand page structure.

---

# 7.5.14.7 Main Content Landmark

Every page should contain:

```html id="ij3iow"
<main>
```

exactly once.

Users should quickly reach primary content.

---

# 7.5.14.8 Heading Hierarchy

Headings define document structure.

Example:

```text id="q8mnjx"
H1
↓
H2
↓
H3
```

Hierarchy should be logical.

Not visual only.

---

# 7.5.14.9 One Primary Heading

Each page should generally expose:

```text id="v4k5s7"
One H1
```

representing the primary page topic.

---

# 7.5.14.10 Accessible Navigation

Navigation should be:

* keyboard accessible;
* screen-reader accessible;
* logically organized.

Navigation should not depend on mouse usage.

---

# 7.5.14.11 Skip Navigation

Every major page should provide:

```text id="t3a9qh"
Skip to Content
```

This reduces repetitive navigation.

Especially for keyboard users.

---

# 7.5.14.12 Keyboard Philosophy

Everything interactive must be operable by keyboard.

No exceptions.

Users should never require:

```text id="gmx8qf"
Mouse Only
```

interaction.

---

# 7.5.14.13 Keyboard Reachability

All interactive elements must be reachable through:

```text id="5qevxe"
Tab Navigation
```

Elements should not become inaccessible.

---

# 7.5.14.14 Keyboard Traps

Avoid:

```text id="4p61g5"
Keyboard Trap
```

where users enter a component but cannot leave it.

Especially important for:

* modals;
* drawers;
* editors.

---

# 7.5.14.15 Focus Visibility

Focused elements must remain visible.

Users should always know:

```text id="uazv4x"
Current Focus
```

Removing focus indicators is prohibited.

---

# 7.5.14.16 Focus Styling

Focus styling should come from the Design System.

Consistency improves usability.

---

# 7.5.14.17 Focus Management

Focus should move intentionally after major actions.

Examples:

* opening a modal;
* closing a modal;
* submitting a form;
* validation failure.

---

# 7.5.14.18 Modal Focus Behaviour

When a modal opens:

Focus moves inside.

When a modal closes:

Focus returns to the triggering element.

---

# 7.5.14.19 Error Focus Behaviour

When form submission fails:

Focus should move to:

```text id="8bh1gw"
Error Summary
```

or

```text id="cfvz3h"
First Invalid Field
```

depending on context.

---

# 7.5.14.20 Screen Reader Support

Frontend implementation should assume:

```text id="4zfr0k"
Screen Reader Usage
```

is a normal use case.

Not an edge case.

---

# 7.5.14.21 Accessible Names

Interactive elements require accessible names.

Good:

```html id="x8mnzs"
<button>Search</button>
```

Good:

```html id="tljlwm"
<button aria-label="Close dialog">
```

Bad:

```html id="2i9avm"
<button>
  <Icon />
</button>
```

with no accessible label.

---

# 7.5.14.22 Form Accessibility

Every form control requires:

* label;
* accessible error association;
* help text association where applicable.

---

# 7.5.14.23 Placeholder Misuse

Placeholders do not replace labels.

Bad:

```html id="n5vjlwm"
<input placeholder="Email">
```

without a label.

---

# 7.5.14.24 Error Announcement

Validation errors should be announced appropriately.

Users should know:

```text id="0ttx4d"
What failed
How to fix it
```

without searching visually.

---

# 7.5.14.25 Required Field Communication

Required fields should be communicated clearly.

Users should know expectations before submission.

---

# 7.5.14.26 Accessible Tables

Tables should preserve:

* row relationships;
* column relationships;
* headers.

Tables should not become inaccessible due to styling.

---

# 7.5.14.27 Accessible Lists

Lists should use:

```html id="nx4hoh"
<ul>
<ol>
```

when representing list content.

Not manually styled paragraphs.

---

# 7.5.14.28 Accessible Images

Images require meaningful alternative text where appropriate.

The frontend should ensure alt text is rendered correctly.

Content policy belongs elsewhere.

Rendering responsibility belongs here.

---

# 7.5.14.29 Decorative Images

Decorative images should not create noise for assistive technologies.

Decorative assets should remain silent.

---

# 7.5.14.30 Responsive Accessibility

Accessibility requirements remain identical across:

```text id="6kbk8r"
Mobile
Tablet
Desktop
```

Responsive design must not reduce accessibility.

---

# 7.5.14.31 Touch Accessibility

Touch targets should remain easy to activate.

Avoid:

```text id="m5zjlwm"
Tiny controls
```

that create interaction difficulty.

---

# 7.5.14.32 Color Independence

Meaning should never depend solely on color.

Bad:

```text id="1hxt0k"
Green = success
Red = error
```

without additional indicators.

---

# 7.5.14.33 Icon Accessibility

Icons should support:

* labels;
* text;
* accessible descriptions.

Meaning should not depend solely on icon recognition.

---

# 7.5.14.34 Motion Accessibility

Users may prefer reduced motion.

Frontend should respect those preferences.

Animations should remain optional.

---

# 7.5.14.35 Animation Restraint

Animations should never interfere with:

* reading;
* navigation;
* comprehension.

---

# 7.5.14.36 Media Accessibility

Future media content should support:

```text id="3xoqwl"
Captions
Transcripts
```

when applicable.

Frontend should expose these capabilities.

---

# 7.5.14.37 Accessible Search

Search should support:

* keyboard navigation;
* screen readers;
* result announcement.

Search must remain usable without a mouse.

---

# 7.5.14.38 Accessible Journey Navigation

Journey readers should understand:

```text id="xjlwm8"
Current Chapter
Progress
Previous
Next
```

through accessible navigation patterns.

---

# 7.5.14.39 Accessible CMS

Editorial interfaces must be accessible too.

Accessibility is not only for public pages.

CMS accessibility improves productivity for everyone.

---

# 7.5.14.40 Rich Text Editor Accessibility

Editors should support:

* keyboard usage;
* screen-reader compatibility;
* predictable focus behaviour.

Complex editing interfaces require special attention.

---

# 7.5.14.41 Accessibility Testing

Every major feature should be tested for:

* keyboard operation;
* focus behaviour;
* screen-reader compatibility;
* form accessibility;
* responsive accessibility.

---

# 7.5.14.42 Accessibility Regression Prevention

Accessibility should be protected through:

* reviews;
* automated checks;
* manual testing.

Accessibility improvements should not disappear accidentally.

---

# 7.5.14.43 Architecture Decision Record

**Decision:** TWN adopts an accessibility-first frontend architecture built on semantic HTML, keyboard accessibility, screen-reader support, intentional focus management, and accessible component composition.

**Supporting Decisions**

* Semantic HTML first.
* Native controls preferred.
* Keyboard accessibility mandatory.
* Focus visibility required.
* Skip navigation supported.
* Accessible forms required.
* Screen-reader support considered normal.
* Responsive accessibility preserved.
* Reduced motion respected.
* Accessibility testing integrated into development.

**Reasons**

* improves inclusivity;
* improves usability;
* reduces complexity;
* aligns with TWN principles;
* creates a better experience for all users;
* supports long-term platform quality.

---

## End of 7.5.14

**Remaining sections in 7.5:**

```text id="1z3p4m"
7.5.15 Frontend Performance Architecture
7.5.16 Loading, Error & Empty State Architecture
7.5.17 Frontend Security Responsibilities
7.5.18 Frontend Testing Architecture
7.5.19 Frontend Observability Architecture
7.5.20 Future Frontend Evolution Strategy
7.5.21 Frontend Architecture Decision Record & Completion Criteria
```

**7 sections remaining before 7.5 is complete.**

# 7.5.15 Frontend Performance Architecture

**Status:** Frontend Architecture Specification

**Applies to:** Public website, Editorial CMS, Community experiences, Search, Journeys, Notebook, Library, future Books, Podcasts, Courses, Events, and all TWN frontend interfaces

**Primary objective:** Deliver a fast, responsive, and efficient user experience while preserving accessibility, maintainability, and long-term scalability.

---

# Purpose

This section defines frontend performance responsibilities.

It establishes:

* performance philosophy;
* rendering efficiency;
* bundle management;
* code splitting;
* navigation performance;
* image loading;
* font loading;
* interaction responsiveness;
* performance budgets;
* monitoring responsibilities.

The goal is simple:

> Readers should spend their time consuming knowledge, not waiting for interfaces.

---

# 7.5.15.1 Performance Philosophy

Performance is a feature.

Not an optimization phase.

Not a future task.

Not something added after launch.

Every frontend decision should consider performance impact.

---

# 7.5.15.2 Relationship to Other Chapters

This section focuses on:

```text id="a1m2p3"
Frontend Performance
```

It does not define:

```text id="b4n5k6"
Backend Performance
Infrastructure Scaling
Database Optimization
Caching Strategy
CDN Architecture
```

Those belong to later chapters.

---

# 7.5.15.3 Reader-Centric Performance

TWN is primarily a reading platform.

The most important performance outcome is:

```text id="c7d8e9"
Content appears quickly.
```

Users should reach information with minimal delay.

---

# 7.5.15.4 Perceived Performance

Actual speed matters.

Perceived speed also matters.

Users should understand:

```text id="f0g1h2"
What is happening
```

during loading and navigation.

A clear interface often feels faster.

---

# 7.5.15.5 Simplicity First

The fastest code is often:

```text id="i3j4k5"
Code that does not exist.
```

Avoid unnecessary:

* animations;
* dependencies;
* abstractions;
* client-side logic.

---

# 7.5.15.6 Server-First Rendering

TWN's rendering strategy prioritizes:

```text id="l6m7n8"
Server Components
```

where possible.

Benefits:

* smaller bundles;
* less hydration;
* faster delivery.

---

# 7.5.15.7 Client JavaScript Budget

Client JavaScript should be treated as expensive.

Every additional dependency increases:

* download size;
* parse time;
* execution time.

---

# 7.5.15.8 Bundle Philosophy

Users should not download code they do not need.

Example:

A reader viewing an article should not download:

```text id="o9p0q1"
CMS Logic
Admin Tools
Media Management Interfaces
```

---

# 7.5.15.9 Route-Based Performance

Each route should load only what is required.

Examples:

```text id="r2s3t4"
/articles
/search
/journeys
```

should have independent loading characteristics.

---

# 7.5.15.10 Code Splitting

Large functionality should be split into smaller chunks.

Examples:

```text id="u5v6w7"
Editors
Media Uploaders
Analytics Dashboards
```

should not load unnecessarily.

---

# 7.5.15.11 Progressive Loading

Interfaces should load progressively.

Users should receive:

```text id="x8y9z0"
Useful Content
```

before secondary enhancements.

---

# 7.5.15.12 Critical Content Priority

Highest priority:

```text id="a2b3c4"
Readable Content
```

before:

```text id="d5e6f7"
Enhancements
Widgets
Decorative Features
```

---

# 7.5.15.13 Navigation Performance

Moving between pages should feel immediate.

Users should not experience unnecessary waiting after initiating navigation.

---

# 7.5.15.14 Prefetching Philosophy

Prefetching should be:

```text id="g8h9i0"
Intentional
```

not aggressive.

Prefetch likely destinations.

Avoid wasting bandwidth.

---

# 7.5.15.15 Search Performance

Search interactions should feel responsive.

Users should receive feedback quickly.

Slow search experiences discourage exploration.

---

# 7.5.15.16 Journey Performance

Journey navigation should support seamless progression between chapters.

Readers should remain immersed in the narrative.

---

# 7.5.15.17 Form Performance

Forms should remain responsive while:

* typing;
* validating;
* saving;
* submitting.

Typing latency should never become noticeable.

---

# 7.5.15.18 Editor Performance

Editorial tools require special attention.

Examples:

```text id="j1k2l3"
Article Editor
Notebook Editor
Journey Editor
```

must remain responsive even with long content.

---

# 7.5.15.19 Long Document Performance

Large documents should not degrade dramatically as content grows.

Examples:

```text id="m4n5o6"
Long Articles
Books
Large Journeys
```

must remain manageable.

---

# 7.5.15.20 List Performance

Large collections should remain efficient.

Examples:

```text id="p7q8r9"
Article Lists
Search Results
Media Libraries
Journey Chapters
```

---

# 7.5.15.21 Image Performance

Images are often the largest frontend assets.

Frontend should prioritize:

* appropriate sizing;
* lazy loading where appropriate;
* responsive delivery.

Detailed image architecture belongs later.

---

# 7.5.15.22 Above-the-Fold Priority

Content visible immediately should receive priority.

Users should not wait for critical content.

---

# 7.5.15.23 Below-the-Fold Loading

Content outside the initial viewport may be deferred.

This improves initial responsiveness.

---

# 7.5.15.24 Font Performance

Typography is important to TWN.

Font loading should avoid:

```text id="s0t1u2"
Layout Shifts
Delayed Rendering
```

Reading comfort must remain intact.

---

# 7.5.15.25 Layout Stability

Content should not unexpectedly move after appearing.

Stable layouts improve:

* usability;
* accessibility;
* reader trust.

---

# 7.5.15.26 Interaction Performance

Interactive elements should feel immediate.

Examples:

```text id="v3w4x5"
Buttons
Menus
Forms
Search
```

Users should perceive direct response.

---

# 7.5.15.27 Animation Performance

Animations should remain smooth.

If performance suffers:

```text id="y6z7a8"
Performance Wins
```

over:

```text id="b9c0d1"
Visual Flourish
```

---

# 7.5.15.28 Mobile Performance

Mobile devices often have:

* weaker CPUs;
* slower networks;
* battery constraints.

Performance decisions should account for this reality.

---

# 7.5.15.29 Low-End Device Support

Performance should remain acceptable on modest hardware.

TWN should not assume flagship devices.

---

# 7.5.15.30 Accessibility and Performance

Performance improvements must not reduce accessibility.

Both are core quality attributes.

Neither should be sacrificed unnecessarily.

---

# 7.5.15.31 Performance Budgets

Frontend development should operate within performance budgets.

Budgets create accountability.

Without budgets, applications naturally grow heavier.

---

# 7.5.15.32 Dependency Governance

Every dependency introduces cost.

Before adding a package, ask:

```text id="e2f3g4"
Is this worth the performance cost?
```

---

# 7.5.15.33 Third-Party Script Discipline

Third-party scripts should be minimized.

Each additional script affects:

* loading;
* execution;
* reliability.

---

# 7.5.15.34 Feature Performance Review

Major features should consider:

* bundle impact;
* interaction impact;
* navigation impact.

Performance becomes part of feature design.

---

# 7.5.15.35 Performance Testing

Performance should be measured.

Not assumed.

Testing should occur throughout development.

---

# 7.5.15.36 Performance Regression Prevention

New features should not silently degrade performance.

Performance regressions should be detected early.

---

# 7.5.15.37 Performance Ownership

Performance belongs to:

```text id="h5i6j7"
Every Frontend Contributor
```

Not a single specialist.

---

# 7.5.15.38 Future Content Types

Future:

```text id="k8l9m0"
Books
Podcasts
Videos
Courses
```

must integrate without compromising overall performance.

Performance considerations should begin before implementation.

---

# 7.5.15.39 Architecture Decision Record

**Decision:** TWN adopts a content-first performance architecture emphasizing server-first rendering, minimal client-side JavaScript, progressive loading, and disciplined dependency management.

**Supporting Decisions**

* Content loads first.
* JavaScript is treated as expensive.
* Server Components preferred.
* Route-based loading.
* Intentional prefetching.
* Progressive enhancement.
* Performance budgets required.
* Dependency scrutiny required.
* Mobile-first performance thinking.
* Performance regressions actively monitored.

**Reasons**

* faster reading experiences;
* lower bandwidth requirements;
* improved accessibility;
* better search performance;
* greater scalability;
* stronger long-term maintainability.

---

## End of 7.5.15

Next:

# **7.5.16 Loading, Error & Empty State Architecture**

This is still strictly frontend architecture and will define how the UI behaves when content is loading, unavailable, missing, partially available, or has encountered failures. After that, only **five sections remain** before 7.5 is complete.

# 7.5.16 Loading, Error & Empty State Architecture

**Status:** Frontend Architecture Specification

**Applies to:** Public website, Editorial CMS, Search, Journeys, Notebook, Library, Community features, Media experiences, and all future TWN frontend surfaces

**Primary objective:** Ensure users always understand what the application is doing, what has happened, and what actions are available next.

---

# Purpose

This section defines how TWN handles states where content is:

* loading;
* unavailable;
* empty;
* incomplete;
* failed;
* restricted;
* recovering.

The goal is simple:

> The interface should never leave users wondering what is happening.

---

# 7.5.16.1 State Communication Philosophy

Every screen exists in one of three broad states:

```text
Loading
Success
Failure
```

Users should always understand which state they are currently experiencing.

Ambiguity is considered a design failure.

---

# 7.5.16.2 Explicit State Principle

The interface should communicate state explicitly.

Avoid:

```text
Blank Screen
```

Avoid:

```text
Silent Failure
```

Avoid:

```text
Frozen Interface
```

Users should receive clear feedback.

---

# 7.5.16.3 Loading Philosophy

Loading is not an exception.

Loading is a normal application state.

Every major interface should define:

```text
Loading Experience
```

before implementation begins.

---

# 7.5.16.4 Perceived Progress

Users tolerate waiting better when they understand:

```text
What is loading
Why it is loading
```

The UI should communicate progress whenever possible.

---

# 7.5.16.5 Skeleton First Strategy

Prefer:

```text
Skeleton UI
```

over:

```text
Global Spinner
```

for content-driven experiences.

Skeletons preserve layout expectations.

---

# 7.5.16.6 Content Shape Preservation

Loading states should resemble final content.

Example:

```text
Article Page
```

should display:

```text
Article Skeleton
```

rather than:

```text
Loading...
```

in the center of an empty page.

---

# 7.5.16.7 Progressive Content Loading

Content should appear progressively when possible.

Users should receive usable information as soon as it becomes available.

Avoid waiting for everything before showing anything.

---

# 7.5.16.8 Route Loading States

Each major route should define a dedicated loading experience.

Examples:

```text
Articles
Notebook
Journeys
Library
Search
```

should not all share identical placeholders.

---

# 7.5.16.9 Search Loading States

Search loading should communicate:

```text
Searching...
```

Users should understand the system is actively working.

---

# 7.5.16.10 Journey Loading States

Journey pages should preserve orientation.

Examples:

```text
Journey Header
Chapter Structure
Progress Context
```

may appear before all content is loaded.

---

# 7.5.16.11 Media Loading States

Media experiences should support:

```text
Loading
Processing
Ready
```

Users should understand the media lifecycle.

---

# 7.5.16.12 Form Loading States

Form submissions should display:

```text
Submitting...
Saving...
Publishing...
Uploading...
```

Avoid generic loading labels where a specific action exists.

---

# 7.5.16.13 Loading Duration Awareness

Long-running operations should communicate ongoing activity.

Examples:

```text
Uploading Media
Generating Preview
Importing Content
```

should provide feedback.

---

# 7.5.16.14 Empty State Philosophy

Empty states are not errors.

An empty state simply means:

```text
No Data Exists
```

for the current context.

---

# 7.5.16.15 Empty States as Guidance

Empty states should help users understand:

```text
What happened
What to do next
```

The interface should provide direction.

---

# 7.5.16.16 First-Time Empty States

Examples:

```text
No Journeys Yet
No Notebook Entries Yet
No Media Uploaded Yet
```

These are onboarding opportunities.

Not failures.

---

# 7.5.16.17 Search Empty States

Example:

```text
No results found
```

Users should be offered recovery options.

Examples:

* adjust filters;
* broaden search;
* clear search terms.

---

# 7.5.16.18 Filter Empty States

Example:

```text
No articles match these filters.
```

Users should understand the cause.

---

# 7.5.16.19 Personal Empty States

Future user areas may contain:

```text
No Bookmarks
No Reading History
No Saved Searches
```

The interface should explain the feature.

---

# 7.5.16.20 Error Philosophy

Errors should be:

```text
Clear
Actionable
Recoverable
```

Whenever possible.

---

# 7.5.16.21 Human Language

Error messages should use human language.

Good:

```text
Unable to save your changes.
Please try again.
```

Bad:

```text
HTTP 500
```

for end users.

---

# 7.5.16.22 Technical Details

Technical details belong in:

```text
Logs
Monitoring
Diagnostics
```

not user-facing error messages.

---

# 7.5.16.23 Error Categories

Frontend errors generally fall into:

```text
Network Errors
Permission Errors
Not Found Errors
Validation Errors
Unexpected Errors
```

Each category requires different presentation.

---

# 7.5.16.24 Network Error States

Example:

```text
Unable to connect.
Please check your connection and try again.
```

Users should understand recovery options.

---

# 7.5.16.25 Permission Error States

Example:

```text
You do not have access to this content.
```

Permission errors should be clear and respectful.

---

# 7.5.16.26 Not Found States

Examples:

```text
Article Not Found
Journey Not Found
Notebook Entry Not Found
```

Users should receive navigation options.

---

# 7.5.16.27 Recovery-Oriented Design

Whenever possible provide:

```text
Retry
Return
Search
Explore
```

Users should never reach dead ends.

---

# 7.5.16.28 Retry Philosophy

Failures should support retrying when appropriate.

Examples:

```text
Search Failure
Upload Failure
Save Failure
```

Recovery should be easy.

---

# 7.5.16.29 Error Boundaries

Frontend should isolate failures.

A failure in one section should not destroy the entire page experience.

---

# 7.5.16.30 Partial Failure Handling

Example:

```text
Related Content Failed
```

The main article should remain readable.

Graceful degradation is preferred.

---

# 7.5.16.31 Global Failure States

Rare catastrophic failures may require full-page error experiences.

Examples:

```text
Application Unavailable
Unexpected Failure
```

These should remain informative.

---

# 7.5.16.32 Offline States

Future offline awareness may include:

```text
Offline
Reconnecting
Connected
```

Users should understand system status.

---

# 7.5.16.33 Autosave Failure States

Editorial workflows should clearly communicate:

```text
Autosave Failed
```

Users must never assume content was saved when it was not.

---

# 7.5.16.34 Draft Recovery States

Long-form writing experiences should support:

```text
Recovery Available
```

when recoverable content exists.

---

# 7.5.16.35 Loading Accessibility

Loading states should remain accessible.

Screen readers should understand:

```text
Loading Content
```

when appropriate.

---

# 7.5.16.36 Error Accessibility

Errors should be announced appropriately.

Users should not be forced to visually search for failures.

---

# 7.5.16.37 Empty State Accessibility

Empty states should remain understandable through assistive technologies.

Meaning should not depend on visuals alone.

---

# 7.5.16.38 Mobile Considerations

Loading, error, and empty states should remain:

```text
Readable
Reachable
Actionable
```

on smaller screens.

---

# 7.5.16.39 Future Content Types

Future:

```text
Books
Podcasts
Videos
Courses
Events
```

must define:

* loading states;
* empty states;
* failure states;

before implementation.

---

# 7.5.16.40 Architecture Decision Record

**Decision:** TWN adopts an explicit-state architecture where loading, empty, and error experiences are treated as first-class interface states rather than implementation afterthoughts.

**Supporting Decisions**

* Skeleton-first loading.
* Human-readable errors.
* Empty states are onboarding opportunities.
* Recovery actions provided whenever possible.
* Partial failures isolated.
* Accessibility required.
* Retry mechanisms supported.
* State-specific experiences encouraged.

**Reasons**

* improves clarity;
* reduces confusion;
* improves perceived performance;
* supports accessibility;
* increases user confidence;
* creates resilient user experiences.

---


# 7.5.17 Frontend Security Responsibilities

**Status:** Frontend Architecture Specification

**Applies to:** Public website, Editorial CMS, Community experiences, Search, Journeys, Notebook, Library, future Books, Podcasts, Courses, Events, and all TWN frontend interfaces

**Primary objective:** Define the security responsibilities of the frontend without duplicating backend, infrastructure, authentication, authorization, or Security Volume specifications.

---

# Purpose

This section defines what the frontend is responsible for protecting.

It establishes:

* trust boundaries;
* input handling;
* output handling;
* session awareness;
* secure UI behaviour;
* secure navigation;
* content rendering safety;
* frontend security ownership.

The goal is simple:

> The frontend should never create security vulnerabilities through careless implementation.

---

# 7.5.17.1 Security Philosophy

Security is not a backend-only concern.

Every frontend decision can affect:

* privacy;
* integrity;
* user trust;
* platform safety.

Frontend security focuses on:

```text id="f1s1"
Safe Presentation
Safe Interaction
Safe Data Handling
```

---

# 7.5.17.2 Frontend Trust Model

The browser is:

```text id="f1s2"
Untrusted
```

Everything running inside a browser should be treated as observable and modifiable.

Frontend code cannot enforce security.

It can only support it.

---

# 7.5.17.3 Security Ownership

The frontend is responsible for:

* secure rendering;
* safe user interactions;
* safe data presentation;
* safe content handling;
* secure navigation patterns.

The frontend is not responsible for:

* authorization enforcement;
* database protection;
* infrastructure security.

Those belong elsewhere.

---

# 7.5.17.4 Validation Is Not Security

Frontend validation improves usability.

It does not provide security.

Example:

```text id="f1s3"
Required fields
Character limits
Formatting rules
```

can all be bypassed.

Security validation must exist elsewhere.

---

# 7.5.17.5 Never Trust User Input

All user-generated content should be treated as potentially unsafe.

Examples:

```text id="f1s4"
Margin Notes
Notebook Entries
Community Submissions
Profile Information
```

Frontend rendering must assume hostile input is possible.

---

# 7.5.17.6 Safe Content Rendering

User content should be rendered safely.

Avoid rendering raw content without proper safeguards.

This is especially important for:

```text id="f1s5"
Rich Text
Embeds
Markdown
Future Community Features
```

---

# 7.5.17.7 HTML Rendering Caution

Direct HTML rendering should be rare.

Whenever HTML is rendered:

```text id="f1s6"
Additional Review
```

should occur.

The safest content is content that cannot execute code.

---

# 7.5.17.8 Content Source Awareness

The frontend should distinguish between:

```text id="f1s7"
Trusted Content
```

and

```text id="f1s8"
User Generated Content
```

These may require different rendering strategies.

---

# 7.5.17.9 URL Safety

Links should be treated carefully.

The frontend should avoid creating unsafe navigation experiences.

Users should understand where links lead.

---

# 7.5.17.10 External Link Behaviour

External destinations should be identifiable.

Readers should not be surprised when leaving TWN.

---

# 7.5.17.11 Session Awareness

The frontend may display:

```text id="f1s9"
Authenticated
Unauthenticated
Expired Session
```

states.

The frontend should communicate session status clearly.

---

# 7.5.17.12 Secure Logout Experience

Logout should:

* be intentional;
* provide clear feedback;
* return the interface to an unauthenticated state.

Users should understand what happened.

---

# 7.5.17.13 Sensitive Information Display

The frontend should avoid exposing information unnecessarily.

Only display information required by the current experience.

---

# 7.5.17.14 Error Message Safety

Error messages should help users.

They should not expose internal implementation details.

Bad:

```text id="f2s0"
Database query failed
```

Good:

```text id="f2s1"
Something went wrong. Please try again.
```

---

# 7.5.17.15 Developer Information Leakage

Frontend experiences should avoid exposing:

* internal architecture;
* implementation details;
* sensitive operational information.

Users only need actionable information.

---

# 7.5.17.16 Permission-Aware UI

The interface should respect permissions.

Example:

```text id="f2s2"
Edit Button
Delete Button
Admin Navigation
```

should only appear when appropriate.

This improves usability.

Actual enforcement belongs elsewhere.

---

# 7.5.17.17 Hidden Is Not Secure

Important principle:

```text id="f2s3"
Hidden ≠ Protected
```

Removing a button does not create security.

It only improves the interface.

---

# 7.5.17.18 Secure Defaults

New frontend features should begin from:

```text id="f2s4"
Least Exposure
```

rather than:

```text id="f2s5"
Expose Everything
```

---

# 7.5.17.19 Upload Safety Awareness

Upload interfaces should clearly communicate:

* accepted formats;
* file requirements;
* upload status.

The frontend should reduce accidental misuse.

---

# 7.5.17.20 Media Preview Safety

Media previews should remain controlled.

Preview functionality should not introduce unnecessary risk.

---

# 7.5.17.21 Clipboard Interactions

Future clipboard functionality should be explicit.

Users should know when content is copied.

---

# 7.5.17.22 Confirmation for Risky Actions

Examples:

```text id="f2s6"
Delete
Archive
Remove
Publish
```

may require confirmation depending on context.

The goal is preventing accidental actions.

---

# 7.5.17.23 Frontend Security and Accessibility

Security measures should not reduce accessibility.

Both are platform requirements.

Neither should be sacrificed unnecessarily.

---

# 7.5.17.24 Search Security Awareness

Search interfaces should safely display:

```text id="f2s7"
Queries
Results
Suggestions
```

without introducing unsafe rendering behaviour.

---

# 7.5.17.25 Journey Security Awareness

Journey content may aggregate:

```text id="f2s8"
Articles
Notebook Entries
Videos
Podcasts
Books
```

Frontend composition should remain safe regardless of content type.

---

# 7.5.17.26 Third-Party Integrations

Third-party integrations introduce risk.

New integrations should be evaluated carefully before frontend adoption.

---

# 7.5.17.27 Dependency Awareness

Every dependency expands the attack surface.

Dependency selection should be intentional.

Avoid unnecessary packages.

---

# 7.5.17.28 Browser Storage Awareness

Browser storage should not become a source of sensitive data exposure.

Store only what is genuinely required.

---

# 7.5.17.29 Security Reviews

Major frontend features should undergo security review during development.

Security should not be postponed until launch.

---

# 7.5.17.30 Architecture Decision Record

**Decision:** TWN adopts a security-aware frontend architecture focused on safe rendering, secure interactions, minimal exposure, and clear trust boundaries.

**Supporting Decisions**

* Browser treated as untrusted.
* Frontend validation is not security.
* User-generated content treated carefully.
* Permission-aware UI.
* Hidden does not mean protected.
* Safe rendering practices required.
* Sensitive information minimized.
* Third-party integrations reviewed.
* Security considered during development.

**Reasons**

* protects users;
* preserves trust;
* reduces avoidable vulnerabilities;
* improves long-term maintainability;
* aligns with TWN's quality standards.

---


# 7.5.18 Frontend Testing Architecture

**Status:** Frontend Architecture Specification

**Applies to:** Public website, Editorial CMS, Community experiences, Search, Journeys, Notebook, Library, Media experiences, and all future frontend systems

**Primary objective:** Ensure the TWN frontend remains reliable, maintainable, accessible, and safe to evolve as the platform grows.

---

# Purpose

This section defines how frontend functionality is verified.

It establishes:

* testing philosophy;
* testing layers;
* ownership;
* test boundaries;
* component testing;
* integration testing;
* end-to-end testing;
* accessibility testing;
* regression prevention.

The goal is simple:

> Every important user experience should be protected from accidental breakage.

---

# 7.5.18.1 Testing Philosophy

Testing exists to create confidence.

The purpose is not:

```text
100% coverage
```

The purpose is:

```text
Confidence in Change
```

Engineers should be able to improve the system without fear.

---

# 7.5.18.2 Testing Pyramid

Frontend testing should prioritize:

```text
Unit Tests
↓
Integration Tests
↓
End-to-End Tests
```

The majority of testing should occur lower in the pyramid.

---

# 7.5.18.3 User-Centric Testing

Tests should reflect:

```text
Real User Behaviour
```

rather than implementation details.

Good:

```text
User can submit newsletter form.
```

Bad:

```text
Component state changed to true.
```

---

# 7.5.18.4 Testing Ownership

Every frontend feature owner is responsible for testing.

Testing is not a separate phase.

Testing is part of implementation.

---

# 7.5.18.5 What Must Be Tested

Critical experiences include:

```text
Navigation
Reading
Search
Forms
Authentication Flows
Journey Navigation
CMS Workflows
Media Uploads
```

These experiences require protection.

---

# 7.5.18.6 Unit Testing Philosophy

Unit tests verify:

```text
Individual Behaviour
```

Examples:

* utility functions;
* formatting logic;
* validation logic;
* state transformations.

---

# 7.5.18.7 Component Testing Philosophy

Shared UI components should be tested independently.

Examples:

```text
Button
Input
Modal
Dialog
Dropdown
Tabs
```

The goal is confidence in reuse.

---

# 7.5.18.8 Design System Protection

Design-system components are used everywhere.

A regression in one component may affect the entire platform.

These components deserve strong test coverage.

---

# 7.5.18.9 Form Testing

Forms should verify:

* validation;
* submission;
* pending states;
* error states;
* recovery states.

Forms are among the highest-risk UI surfaces.

---

# 7.5.18.10 Journey Testing

Journey functionality should verify:

* navigation;
* chapter transitions;
* progress tracking;
* related content linking.

Journeys are a core TWN experience.

---

# 7.5.18.11 Search Testing

Search should verify:

* query handling;
* filter behaviour;
* empty states;
* result presentation.

Search is a primary discovery mechanism.

---

# 7.5.18.12 Integration Testing Philosophy

Integration tests verify:

```text
Multiple Components Working Together
```

These tests provide higher confidence than isolated unit tests.

---

# 7.5.18.13 Route Integration Testing

Examples:

```text
Article Page
Journey Page
Notebook Page
```

should verify complete user flows.

---

# 7.5.18.14 State Integration Testing

Important state transitions should be tested.

Examples:

```text
Loading
Success
Failure
```

State bugs often emerge during integration.

---

# 7.5.18.15 End-to-End Philosophy

End-to-end testing verifies:

```text
Entire User Journeys
```

from beginning to completion.

---

# 7.5.18.16 Critical E2E Flows

Examples:

```text
Read Article
Search Content
Subscribe Newsletter
Create Article
Publish Journey
Upload Media
```

These workflows deserve full-path verification.

---

# 7.5.18.17 Smoke Tests

A small suite of smoke tests should verify:

```text
Core Platform Availability
```

after deployments.

---

# 7.5.18.18 Accessibility Testing

Accessibility testing is mandatory.

It should not depend solely on manual review.

---

# 7.5.18.19 Accessibility Coverage

Accessibility testing should verify:

* keyboard navigation;
* focus behaviour;
* semantic structure;
* form accessibility;
* screen-reader compatibility indicators.

---

# 7.5.18.20 Responsive Testing

Frontend testing should cover:

```text
Mobile
Tablet
Desktop
```

A feature working on desktop alone is insufficient.

---

# 7.5.18.21 Visual Regression Testing

Visual regressions can damage:

* readability;
* accessibility;
* trust.

Shared UI should be protected from unintended visual changes.

---

# 7.5.18.22 Loading State Testing

Loading experiences should be verified.

Examples:

```text
Skeletons
Pending States
Progress Indicators
```

must not silently disappear.

---

# 7.5.18.23 Error State Testing

Error experiences require testing.

Users often encounter failures.

Failure paths are product experiences too.

---

# 7.5.18.24 Empty State Testing

Empty states should be verified.

Examples:

```text
No Results
No Journeys
No Media
```

These states are common.

---

# 7.5.18.25 CMS Testing

Editorial workflows deserve strong testing.

Examples:

```text
Draft Creation
Editing
Publishing
Journey Management
```

These are high-value workflows.

---

# 7.5.18.26 Performance Verification

Major frontend features should be evaluated for performance impact.

Performance regressions should be detected early.

---

# 7.5.18.27 Regression Philosophy

Every bug fixed should be difficult to reintroduce.

Whenever practical:

```text
Bug Fix
↓
Regression Test
```

---

# 7.5.18.28 Test Reliability

Unstable tests reduce trust.

Tests should be:

```text
Reliable
Predictable
Maintainable
```

---

# 7.5.18.29 Test Maintainability

Tests are production assets.

Poorly maintained tests become technical debt.

---

# 7.5.18.30 Testing Documentation

Important testing patterns should be documented.

Future contributors should understand:

```text
What to Test
How to Test
Why It Matters
```

---

# 7.5.18.31 Architecture Decision Record

**Decision:** TWN adopts a layered frontend testing strategy combining unit, integration, accessibility, responsive, and end-to-end testing to protect critical user experiences.

**Supporting Decisions**

* User-centric testing.
* Strong design-system coverage.
* Accessibility testing required.
* Responsive testing required.
* Critical journeys protected by E2E tests.
* Regression prevention integrated into development.
* Testing responsibility shared by contributors.

**Reasons**

* increases confidence;
* reduces regressions;
* improves maintainability;
* protects user experience;
* supports long-term growth.

---
# 7.5.19 Frontend Observability Architecture

**Status:** Frontend Architecture Specification

**Applies to:** Public website, Editorial CMS, Community experiences, Search, Journeys, Notebook, Library, Media experiences, and all future TWN frontend systems

**Primary objective:** Ensure engineers can understand how the frontend behaves in production, detect issues early, diagnose failures quickly, and continuously improve user experience.

---

# Purpose

This section defines how the frontend communicates operational information about itself.

It establishes:

* frontend observability philosophy;
* error visibility;
* client-side diagnostics;
* user experience monitoring;
* performance visibility;
* production debugging;
* observability ownership.

The goal is simple:

> Problems should be discoverable before users start reporting them.

---

# 7.5.19.1 Observability Philosophy

Observability exists to answer:

```text id="obs1"
What happened?
Why did it happen?
Who was affected?
Can we reproduce it?
```

Without observability:

```text id="obs2"
Failures become guesses.
```

---

# 7.5.19.2 Relationship to Other Chapters

This section covers:

```text id="obs3"
Frontend Observability
```

It does not define:

```text id="obs4"
Backend Monitoring
Infrastructure Monitoring
Logging Systems
Incident Response
```

Those belong to later sections of Volume 7.

---

# 7.5.19.3 User Experience Visibility

The most important frontend signals are:

```text id="obs5"
Can users load pages?
Can users navigate?
Can users search?
Can users submit forms?
Can users complete journeys?
```

Observability should prioritize user outcomes.

---

# 7.5.19.4 Production Reality

Testing environments are useful.

Production behaviour matters more.

Real users encounter:

* different devices;
* different browsers;
* different networks;
* different content.

Observability bridges this gap.

---

# 7.5.19.5 Error Visibility

Frontend errors should not disappear silently.

Unexpected failures should become visible to engineering teams.

---

# 7.5.19.6 Error Categories

Examples:

```text id="obs6"
Rendering Errors
Navigation Errors
Form Errors
Search Errors
Upload Errors
Unexpected Exceptions
```

These should be distinguishable.

---

# 7.5.19.7 Client-Side Error Collection

Unexpected frontend failures should be captured.

The objective is diagnosis.

Not surveillance.

---

# 7.5.19.8 Error Context

Useful error reports include context such as:

```text id="obs7"
Route
Feature Area
Device Type
Application Version
```

Enough information to investigate.

Not excessive personal information.

---

# 7.5.19.9 User Privacy

Observability must respect privacy.

Avoid collecting unnecessary:

* personal content;
* private drafts;
* sensitive information.

Observability should be proportional.

---

# 7.5.19.10 Route Visibility

Engineering teams should understand:

```text id="obs8"
Which routes are used
Which routes fail
Which routes perform poorly
```

This supports prioritization.

---

# 7.5.19.11 Navigation Monitoring

Frontend navigation should remain observable.

Examples:

```text id="obs9"
Failed Route Transition
Broken Navigation Flow
Unexpected Redirect
```

These impact usability significantly.

---

# 7.5.19.12 Form Observability

Critical forms should expose operational visibility.

Examples:

```text id="obs10"
Newsletter Signup
Article Publishing
Journey Creation
Media Upload
```

Engineers should know when workflows break.

---

# 7.5.19.13 Search Observability

Search is a core TWN capability.

Observability should help answer:

```text id="obs11"
Are searches succeeding?
Are searches failing?
Are users finding content?
```

---

# 7.5.19.14 Journey Observability

Journeys are a first-class TWN experience.

Visibility should help answer:

```text id="obs12"
Can readers move through journeys successfully?
```

---

# 7.5.19.15 Performance Visibility

Performance should be measured.

Not assumed.

Key areas include:

```text id="obs13"
Page Load
Navigation
Interaction Responsiveness
Rendering
```

---

# 7.5.19.16 Real User Experience

Production metrics should reflect:

```text id="obs14"
Actual User Experience
```

rather than only laboratory environments.

---

# 7.5.19.17 Deployment Awareness

Observability should help correlate:

```text id="obs15"
Deployment
↓
Issue
```

Understanding change impact is essential.

---

# 7.5.19.18 Feature Visibility

Major frontend features should expose enough signals to determine:

```text id="obs16"
Healthy
Degraded
Broken
```

---

# 7.5.19.19 Accessibility Observability

Accessibility regressions are often invisible.

Frontend observability should support identifying accessibility-related issues when possible.

---

# 7.5.19.20 CMS Visibility

Editorial workflows deserve observability too.

Publishing failures should be discoverable quickly.

---

# 7.5.19.21 Upload Visibility

Media workflows should expose:

```text id="obs17"
Upload Failures
Processing Failures
Completion Rates
```

---

# 7.5.19.22 Alert Philosophy

Not every event deserves an alert.

Excessive alerts create noise.

Observability should prioritize meaningful signals.

---

# 7.5.19.23 Actionable Signals

Useful signals answer:

```text id="obs18"
What should we do next?
```

Observability without actionability has limited value.

---

# 7.5.19.24 Frontend Ownership

Frontend teams own frontend observability.

Operational visibility should be considered part of feature delivery.

---

# 7.5.19.25 Architecture Decision Record

**Decision:** TWN adopts a user-centric frontend observability strategy focused on production visibility, actionable diagnostics, privacy-conscious monitoring, and continuous improvement.

**Supporting Decisions**

* Error visibility required.
* Performance visibility required.
* Privacy-conscious collection.
* Route-level visibility.
* Search visibility.
* Journey visibility.
* CMS visibility.
* Deployment awareness.
* User experience prioritization.

**Reasons**

* faster diagnosis;
* improved reliability;
* better user experience;
* reduced operational uncertainty;
* safer platform evolution.

---

## End of 7.5.19

---

# 7.5.20 Future Frontend Evolution Strategy

**Status:** Frontend Architecture Specification

**Applies to:** All current and future TWN frontend experiences

**Primary objective:** Ensure the frontend architecture remains adaptable over the next decade without requiring large-scale rewrites.

---

# Purpose

This section defines how the frontend should evolve over time.

The goal is simple:

> TWN should evolve through controlled improvement, not periodic reinvention.

---

# 7.5.20.1 Evolution Philosophy

The frontend should evolve incrementally.

Avoid:

```text id="evo1"
Rewrite Everything
```

Prefer:

```text id="evo2"
Improve Continuously
```

---

# 7.5.20.2 Stability Over Novelty

New technologies should not be adopted simply because they are popular.

Adoption should be driven by:

* proven value;
* measurable improvement;
* strategic necessity.

---

# 7.5.20.3 Architecture Longevity

Architectural decisions should favor:

```text id="evo3"
10-Year Maintainability
```

over:

```text id="evo4"
6-Month Excitement
```

---

# 7.5.20.4 Design System Growth

The design system will expand.

Frontend architecture should accommodate:

```text id="evo5"
New Components
New Tokens
New Patterns
```

without structural disruption.

---

# 7.5.20.5 Content-Type Expansion

Future content types may include:

```text id="evo6"
Books
Podcasts
Videos
Courses
Events
```

The frontend should integrate these as new domains rather than forcing them into article structures.

---

# 7.5.20.6 Journey Expansion

Journeys should remain content-type agnostic.

Future Journey chapters may contain:

```text id="evo7"
Article
Notebook Entry
Podcast
Video
Book Chapter
Course Module
```

without architectural changes.

---

# 7.5.20.7 Accessibility Evolution

Accessibility standards will evolve.

TWN should continuously improve accessibility rather than treating compliance as complete.

---

# 7.5.20.8 Performance Evolution

Performance budgets should be revisited periodically.

Growth should not automatically justify slower experiences.

---

# 7.5.20.9 Mobile Evolution

Mobile usage patterns will change.

Frontend architecture should remain adaptable to future device categories.

---

# 7.5.20.10 Dependency Governance

Dependencies should be reviewed regularly.

Unused or problematic dependencies should be removed.

---

# 7.5.20.11 Frontend Simplification

As the platform matures:

```text id="evo8"
Simplification
```

should be considered a feature.

Reducing complexity improves sustainability.

---

# 7.5.20.12 Future Team Growth

The architecture should remain understandable by:

* future contributors;
* contractors;
* maintainers;
* full-time teams.

Knowledge should not remain founder-dependent.

---

# 7.5.20.13 Architecture Decision Preservation

Major frontend decisions should be documented.

Future teams should understand:

```text id="evo9"
Why
```

not just:

```text id="evo10"
What
```

was implemented.

---

# 7.5.20.14 Architecture Decision Record

**Decision:** TWN adopts an evolutionary frontend strategy prioritizing stability, maintainability, extensibility, and gradual improvement.

**Supporting Decisions**

* Incremental evolution.
* Stable foundations.
* Content-type expansion support.
* Journey-first extensibility.
* Accessibility improvement.
* Performance accountability.
* Dependency governance.
* Decision preservation.

**Reasons**

* reduces risk;
* protects long-term investment;
* supports future growth;
* minimizes rewrites.

---


# 7.5.21 Frontend Architecture Decision Record, Governance & Completion Criteria

**Status:** Final Frontend Architecture Specification
**Parent chapter:** Volume 7 — Technical Architecture
**Current scope:** Section 7.5 — Frontend Architecture
**Applies to:** TWN public website, Editorial CMS, Notebook, Journeys, Library, Search, Community interfaces, Media interfaces, reader accounts, and future Books, Podcasts, Videos, Courses, Events, and other browser-facing experiences
**Primary objective:** Consolidate the decisions made throughout Section 7.5, define how those decisions are governed, identify what remains outside Frontend Architecture, and establish objective criteria for declaring the frontend architecture complete

---

# Purpose

This final subsection closes **7.5 Frontend Architecture**.

It does not introduce another large frontend subsystem.

Instead, it records:

* the final frontend architecture;
* the decisions that govern implementation;
* the boundaries that protect Frontend Architecture from absorbing Backend, API, Database, Security, or Design-System responsibilities;
* the rules for changing architectural decisions;
* the required implementation evidence;
* the risks that remain;
* the conditions under which Section 7.5 may be considered complete.

The purpose is to ensure that TWN does not merely have many frontend documents.

It must have one coherent frontend architecture.

---

# 7.5.21.1 Final Frontend Architecture Position

TWN adopts a:

```text
Server-first
Content-first
Domain-oriented
Progressively enhanced
Accessible
Responsive
Performance-conscious
Frontend architecture
```

The system uses Next.js App Router and React as its frontend framework foundation.

In the App Router, layouts and pages are Server Components by default. Client Components are added where browser interactivity or browser APIs are required. Next.js also supports server-side data access, streaming, nested layouts, loading states, and error handling through this architecture. ([Next.js][1])

The frontend must remain centered on TWN’s primary purpose:

```text
Preserve and present thoughtful knowledge.
```

It must not gradually become:

```text
A large browser application
that happens to display articles.
```

---

# 7.5.21.2 Architecture Summary

The frontend architecture can be summarized as:

```text
TWN Frontend
│
├── Next.js App Router
│   ├── Route Groups
│   ├── Layouts
│   ├── Pages
│   ├── Loading Boundaries
│   ├── Error Boundaries
│   └── Metadata
│
├── Server Component Layer
│   ├── Public Content Composition
│   ├── Private Route Composition
│   ├── Query Invocation
│   ├── View-Model Mapping
│   └── Safe Data Transfer
│
├── Client Interaction Layer
│   ├── Forms
│   ├── Editors
│   ├── Search Interaction
│   ├── Media Controls
│   ├── Local Recovery
│   └── Browser-Specific Behaviour
│
├── Domain Frontend Modules
│   ├── Editorial
│   ├── Notebook
│   ├── Journeys
│   ├── Community
│   ├── Search
│   ├── Media
│   ├── Newsletter
│   └── Identity
│
├── Design-System Integration
│   ├── Tokens
│   ├── Primitives
│   ├── Shared Components
│   └── Feature Components
│
└── Frontend Quality Controls
    ├── Accessibility
    ├── Performance
    ├── Testing
    ├── Error Handling
    ├── Security Responsibilities
    └── Observability
```

---

# 7.5.21.3 Core Architecture Decisions

The following decisions are binding unless replaced through a documented Architecture Decision Record.

---

## Decision 1 — Next.js App Router is the frontend framework

TWN uses the App Router for:

* file-system routing;
* nested layouts;
* Server Components;
* Client Component boundaries;
* loading and error boundaries;
* route metadata;
* streaming;
* Server Functions;
* Route Handlers where genuine HTTP boundaries are required.

The Pages Router should not be introduced for new TWN functionality.

A legacy Pages Router route may exist only during an intentional migration.

---

## Decision 2 — React Server Components are the default

Server Components are the default rendering and composition mechanism.

React Server Components execute in a server environment and may be rendered at build time or per request according to the framework’s rendering strategy. ([React][2])

Use Server Components for:

* published Article pages;
* Notebook pages;
* Journey pages;
* Author and Category pages;
* public archives;
* page metadata;
* CMS page shells;
* initial private data retrieval;
* permission-aware navigation;
* server-derived view models.

A component must not become a Client Component merely because it displays dynamic data.

---

## Decision 3 — Client Components are narrow interaction islands

A Client Component is justified when the feature requires:

* browser events;
* mutable browser state;
* browser APIs;
* local recovery;
* drag-and-drop;
* interactive media;
* client animation;
* editor interactions;
* live upload progress;
* keyboard shortcuts.

The `"use client"` directive should be placed as low in the component tree as practical.

The goal is:

```text
Large server-rendered experience
+
Small interactive browser islands
```

not:

```text
Entire application hydrated by default
```

---

## Decision 4 — Public content remains readable without extensive client execution

Canonical public content should not depend on client-side fetching before it becomes readable.

Articles, Notebook Entries, Journeys, Author pages, and institutional pages should arrive as meaningful server-rendered HTML.

Client JavaScript may enhance them.

It must not be required merely to reveal their primary content.

---

## Decision 5 — Rendering strategy is route-specific

TWN does not use one rendering strategy for every route.

The route classification remains:

| Route class                           | Rendering position                                     |
| ------------------------------------- | ------------------------------------------------------ |
| Stable public institutional content   | Static rendering                                       |
| Published editorial content           | Prerendered or cached server rendering                 |
| Query-specific discovery              | Dynamic server rendering                               |
| Authenticated CMS and admin           | Dynamic private rendering                              |
| Public content with personal features | Cached public content with isolated dynamic boundaries |

Caching details remain governed by the chosen, pinned Next.js version. Current Next.js documentation distinguishes newer Cache Components APIs from the previous App Router caching model, so TWN must not combine incompatible examples from different framework generations. ([Next.js][3])

---

## Decision 6 — Published content renders from published state

The public frontend must never infer publication from the latest editable data.

At the frontend level, the public query contract must return:

```text
The currently public representation
```

not:

```text
The most recently edited representation
```

The detailed revision and database mechanism belongs to later technical chapters and Volume 8.

The frontend responsibility is to consume a clearly public query contract.

---

## Decision 7 — Frontend routes do not query database infrastructure directly

Route files and ordinary presentation components should consume:

```text
Frontend Query
or
Application Query
```

They should not contain:

* SQL;
* database client calls;
* repository implementation details;
* service-role credentials;
* provider-specific persistence logic.

The expected direction is:

```text
Route
    ↓
Frontend Query
    ↓
Application Query
    ↓
Infrastructure
```

The detailed Backend and Database architecture is deferred to Sections 7.6 and 7.8.

---

## Decision 8 — Server Components do not call internal HTTP APIs unnecessarily

A Server Component running inside the same application should call an application or query service directly.

It should not call:

```text
/api/articles/...
```

solely to retrieve data from its own application.

HTTP Route Handlers are reserved for actual HTTP boundaries such as:

* external clients;
* webhooks;
* public feeds;
* browser-specific endpoints where a Server Action is unsuitable.

The complete HTTP design belongs to Section 7.7 and Volume 9.

---

## Decision 9 — Frontend data is expressed through view models

Frontend components should not receive unrestricted database rows or oversized domain objects.

Use interface-specific models such as:

```text
ArticleCardView
ArticlePageView
ArticleEditorView
JourneyPageView
SearchResultView
NotebookHeroView
```

Each model should include only what the interface needs.

This protects:

* frontend clarity;
* payload size;
* privacy;
* serialization boundaries;
* future domain changes.

---

## Decision 10 — TypeScript is mandatory

TWN frontend code uses TypeScript with strict checking.

The TypeScript `strict` option enables a collection of stronger type-checking behaviours designed to provide greater correctness guarantees. ([typescriptlang.org][4])

TypeScript should cover:

* component props;
* frontend view models;
* route parameters;
* state models;
* form states;
* action-result contracts;
* domain-facing interfaces.

TypeScript does not replace runtime input validation.

---

## Decision 11 — State remains minimal and locally owned

State ownership follows this priority:

```text
Derived value
    ↓
Do not store

Navigation or search state
    ↓
URL

Temporary interaction state
    ↓
Local component

Form state
    ↓
Form or editor boundary

Shared presentation preference
    ↓
Focused Context

Global browser store
    ↓
Only with demonstrated need
```

TWN does not begin with Redux, Zustand, or another universal state store.

A global store may later be introduced for a clearly defined complex browser workspace.

It must not become a second copy of server data.

---

## Decision 12 — Forms use native semantics and progressive enhancement

Forms begin with:

```html
<form>
<label>
<input>
<button>
```

and use server-backed submission mechanisms.

Next.js supports forms using React Server Functions, which execute on the server and can be invoked from Server or Client Components. ([Next.js][5])

The frontend owns:

* field presentation;
* pending states;
* accessible errors;
* preserving input;
* success feedback;
* unsaved-change warnings;
* upload progress;
* conflict presentation.

Backend commands, transactions, and persistence rules belong to later sections.

---

## Decision 13 — Validation is layered

Within Frontend Architecture:

* HTML attributes provide immediate feedback;
* client validation may improve usability;
* frontend schemas validate submitted shape;
* all authoritative decisions remain server-controlled.

The frontend must not imply that client validation secures an operation.

Full domain, API, database, and security validation is specified later.

---

## Decision 14 — Tailwind CSS implements the visual system

TWN uses Tailwind CSS 4 and semantic CSS variables to implement the Design System.

Tailwind supports responsive utility variants and theme-level custom breakpoint variables. ([Tailwind CSS][6])

The frontend consumes Volume 4.

It must not redefine it.

Feature code should prefer:

```text
Semantic tokens
Design-system components
Approved variants
```

over:

```text
Random colours
Arbitrary spacing
Page-specific button styles
```

---

## Decision 15 — The Design System remains the visual authority

Volume 4 owns:

* colour system;
* typography scale;
* spacing;
* radius;
* elevation;
* motion tokens;
* component specifications;
* accessibility behaviours.

Section 7.5 owns how those definitions are implemented and consumed.

A page may not create a new visual primitive merely because the existing Design System lacks one.

The correct process is:

```text
Identify missing pattern
    ↓
Review against Volume 4
    ↓
Add or extend design-system component
    ↓
Consume it in the feature
```

---

## Decision 16 — Responsive design is content-first and mobile-first

TWN builds from the smallest supported viewport upward.

Responsive behaviour should use shared breakpoint and container rules, not device-specific assumptions.

The same capability should remain available across supported device classes, though its presentation may change.

Examples:

```text
Desktop filter sidebar
    ↓
Mobile filter drawer
```

```text
Desktop CMS multi-panel layout
    ↓
Mobile sequential panels
```

The frontend should adapt layout rather than create separate mobile and desktop products.

---

## Decision 17 — Accessibility is an implementation requirement

Every frontend feature must support:

* semantic HTML;
* keyboard operation;
* visible focus;
* heading structure;
* landmarks;
* accessible names;
* labelled forms;
* error associations;
* reduced-motion preferences;
* screen-reader-compatible state communication.

Automated testing helps identify issues but does not replace manual keyboard and assistive-technology review.

Accessibility remains governed jointly by:

* Volume 3 — UX Bible;
* Volume 4 — Design System;
* Section 7.5 — implementation architecture.

---

## Decision 18 — Performance is controlled primarily through architecture

Frontend performance should result from:

* Server Components;
* narrow Client Components;
* route-level code splitting;
* minimal dependencies;
* responsive media;
* controlled font loading;
* stable layouts;
* scoped streaming;
* intentional prefetching;
* small browser payloads.

Performance should not rely on hiding slow architecture behind spinners.

---

## Decision 19 — Loading, empty, error, and recovery states are first-class

No important feature is complete if only its successful state is implemented.

Every major interface must define:

```text
Idle
Loading
Success
Empty
Validation Failure
Permission Failure
Dependency Failure
Unexpected Failure
Recovery
```

where applicable.

React Suspense can coordinate loading fallbacks for supported server and client rendering patterns, but boundaries must correspond to meaningful product sections rather than every small component. ([React][7])

---

## Decision 20 — Frontend security is defensive, not authoritative

The browser is untrusted.

The frontend is responsible for:

* safe output rendering;
* avoiding secret exposure;
* safe external links;
* controlled browser storage;
* permission-aware presentation;
* secure third-party script usage;
* preventing accidental destructive actions.

The frontend does not enforce:

* authorization;
* database integrity;
* authentication validity;
* secure storage;
* role permissions.

Those responsibilities belong to Sections 7.9, 7.10, 7.26 and Volume 10.

---

## Decision 21 — Frontend testing is user-oriented

Tests should focus on behaviour visible to users and editors.

Required layers include:

* utility and state tests;
* component tests;
* feature integration tests;
* accessibility checks;
* responsive checks;
* end-to-end workflow tests;
* visual regression where valuable.

Implementation details should not become the primary test contract.

---

## Decision 22 — Frontend observability measures user outcomes

Frontend visibility should answer:

* Are routes rendering?
* Can users navigate?
* Can readers find content?
* Can editors save?
* Can editors publish from the interface?
* Are uploads completing?
* Are client errors increasing?
* Did a deployment introduce regressions?
* Which browsers or devices are affected?

Observability must not capture private draft content or unnecessary personal information.

The complete operational monitoring system belongs to later Volume 7 chapters.

---

## Decision 23 — Frontend evolution is incremental

TWN should not periodically rewrite its entire frontend merely to follow framework trends.

Change should occur through:

* small migrations;
* feature-owned improvements;
* explicit deprecation;
* dependency review;
* measurable performance work;
* documented architectural decisions.

New content types should become new domain modules while sharing stable frontend foundations.

---

# 7.5.21.4 Final Rendering Model

The frontend rendering model is:

```text
Public and stable
    ↓
Static or reusable cached server rendering

Public and editorially updated
    ↓
Reusable server rendering with explicit freshness handling

Query-dependent public page
    ↓
Dynamic server rendering

Private or permission-dependent
    ↓
Dynamic private server rendering

Browser interaction required
    ↓
Small Client Component

Slow independent secondary content
    ↓
Meaningful streaming boundary
```

The specific cache APIs are implementation-version dependent.

The architectural intent is stable even when the framework API changes.

---

# 7.5.21.5 Final State Ownership Model

| State                  | Authoritative owner                                           |
| ---------------------- | ------------------------------------------------------------- |
| Published content      | Server                                                        |
| Draft persistence      | Server                                                        |
| User permissions       | Server                                                        |
| Search query           | URL                                                           |
| Pagination and filters | URL                                                           |
| Open dialog            | Local component                                               |
| Editor cursor          | Editor Client Component                                       |
| Unsaved editor text    | Editor boundary                                               |
| Local recovery copy    | Browser recovery layer                                        |
| Theme preference       | Cookie or browser preference layer                            |
| Bookmark state         | Server, with optional optimistic UI                           |
| Journey progress       | Server for authenticated readers; temporary client projection |
| Loading state          | Relevant interaction boundary                                 |
| Form errors            | Form state                                                    |
| Route error            | Route or localized error boundary                             |

No state should have several competing authorities.

---

# 7.5.21.6 Final Module Dependency Rules

The allowed frontend dependency direction is:

```text
app/
    ↓
domain frontend modules
    ↓
shared design-system components
    ↓
frontend infrastructure adapters
```

Application and infrastructure dependencies may be invoked through defined server-only contracts.

Forbidden dependencies include:

```text
Design system
    ↓
Article domain
```

```text
Journey module
    ↓
Concrete Article database repository
```

```text
Infrastructure adapter
    ↓
Page component
```

```text
Shared Button
    ↓
CMS business workflow
```

```text
Public Article component
    ↓
Admin role-management component
```

Cross-domain presentation should use narrow contracts.

---

# 7.5.21.7 Public and Editorial Surface Separation

TWN contains two different interface categories.

## Public surface

Priorities:

* readability;
* navigation;
* accessibility;
* search discoverability;
* low browser cost;
* narrative continuity;
* calm presentation.

## Editorial surface

Priorities:

* system-state visibility;
* safe mutation;
* precise feedback;
* conflict awareness;
* draft recovery;
* workflow efficiency;
* permission-aware actions.

They share:

* framework;
* Design System;
* domain language;
* accessibility rules;
* frontend infrastructure.

They should not be forced into identical layouts or interaction density.

---

# 7.5.21.8 Frontend Content-Type Model

Frontend architecture must support multiple content types without treating all of them as Articles.

Current first-class frontend content types include:

```text
Article
Notebook Entry
Journey
Journey Chapter
Shared Page
Margin Note
```

Future first-class types may include:

```text
Book
Book Chapter
Podcast
Podcast Episode
Video Essay
Course
Course Module
Event
Collection
Download
```

Shared interfaces may exist where content shares presentation needs.

Examples:

```text
ContentCard
MediaCover
PublicationMeta
AuthorSummary
JourneyContext
```

However, a generic abstraction must not erase meaningful differences.

A Podcast player is not an Article body.

A Book Chapter is not automatically a Notebook Entry.

---

# 7.5.21.9 Journey Integration Decision

Journeys are first-class navigation and presentation structures.

Frontend architecture must expose Journey context through:

* Journey landing pages;
* Chapter navigation;
* content membership indicators;
* previous and next navigation;
* cross-content progression;
* Journey cards;
* Journey search results;
* future reader progress.

Journeys may contain different content types.

The frontend follows the curated narrative order rather than grouping everything only by content format.

---

# 7.5.21.10 Frontend Non-Goals

Section 7.5 does not authorize the following for the initial architecture:

* micro-frontends;
* separate public and CMS repositories;
* universal global state;
* browser-only content rendering;
* unrestricted MDX components;
* client-side privileged database access;
* offline-first synchronization;
* real-time collaborative editing;
* separate mobile application;
* route-specific visual systems;
* multiple component libraries;
* multiple competing form libraries;
* a frontend GraphQL layer without actual need;
* automatic AI-generated UI;
* custom rendering framework;
* business rules embedded across React components.

These may only be reconsidered through documented evidence and architecture review.

---

# 7.5.21.11 Decisions Deferred to Later Volume 7 Chapters

To prevent context drift, the following are explicitly outside Section 7.5.

## Section 7.6 — Backend Architecture

Will define:

* application services;
* domain services;
* command handlers;
* repositories;
* transaction boundaries;
* service abstractions;
* backend module organization.

## Section 7.7 — API Architecture

Will define:

* Route Handler contracts;
* external APIs;
* webhooks;
* status codes;
* request and response conventions;
* versioning;
* rate limiting at the API boundary.

## Section 7.8 — Database Architecture

Will define:

* high-level database strategy;
* relational ownership;
* transaction use;
* database access approach;
* database boundaries.

Detailed table-by-table specifications remain primarily in Volume 8.

## Sections 7.9 and 7.10

Will define:

* authentication;
* identity resolution;
* authorization;
* roles;
* permissions;
* resource access.

## Section 7.11 — CMS Architecture

Will define:

* editorial workflows;
* revisions;
* autosave rules;
* publishing;
* scheduling;
* editorial review;
* content lifecycle.

## Section 7.12 — Search Architecture

Will define:

* search indexing;
* ranking;
* typo tolerance;
* semantic retrieval;
* search freshness.

## Section 7.13 — Caching Strategy

Will define:

* caching ownership;
* cache keys;
* tags;
* invalidation;
* freshness;
* CDN relationships.

## Later chapters

Will define:

* storage;
* Media processing;
* email;
* queues;
* background jobs;
* Analytics;
* monitoring;
* observability infrastructure;
* logging;
* security;
* backups;
* deployment;
* CI/CD;
* full testing strategy.

Section 7.5 should reference these systems only through frontend contracts.

---

# 7.5.21.12 Frontend Governance Model

Frontend architecture requires governance because frameworks and dependencies evolve.

Governance is not intended to slow development.

It prevents accidental architectural drift.

---

## Local implementation decisions

A developer may make local decisions without an ADR when they:

* remain inside an existing component;
* use approved patterns;
* do not create new global dependencies;
* do not change module boundaries;
* do not alter route architecture;
* do not change state ownership;
* do not alter public URLs;
* do not increase major client-side scope.

Examples:

* extracting a small helper;
* adjusting an existing component variant;
* adding an approved field to a form;
* improving an existing loading skeleton.

---

## Architecture review required

Review is required when a change proposes:

* a new global state library;
* a new component system;
* a new form framework;
* a new animation framework;
* a large third-party script;
* converting a public route to client rendering;
* making a cached public route dynamic;
* introducing a new browser persistence layer;
* changing primary URL structure;
* adding an unrestricted content renderer;
* adding a new frontend domain;
* bypassing established query boundaries;
* changing Server–Client ownership;
* a significant framework upgrade.

---

## Architecture Decision Record required

A formal ADR is required for decisions that are:

* difficult to reverse;
* platform-wide;
* security-sensitive;
* high-cost;
* likely to affect several domains;
* likely to remain for years.

---

# 7.5.21.13 Frontend ADR Template

```text
Decision ID

Title

Status
Proposed
Accepted
Superseded
Deprecated
Rejected

Date

Context

Problem

Decision

Alternatives Considered

Reasons

Consequences

Risks

Migration Requirements

Rollback Strategy

Affected Domains

Affected Routes

Performance Impact

Accessibility Impact

Security Impact

Testing Requirements

Approved By

Related Decisions
```

The ADR should explain reasoning.

It should not merely record the selected library.

---

# 7.5.21.14 Architecture Conformance Rules

Frontend implementation should be reviewed against the following rules.

## Server and client boundaries

* Is the component a Server Component by default?
* Does the Client Component require browser execution?
