* Is `"use client"` placed low enough?
* Has unnecessary hydration been introduced?

## Data access

* Does the route use an approved query contract?
* Is internal self-fetching avoided?
* Is the view model minimal?
* Is sensitive data excluded?

## State

* Is the state derived, URL-owned, local, form-owned, or server-owned?
* Is the same value duplicated in several stores?
* Does browser storage contain anything sensitive?

## Design System

* Are approved tokens and components used?
* Were arbitrary visual rules introduced?
* Is the component accessible by default?

## Performance

* Does the route send unnecessary JavaScript?
* Was a large dependency added?
* Does an optional feature block primary content?
* Does the layout remain stable?

## Accessibility

* Can the feature be used by keyboard?
* Are labels and accessible names present?
* Is focus managed?
* Is state announced appropriately?

## Resilience

* Are loading, empty, error, and recovery states defined?
* Does optional failure remain localized?
* Is user work preserved?

---

# 7.5.21.15 Architecture Enforcement

Where practical, frontend rules should be enforced automatically.

Possible mechanisms include:

* TypeScript strict mode;
* ESLint import-boundary rules;
* restricted-import rules;
* server-only markers;
* client-only markers;
* dependency checks;
* bundle analysis;
* accessibility linting;
* automated tests;
* route smoke tests;
* CI build checks.

Documentation without enforcement will gradually drift.

Not every architectural rule can be automated.

Code review remains necessary.

---

# 7.5.21.16 Required Frontend Documentation

The frontend codebase should maintain:

```text
README
Architecture overview
Directory map
Route map
State ownership guide
Server–Client boundary guide
Design-system usage guide
Testing guide
Accessibility checklist
Performance checklist
ADR index
```

Documentation should remain close enough to the code to evolve with it.

This blueprint remains the high-level constitutional document.

Repository documentation translates it into current implementation instructions.

---

# 7.5.21.17 Minimum Implementation Evidence

The architecture is not proven merely because it is written.

Before the frontend architecture can be considered implemented, TWN should demonstrate at least the following.

---

## Public homepage

Must demonstrate:

* Server Component composition;
* Living Hero presentation;
* featured content;
* Journey discovery;
* responsive layout;
* loading behaviour;
* low client interaction scope;
* accessible navigation.

---

## Published Article route

Must demonstrate:

* public server rendering;
* meaningful metadata;
* Article body rendering;
* Author context;
* Category and Tags;
* Journey context;
* responsive reading layout;
* safe typed-block rendering;
* local optional sections;
* not-found behaviour.

---

## Notebook route

Must demonstrate:

* Notebook identity;
* public rendering;
* Today’s Page or highlighted Entry;
* responsive reflective layout;
* Journey linking.

---

## Journey route

Must demonstrate:

* Journey overview;
* Chapters;
* ordered content;
* multiple content-type readiness;
* previous and next navigation;
* responsive experience;
* accessible progress context.

---

## Search route

Must demonstrate:

* URL-based query state;
* server-produced results;
* filters;
* loading state;
* empty results;
* error handling;
* keyboard-accessible search interaction.

---

## CMS Article editor

Must demonstrate:

* dynamic private route;
* Server Component initial data;
* isolated Client Component editor;
* form state;
* save feedback;
* unsaved-change visibility;
* local recovery;
* responsive layout;
* accessible errors.

---

## Community form

Must demonstrate:

* progressive enhancement;
* accessible labels;
* pending state;
* preserved input;
* clear confirmation;
* safe failure behaviour.

---

## Shared design-system components

Must demonstrate:

* consistent token use;
* keyboard accessibility;
* reusable variants;
* responsive behaviour;
* controlled ownership.

---

# 7.5.21.18 Required Quality Gates

A frontend change should not be accepted when it fails any mandatory gate.

## Type gate

```text
TypeScript check passes.
```

## Lint gate

```text
Linting and import-boundary checks pass.
```

## Test gate

```text
Relevant unit and integration tests pass.
```

## Build gate

```text
Production build succeeds.
```

## Accessibility gate

```text
No known critical accessibility defect is introduced.
```

## Responsive gate

```text
Critical flow works on supported small and large viewports.
```

## Security gate

```text
No secret, private content, or unsafe rendering path is introduced.
```

## Performance gate

```text
No unexplained major browser-bundle or rendering regression is introduced.
```

The complete CI/CD implementation belongs to Section 7.34.

---

# 7.5.21.19 Frontend Performance Acceptance Criteria

Exact numeric budgets should be measured and maintained in implementation documentation rather than invented permanently in the blueprint.

However, the frontend must satisfy these architectural expectations:

* primary public content is server-rendered;
* non-essential Client Components do not block reading;
* CMS-only code does not enter public bundles;
* images reserve layout space;
* fonts do not make content unreadable while loading;
* navigation gives immediate visible feedback;
* typing remains responsive in editors;
* long documents remain usable;
* third-party scripts are limited;
* performance is tested on representative mobile devices and slower network conditions.

Any future numeric budget must be based on:

* actual application measurements;
* target user devices;
* network realities;
* business priorities;
* current web performance standards.

---

# 7.5.21.20 Accessibility Acceptance Criteria

A critical frontend route should not be considered complete until:

* it has a logical heading structure;
* it contains appropriate landmarks;
* keyboard users can reach and operate every control;
* focus is visible;
* dialogs manage and restore focus;
* forms have labels;
* errors are programmatically associated;
* icon-only controls have accessible names;
* content does not rely on colour alone;
* reduced-motion preferences are respected;
* mobile reflow does not hide capability;
* images receive appropriate alternative-text behaviour;
* loading and success states are understandable to assistive technologies;
* manual keyboard review has been completed.

---

# 7.5.21.21 Frontend Security Acceptance Criteria

A frontend feature is not complete until:

* no secret enters client code;
* private data is excluded from public view models;
* raw untrusted HTML is not rendered casually;
* client validation is not treated as authority;
* protected controls are permission-aware;
* server enforcement is assumed and required;
* external links use safe behaviour;
* browser storage is reviewed;
* error messages avoid implementation leakage;
* destructive actions communicate consequences;
* third-party dependencies are reviewed;
* Preview content cannot contaminate public presentation.

---

# 7.5.21.22 Testing Acceptance Criteria

Each important frontend feature should include the appropriate combination of:

* component tests;
* state-transition tests;
* form-error tests;
* accessibility checks;
* responsive verification;
* route integration tests;
* end-to-end tests for critical workflows;
* regression tests for resolved defects.

A feature does not need every possible test type.

Its tests should correspond to its risk.

---

# 7.5.21.23 Observability Acceptance Criteria

A production-critical frontend feature should provide enough information to diagnose:

* unexpected rendering failures;
* broken route navigation;
* repeated form failures;
* upload failures;
* Search failures;
* editor save failures;
* browser-specific regressions;
* deployment-related error spikes.

Observability must be privacy-conscious.

It must not collect full Article drafts, community submissions, credentials, or sensitive personal data merely for debugging convenience.

---

# 7.5.21.24 Known Frontend Risks

The following risks must remain visible.

## Framework evolution risk

Next.js caching, rendering, and Server Function APIs continue to evolve.

Mitigation:

* pin versions;
* use official documentation matching the installed version;
* test major upgrades;
* avoid undocumented framework behaviour.

---

## Client-boundary growth

Features may gradually convert more of the application into client code.

Mitigation:

* code review;
* bundle monitoring;
* explicit `"use client"` justification;
* server-first default.

---

## Design inconsistency

Rapid building may introduce arbitrary visual decisions.

Mitigation:

* Design System enforcement;
* semantic tokens;
* shared components;
* visual review.

---

## State duplication

Search, editor, or reader state may be represented in several locations.

Mitigation:

* explicit state ownership;
* URL-first navigation state;
* minimal global state.

---

## CMS complexity

The editor may accumulate panels, fields, and interactions.

Mitigation:

* feature boundaries;
* progressive disclosure;
* performance measurement;
* accessible workflows;
* staged CMS capabilities.

---

## Content-rendering risk

Typed blocks and future multimedia may make rendering complex.

Mitigation:

* block registry;
* versioned renderers;
* safe fallbacks;
* restricted component mapping;
* test coverage.

---

## Accessibility regression

Custom interactions may weaken native semantics.

Mitigation:

* native elements first;
* shared accessible primitives;
* automated and manual testing.

---

## Dependency growth

AI-assisted development may add packages too easily.

Mitigation:

* dependency review;
* one-library-per-capability policy;
* regular removal audits;
* bundle visibility.

---

## Architecture duplication

Frontend may begin implementing work belonging to Backend, API, CMS, or Security chapters.

Mitigation:

* section boundaries;
* narrow contracts;
* defer detailed ownership to the correct chapter;
* use this blueprint as the reference.

---

# 7.5.21.25 Frontend Architecture Review Cadence

## During initial implementation

Review after every major frontend surface:

* Homepage;
* Article;
* Notebook;
* Journey;
* Search;
* CMS editor;
* Media workflow.

## Before launch

Perform a complete frontend architecture review.

## After launch

Review:

* quarterly during early growth;
* before a major framework upgrade;
* before introducing a major new content type;
* before adding global state or another UI foundation;
* after serious performance or accessibility incidents.

## Mature platform

Review at least annually and alongside major roadmap changes.

---

# 7.5.21.26 Architecture Completion Checklist

Section 7.5 is complete at the blueprint level when all of the following have been defined.

### Foundation

* [x] Frontend purpose and philosophy
* [x] Technology stack
* [x] Project structure
* [x] route and module ownership

### Rendering

* [x] Server Component default
* [x] Client Component boundaries
* [x] route rendering strategy
* [x] streaming position
* [x] public/private rendering separation

### Data and state

* [x] Server query boundaries
* [x] view-model composition
* [x] Client Component data boundaries
* [x] local, URL, form, and persistent state ownership
* [x] minimal global-state position

### Interaction

* [x] form architecture
* [x] submission states
* [x] accessible error handling
* [x] loading and recovery states
* [x] navigation architecture

### Presentation

* [x] Design System integration
* [x] responsive architecture
* [x] multi-device behaviour
* [x] accessibility architecture

### Quality

* [x] performance responsibilities
* [x] frontend security responsibilities
* [x] frontend testing boundaries
* [x] frontend observability responsibilities
* [x] future evolution strategy

### Governance

* [x] architecture decision summary
* [x] conformance rules
* [x] review thresholds
* [x] completion criteria
* [x] deferred responsibilities identified

At the documentation level, all required Frontend Architecture topics are now covered.

---

# 7.5.21.27 Conditions That Would Reopen Section 7.5

Section 7.5 should be revisited when TWN adopts:

* a separate mobile application;
* a new frontend framework;
* micro-frontends;
* real-time collaborative editing;
* offline-first reader functionality;
* a major new Design System;
* reader personalization that changes route rendering significantly;
* a new global state platform;
* a multilingual interface architecture;
* immersive or spatial interfaces;
* extensive plugin-rendered content;
* a separate frontend repository;
* multiple independent frontend teams.

A change does not automatically invalidate the architecture.

It requires reviewing which decisions are affected.

---

# 7.5.21.28 Final Architecture Decision Record

**Decision:** TWN adopts Next.js App Router, React, TypeScript, Tailwind CSS, and a centrally governed Design System for its frontend. The architecture is server-first, with Server Components as the default and small Client Component islands for genuine browser interaction. Public content is server-rendered and designed for long-term readability, accessibility, responsiveness, and performance.

**Detailed decisions:**

* Route groups separate public, editorial, authentication, account, and administrative experiences.
* Domain frontend modules organize feature ownership.
* Routes compose features and remain thin.
* Public content does not depend on client-side fetching for primary readability.
* Rendering strategy is selected by visibility, freshness, personalization, and cacheability.
* Frontend query contracts mediate server data.
* View models protect presentation boundaries.
* Internal HTTP self-fetching is avoided.
* Client state remains minimal and locally owned.
* Navigation and filtering state live in URLs.
* Native forms and progressive enhancement are preferred.
* The frontend displays pending, success, error, empty, and recovery states explicitly.
* Design tokens and shared components prevent page-level visual invention.
* Mobile-first responsive implementation is required.
* Accessibility is a completion requirement.
* Public client JavaScript is minimized.
* Frontend security focuses on safe presentation and minimal exposure.
* Testing reflects user behaviour.
* Observability prioritizes reader and editor outcomes.
* Architecture changes are documented and reviewed.
* Future content types extend stable foundations rather than forcing periodic rewrites.

**Alternatives rejected:**

* client-side single-page application as the default;
* universal global state;
* separate APIs for every internal read;
* unrestricted database access from browser code;
* one universal content component;
* heavily styled third-party component systems defining TWN’s brand;
* unrestricted MDX;
* building separate mobile and desktop products;
* premature micro-frontends;
* rebuilding the frontend whenever framework trends change.

**Consequences:**

Positive:

* reduced public JavaScript;
* strong content delivery;
* clearer module ownership;
* improved accessibility;
* better mobile performance;
* easier long-term maintenance;
* safer frontend data boundaries;
* controlled future expansion.

Costs:

* developers must understand Server and Client Component boundaries;
* rendering and cache behaviour require deliberate decisions;
* shared view models require mapping;
* Design System discipline is required;
* browser-only libraries cannot be installed casually;
* framework upgrades require careful testing;
* frontend and backend responsibilities must remain clearly separated.

**Expected lifetime:** The server-first, content-first, accessible, domain-oriented architecture is intended to remain through TWN’s early and medium growth stages and potentially for the long term. Specific framework APIs may change, but the principles should remain stable.

---


It should not become the Database Bible, API Bible, Security Volume, or CMS specification.

[1]: https://nextjs.org/docs/app/getting-started/fetching-data?utm_source=chatgpt.com "Getting Started: Fetching Data"
[2]: https://react.dev/reference/rsc/server-components?utm_source=chatgpt.com "Server Components"
[3]: https://nextjs.org/docs/app/guides/caching-without-cache-components?utm_source=chatgpt.com "Caching and Revalidating (Previous Model)"
[4]: https://www.typescriptlang.org/tsconfig/strict.html?utm_source=chatgpt.com "TSConfig Option: strict"
[5]: https://nextjs.org/docs/app/getting-started/mutating-data?utm_source=chatgpt.com "Getting Started: Mutating Data"
[6]: https://tailwindcss.com/docs/responsive-design?utm_source=chatgpt.com "Responsive design - Core concepts"
[7]: https://react.dev/reference/react/use?utm_source=chatgpt.com "use"

# 7.6 Backend Architecture

---

# 7.6.1 Purpose, Scope & Backend Philosophy

**Status:** Foundational Backend Architecture Specification
**Parent chapter:** Volume 7 — Technical Architecture
**Applies to:** Editorial operations, Notebook operations, Journeys, Community submissions, Media coordination, Newsletter workflows, Search coordination, Identity integration, administrative operations, and future Books, Podcasts, Videos, Courses, Events, Collections, and AI-assisted workflows
**Initial architectural style:** Modular monolith
**Primary runtime:** Node.js within the Next.js application environment
**Primary objective:** Create a backend that protects TWN’s business rules, remains understandable to a small team, supports reliable growth, and can evolve without premature distributed-system complexity

---

# Purpose

This chapter defines how TWN’s server-side application logic is organized and executed.

It establishes:

* the backend’s responsibilities;
* the boundary between frontend and backend;
* the initial modular-monolith architecture;
* domain and module ownership;
* dependency direction;
* application services;
* command and query handling;
* repositories;
* domain services;
* provider adapters;
* transaction coordination;
* synchronous and asynchronous execution boundaries;
* error handling;
* idempotency;
* concurrency;
* server-only code organization;
* backend testing and evolution.

This chapter does **not** define:

* every database table or index;
* every HTTP endpoint;
* the complete authentication implementation;
* the complete authorization matrix;
* detailed CMS workflows;
* detailed Search indexing;
* complete queue infrastructure;
* the complete Security model.

Those subjects have their own chapters and future volumes.

This chapter defines the backend structure that those systems use.

---

# 7.6.1.1 Backend Mission

The backend exists to protect and execute TWN’s business capabilities.

It must ensure that operations such as:

```text
Publish an Article
Save a Notebook Entry
Reorder a Journey
Approve a Shared Page
Assign an Author
Schedule publication
Register a Media asset
Subscribe a reader
Restore a revision
```

occur according to TWN’s rules.

The backend is not merely:

```text
A collection of database queries
```

or:

```text
A place where frontend code is hidden
```

It is the authoritative execution layer of the product.

---

# 7.6.1.2 Backend Responsibilities

The backend owns:

* business workflows;
* business-rule enforcement;
* application commands;
* application queries;
* transaction coordination;
* authorization enforcement;
* persistent state changes;
* concurrency protection;
* idempotency;
* provider communication;
* durable side-effect coordination;
* backend error classification;
* server-side validation;
* audit-event generation;
* background-work dispatch;
* resource ownership enforcement.

The backend does not own:

* visual presentation;
* browser interaction;
* frontend loading states;
* responsive layouts;
* typography;
* component styling;
* browser-only state;
* page animation.

---

# 7.6.1.3 Frontend and Backend Boundary

The relationship is:

```text
Frontend
    ↓
Expresses user intent
    ↓
Backend
    ↓
Validates and executes the operation
    ↓
Frontend
    ↓
Presents the confirmed result
```

Example:

```text
Editor presses Publish
    ↓
Frontend submits a publish request
    ↓
Backend verifies:
    identity
    permission
    workflow state
    revision
    publication readiness
    concurrency version
    ↓
Backend publishes transactionally
    ↓
Frontend displays the confirmed result
```

The frontend may request an operation.

It cannot declare that the operation succeeded.

---

# 7.6.1.4 Backend as the Authority

The backend is authoritative for:

```text
What is published
Who may edit
What revision is current
Which Journey order is valid
Which submission is approved
Whether a User is active
Whether a workflow transition is allowed
```

The frontend may hide or disable controls according to capabilities returned by the backend.

Those controls remain usability aids.

They are not enforcement.

---

# 7.6.1.5 Initial Architecture Decision: Modular Monolith

TWN begins as a **modular monolith**.

This means:

```text
One deployable application
+
Clearly separated internal business modules
```

A monolithic application normally runs its core behaviour within one application process and is deployed as one unit, although it may interact with external databases and services. A well-structured monolith can still contain internal modules and layers. ([Microsoft Learn][1])

The TWN modular monolith contains domains such as:

```text
Editorial
Notebook
Journeys
Community
Media
Newsletter
Search
Identity
Analytics
```

inside one initial backend application.

---

# 7.6.1.6 Why a Modular Monolith Fits TWN

TWN’s current realities include:

* one founder or a small engineering team;
* one primary product;
* limited operating budget;
* rapidly evolving product understanding;
* closely related content domains;
* shared transactional workflows;
* no demonstrated need for independent service deployments;
* no proven traffic pattern requiring service-specific scaling.

Microservices introduce genuine additional complexity, including independent deployment, network communication, distributed tracing, data ownership coordination, failure handling, and service operations. Even Microsoft’s architecture guidance presents a well-designed monolith as a pragmatic option where microservice complexity is not justified. ([Microsoft Learn][2])

The modular monolith therefore provides:

* simple deployment;
* straightforward local development;
* easier transactions;
* lower infrastructure cost;
* easier debugging;
* faster early development;
* fewer network boundaries;
* clear future extraction paths.

---

# 7.6.1.7 Modular Does Not Mean Unstructured

A modular monolith must not become:

```text
One large application
where every file imports every other file
```

The architecture should instead resemble:

```text
TWN Backend
│
├── Editorial Module
├── Notebook Module
├── Journey Module
├── Community Module
├── Media Module
├── Newsletter Module
├── Search Module
├── Identity Module
└── Shared Backend Foundations
```

Each module owns:

* its business terminology;
* its application operations;
* its domain rules;
* its persistence contracts;
* its provider interactions;
* its public internal interfaces.

---

# 7.6.1.8 The Monolith Is a Deployment Boundary, Not a Domain Boundary

TWN initially has one application deployment.

That does not mean it has one undifferentiated business domain.

```text
One deployment
≠
One module
```

The application may be deployed as one unit while preserving strict internal ownership.

This distinction matters because future extraction becomes possible only when internal boundaries already exist.

---

# 7.6.1.9 No Premature Microservices

TWN should not create separate services for:

* Articles;
* Notebook Entries;
* Journeys;
* Tags;
* Categories;
* Authors;
* Margin Notes;

merely because they are different entities.

A service boundary should exist only when operational evidence justifies it.

Possible future evidence includes:

* a capability must scale independently;
* it requires a different runtime;
* it has a separate security boundary;
* it has independent release ownership;
* its failure must be isolated;
* it requires a separate data store;
* external customers depend on it independently;
* the modular monolith has become an actual bottleneck.

Without such evidence, a network boundary adds complexity without creating meaningful value.

---

# 7.6.1.10 Extraction-Ready, Not Extraction-Obsessed

The backend should be built so a module **could** become a service later.

It should not behave as though every module is already remote.

Avoid unnecessary internal HTTP communication such as:

```text
Editorial Module
    ↓ HTTP
Journey Module
```

inside the same application.

Use:

```text
Editorial Module
    ↓ Internal contract
Journey Module
```

If the Journey module is extracted later, the internal contract can be replaced with a remote adapter.

---

# 7.6.1.11 Backend Runtime Position

TWN’s initial backend runs in the Node.js environment used by the Next.js application.

Node.js is a cross-platform JavaScript runtime for servers and web applications, and much of its core API follows an asynchronous, event-driven model. ([nodejs.org][3])

This provides:

* shared TypeScript across frontend and backend;
* one application repository;
* reduced context switching;
* compatibility with Next.js server execution;
* broad library support;
* efficient handling of I/O-oriented work.

However, shared language must not erase architectural boundaries.

Frontend React code and backend domain logic remain separate even when both use TypeScript.

---

# 7.6.1.12 Backend Runtime Constraints

Node.js is well suited to I/O-oriented workloads such as:

* database access;
* storage coordination;
* email-provider calls;
* Search-provider calls;
* webhook handling;
* lightweight content transformations;
* application workflows.

CPU-intensive work should not block ordinary request execution.

Potential CPU-intensive work includes:

* large image processing;
* video transcoding;
* audio conversion;
* substantial document conversion;
* large-scale embedding generation;
* complex archive generation.

Such work should eventually move to:

* background workers;
* specialized providers;
* separate processing processes;
* queue-driven jobs.

The detailed worker architecture belongs to Sections 7.18 and 7.19.

---

# 7.6.1.13 Backend Architectural Layers

Each backend module may contain four principal layers:

```text
Presentation / Transport
        ↓
Application
        ↓
Domain
        ↓
Infrastructure
```

These layers define responsibility.

They do not require a separate package or directory for every trivial function.

---

## Transport Layer

Receives requests from:

* Server Actions;
* Route Handlers;
* scheduled triggers;
* background workers;
* administrative scripts;
* webhooks.

Responsibilities:

* parse transport input;
* resolve request context;
* invoke the relevant application operation;
* map results to the transport response.

It must remain thin.

---

## Application Layer

Coordinates a business use case.

Examples:

* create an Article;
* save a draft;
* publish a revision;
* attach content to a Journey;
* approve a Shared Page;
* request a Media upload;
* suspend a User.

Responsibilities:

* load required state;
* invoke authorization;
* coordinate domain rules;
* manage transaction boundaries;
* call repositories;
* record audit information;
* dispatch durable follow-up work;
* return a typed result.

---

## Domain Layer

Contains the core business meaning.

Examples:

* Article lifecycle rules;
* Journey structural rules;
* publication readiness;
* moderation state transitions;
* contributor eligibility;
* content-membership invariants.

The domain layer should not depend directly on:

* Next.js;
* React;
* HTTP;
* PostgreSQL clients;
* Supabase SDKs;
* email-provider SDKs;
* Vercel APIs.

---

## Infrastructure Layer

Connects TWN to technical systems.

Examples:

* PostgreSQL repository;
* object-storage adapter;
* email adapter;
* Search adapter;
* AI-provider adapter;
* cache adapter;
* clock implementation;
* identifier generator;
* job dispatcher.

Infrastructure implements contracts defined by inner layers.

---

# 7.6.1.14 Dependency Direction

Dependencies should flow toward business meaning.

```text
Transport
    ↓
Application
    ↓
Domain
```

Infrastructure connects through interfaces:

```text
Application
    ↓
Repository contract
    ↑
PostgreSQL implementation
```

The domain should not import infrastructure.

Bad:

```typescript
import { createClient } from "@supabase/supabase-js";

export class Article {
  async publish() {
    await createClient(...).from("articles").update(...);
  }
}
```

Good:

```typescript
export interface ArticleRepository {
  save(article: Article): Promise<void>;
}
```

The concrete database implementation belongs outside the domain.

---

# 7.6.1.15 Pragmatic Layering

TWN should avoid both extremes.

## Extreme 1 — No structure

```text
Server Action
    ↓
SQL
    ↓
Email
    ↓
Cache clearing
```

all in one function.

This becomes difficult to test and maintain.

## Extreme 2 — Ceremony for every line

```text
Controller
Factory
Coordinator
Manager
Processor
Executor
Dispatcher
Handler
```

for a simple newsletter subscription.

This creates architecture without value.

The rule is:

> Add a layer or abstraction when it protects a meaningful boundary, business rule, or replaceable dependency.

---

# 7.6.1.16 Domain-Oriented Backend Modules

The initial module map should align with TWN’s business language.

```text
backend/
├── editorial/
├── notebook/
├── journeys/
├── community/
├── media/
├── newsletter/
├── search/
├── identity/
├── analytics/
└── shared/
```

Modules should not be organized primarily as:

```text
controllers/
services/
repositories/
models/
```

across the entire application.

That structure groups technical artifacts while separating related business behaviour.

---

# 7.6.1.17 Module Internal Structure

A substantial module may use:

```text
editorial/
├── domain/
│   ├── entities/
│   ├── value-objects/
│   ├── policies/
│   ├── events/
│   └── errors/
│
├── application/
│   ├── commands/
│   ├── queries/
│   ├── handlers/
│   ├── ports/
│   └── results/
│
├── infrastructure/
│   ├── repositories/
│   ├── mappers/
│   └── adapters/
│
└── transport/
    ├── server-actions/
    ├── route-handlers/
    └── jobs/
```

This is a model, not a command to create every folder immediately.

A folder should exist only when it contains meaningful code.

---

# 7.6.1.18 Module Ownership

Each business capability has one primary owner.

| Capability               | Owning backend module |
| ------------------------ | --------------------- |
| Articles                 | Editorial             |
| Categories               | Editorial             |
| Tags                     | Editorial             |
| Authors                  | Editorial             |
| Notebook Entries         | Notebook              |
| Journeys                 | Journeys              |
| Journey Chapters         | Journeys              |
| Shared Pages             | Community             |
| Margin Notes             | Community             |
| Media metadata           | Media                 |
| Newsletter subscribers   | Newsletter            |
| Search documents         | Search                |
| Users and identity links | Identity              |
| Product-event collection | Analytics             |

Another module may reference an entity.

It must not silently take ownership of it.

---

# 7.6.1.19 Cross-Module Communication

Modules communicate through explicit contracts.

Example:

```typescript
export interface EditorialPublicQueries {
  getPublishedContentReference(
    contentId: string,
  ): Promise<PublishedContentReference | null>;
}
```

The Journey module may use this contract to resolve Article references.

It should not query the Editorial module’s tables directly through hidden SQL.

---

# 7.6.1.20 Public Internal Interfaces

Each module should expose a narrow internal interface.

Example:

```text
Editorial exports:
- createArticle
- saveArticleDraft
- publishArticle
- getPublishedArticle
- getContentReference

Editorial does not export:
- raw repository
- internal table mapper
- database client
- private helper functions
```

A module’s index or public contract should define what other modules may use.

---

# 7.6.1.21 Cross-Module Reads

Not every cross-module read requires a network call or domain event.

Within the modular monolith, an application query may call another module’s exported read interface.

Example:

```text
Journey page query
    ↓
Journey module retrieves structure
    ↓
Editorial public query resolves Article summaries
    ↓
Notebook public query resolves Notebook summaries
```

This is acceptable when:

* the contract is explicit;
* ownership remains clear;
* no internal repository is exposed;
* circular dependencies are avoided.

---

# 7.6.1.22 Cross-Module Writes

A module should not directly modify another module’s state.

Bad:

```text
Journey module
    ↓
Updates Article table directly
```

Preferred:

```text
Journey module
    ↓
Calls Editorial application command
```

or:

```text
Journey module
    ↓
Records Journey relationship it owns
```

The correct choice depends on entity ownership.

---

# 7.6.1.23 Circular Dependency Prevention

Circular dependencies are a warning sign.

Bad:

```text
Editorial
    ↓
Journeys
    ↓
Editorial
```

Potential solutions:

* define a smaller shared contract;
* move shared policy to the correct owner;
* use an application-level coordinator;
* use domain events for secondary reactions;
* reconsider ownership.

Do not hide circular dependencies through dynamic imports.

---

# 7.6.1.24 Shared Backend Foundations

A small shared foundation may contain genuinely cross-cutting concepts such as:

* entity identifiers;
* application-result primitives;
* clock interface;
* transaction interface;
* pagination primitives;
* idempotency primitives;
* request context;
* common error base;
* event metadata.

It should not become:

```text
shared/
    everything that did not fit elsewhere
```

Business concepts remain in their owning domains.

---

# 7.6.1.25 Application Use Cases

The backend should be organized around business use cases.

Examples:

```text
CreateArticle
SaveArticleDraft
SubmitArticleForReview
PublishArticle
ScheduleArticle
RestoreArticleRevision

CreateJourney
AddJourneyChapter
AttachContentToJourney
ReorderJourneyItems
PublishJourney

SubmitSharedPage
ApproveSharedPage
RejectSharedPage

RequestMediaUpload
ConfirmMediaUpload
ArchiveMedia
```

These names communicate intent more clearly than generic technical operations.

---

# 7.6.1.26 Commands and Queries

The backend uses a lightweight separation between:

```text
Commands
    ↓
Change state

Queries
    ↓
Read state
```

This is not a commitment to:

* separate databases;
* event sourcing;
* complex command buses;
* distributed CQRS.

It is a responsibility distinction.

---

# 7.6.1.27 Command Characteristics

A command:

* represents business intent;
* may change state;
* requires authorization;
* may require a transaction;
* may emit audit information;
* may dispatch follow-up work;
* returns a defined result.

Example:

```typescript
type PublishArticleCommand = {
  articleId: string;
  revisionId: string;
  expectedVersion: number;
  actor: ActorContext;
};
```

---

# 7.6.1.28 Query Characteristics

A query:

* retrieves information;
* should not intentionally change business state;
* may use read-optimized projections;
* may be cacheable;
* returns a view or application result;
* should have explicit visibility semantics.

Examples:

```text
GetPublishedArticle
GetArticleEditorView
ListPublicJourneys
GetModerationQueue
```

Avoid generic queries such as:

```text
GetArticle
```

when public and private visibility differ.

---

# 7.6.1.29 Business Logic Placement

Business logic should live as close as possible to the business concept it protects.

Examples:

```text
Can Article be published?
    ↓
Editorial domain policy

Can content belong to Journey Chapter?
    ↓
Journey domain policy

Can submission move from pending to approved?
    ↓
Community domain policy
```

Do not scatter these rules across:

* React components;
* Server Actions;
* repositories;
* database mappers;
* email templates.

---

# 7.6.1.30 Domain Entities

A domain entity represents a business concept with identity and behaviour.

Potential examples:

* Article;
* Article Draft;
* Journey;
* Journey Chapter;
* Shared Page;
* Margin Note;
* Media Asset;
* Subscriber.

Not every database table requires a rich domain class.

Simple records may remain typed application data.

Introduce behavioural entities when they protect meaningful invariants.

---

# 7.6.1.31 Value Objects

A value object represents a meaningful value without independent identity.

Potential examples:

* Slug;
* Email Address;
* Publication Schedule;
* Content Reference;
* Journey Position;
* Media Dimensions;
* Revision Number.

Value objects are useful when they centralize:

* validation;
* normalization;
* comparison;
* invariant protection.

Do not create classes for every primitive merely to appear domain-driven.

---

# 7.6.1.32 Domain Policies

Some rules span several entities and do not naturally belong to one entity.

Example:

```text
Article publication readiness
```

may depend on:

* Article;
* selected revision;
* Author;
* Category;
* Media availability;
* review blockers.

This can be represented through a domain policy such as:

```typescript
evaluateArticlePublicationReadiness(context);
```

The policy receives the required state.

It should not perform hidden database queries.

---

# 7.6.1.33 Application Services

Application Services coordinate use cases.

They may:

* authenticate or receive an Actor context;
* authorize the operation;
* load entities;
* invoke domain behaviour;
* persist changes;
* coordinate several modules;
* manage transactions;
* create outbox records;
* return typed results.

They should not contain page-rendering logic.

---

# 7.6.1.34 Domain Services

A Domain Service contains business logic that:

* belongs to the domain;
* does not fit naturally inside one entity;
* remains independent of infrastructure.

Example:

```text
JourneyStructureValidator
PublicationReadinessPolicy
ContentRelationshipPolicy
```

Avoid naming every backend class `Service`.

The name should communicate actual responsibility.

---

# 7.6.1.35 Repository Contracts

A repository provides domain-oriented persistence operations.

Good:

```typescript
interface JourneyRepository {
  getById(id: JourneyId): Promise<Journey | null>;
  save(journey: Journey): Promise<void>;
}
```

Potentially weak:

```typescript
interface JourneyRepository {
  query(sql: string): Promise<unknown>;
}
```

The repository hides persistence mechanics.

It does not hide business meaning.

---

# 7.6.1.36 Read Repositories and Write Repositories

TWN may use separate read and write contracts where useful.

Example:

```text
ArticleRepository
    ↓
Loads and saves Article state

ArticleQueries
    ↓
Returns public and editorial projections
```

This avoids forcing every read through a fully reconstructed domain entity.

It remains lightweight CQRS, not distributed CQRS.

---

# 7.6.1.37 Infrastructure Adapters

Provider-specific code belongs behind adapters.

Examples:

```typescript
interface EmailSender {
  send(message: OutboundEmail): Promise<SendResult>;
}

interface ObjectStorage {
  createUploadTarget(
    input: UploadTargetRequest,
  ): Promise<UploadTarget>;
}

interface SearchIndexer {
  index(document: SearchDocument): Promise<void>;
}
```

Concrete implementations may use:

* a selected email provider;
* Supabase Storage;
* PostgreSQL Search;
* a future external Search engine.

The Application layer depends on the contract.

Not the provider SDK.

---

# 7.6.1.38 Provider Independence

Provider abstraction does not mean pretending all providers are identical.

The adapter should expose the capabilities TWN actually needs.

Bad abstraction:

```typescript
interface UniversalProvider {
  doEverything(input: unknown): Promise<unknown>;
}
```

Good abstraction:

```typescript
interface NewsletterDeliveryProvider {
  sendCampaign(
    campaign: PreparedCampaign,
  ): Promise<CampaignSubmissionResult>;
}
```

The contract should be:

* narrow;
* typed;
* domain-relevant;
* testable.

---

# 7.6.1.39 Transaction Philosophy

A transaction should protect one coherent state change.

Example:

```text
Publish Article
├── create publication revision
├── update Article publication pointer
├── record publication event
├── write Audit Log
└── write Outbox event
```

These database changes should either succeed together or fail together.

Detailed transaction implementation belongs to later backend and database subsections.

---

# 7.6.1.40 Transactions Must Remain Short

A database transaction should not remain open while the application:

* sends an email;
* uploads a file;
* calls an AI provider;
* waits for a Search engine;
* invokes an external webhook;
* performs long media processing.

Preferred:

```text
Database transaction
    ↓
Commit
    ↓
Durable outbox or job
    ↓
External side effect
```

This prevents external latency from holding database locks.

---

# 7.6.1.41 Synchronous Work

Work may remain synchronous when the requester needs the result to continue safely.

Examples:

* validate input;
* authorize action;
* save draft;
* publish database state;
* reserve a slug;
* confirm a version;
* create an upload target;
* return a Search page.

Synchronous work should remain bounded.

---

# 7.6.1.42 Asynchronous Work

Work should become asynchronous when it:

* is slow;
* is retryable;
* does not need to finish before the response;
* involves unreliable external systems;
* performs significant computation;
* affects derived data.

Examples:

* send email;
* rebuild Search projection;
* generate image variants;
* extract audio metadata;
* generate embeddings;
* create an export;
* notify subscribers;
* update Analytics projections.

The queue and job architecture is documented later.

---

# 7.6.1.43 Side-Effect Ordering

The backend must not produce a public side effect before the authoritative state change succeeds.

Bad:

```text
Send “Article Published” email
    ↓
Publication transaction fails
```

Preferred:

```text
Publication transaction succeeds
    ↓
Outbox event becomes durable
    ↓
Email or notification is dispatched
```

---

# 7.6.1.44 Idempotency Position

Backend operations may be repeated because of:

* network retries;
* browser retries;
* double submission;
* webhook redelivery;
* worker retry;
* timeout uncertainty.

Operations with duplicate risk should define idempotent behaviour.

Examples:

* newsletter subscription;
* publication;
* webhook processing;
* Media upload confirmation;
* background-job execution;
* campaign dispatch.

Detailed idempotency-key design will be documented in later backend subsections and the API chapter.

---

# 7.6.1.45 Concurrency Position

Concurrent operations must not silently overwrite each other.

Examples:

* two editors saving the same Article;
* two administrators changing a User;
* two workers processing the same event;
* two editors reordering one Journey.

The backend should use appropriate combinations of:

* expected versions;
* conditional updates;
* unique constraints;
* database transactions;
* row locking where justified;
* idempotency records.

The frontend may present the conflict.

The backend must detect it.

---

# 7.6.1.46 Backend Error Philosophy

Backend failures should be classified.

Primary categories:

```text
Validation
Unauthenticated
Forbidden
Not Found
Conflict
Rate Limited
Dependency Failure
Unavailable
Unexpected
```

The backend should not expose:

* raw database errors;
* stack traces;
* provider credentials;
* internal SQL;
* sensitive business state.

Transport adapters map backend results into:

* Server Action state;
* HTTP responses;
* job outcomes;
* logs.

---

# 7.6.1.47 Expected and Unexpected Failures

## Expected failure

Part of ordinary product behaviour.

Examples:

* invalid title;
* missing permission;
* stale draft version;
* unavailable slug;
* expired invitation.

Return a typed result.

## Unexpected failure

Indicates:

* defect;
* broken dependency;
* infrastructure problem;
* unhandled state.

Record detailed internal diagnostics and return a safe external failure.

---

# 7.6.1.48 Backend Validation Position

The backend validates:

* transport structure;
* command input;
* business invariants;
* entity relationships;
* provider responses;
* state transitions.

The browser is not trusted.

Detailed validation rules belong to the owning domain, CMS, API, Database, and Security chapters.

This Backend Architecture chapter defines where those rules execute.

---

# 7.6.1.49 Authentication and Authorization Position

The backend receives or resolves an authenticated Actor context.

Example:

```typescript
type ActorContext = {
  userId: string;
  sessionId: string;
  authenticationLevel: "standard" | "elevated";
};
```

The actual structure may differ after Section 7.9 is specified.

Application operations use the Actor context to evaluate authorization.

The backend must never trust a client-submitted `actorId`.

---

# 7.6.1.50 Request Context

A backend operation may receive a request context containing:

* request ID;
* Actor;
* originating channel;
* locale;
* current time abstraction;
* idempotency key;
* trace information.

Avoid passing the entire framework request object throughout the domain.

Convert framework information into a controlled application context.

---

# 7.6.1.51 Framework Isolation

Next.js provides Server Functions for server-side mutations and Route Handlers for custom HTTP request handling. ([Next.js][4])

TWN should use these capabilities without embedding Next.js throughout its business logic.

Framework-specific code belongs at the transport boundary.

Example:

```text
Next.js Server Action
    ↓
PublishArticle application handler
```

not:

```text
Article domain entity
    ↓
imports next/cache
```

---

# 7.6.1.52 `use server` Boundary

A `"use server"` function marks server-executed code.

It does not automatically make the function:

* authorized;
* validated;
* transactional;
* idempotent;
* architecturally correct.

A Server Function still requires:

* authentication;
* authorization;
* input validation;
* domain execution;
* safe result mapping.

Next.js Server Functions execute on the server and can be invoked for mutations, but they remain callable application entry points that require ordinary security controls. ([Next.js][5])

---

# 7.6.1.53 Server-Only Enforcement

Backend modules containing:

* database clients;
* secrets;
* repositories;
* provider SDKs;
* privileged operations;

must be protected from client imports.

Use:

* `server-only`;
* `.server.ts` conventions;
* lint restrictions;
* dependency boundaries;
* separate environment modules.

The build should fail when privileged backend code is imported into a Client Component.

---

# 7.6.1.54 Backend Configuration

Configuration should be:

* validated;
* centralized;
* environment-aware;
* typed;
* separated into public and private values.

Business modules should not read `process.env` throughout their implementation.

Preferred:

```text
Validated server configuration
    ↓
Injected into infrastructure adapters
```

This improves:

* testing;
* startup failure clarity;
* provider replacement;
* secret handling.

---

# 7.6.1.55 Time as a Dependency

Business rules involving time should use a controlled clock.

Examples:

* publication scheduling;
* invitation expiry;
* temporary permissions;
* embargo;
* token expiry;
* retry timing.

Instead of calling:

```typescript
new Date()
```

throughout domain policies, use:

```typescript
interface Clock {
  now(): Date;
}
```

This enables deterministic tests.

---

# 7.6.1.56 Identifier Generation

Entity identifiers should be generated through a controlled abstraction or approved database mechanism.

Business logic should not assume:

* sequential integers;
* provider-generated IDs;
* browser-generated identity;
* timestamp-only uniqueness.

The exact identifier policy belongs to Database Architecture and Volume 8.

Backend code should treat identifiers as opaque typed values.

---

# 7.6.1.57 Backend Logging Position

Backend operations should emit structured operational information.

Useful context includes:

* operation name;
* request ID;
* Actor ID where appropriate;
* entity type;
* entity ID;
* duration;
* outcome;
* error category.

Do not log:

* full Article drafts;
* subscriber lists;
* access tokens;
* passwords;
* private provider payloads;
* unnecessary personal data.

Detailed Logging Architecture belongs to Section 7.25.

---

# 7.6.1.58 Backend Observability Position

Important application operations should expose enough information to answer:

* Did the operation succeed?
* How long did it take?
* Which dependency failed?
* Was the failure expected?
* Was a retry scheduled?
* Which deployment handled it?

Detailed metrics, traces, alerts, and monitoring infrastructure belong to Sections 7.23–7.25.

---

# 7.6.1.59 Backend Testing Position

Backend testing should cover:

* domain policies;
* application operations;
* authorization decisions;
* transaction behaviour;
* repository contracts;
* provider adapters;
* idempotency;
* concurrency;
* error mapping;
* cross-module contracts.

Detailed Testing Strategy belongs to Section 7.35.

This chapter defines what backend seams must remain testable.

---

# 7.6.1.60 Dependency Injection Position

TWN does not require a large dependency-injection framework initially.

Dependencies may be assembled explicitly.

Example:

```typescript
const publishArticle = createPublishArticleHandler({
  articleRepository,
  publicationRepository,
  authorizationService,
  auditWriter,
  outboxWriter,
  clock,
});
```

Benefits:

* dependencies are visible;
* tests can replace them;
* no global service locator;
* no framework lock-in.

A dependency container may later be considered if manual composition becomes genuinely difficult.

---

# 7.6.1.61 Avoid Global Mutable Services

Avoid global mutable state such as:

```typescript
let currentUser;
let activeTransaction;
let currentArticle;
```

Server environments may handle concurrent requests.

Request-specific information must remain request-scoped.

Stateless provider clients may be shared when safe.

Mutable business context must not be global.

---

# 7.6.1.62 Backend Portability

The business layers should remain portable across:

* Next.js Server Actions;
* Route Handlers;
* background workers;
* administrative scripts;
* tests;
* future service extraction.

Example:

```text
PublishArticleHandler
```

should not require a React form to execute.

It should receive a typed command.

---

# 7.6.1.63 Backend Non-Goals

The initial backend does not require:

* microservices;
* Kubernetes;
* event sourcing;
* a distributed command bus;
* separate write and read databases;
* a general-purpose workflow engine;
* GraphQL federation;
* a service mesh;
* distributed sagas;
* multiple backend repositories;
* separate runtime per domain;
* custom dependency-injection framework;
* complex enterprise abstractions;
* direct browser access to privileged persistence.

These may be evaluated only when actual product requirements justify them.

---

# 7.6.1.64 Backend Growth Model

The expected evolution is:

```text
Stage 1
Modular monolith
One application deployment

Stage 2
Modular monolith
Dedicated background workers
External provider adapters

Stage 3
Selected independent processing services
Only for measured operational needs

Stage 4
Extracted domain services
Only when ownership, scaling or isolation demands it
```

The goal is not to reach microservices.

The goal is to maintain the simplest architecture that reliably supports TWN.

---

# 7.6.1.65 Service-Extraction Candidates

Potential future extraction candidates may include:

* Media processing;
* Search indexing;
* email delivery;
* AI generation;
* Analytics ingestion;
* document export;
* notification delivery.

These are candidates because they may have:

* independent scaling patterns;
* different runtime requirements;
* high computational cost;
* external failure behaviour;
* queue-based execution.

Core Editorial and Journey operations should not be extracted without strong evidence because their workflows are closely related.

---

# 7.6.1.66 Architecture Quality Questions

Every backend feature should answer:

* Which module owns it?
* Is it a command or query?
* What business intent does it represent?
* Which Actor may perform it?
* Which rules apply?
* Which data must be loaded?
* What is the transaction boundary?
* What can fail?
* Which failures are expected?
* Is it idempotent?
* Can concurrent execution conflict?
* Which side effects occur?
* Must those side effects be asynchronous?
* Which caches or projections become stale?
* What Audit information is required?
* Can the operation be tested without Next.js?
* Does it depend directly on a provider?
* Is the abstraction solving a real boundary?

---

# 7.6.1.67 Backend Anti-Patterns

## Fat Server Actions

```text
Server Action
    ↓
Validation
SQL
Business rules
Email
Caching
Logging
Provider calls
```

all mixed together.

---

## Anemic “service” dumping ground

```text
services/articleService.ts
```

containing every Article operation with no internal boundaries.

---

## Domain logic in repositories

A repository should persist.

It should not decide whether an Article is ready to publish.

---

## Domain logic in React components

A disabled Publish button is not a publication rule.

---

## Shared database access across modules

A Journey feature should not edit Editorial-owned tables directly.

---

## Provider SDK throughout the codebase

Provider details should remain behind adapters.

---

## Network calls inside database transactions

Creates long transactions and failure coupling.

---

## One generic CRUD service

TWN workflows contain business meaning.

They should not be reduced to:

```text
create
read
update
delete
```

only.

---

## Premature microservices

Creates operational work before product evidence exists.

---

## Premature generic abstractions

A universal repository, universal service, or universal entity often removes useful domain language.

---

## Returning raw exceptions

Application results should be controlled and typed.

---

## Global request state

Creates concurrency and privacy risks.

---

# 7.6.1.68 Initial Backend Decision Summary

TWN’s backend is:

```text
Modular
Monolithic
Domain-oriented
Use-case driven
Transaction-aware
Provider-contained
Testable
Extraction-ready
```

It is not:

```text
Microservice-first
CRUD-only
Framework-coupled
Database-driven
Provider-dependent
Frontend-controlled
```

---

# 7.6.1.69 Architecture Decision Record

**Decision:** TWN begins with a TypeScript modular monolith running in the Node.js server environment of the Next.js application. The backend is divided into domain-oriented modules with explicit internal contracts and a layered separation between transport, application, domain, and infrastructure responsibilities.

**Supporting decisions:**

* One primary deployable application is used initially.
* Business modules remain internally separated.
* Server Actions and Route Handlers are transport adapters.
* Application handlers coordinate use cases.
* Domain entities and policies protect business meaning.
* Repositories abstract persistence.
* Provider SDKs remain behind adapters.
* Commands and queries are distinguished without adopting full distributed CQRS.
* Cross-module writes use explicit module operations.
* Cross-module reads use narrow exported contracts.
* Database transactions protect coherent state changes.
* External side effects do not hold database transactions open.
* Slow or retryable work moves toward background execution.
* Idempotency and concurrency are explicit backend concerns.
* Backend errors are classified and safely mapped.
* Framework request objects do not enter the domain.
* Dependencies are composed explicitly without a mandatory DI framework.
* Microservices are deferred until measured operational evidence justifies them.
* Modules are designed for possible future extraction without simulating network boundaries prematurely.

**Reasons:**

* matches TWN’s current team and budget;
* reduces deployment and operational complexity;
* supports rapid product learning;
* preserves business-rule clarity;
* enables reliable transactions;
* avoids distributed-system failure modes;
* keeps the system testable;
* supports future Books, Podcasts, Courses, Community, and AI capabilities;
* creates a controlled path toward workers or selected services later.

**Consequences:**

Positive:

* simpler development;
* easier debugging;
* lower infrastructure cost;
* fewer network dependencies;
* clearer business ownership;
* easier transactional consistency;
* faster initial implementation.

Costs:

* module boundaries require discipline;
* one deployment may eventually scale more broadly than necessary;
* developers must resist cross-module database access;
* future service extraction may require migration work;
* backend and frontend TypeScript may become mixed unless directory and import rules are enforced.

**Expected lifetime:** The modular-monolith architecture is expected to support TWN through its early and medium growth stages. Individual capabilities may be extracted later when demonstrated scaling, processing, security, or ownership needs justify the added complexity.

---

# Completion Criteria for 7.6.1

This subsection is complete when the team agrees that:

* the backend is authoritative for business state;
* TWN begins as a modular monolith;
* one deployment does not imply one undifferentiated domain;
* backend modules align with product domains;
* dependencies point toward business logic;
* Server Actions and Route Handlers are transport layers;
* commands and queries have different responsibilities;
* repositories do not own business rules;
* providers remain behind adapters;
* external calls do not remain inside long database transactions;
* asynchronous work is separated when justified;
* idempotency and concurrency are backend responsibilities;
* framework-specific code remains outside the domain;
* microservices are not an initial requirement;
* future extraction remains possible through explicit contracts.

---

## End of 7.6.1

The next subsection is:

# **7.6.2 — Backend Module Structure, Boundaries & Dependency Rules**

It will remain strictly within Backend Architecture and define:

* the complete backend directory structure;
* module public interfaces;
* internal versus exported code;
* application, domain, infrastructure and transport placement;
* allowed cross-module dependencies;
* cycle prevention;
* shared-kernel limits;
* module composition;
* server-only import enforcement;
* how new domains such as Books, Podcasts, Courses, Events and AI capabilities are added without weakening the modular monolith.

[1]: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/architecture/modern-web-apps-azure/common-web-application-architectures?utm_source=chatgpt.com "Common web application architectures - .NET"
[2]: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/shows/on-dotnet/on-dotnet-live-modular-monoliths-with-aspnet-core?utm_source=chatgpt.com "On .NET Live - Modular Monoliths with ASP.NET Core"
[3]: https://nodejs.org/en/about?utm_source=chatgpt.com "About Node.js"
[4]: https://nextjs.org/docs/app/getting-started/mutating-data?utm_source=chatgpt.com "Getting Started: Mutating Data"
[5]: https://nextjs.org/docs/13/app/building-your-application/data-fetching/server-actions-and-mutations?utm_source=chatgpt.com "Server Actions and Mutations - Data Fetching"

# 7.6.2 Backend Module Structure, Boundaries & Dependency Rules

**Status:** Backend Architecture Specification
**Parent chapter:** Volume 7 — Technical Architecture
**Current scope:** Section 7.6 — Backend Architecture
**Applies to:** Editorial, Notebook, Journeys, Community, Media, Newsletter, Search, Identity integration, Analytics coordination, administrative operations, and future content domains
**Primary objective:** Define how TWN’s backend code is divided into modules, how those modules communicate, what they may depend on, and how the modular monolith avoids becoming an unstructured monolith

---

# Purpose

This subsection defines the internal structure of the TWN backend.

It establishes:

* the initial backend directory structure;
* module ownership;
* module public interfaces;
* internal versus exported code;
* application, domain, infrastructure, and transport placement;
* allowed dependency directions;
* prohibited dependency directions;
* cross-module communication;
* circular-dependency prevention;
* shared-kernel limits;
* module composition;
* server-only enforcement;
* how new domains are introduced;
* how boundaries remain enforceable as the codebase grows.

The central rule is:

> One deployment does not permit unrestricted internal access.

TWN begins as a modular monolith, but each module must behave as an independently owned business capability inside that monolith.

---

# 7.6.2.1 Structural Goal

The backend should make it easy to answer:

```text
Which module owns this behaviour?

Where does this rule belong?

What may this file import?

Which interface may another module use?

Can this capability be changed without breaking unrelated domains?
```

If these questions cannot be answered quickly, the architecture is becoming unclear.

---

# 7.6.2.2 Module-First Organization

The backend should be organized primarily by business module.

Recommended:

```text
src/
└── backend/
    ├── editorial/
    ├── notebook/
    ├── journeys/
    ├── community/
    ├── media/
    ├── newsletter/
    ├── search/
    ├── identity/
    ├── analytics/
    ├── administration/
    ├── shared/
    └── composition/
```

Avoid a project-wide structure such as:

```text
src/
├── controllers/
├── services/
├── repositories/
├── models/
├── validators/
└── helpers/
```

The technical-layer structure scatters one business capability across several unrelated global folders.

For example, publishing an Article could become distributed across:

```text
controllers/article-controller.ts
services/article-service.ts
repositories/article-repository.ts
validators/article-validator.ts
models/article-model.ts
```

A module-first design keeps related behaviour together.

---

# 7.6.2.3 Initial Backend Module Map

The initial module map should be:

```text
backend/
│
├── editorial/
│   ├── Articles
│   ├── Authors
│   ├── Categories
│   ├── Tags
│   └── Editorial content references
│
├── notebook/
│   ├── Notebook Entries
│   ├── Today’s Page
│   └── Notebook presentation selection
│
├── journeys/
│   ├── Journeys
│   ├── Journey Chapters
│   ├── Ordered memberships
│   └── Journey publication structure
│
├── community/
│   ├── Shared Pages
│   ├── Margin Notes
│   ├── Moderation coordination
│   └── Community submissions
│
├── media/
│   ├── Media metadata
│   ├── Upload coordination
│   ├── Media lifecycle
│   └── Public Media projections
│
├── newsletter/
│   ├── Subscribers
│   ├── Consent state
│   ├── Campaign coordination
│   └── Delivery requests
│
├── search/
│   ├── Search documents
│   ├── Indexing coordination
│   ├── Search queries
│   └── Discovery projections
│
├── identity/
│   ├── Local User records
│   ├── External identity links
│   ├── Invitations
│   └── Account state integration
│
├── analytics/
│   ├── Product-event acceptance
│   ├── Metric projection coordination
│   └── Reporting interfaces
│
└── administration/
    ├── Platform settings
    ├── Operational controls
    └── Cross-domain administrative workflows
```

Detailed entity models remain in their appropriate later chapters and Volume 8.

---

# 7.6.2.4 Module Definition

A backend module is a cohesive business capability that owns:

* terminology;
* rules;
* application operations;
* persistence contracts;
* domain errors;
* internal state;
* exported internal interfaces;
* infrastructure adapters specific to that capability.

A module is not merely a folder containing similarly named files.

It must have:

```text
Clear responsibility
+
Controlled dependencies
+
Explicit public interface
+
Private implementation
```

---

# 7.6.2.5 Module Ownership Rules

Every business concept has one primary owner.

| Business concept           | Owning module  |
| -------------------------- | -------------- |
| Article                    | Editorial      |
| Author                     | Editorial      |
| Category                   | Editorial      |
| Tag                        | Editorial      |
| Notebook Entry             | Notebook       |
| Today’s Page selection     | Notebook       |
| Journey                    | Journeys       |
| Journey Chapter            | Journeys       |
| Journey membership         | Journeys       |
| Shared Page                | Community      |
| Margin Note                | Community      |
| Community moderation state | Community      |
| Media record               | Media          |
| Newsletter subscriber      | Newsletter     |
| Search document            | Search         |
| Local User                 | Identity       |
| Product event              | Analytics      |
| Platform setting           | Administration |

Ownership means:

* the module defines valid state;
* the module defines mutation operations;
* the module controls persistence contracts;
* other modules do not update its internal data directly.

---

# 7.6.2.6 Internal Module Structure

A substantial module may use:

```text
editorial/
├── domain/
├── application/
├── infrastructure/
├── transport/
├── public/
└── index.ts
```

A more detailed structure may be:

```text
editorial/
├── domain/
│   ├── entities/
│   ├── value-objects/
│   ├── policies/
│   ├── events/
│   ├── errors/
│   └── contracts/
│
├── application/
│   ├── commands/
│   ├── queries/
│   ├── handlers/
│   ├── ports/
│   ├── results/
│   └── mappers/
│
├── infrastructure/
│   ├── persistence/
│   ├── repositories/
│   ├── provider-adapters/
│   └── mappers/
│
├── transport/
│   ├── server-actions/
│   ├── route-handlers/
│   ├── jobs/
│   └── scheduled-tasks/
│
├── public/
│   ├── commands.ts
│   ├── queries.ts
│   ├── events.ts
│   └── types.ts
│
└── index.ts
```

Not every module requires every directory immediately.

Folders should be created when they represent real responsibility, not to imitate enterprise architecture.

---

# 7.6.2.7 Domain Directory

The `domain/` directory contains business meaning that should remain independent of frameworks and providers.

Appropriate contents:

```text
Entities
Value Objects
Policies
Domain Events
Domain Errors
Pure Business Functions
Domain Contracts
```

Examples:

```text
domain/entities/article.ts
domain/value-objects/article-slug.ts
domain/policies/publication-readiness.ts
domain/errors/article-not-publishable.error.ts
```

The domain layer should not import:

* Next.js;
* React;
* database clients;
* Supabase SDKs;
* storage SDKs;
* email SDKs;
* Search SDKs;
* analytics SDKs;
* framework request objects.

---

# 7.6.2.8 Application Directory

The `application/` directory contains use-case coordination.

Appropriate contents:

```text
Commands
Queries
Handlers
Application Services
Ports
Result Types
Application Mappers
```

Examples:

```text
application/commands/publish-article.command.ts
application/handlers/publish-article.handler.ts
application/queries/get-published-article.query.ts
application/ports/article-repository.ts
application/results/publish-article.result.ts
```

The Application layer may depend on:

* its own Domain layer;
* shared backend primitives;
* contracts exported by other modules;
* infrastructure interfaces.

It should not depend directly on:

* React;
* page components;
* browser state;
* HTTP response objects;
* concrete database adapters;
* provider SDKs.

---

# 7.6.2.9 Infrastructure Directory

The `infrastructure/` directory contains technical implementations of ports.

Examples:

```text
PostgreSQL repositories
Supabase adapters
Object-storage adapters
Email-provider adapters
Search-provider adapters
Database mappers
Provider response mappers
```

Example:

```text
infrastructure/repositories/postgres-article.repository.ts
```

Infrastructure may import:

* database libraries;
* provider SDKs;
* server configuration;
* infrastructure utilities.

It may implement contracts from:

* the Application layer;
* the Domain layer where justified.

Infrastructure should not define business policy.

---

# 7.6.2.10 Transport Directory

The `transport/` directory adapts external invocation mechanisms to Application operations.

Possible transport sources:

* Server Actions;
* Route Handlers;
* background workers;
* scheduled jobs;
* command-line scripts;
* webhooks.

Example:

```text
transport/server-actions/publish-article.action.ts
```

Its responsibility is:

```text
Receive transport input
↓
Parse and validate transport shape
↓
Resolve request context
↓
Call application operation
↓
Map result to transport response
```

It should not contain the full use case.

---

# 7.6.2.11 Public Module Interface

Every module should expose an intentional public internal interface.

Recommended:

```text
editorial/
├── public/
│   ├── commands.ts
│   ├── queries.ts
│   ├── types.ts
│   └── events.ts
└── index.ts
```

Example:

```typescript
export type {
  PublishedContentReference,
  PublishedArticleSummary,
} from "./public/types";

export {
  getPublishedContentReference,
  listPublishedArticleSummaries,
} from "./public/queries";

export {
  publishArticle,
  archiveArticle,
} from "./public/commands";
```

Other modules should import from:

```text
backend/editorial
```

or:

```text
backend/editorial/public
```

They should not import from:

```text
backend/editorial/infrastructure/repositories/...
```

---

# 7.6.2.12 Internal Versus Public Code

A module’s internal code is private by default.

Private examples:

* repository implementations;
* database mappers;
* internal validation helpers;
* internal entity factories;
* private policies;
* provider-specific adapters;
* implementation-only result shapes.

Public examples:

* approved application commands;
* approved application queries;
* cross-module data contracts;
* published domain events;
* capability interfaces.

The absence of an explicit public export should mean:

```text
Not available to other modules.
```

---

# 7.6.2.13 Barrel File Discipline

Barrel files can simplify imports, but uncontrolled barrels may:

* expose private code accidentally;
* create circular imports;
* pull server-only modules into browser bundles;
* hide dependency direction.

Rules:

* each module may have one intentional public entry point;
* internal directories should not use broad wildcard exports casually;
* server-only and client-accessible exports must not be mixed;
* exports should be explicit;
* infrastructure code should not appear in the public module entry point.

Avoid:

```typescript
export * from "./domain";
export * from "./application";
export * from "./infrastructure";
```

Prefer explicit exports.

---

# 7.6.2.14 Allowed Intra-Module Dependencies

Inside one module, the normal dependency direction is:

```text
transport
    ↓
application
    ↓
domain
```

Infrastructure connects through abstractions:

```text
transport
    ↓
application
    ↓
port/interface
    ↑
infrastructure implementation
```

Allowed examples:

```text
transport → application
application → domain
infrastructure → application port
infrastructure → domain type
composition root → all module layers
```

---

# 7.6.2.15 Prohibited Intra-Module Dependencies

Prohibited examples:

```text
domain → infrastructure
domain → transport
application → transport
infrastructure → React component
domain → Next.js cache API
repository → Server Action
```

A Domain entity must not know:

* where it is stored;
* how a request arrived;
* which framework route triggered it;
* which UI is displaying it.

---

# 7.6.2.16 Cross-Module Dependency Rule

A module may depend only on another module’s declared public interface.

Good:

```typescript
import {
  getPublishedContentReference,
  type PublishedContentReference,
} from "@/backend/editorial";
```

Bad:

```typescript
import {
  PostgresArticleRepository,
} from "@/backend/editorial/infrastructure/repositories/postgres-article.repository";
```

Bad:

```typescript
import {
  ArticleRow,
} from "@/backend/editorial/infrastructure/persistence/article-row";
```

Cross-module access must use business-facing contracts.

---

# 7.6.2.17 Cross-Module Read Rule

Cross-module reads are permitted through exported queries.

Example:

```text
Journeys module
    ↓
Editorial public query:
getPublishedContentReference
```

The returned contract should be narrow.

Example:

```typescript
export type PublishedContentReference = {
  contentId: string;
  contentType: "article";
  slug: string;
  title: string;
  excerpt: string | null;
  publishedAt: string;
};
```

Journeys should not receive the full internal Article model merely to display or validate a reference.

---

# 7.6.2.18 Cross-Module Write Rule

A module may not directly mutate another module’s state.

Bad:

```text
Community module
    ↓
Directly updates Article publication record
```

Preferred:

```text
Community module
    ↓
Calls Editorial command
```

or:

```text
Application coordinator
    ↓
Invokes both modules through public commands
```

A cross-module operation must preserve the ownership of each affected concept.

---

# 7.6.2.19 Cross-Module Database Access

A module should not query another module’s tables as an ordinary application shortcut.

Bad:

```text
Journeys repository
    ↓
SELECT * FROM articles
```

Preferred:

```text
Journeys application query
    ↓
Editorial public query
```

Exceptions may exist for carefully designed read projections or reporting systems, but these must be explicit architectural decisions.

They must not become the default.

---

# 7.6.2.20 Shared Database Does Not Mean Shared Ownership

TWN’s modular monolith may initially use one PostgreSQL database.

That does not make all tables globally writable.

```text
Shared physical database
≠
Shared logical ownership
```

Logical ownership remains module-specific.

This distinction supports:

* clearer business boundaries;
* safer migrations;
* future extraction;
* reduced accidental coupling.

Detailed database boundaries belong to Section 7.8 and Volume 8.

---

# 7.6.2.21 Cross-Module Coordinators

Some workflows legitimately span multiple modules.

Example:

```text
Publish Journey
```

may require:

* Journey state from Journeys;
* content visibility from Editorial;
* content visibility from Notebook;
* Media availability from Media.

A cross-module Application coordinator may orchestrate the workflow.

Example:

```text
application-coordinators/
└── publish-journey.coordinator.ts
```

It should:

* call each module through public contracts;
* avoid accessing private repositories;
* preserve module ownership;
* manage the overall use-case sequence;
* return one typed result.

---

# 7.6.2.22 Coordinator Ownership

A coordinator should live:

* in the module owning the primary business operation; or
* in a small orchestration layer when no single module reasonably owns it.

Example:

```text
Publish Journey
```

belongs primarily to Journeys.

Therefore the Journey Application layer may coordinate public queries from Editorial, Notebook, and Media.

Do not create a global coordinator layer for every ordinary operation.

---

# 7.6.2.23 Domain Events for Secondary Reactions

A module may publish a domain event when a completed state change should trigger secondary reactions.

Example:

```text
ArticlePublished
```

Potential consumers:

* Search indexing;
* Analytics;
* Newsletter;
* cache invalidation;
* notification coordination.

The publishing module owns the event definition.

Consumers own their reactions.

Events are appropriate for:

* secondary effects;
* derived projections;
* asynchronous work;
* decoupled reactions.

They should not replace direct calls when an immediate result is required for the main transaction.

---

# 7.6.2.24 Commands Versus Events Across Modules

Use a command when:

```text
One module explicitly asks another module to perform an operation.
```

Example:

```text
Administration
    ↓
Suspend User command
```

Use an event when:

```text
A module announces that something has already happened.
```

Example:

```text
ArticlePublished
```

A command is imperative.

An event is historical.

Do not name a command like an event or an event like a command.

---

# 7.6.2.25 Event Payload Boundaries

A domain event should contain enough information for consumers to identify and process the event.

Example:

```typescript
type ArticlePublishedEvent = {
  eventId: string;
  occurredAt: string;
  articleId: string;
  revisionId: string;
  actorUserId: string;
};
```

Avoid embedding the full Article body unless a consumer genuinely needs a snapshot and the consequences are understood.

Consumers may retrieve additional public information through the owning module’s query interface.

---

# 7.6.2.26 Circular Dependency Prevention

The target dependency graph must remain acyclic.

Bad:

```text
Editorial
    ↓
Journeys
    ↓
Editorial
```

Circular imports create:

* unclear ownership;
* initialization problems;
* difficult testing;
* extraction difficulty;
* hidden coupling.

---

# 7.6.2.27 Resolving Circular Dependencies

When a cycle appears, use one of these approaches.

## Option 1 — Reverse the dependency

Determine which module truly owns the operation.

## Option 2 — Extract a narrow contract

Example:

```text
ContentReferenceResolver
```

rather than exposing the entire Editorial module.

## Option 3 — Use an application coordinator

A higher orchestration layer invokes both modules.

## Option 4 — Use a domain event

Appropriate when the dependency concerns a completed secondary reaction.

## Option 5 — Move a shared concept

Only when the concept is genuinely shared and not owned by either existing module.

Do not solve cycles by:

* dynamic imports;
* global service locators;
* duplicated business logic;
* importing internal files through relative-path tricks.

---

# 7.6.2.28 Shared Kernel Definition

A shared kernel is a small set of backend concepts that several modules may depend on.

Potential contents:

```text
Entity ID type
Clock interface
Request context
Pagination primitives
Application result base
Domain-event metadata
Idempotency key
Transaction interface
```

The shared kernel must remain deliberately small.

---

# 7.6.2.29 What Does Not Belong in Shared

Do not place the following in `shared/` merely because several modules use them:

* Article rules;
* Journey rules;
* Media policy;
* Community moderation logic;
* User role rules;
* publication workflows;
* newsletter consent rules.

Shared business logic often indicates unclear ownership.

---

# 7.6.2.30 Shared Utility Criteria

A utility belongs in shared backend foundations only when:

1. it is business-neutral;
2. at least two modules genuinely need it;
