* Idempotency keys are scoped and bound to normalized command input.
* Durable atomic mechanisms prevent concurrent duplicate execution.
* Concurrency conflicts are detected rather than silently overwritten.
* Bulk commands explicitly define atomic or partial-success behaviour.
* Large bulk work moves to bounded asynchronous execution.
* Destructive commands clearly distinguish archive, delete, unlink, and purge.
* High-risk commands support stronger authorization and confirmation.
* Scheduled and worker commands use explicit System Actors.
* Commands may become obsolete and must handle authoritative current state.
* Direct function invocation remains preferred over a command bus.
* Middleware and command-bus infrastructure are deferred until demonstrated need.

**Reasons:**

* preserves TWN’s business language;
* prevents generic CRUD from controlling workflows;
* provides one clear home for each mutation;
* makes authorization and concurrency explicit;
* supports Server Actions, APIs, workers, and schedulers;
* protects retries from duplicate mutation;
* improves Audit and event generation;
* keeps command flows testable;
* reduces hidden framework and provider coupling;
* fits the modular-monolith architecture.

**Consequences:**

Positive:

* mutations are easy to identify and trace;
* expected failures are explicit;
* business operations are reusable across transports;
* duplicate and concurrent execution can be handled deliberately;
* high-risk operations become reviewable;
* command tests can isolate coordination.

Costs:

* more explicit command and result types;
* mapping is required at transport boundaries;
* idempotency storage adds complexity for selected commands;
* cross-module command coordination requires discipline;
* developers must resist generic update shortcuts;
* direct execution conventions must remain consistent without a bus.

**Expected lifetime:** The explicit command model is permanent. Execution composition, middleware, and dispatch mechanisms may evolve if TWN’s scale and number of transports justify them.

---

# Completion Criteria for 7.6.7

This subsection is complete when:

* a command is clearly defined;
* command, query, event, job, form, and database mutation are distinguished;
* naming and granularity rules are established;
* command input categories are documented;
* Actor and operation contexts are controlled;
* validation and authorization stages are defined;
* handler execution order is established;
* Domain rule ownership is preserved;
* transaction and side-effect boundaries are clear;
* typed command results are required;
* expected and unexpected failures are separated;
* idempotency principles are defined;
* concurrency expectations are documented;
* bulk and destructive command rules are established;
* system and scheduled commands are covered;
* testing requirements are defined;
* direct handler invocation is selected over an initial command bus;
* command governance and anti-patterns are recorded.

---

## End of 7.6.7

The next subsection is:

# **7.6.8 — Query Architecture**

It will remain strictly within Backend Architecture and define:

* query semantics;
* public versus private queries;
* read-model design;
* query handlers;
* visibility and authorization;
* projections;
* filtering;
* pagination;
* stable ordering;
* cross-module read composition;
* read consistency;
* query caching boundaries;
* large result protection;
* query errors;
* query testing;
* why query paths do not need to reconstruct Domain aggregates.

[1]: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/architecture/patterns/cqrs?utm_source=chatgpt.com "CQRS Pattern - Azure Architecture Center"
[2]: https://nextjs.org/docs/app/guides/data-security?utm_source=chatgpt.com "Guides: Data Security"
[3]: https://www.postgresql.org/docs/current/tutorial-transactions.html?utm_source=chatgpt.com "Documentation: 18: 3.4. Transactions"
[4]: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/architecture/microservices/microservice-ddd-cqrs-patterns/infrastructure-persistence-layer-design?utm_source=chatgpt.com "Designing the infrastructure persistence layer - .NET"
[5]: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/architecture/microservices/microservice-ddd-cqrs-patterns/domain-events-design-implementation?utm_source=chatgpt.com "Domain events: Design and implementation - .NET"
[6]: https://docs.aws.amazon.com/wellarchitected/latest/framework/rel_prevent_interaction_failure_idempotent.html?utm_source=chatgpt.com "REL04-BP04 Make mutating operations idempotent"
[7]: https://docs.aws.amazon.com/powertools/python/latest/utilities/idempotency/?utm_source=chatgpt.com "Idempotency - Powertools for AWS Lambda (Python)"
[8]: https://www.postgresql.org/docs/current/transaction-iso.html?utm_source=chatgpt.com "Documentation: 18: 13.2. Transaction Isolation"

# 7.6.8 Query Architecture

**Status:** Backend Architecture Specification
**Parent chapter:** Volume 7 — Technical Architecture
**Current scope:** Section 7.6 — Backend Architecture
**Applies to:** Editorial, Notebook, Journeys, Community, Search, Profiles, Analytics, Newsletter, Media, Administration, and all future TWN read operations
**Primary objective:** Define how TWN retrieves information, composes read models, enforces visibility rules, optimizes performance, and serves data without leaking business mutations into read operations

---

# Purpose

This subsection defines TWN's Query Architecture.

It establishes:

* what a query is;
* query naming;
* query responsibilities;
* query handlers;
* read models;
* view models;
* visibility rules;
* authorization;
* filtering;
* sorting;
* pagination;
* projections;
* cross-module reads;
* caching boundaries;
* consistency expectations;
* search integration;
* large dataset protection;
* query testing;
* query governance.

The central rule is:

> Queries answer questions. They do not change authoritative business state.

---

# 7.6.8.1 Query Definition

A query is a request for information.

Examples:

```text
GetHomepage
GetFeaturedArticles
GetArticleBySlug
GetJourneyPage
GetNotebookEntry
GetReaderProfile
SearchArticles
GetAdminDashboard
GetNewsletterAnalytics
```

A query retrieves information.

It does not request a mutation.

---

# 7.6.8.2 Query Meaning

A query answers:

```text
What exists?

What is visible?

What should be displayed?

What information matches?
```

It does not answer:

```text
Please create.

Please publish.

Please archive.

Please update.
```

Those belong to commands.

---

# 7.6.8.3 Query Versus Command

Command:

```text
PublishArticle
```

Question:

```text
Can this article be displayed publicly?
```

Query:

```text
GetPublicArticle
```

Command changes state.

Query observes state.

---

# 7.6.8.4 Query Versus Repository

This distinction is important.

A repository retrieves domain-owned state.

A query retrieves presentation-ready information.

Repository:

```typescript
articleRepository.findById(id)
```

Query:

```typescript
getHomepage()
```

The homepage is not a Domain entity.

It is a composition of information.

Queries may aggregate data from many sources.

---

# 7.6.8.5 Query Versus Search

Search is a specialized query.

Example:

```text
SearchArticles
```

still remains a query because it retrieves information.

Search architecture will be expanded later in Section 7.12.

---

# 7.6.8.6 Query Naming

Queries should be named according to the information being requested.

Good:

```text
GetHomepage

GetArticlePage

GetJourneyOverview

GetCommunityFeed

SearchArticles
```

Weak:

```text
FetchData

LoadStuff

GetEverything

QueryDatabase
```

Names should reveal intent.

---

# 7.6.8.7 Query Handler Position

Each query should have a dedicated handler.

Example:

```typescript
type GetHomepageHandler = (
  query: GetHomepageQuery,
) => Promise<GetHomepageResult>;
```

The handler coordinates retrieval.

It does not perform business mutations.

---

# 7.6.8.8 Query Input

Queries should contain only information required to retrieve results.

Example:

```typescript
type GetArticlePageQuery = {
  slug: string;
  actor: ActorContext;
};
```

The query contains:

* lookup information;
* viewer context;
* filters;
* pagination;
* sorting.

It should not contain mutation intent.

---

# 7.6.8.9 Query Output

Queries return read models.

Not domain entities.

Example:

Bad:

```typescript
ArticleAggregate
```

Preferred:

```typescript
ArticlePageView
```

The UI needs presentation data.

Not internal domain behavior.

---

# 7.6.8.10 Read Model Definition

A read model is a projection optimized for retrieval.

Example:

```typescript
type ArticleCard = {
  id: string;
  slug: string;
  title: string;
  excerpt: string;
  coverImage: string | null;
  readingTime: number;
  publishedAt: string;
};
```

This structure exists for reading.

Not for editing.

Not for persistence.

---

# 7.6.8.11 Read Models Are Disposable

A read model may be regenerated.

A read model is not the source of truth.

The database remains authoritative.

The read model is a representation.

---

# 7.6.8.12 Query Handler Responsibilities

A query handler may:

* validate query input;
* resolve visibility;
* apply authorization;
* fetch required data;
* compose information;
* map data into read models;
* paginate results;
* return projections.

It should not:

* publish content;
* update state;
* write audit records;
* send emails;
* create entities.

---

# 7.6.8.13 No Hidden Writes

Queries should not secretly mutate state.

Avoid:

```text
GetArticle
    ↓
Create reading history
```

unless the mutation is explicitly modeled elsewhere.

Hidden writes create:

* caching issues;
* repeatability issues;
* debugging complexity.

---

# 7.6.8.14 Public Queries

Public queries require no authenticated user.

Examples:

```text
GetHomepage

GetPublicArticle

GetPublicJourney

SearchPublishedContent
```

These queries operate with an anonymous actor.

---

# 7.6.8.15 Private Queries

Private queries require authenticated users.

Examples:

```text
GetEditorDashboard

GetMyDrafts

GetMyBookmarks

GetAdminAnalytics
```

These require authorization checks.

---

# 7.6.8.16 Query Authorization

Queries require authorization just like commands.

Question:

```text
May this actor see this information?
```

This is separate from:

```text
May this actor modify this information?
```

Both matter.

---

# 7.6.8.17 Visibility Rules

Visibility determines what content may be shown.

Example:

Public article:

```text
published = true
```

Draft article:

```text
published = false
```

Anonymous readers should not receive draft content.

The query layer enforces this.

---

# 7.6.8.18 Resource Existence vs Visibility

A resource may exist but remain invisible.

Example:

```text
Draft article exists
```

Anonymous query result:

```text
Not Found
```

rather than:

```text
Forbidden
```

to avoid leaking private content existence.

Visibility policy determines this behavior.

---

# 7.6.8.19 Actor-Aware Queries

The same query may return different results.

Example:

```text
GetArticlePage
```

Anonymous:

```text
Published content only
```

Editor:

```text
Published + draft preview
```

Administrator:

```text
All states
```

The query handler applies the appropriate visibility rules.

---

# 7.6.8.20 Query Composition

Many pages require information from several modules.

Example homepage:

```text
Featured Articles
Latest Articles
Featured Journey
Community Highlights
Newsletter CTA
```

The homepage query composes these pieces.

The homepage itself is not a domain entity.

---

# 7.6.8.21 Cross-Module Read Composition

Read composition is allowed.

Example:

```text
Editorial
+
Community
+
Newsletter
```

combined into:

```text
Homepage
```

This does not violate modular boundaries because reading information is different from mutating ownership.

---

# 7.6.8.22 Query Projections

Queries should retrieve only required fields.

Bad:

```sql
SELECT *
```

Preferred:

```sql
SELECT
 title,
 slug,
 excerpt,
 published_at
```

Projection reduces:

* memory;
* transfer size;
* serialization;
* latency.

---

# 7.6.8.23 Query-Specific Views

A query may have a dedicated projection.

Example:

Homepage card:

```typescript
ArticleCard
```

Article page:

```typescript
ArticlePage
```

Search result:

```typescript
SearchArticleResult
```

Do not force every screen to reuse one giant model.

---

# 7.6.8.24 Stable Sorting

Queries returning collections must define ordering.

Bad:

```text
Latest articles
```

without ordering.

Preferred:

```text
published_at DESC,
id DESC
```

Stable ordering prevents inconsistent pagination.

---

# 7.6.8.25 Filtering

Filtering should be explicit.

Example:

```typescript
type SearchArticlesQuery = {
  keyword?: string;
  category?: string;
  author?: string;
  page?: number;
};
```

Filters should be validated.

Unknown filters should be rejected or ignored according to policy.

---

# 7.6.8.26 Pagination

Every potentially large collection requires pagination.

Examples:

```text
Articles
Journeys
Notebook entries
Users
Comments
Search results
```

Avoid unbounded queries.

---

# 7.6.8.27 Offset Pagination

Simple pages may use:

```text
page
limit
```

Example:

```text
Articles page 3
```

Offset pagination is simple.

But becomes expensive for very large datasets.

---

# 7.6.8.28 Cursor Pagination

Large feeds should use cursor pagination.

Example:

```text
Community feed
Search feed
Activity feed
```

Cursor pagination scales better.

The exact strategy belongs to Volume 9 and Database Architecture.

---

# 7.6.8.29 Pagination Limits

Every query should define maximum limits.

Bad:

```text
limit=100000
```

Preferred:

```text
max limit = 50
```

or:

```text
max limit = 100
```

depending on the use case.

---

# 7.6.8.30 Query Result Size Protection

Query handlers must protect infrastructure.

Examples:

* maximum page size;
* maximum filter count;
* maximum sort count;
* maximum nested expansions.

A reader should never accidentally request the entire database.

---

# End of 7.6.8.30

We are still inside **7.6.8 Query Architecture**.

Remaining major sections include:

* 7.6.8.31 Read Models & DTO Design
* 7.6.8.32 Search Query Architecture
* 7.6.8.33 Caching Boundaries
* 7.6.8.34 Consistency Models
* 7.6.8.35 Query Performance Rules
* 7.6.8.36 Query Security
* 7.6.8.37 Query Testing
* 7.6.8.38 Query Governance
* 7.6.8.39 Architecture Decision Record
* 7.6.8.40 Completion Criteria

So 7.6.8 is roughly **30–35% complete**. The remaining sections are the more advanced and important parts where we define caching, search, consistency, projections, and performance strategy for TWN.

# 7.6.8 Query Architecture — Continued

---

# 7.6.8.31 Read Model and DTO Design

A query should return a model designed for the consumer of that query.

It should not return:

* a Domain aggregate;
* an ORM entity;
* a database row;
* an unrestricted generic object;
* a provider response.

The query model should represent exactly what the caller is allowed and expected to read.

Example:

```typescript
export type PublishedArticlePageReadModel = {
  articleId: string;
  slug: string;
  title: string;
  subtitle: string | null;
  excerpt: string;
  body: PublishedContentDocument;
  publishedAt: string;

  author: {
    authorId: string;
    displayName: string;
    slug: string;
    avatarUrl: string | null;
  };

  category: {
    categoryId: string;
    name: string;
    slug: string;
  };

  tags: Array<{
    tagId: string;
    name: string;
    slug: string;
  }>;

  journeyContexts: Array<{
    journeyId: string;
    journeySlug: string;
    journeyTitle: string;
    chapterId: string;
    chapterTitle: string;
    position: number;
  }>;
};
```

This model exists for one read purpose:

```text
Render a published Article page.
```

It should not automatically be reused for:

* Article editing;
* Search results;
* homepage cards;
* exports;
* mobile APIs;
* internal Analytics.

Microsoft’s simplified CQRS guidance supports building query-specific view models directly for the information required by a consumer, without forcing the query side to reconstruct write-side aggregates. ([Microsoft Learn][1])

---

# 7.6.8.32 Read Model Naming

Read models should reveal:

* visibility;
* purpose;
* audience;
* content scope.

Good:

```text
PublishedArticlePageReadModel
ArticleEditorReadModel
JourneyCardReadModel
ModerationQueueItemReadModel
NewsletterSubscriberAdminReadModel
```

Weak:

```text
ArticleDTO
ContentData
Result
ArticleObject
```

`ArticleDTO` does not reveal whether it contains:

* public content;
* unpublished content;
* editorial metadata;
* private Author information;
* administrative state.

Purpose-specific naming reduces accidental data exposure.

---

# 7.6.8.33 Read Model Ownership

The module owning the query should normally own its read model.

Example:

```text
Editorial module
    owns
PublishedArticlePageReadModel
```

A frontend-specific mapping layer may convert that result into a page view model.

```text
Backend query result
    ↓
Frontend mapper
    ↓
React component props
```

This prevents the Backend Query layer from depending on:

* React;
* JSX;
* frontend Design System components;
* browser state.

---

# 7.6.8.34 Read Models Must Be Immutable to Consumers

Query results should be treated as immutable snapshots.

A consumer must not change a read model and assume authoritative state changed.

Bad:

```typescript
const article = await getArticlePage(query);

article.title = "New title";
```

This only mutates local memory.

Authoritative changes require commands such as:

```text
SaveArticleDraft
RenameJourney
UpdateAuthorProfile
```

This separation prevents confusion between:

```text
What was read
```

and:

```text
What was persisted
```

---

# 7.6.8.35 Read Model Data Minimization

Every field returned by a query should answer:

```text
Why does this consumer need this field?
```

Do not return:

* password hashes;
* session identifiers;
* provider tokens;
* unpublished content;
* private email addresses;
* internal moderation notes;
* Audit metadata;
* deleted content;
* raw storage keys;

unless the specific authorized read use case genuinely requires them.

Data minimization improves:

* privacy;
* security;
* payload size;
* clarity;
* future compatibility.

Public queries should have especially narrow contracts.

---

# 7.6.8.36 Separate Public and Private Read Models

Do not use one model and remove fields conditionally across several call sites.

Prefer separate explicit contracts.

Example:

```typescript
export type PublicAuthorReadModel = {
  authorId: string;
  displayName: string;
  slug: string;
  biography: string | null;
  avatarUrl: string | null;
};
```

Private editorial model:

```typescript
export type AuthorAdminReadModel = {
  authorId: string;
  displayName: string;
  slug: string;
  biography: string | null;
  avatarUrl: string | null;

  status: "draft" | "active" | "suspended";
  internalNotes: string | null;
  createdAt: string;
  updatedAt: string;
  version: number;
};
```

This is safer than:

```typescript
type AuthorReadModel = {
  // Every possible field.
};
```

followed by caller-specific filtering.

---

# 7.6.8.37 Query Result Contracts

Query handlers should return controlled results.

Example:

```typescript
export type GetPublishedArticleResult =
  | {
      kind: "found";
      article: PublishedArticlePageReadModel;
    }
  | {
      kind: "not-found";
    };
```

Private query:

```typescript
export type GetArticleEditorResult =
  | {
      kind: "found";
      article: ArticleEditorReadModel;
    }
  | {
      kind: "not-found";
    }
  | {
      kind: "forbidden";
    };
```

Expected query outcomes should not require exception handling.

---

# 7.6.8.38 Query Exceptions

Exceptions are reserved for unexpected failures such as:

* database unavailable;
* malformed persisted data;
* broken infrastructure contract;
* unexpected provider response;
* programming error;
* missing required configuration.

Expected absence is not an exception.

Bad:

```typescript
throw new Error("Article not found");
```

for an ordinary public lookup.

Preferred:

```typescript
return {
  kind: "not-found",
};
```

---

# 7.6.8.39 Query Mapping Boundaries

Query mapping may occur in stages.

```text
Database projection
    ↓
Backend read model
    ↓
Transport response
    ↓
Frontend view model
    ↓
Component props
```

Not every stage requires a new object.

Mapping is justified when it protects a meaningful boundary.

For example:

* database timestamps become ISO strings;
* storage keys become approved public URLs;
* internal status names become public visibility states;
* provider result types become TWN-owned Search results.

Avoid creating mapping layers that merely copy identical objects without providing isolation or meaning.

---

# 7.6.8.40 Query Handler Structure

Conceptual implementation:

```typescript
export function createGetPublishedArticleHandler(
  dependencies: {
    articleQueries: PublishedArticleQueries;
  },
) {
  return async function getPublishedArticle(
    query: GetPublishedArticleQuery,
  ): Promise<GetPublishedArticleResult> {
    const article =
      await dependencies.articleQueries.findBySlug(
        query.slug,
      );

    if (!article) {
      return {
        kind: "not-found",
      };
    }

    return {
      kind: "found",
      article,
    };
  };
}
```

A more complex private query may include authorization:

```typescript
export function createGetArticleEditorHandler(
  dependencies: {
    articleQueries: EditorialArticleQueries;
    authorization: EditorialAuthorization;
  },
) {
  return async function getArticleEditor(
    query: GetArticleEditorQuery,
  ): Promise<GetArticleEditorResult> {
    const coarseDecision =
      await dependencies.authorization
        .canAccessEditorialWorkspace(
          query.actor,
        );

    if (!coarseDecision.allowed) {
      return {
        kind: "forbidden",
      };
    }

    const article =
      await dependencies.articleQueries
        .findEditorView(query.articleId);

    if (!article) {
      return {
        kind: "not-found",
      };
    }

    const resourceDecision =
      await dependencies.authorization
        .canViewArticleDraft({
          actor: query.actor,
          article,
        });

    if (!resourceDecision.allowed) {
      return {
        kind: "forbidden",
      };
    }

    return {
      kind: "found",
      article,
    };
  };
}
```

---

# 7.6.8.41 Queries Do Not Need Domain Aggregate Reconstruction

The write side may require:

```text
Article aggregate
Journey aggregate
Moderation entity
```

to enforce state transitions.

The read side may directly retrieve a purpose-specific projection.

Example:

```sql
SELECT
  a.id,
  a.slug,
  r.title,
  r.excerpt,
  a.published_at,
  au.display_name
FROM articles AS a
JOIN article_revisions AS r
  ON r.id = a.published_revision_id
JOIN authors AS au
  ON au.id = a.author_id
WHERE a.slug = $1
  AND a.status = 'published';
```

The query does not need to:

1. load every Article revision;
2. reconstruct the complete Article aggregate;
3. invoke Article behaviour;
4. convert it back into a display object.

A simplified CQRS read side may query storage directly and return consumer-oriented projections, while the write side retains richer Domain modelling. ([Microsoft Learn][1])

---

# 7.6.8.42 Query Persistence Ports

Read operations should use query-oriented ports.

Example:

```typescript
export interface PublishedArticleQueries {
  findBySlug(
    slug: string,
  ): Promise<PublishedArticlePageReadModel | null>;

  listCards(
    request: ListPublishedArticleRequest,
  ): Promise<CursorPage<ArticleCardReadModel>>;
}
```

Editorial private reads:

```typescript
export interface EditorialArticleQueries {
  findEditorView(
    articleId: string,
  ): Promise<ArticleEditorReadModel | null>;

  listDrafts(
    request: ListArticleDraftsRequest,
  ): Promise<CursorPage<ArticleDraftListItem>>;
}
```

Avoid forcing query handlers to use mutation-oriented repository methods when those methods do not match the read requirement.

---

# 7.6.8.43 Direct SQL Position

Query adapters may use:

* parameterized SQL;
* a query builder;
* a lightweight ORM;
* database views;
* materialized projections;

depending on the selected persistence stack.

Direct SQL is acceptable inside the Infrastructure Query layer when:

* it is parameterized;
* it remains module-owned;
* it returns a defined read model;
* it is tested;
* it does not leak outward;
* it does not duplicate business mutation rules.

OWASP recommends parameterized queries or prepared statements rather than concatenating untrusted values into SQL. ([OWASP Cheat Sheet Series][2])

Bad:

```typescript
const sql =
  `SELECT * FROM articles
   WHERE author_id = '${input.authorId}'`;
```

Preferred:

```typescript
await database.query(
  `
    SELECT id, slug, title
    FROM published_article_cards
    WHERE author_id = $1
  `,
  [input.authorId],
);
```

---

# 7.6.8.44 Dynamic Filter Construction

Dynamic queries must use approved allow-lists.

Bad:

```typescript
const orderBy = request.query.sort;

const sql =
  `SELECT ... ORDER BY ${orderBy}`;
```

Parameter placeholders normally represent values, not arbitrary SQL identifiers.

Therefore sortable fields should be mapped explicitly:

```typescript
const sortColumns = {
  newest: "published_at DESC, id DESC",
  oldest: "published_at ASC, id ASC",
  title: "normalized_title ASC, id ASC",
} as const;

const orderBy =
  sortColumns[input.sort];
```

Only known keys are accepted.

Do not pass raw client-supplied:

* column names;
* directions;
* table names;
* SQL fragments;
* expressions.

---

# 7.6.8.45 Stable Ordering Requirement

Collection results must use deterministic ordering.

PostgreSQL does not guarantee a particular row order unless an `ORDER BY` clause defines it. Pagination without deterministic ordering can therefore produce inconsistent pages. ([PostgreSQL][3])

Bad:

```sql
SELECT id, title
FROM articles
LIMIT 20;
```

Preferred:

```sql
SELECT id, title, published_at
FROM articles
WHERE status = 'published'
ORDER BY published_at DESC, id DESC
LIMIT 20;
```

The secondary unique field creates a stable tie-breaker when several records share the same publication time.

---

# 7.6.8.46 Sort Semantics

Every supported sort should define:

* primary field;
* direction;
* tie-breaker;
* null placement;
* locale or collation expectations;
* whether the order remains stable across pagination.

Example:

```text
Newest published content

1. published_at descending
2. id descending
```

Example:

```text
Journey item order

1. chapter_position ascending
2. item_position ascending
3. membership_id ascending
```

Do not rely on accidental insertion order.

---

# 7.6.8.47 Offset Pagination Position

Offset pagination may be suitable for:

* small administrative tables;
* bounded archives;
* pages where jumping to an approximate numbered page matters;
* datasets known to remain modest.

Example:

```sql
ORDER BY published_at DESC, id DESC
LIMIT $1
OFFSET $2;
```

Limitations include:

* increasing work at deep offsets;
* instability when records are inserted or removed between requests;
* repeated or missing entries during changing feeds.

TWN should not assume offset pagination is ideal for every collection.

---

# 7.6.8.48 Keyset Pagination Position

Keyset pagination, often exposed as cursor pagination, is preferred for large or frequently changing ordered feeds.

Example:

First page:

```sql
SELECT
  id,
  slug,
  title,
  published_at
FROM articles
WHERE status = 'published'
ORDER BY published_at DESC, id DESC
LIMIT $1;
```

Next page:

```sql
SELECT
  id,
  slug,
  title,
  published_at
FROM articles
WHERE status = 'published'
  AND (
    published_at,
    id
  ) < (
    $2,
    $3
  )
ORDER BY published_at DESC, id DESC
LIMIT $1;
```

The cursor represents the final ordering values from the previous page.

The exact implementation must match:

* sort direction;
* null behaviour;
* selected indexes;
* cursor serialization.

---

# 7.6.8.49 Application Cursor Versus Database Cursor

A public pagination cursor is not the same as a long-lived PostgreSQL server cursor.

TWN’s application cursor should normally be:

```text
An opaque encoded representation
of stable ordering values.
```

Example internal cursor payload:

```typescript
type PublishedArticleCursorPayload = {
  version: 1;
  publishedAt: string;
  articleId: string;
};
```

PostgreSQL server cursors maintain a database-side position and are primarily useful for retrieving rows incrementally within a database session. They should not be confused with stateless web-pagination tokens. ([PostgreSQL][4])

---

# 7.6.8.50 Cursor Requirements

An application cursor should be:

* opaque to clients;
* versioned;
* integrity-protected where tampering matters;
* tied to one sorting strategy;
* bounded in length;
* validated on receipt;
* free from sensitive information;
* rejected when malformed or unsupported.

Example:

```typescript
type CursorEnvelope = {
  version: 1;
  sort: "published-desc";
  publishedAt: string;
  articleId: string;
};
```

The transport may encode it.

The query handler interprets the validated cursor contract.

---

# 7.6.8.51 Cursor Tampering

A cursor should not grant authorization.

Even when a caller modifies a valid cursor value, the query must still apply:

* visibility;
* tenancy or ownership scope;
* authorization;
* filter constraints.

The cursor only indicates where pagination should continue.

It must not contain trusted permission state.

---

# 7.6.8.52 Filter and Cursor Compatibility

The cursor must correspond to the filter and sort context in which it was created.

Example:

```text
First request:
category = engineering
sort = newest

Next cursor:
must continue
category = engineering
sort = newest
```

Changing to:

```text
category = leadership
```

while reusing the previous cursor should:

* reject the cursor; or
* treat it as a fresh query.

Do not silently combine unrelated cursor state and filters.

A cursor fingerprint may include normalized filter identity where needed.

---

# 7.6.8.53 Page Result Contract

Recommended:

```typescript
export type CursorPage<TItem> = {
  items: TItem[];
  pageInfo: {
    hasNextPage: boolean;
    nextCursor: string | null;
  };
};
```

When bidirectional navigation is required:

```typescript
export type BidirectionalCursorPage<TItem> = {
  items: TItem[];
  pageInfo: {
    hasNextPage: boolean;
    hasPreviousPage: boolean;
    nextCursor: string | null;
    previousCursor: string | null;
  };
};
```

Do not calculate expensive total counts for every feed unless the product genuinely needs them.

---

# 7.6.8.54 Total Count Position

Exact total counts may require substantial database work on large datasets.

PostgreSQL documentation notes that an exact `count(*)` over an entire table requires work proportional to the table or index size rather than being a free metadata lookup. ([PostgreSQL][5])

Therefore queries should distinguish:

```text
Exact total required
```

from:

```text
Only need to know whether more results exist
```

For cursor feeds, fetch:

```text
requested limit + 1
```

to determine `hasNextPage` without executing a separate exact count.

---

# 7.6.8.55 Approximate Counts

Approximate counts may be acceptable for:

* large public archives;
* Analytics dashboards;
* operational estimates;
* discovery labels.

They are not acceptable where exactness affects:

* billing;
* permissions;
* legal obligations;
* completion checks;
* bounded workflow limits.

The read model must communicate whether a count is:

```text
exact
estimated
stale
```

when that distinction matters.

---

# 7.6.8.56 Query Index Alignment

Indexes should support observed query predicates and ordering.

Example query:

```sql
WHERE status = 'published'
ORDER BY published_at DESC, id DESC
```

may justify an index aligned with:

```text
status
published_at
id
```

depending on data distribution and actual query plans.

B-tree indexes support ordered comparisons and can be useful for equality and range conditions on sortable values. ([PostgreSQL][3])

Index decisions must be validated through measurement rather than copied mechanically from a query’s field order.

Detailed index design belongs to Database Architecture and Volume 8.

---

# 7.6.8.57 Query Plan Verification

Important queries should be verified using PostgreSQL planning tools such as:

```sql
EXPLAIN
```

and, in controlled environments:

```sql
EXPLAIN ANALYZE
```

The objective is to determine:

* whether intended indexes are used;
* whether large scans occur;
* whether sorting spills;
* how many rows are processed;
* where execution time is spent.

Do not optimize solely from intuition.

`EXPLAIN ANALYZE` executes the query, so it must be used carefully with mutations and production data.

---

# 7.6.8.58 Avoid `SELECT *`

Query adapters should list required columns explicitly.

Reasons:

* prevents unintended sensitive-field exposure;
* reduces transferred data;
* stabilizes mappings;
* makes schema dependencies visible;
* avoids silently expanding payloads when columns are added.

Example:

```sql
SELECT
  id,
  slug,
  title,
  excerpt,
  published_at
FROM published_articles;
```

not:

```sql
SELECT *
FROM published_articles;
```

---

# 7.6.8.59 Avoid N+1 Query Patterns

An N+1 pattern occurs when:

1. one query retrieves a collection;
2. one additional query runs for each item.

Example:

```text
1 query: 20 Articles

20 queries:
one Author query per Article
```

Potential solutions:

* joins;
* batched lookups;
* precomputed projections;
* query-specific aggregate reads;
* a DataLoader-like batching layer where justified.

Do not solve every N+1 issue by loading one enormous object graph.

The query should retrieve the specific data required for the read model.

---

# 7.6.8.60 Parallel Query Execution

Independent query components may run concurrently.

Example homepage:

```text
Featured Article
Featured Journey
Approved Shared Pages
```

may be retrieved in parallel when:

* they are independent;
* connection capacity supports it;
* failure policy is defined;
* concurrency is bounded.

Bad:

```typescript
await Promise.all(
  hundredsOfQueries,
);
```

Unbounded parallelism can exhaust:

* database connections;
* provider quotas;
* memory;
* request deadlines.

Parallelism must remain deliberate and measured.

---

# 7.6.8.61 Composite Query Failure Policy

A composite query should classify each component.

Example homepage:

| Component                 | Requirement                |
| ------------------------- | -------------------------- |
| Primary editorial feature | Required                   |
| Main navigation content   | Required                   |
| Community highlight       | Optional                   |
| Newsletter statistics     | Not required for rendering |

Possible result:

```typescript
type HomepageReadModel = {
  featuredArticle: ArticleHeroReadModel;
  journeys: JourneyCardReadModel[];
  communityHighlight:
    | {
        kind: "available";
        items: SharedPageCardReadModel[];
      }
    | {
        kind: "unavailable";
      };
};
```

Do not represent infrastructure failure as:

```text
The Community has no content.
```

Failure and genuine emptiness are different states.

---

# 7.6.8.62 Cross-Module Query Composition

Composite queries should use public module query contracts.

Example:

```text
Homepage composition query
    ├── Editorial public query
    ├── Journeys public query
    ├── Notebook public query
    └── Community public query
```

It should not import:

* module repositories;
* internal database records;
* private infrastructure adapters.

This preserves module ownership even when one page presents several domains.

---

# 7.6.8.63 Cross-Module Join Position

Because the modular monolith may share one physical database, a cross-domain database join can sometimes be efficient.

However, ordinary module code should not bypass ownership merely because a join is technically possible.

A cross-module projection may be justified when:

* it is explicitly owned by a composition or reporting query;
* it is read-only;
* fields and dependencies are documented;
* it does not become a mutation path;
* module owners approve the contract;
* future extraction cost is understood.

Examples may include:

* homepage projection;
* global Search projection;
* Analytics reporting projection.

This should remain an explicit architectural choice, not an accidental shortcut.

---

# 7.6.8.64 Materialized Read Models

As query complexity grows, TWN may introduce precomputed read models.

Examples:

```text
Homepage projection
Global Search document
Journey content summary
Author publication statistics
Analytics daily summary
```

A materialized view or derived projection can reduce repeated expensive joins and computation. CQRS guidance recognizes materialized views as one possible way to optimize the read model independently of the write model. ([Microsoft Learn][6])

This introduces:

* freshness lag;
* rebuild requirements;
* event processing;
* reconciliation;
* additional storage.

It should be introduced only when measurement justifies it.

---

# 7.6.8.65 Materialized Projection Ownership

Every derived read model should define:

* source of truth;
* owning module;
* update trigger;
* expected freshness;
* rebuild method;
* failure behaviour;
* schema version;
* reconciliation process.

Example:

```text
Projection:
Published Article Search Document

Source of truth:
Editorial published revision

Owner:
Search module

Update trigger:
ArticlePublished or ArticleUpdated

Freshness:
Eventually consistent

Rebuild:
Reindex by Article ID or complete index rebuild
```

---

# 7.6.8.66 Query Consistency Models

Not every query needs the same consistency guarantee.

TWN recognizes:

```text
Strongly consistent reads
Read-your-writes
Eventually consistent reads
Cached public reads
Historical snapshot reads
```

The query owner must define which model applies.

---

# 7.6.8.67 Strongly Consistent Query

A strongly consistent query should reflect authoritative committed state at the time of the query, subject to the database transaction isolation and deployment architecture.

Potential uses:

* editor reload immediately after save;
* permission-sensitive administrative state;
* moderation decision verification;
* current scheduling state;
* destructive-action reference check.

Strong consistency should be used where stale data could produce a harmful decision.

---

# 7.6.8.68 Read-Your-Writes Expectation

After a successful mutation, the initiating User often expects to see the updated state.

Example:

```text
Save Article draft
    ↓
Return to Article editor
    ↓
See saved version
```

Possible approaches include:

* returning updated result data from the command;
* issuing a direct authoritative query;
* invalidating relevant cache;
* temporarily bypassing stale projection;
* attaching a version requirement to the subsequent query.

The chosen approach should be explicit.

---

# 7.6.8.69 Eventually Consistent Query

Eventual consistency is appropriate for derived or secondary systems.

Examples:

* Search index;
* recommendation results;
* Analytics summaries;
* public popularity ranking;
* newsletter engagement statistics.

After Article publication:

```text
Article page may become available immediately.

Search index may update shortly afterward.
```

The UI and operational monitoring should not incorrectly claim every system updates atomically.

---

# 7.6.8.70 Historical Snapshot Query

Some queries should retrieve an immutable historical state.

Examples:

* published revision at a specific date;
* Article revision preview;
* Audit investigation;
* previous Journey structure;
* generated export based on one revision.

The query must identify:

```text
Which version?
Which effective time?
Which publication state?
```

Do not substitute the current editable record when historical accuracy is required.

---

# 7.6.8.71 Transaction-Scoped Reads

A command may need to query state inside its transaction.

This is not an ordinary public Query handler.

Example:

```text
Publish Article transaction
    ↓
Verify current version and selected revision
```

Transaction-scoped reads belong to repositories or command-specific persistence ports.

Public Query Architecture should not expose transaction internals.

---

# 7.6.8.72 Cache Ownership Boundary

A Query handler defines:

* what information is requested;
* visibility;
* consistency expectation;
* freshness requirement.

It should not necessarily own framework-specific caching.

Conceptual separation:

```text
Application query
    ↓
Read result

Cache adapter or framework boundary
    ↓
Stores or reuses result
```

Detailed cache-key, invalidation, tagging, and CDN behaviour belongs to Section 7.13.

---

# 7.6.8.73 Cacheability Classification

Each query should be classified.

## Public and broadly reusable

Examples:

* published Article;
* public Journey;
* public Category archive.

Potentially cacheable.

## User-specific

Examples:

* personal bookmarks;
* editor dashboard;
* account preferences.

Must not be placed in shared public caches.

## Permission-sensitive

Examples:

* moderation queue;
* unpublished preview;
* administrative User profile.

Requires private handling and appropriate authorization on every authoritative retrieval.

## Rapidly changing

Examples:

* live operational status;
* active upload processing state.

May require short freshness or no cache.

---

# 7.6.8.74 Cache Key Safety

A cache key must represent all dimensions that materially affect the result.

Potential dimensions:

* query type;
* resource identifier;
* locale;
* publication revision;
* filter;
* sort;
* pagination cursor;
* viewer scope where private caching is explicitly supported.

A public cache key must never omit a dimension that changes visibility.

Bad:

```text
article:{articleId}
```

if the same query can return:

* public Article;
* private draft preview.

Prefer separate query contracts and separate cache namespaces.

---

# 7.6.8.75 Cache Invalidation Signals

Commands should produce explicit signals when query results may be stale.

Example:

```text
ArticlePublished
    affects:
- Article page
- homepage
- Category archive
- Author page
- Journey pages
- RSS
- sitemap
- Search projection
```

The query should not guess that a mutation happened.

The mutation path records the change.

The Cache Architecture later defines how the affected representations are invalidated.

---

# 7.6.8.76 Framework Cache Version Discipline

Next.js caching APIs have changed across framework generations. Current documentation distinguishes Cache Components and previous caching models. TWN must use the official documentation matching its pinned Next.js version and must not combine examples from incompatible models. ([Next.js][7])

The stable architectural rule remains:

```text
Backend queries declare truth and freshness needs.

Framework adapters implement the selected cache mechanism.
```

---

# 7.6.8.77 Search Query Boundary

Global Search is a specialized read subsystem.

A Search query may support:

* keyword;
* filters;
* content types;
* locale;
* publication date;
* Author;
* Category;
* Journey;
* semantic similarity;
* pagination;
* suggestions.

The Backend Query layer should call the Search module’s public query interface.

It should not spread Search-provider syntax into Editorial, Notebook, or Journey modules.

---

# 7.6.8.78 Search Result Model

Example:

```typescript
export type SearchResultReadModel =
  | {
      type: "article";
      contentId: string;
      slug: string;
      title: string;
      excerpt: string;
      publishedAt: string;
      matchedTerms?: string[];
    }
  | {
      type: "notebook-entry";
      contentId: string;
      slug: string;
      title: string | null;
      excerpt: string;
      publishedAt: string;
      matchedTerms?: string[];
    }
  | {
      type: "journey";
      contentId: string;
      slug: string;
      title: string;
      excerpt: string;
      publishedAt: string | null;
      matchedTerms?: string[];
    };
```

The model is content-type aware.

It should not flatten every result into an ambiguous generic object.

---

# 7.6.8.79 Search Visibility

Search results must not reveal:

* drafts;
* scheduled content;
* rejected submissions;
* private profiles;
* archived private Media;
* unpublished Journey Chapters;

unless the authenticated query is specifically authorized for those states.

Visibility must be enforced before or during indexing and again at query boundaries where necessary.

A Search provider result is not automatically authorized merely because the provider returned it.

---

# 7.6.8.80 Query Security

Query security includes:

* authentication for private reads;
* authorization for sensitive resources;
* parameterized persistence access;
* field allow-lists;
* bounded filters and limits;
* prevention of sensitive-field exposure;
* safe error handling;
* privacy-conscious logging.

OWASP recommends enforcing authorization for each protected resource rather than relying on the obscurity of identifiers or frontend controls. ([OWASP Cheat Sheet Series][8])

---

# 7.6.8.81 Object-Level Authorization

A User authorized to access one Article draft may not be authorized to access every Article draft.

Bad:

```text
User has role Editor
    ↓
Return any Article requested by ID
```

Preferred:

```text
User is authenticated
    ↓
Coarse editorial access check
    ↓
Load target or authorization projection
    ↓
Resource-specific authorization
    ↓
Return permitted read model
```

The detailed permission model belongs to Section 7.10.

---

# 7.6.8.82 Field-Level Exposure

Authorization may also apply to fields.

Example public profile:

```text
display name
biography
avatar
```

Administrative profile:

```text
account status
internal notes
last moderation action
```

Do not retrieve one unrestricted model and assume every caller will remove sensitive fields correctly.

Use purpose-specific read models and projections.

---

# 7.6.8.83 Query Enumeration Risk

Sequential or guessable identifiers can allow attackers to request many resources.

UUIDs reduce simple enumeration but do not provide authorization.

Every private lookup must still evaluate:

* Actor;
* resource scope;
* visibility;
* ownership;
* requested operation.

Not-found responses may be preferred where revealing existence would be sensitive.

---

# 7.6.8.84 Query Timing and Disclosure

Different failure paths may reveal information through:

* response messages;
* status codes;
* response timing;
* result counts.

TWN should avoid unnecessary distinctions for concealed resources.

Example:

```text
Private Article exists but Actor cannot view it.
```

Public transport may return the same outward result as:

```text
Article does not exist.
```

The exact disclosure policy belongs to Authorization and API Architecture.

---

# 7.6.8.85 Query Logging Privacy

Do not log full query input automatically.

Potentially sensitive values include:

* Search terms;
* email address;
* private content ID;
* User filters;
* administrative lookup values;
* personal reading history.

Logging should record only what is necessary for:

* diagnosis;
* security;
* performance;
* product metrics.

Where Search Analytics are needed, privacy and retention policy must be defined before collection.

---

# 7.6.8.86 Query Timeouts

Queries should have bounded execution time.

Controls may include:

* application deadline;
* database statement timeout;
* provider timeout;
* bounded result size;
* cancellation signal;
* query complexity limits.

A public request should not execute indefinitely because:

* filters are unbounded;
* indexes are missing;
* a provider is unavailable;
* a recursive query is malformed.

The exact timeout values must be based on measured behaviour and infrastructure limits.

---

# 7.6.8.87 Expensive Query Classification

Queries should be classified as:

```text
Interactive
Background report
Export
Administrative analysis
```

Interactive queries must remain bounded for user-facing response time.

Expensive reports may execute asynchronously.

Example:

```text
Export all newsletter activity for five years
```

should not run as an unrestricted synchronous dashboard query.

It should become:

```text
Request export command
    ↓
Background generation
    ↓
Download result
```

---

# 7.6.8.88 Query Complexity Limits

Complex query interfaces should define limits such as:

* maximum filters;
* maximum date range;
* maximum page size;
* maximum aggregation groups;
* maximum nested expansions;
* supported sorting combinations;
* maximum Search query length;
* maximum export range.

Avoid exposing a general query language to ordinary clients unless there is a strong, governed requirement.

---

# 7.6.8.89 Query Degradation Policy

A secondary query dependency may fail.

The owning query should define:

```text
Fail the whole request
Return partial data
Return stale data
Return fallback data
```

Example:

* Article body unavailable: fail Article page.
* Related content unavailable: render Article without related content.
* Analytics widget unavailable: render dashboard with explicit unavailable state.
* Search suggestions unavailable: Search submission still works.

Fallbacks must remain truthful.

Do not convert:

```text
dependency failed
```

into:

```text
zero results
```

unless zero is genuinely known.

---

# 7.6.8.90 Query Observability

Important query operations should expose:

* query name;
* source;
* duration;
* result category;
* item count bucket;
* cache outcome where available;
* database or provider dependency;
* timeout;
* failure category.

Avoid using high-cardinality entity or User IDs as metrics labels.

Detailed Logging and Observability Architecture belongs later in Volume 7.

---

# 7.6.8.91 Slow Query Monitoring

The system should identify:

* frequently slow queries;
* queries with growing execution time;
* unexpected full scans;
* excessive row processing;
* cache miss spikes;
* N+1 behaviour;
* timeout rates.

Performance monitoring should connect:

```text
Application query name
```

to:

```text
Database or provider operation
```

so engineers can diagnose the actual source of delay.

---

# 7.6.8.92 Query Testing Layers

Query Architecture requires several test layers.

## Handler tests

Verify:

* input handling;
* visibility;
* authorization;
* result variants;
* composition policy;
* optional dependency behaviour.

## Query adapter integration tests

Verify:

* SQL or provider query;
* mapping;
* filtering;
* ordering;
* pagination;
* null behaviour;
* visibility predicates.

## Security tests

Verify:

* unauthorized resource access;
* field exposure;
* malicious filter input;
* sort allow-list;
* oversized limit;
* concealed-resource behaviour.

## Performance tests

Verify critical query behaviour against representative data.

---

# 7.6.8.93 Query Handler Tests

Example scenarios:

* published Article found;
* Article does not exist;
* draft is concealed from anonymous User;
* authorized editor receives editor model;
* unauthorized editor receives forbidden or concealed result;
* optional related-content query fails;
* required dependency fails unexpectedly;
* invalid cursor is rejected;
* page limit is bounded.

---

# 7.6.8.94 Pagination Tests

Test:

* first page;
* next page;
* final page;
* no results;
* duplicate ordering values;
* record inserted between pages;
* record removed between pages;
* malformed cursor;
* unsupported cursor version;
* cursor reused with different filters;
* forward and backward ordering where supported.

For keyset pagination, verify no duplicate or missing items under the documented consistency assumptions.

---

# 7.6.8.95 Query Adapter Integration Tests

Use a real test database for behaviour such as:

* collation;
* null ordering;
* index-supported ordering;
* timestamp precision;
* transaction visibility;
* cursor boundary comparison;
* joins;
* aggregate counts;
* materialized projection freshness.

An in-memory fake cannot prove PostgreSQL-specific semantics.

---

# 7.6.8.96 Query Performance Tests

For high-value queries, test with representative data volume.

Examples:

* public Article archive;
* global Search;
* large Journey;
* moderation queue;
* Media library;
* User administration.

Measure:

* latency distribution;
* rows scanned;
* result size;
* query plan;
* memory;
* database connections;
* behaviour at deep pagination.

Do not wait for production scale before discovering an unbounded query.

---

# 7.6.8.97 Query Contract Tests

Cross-module public queries should have contract tests that protect:

* input shape;
* visibility semantics;
* read-model fields;
* result variants;
* pagination guarantees;
* ordering;
* error categories.

This prevents one module from silently breaking another module’s composition query.

---

# 7.6.8.98 Query Directory Structure

Vertical query organization is recommended.

Example:

```text
editorial/
└── application/
    └── queries/
        ├── get-published-article/
        │   ├── query.ts
        │   ├── handler.ts
        │   ├── result.ts
        │   └── handler.test.ts
        │
        ├── list-published-article-cards/
        │   ├── query.ts
        │   ├── handler.ts
        │   ├── result.ts
        │   └── handler.test.ts
        │
        └── get-article-editor-view/
            ├── query.ts
            ├── handler.ts
            ├── result.ts
            └── handler.test.ts
```

Infrastructure read adapters may be organized separately:

```text
editorial/
└── infrastructure/
    └── queries/
        ├── postgres-published-article.queries.ts
        └── postgres-editorial-article.queries.ts
```

---

# 7.6.8.99 Query Naming Convention

Recommended TypeScript names:

```typescript
GetPublishedArticleQuery
GetPublishedArticleHandler
GetPublishedArticleResult

ListPublishedArticlesQuery
ListPublishedArticlesHandler
ListPublishedArticlesResult
```

Recommended file names:

```text
get-published-article.query.ts
get-published-article.handler.ts
get-published-article.result.ts
```

Avoid vague names such as:

```text
fetch.ts
load.ts
data.ts
reader.ts
```

without domain and use-case context.

---

# 7.6.8.100 Query Documentation

Every significant query should document:

```text
Purpose
Owner
Visibility
Required Actor
Authorization
Filters
Sort
Pagination
Read model
Consistency
Cacheability
Freshness
Maximum result size
Dependencies
Failure policy
Sensitive fields
```

Example:

```text
Query:
GetPublishedArticleBySlug

Owner:
Editorial

Visibility:
Public

Consistency:
Authoritative published revision

Cacheability:
Publicly cacheable

Failure:
Not found

Sensitive fields:
None

Dependencies:
Editorial database projection
Media public URL resolver
```

---

# 7.6.8.101 Query Review Checklist

Before approving a query, verify:

* Does the name express a clear question?
* Is it truly read-only?
* Does it have explicit public or private visibility?
* Is authorization enforced where required?
* Is the result purpose-specific?
* Are sensitive fields excluded?
* Are required columns projected explicitly?
* Is collection ordering deterministic?
* Is pagination bounded?
* Is the cursor versioned and validated?
* Are filters and sorting allow-listed?
* Is SQL parameterized?
* Does the query avoid N+1 access?
* Is total count genuinely required?
* Is consistency expectation defined?
* Is cacheability defined?
* Does a failure differ from an empty result?
* Are cross-module reads made through approved contracts?
* Is an expensive query better implemented as a background export?
* Has representative performance been measured?
* Are query plans reviewed where appropriate?
* Are tests present for visibility, pagination, and mapping?
* Does the query stay within its owning module?

---

# 7.6.8.102 Query Anti-Patterns

## Query changes state

Creates hidden mutation.

## Generic `GetEntity`

Hides visibility and consumer intent.

## Returning Domain aggregate to presentation

Leaks write-side internals.

## Returning database row

Couples consumers to persistence.

## `SELECT *`

Expands data exposure and schema coupling.

## Unbounded collection query

Risks memory and database exhaustion.

## Pagination without stable ordering

Produces inconsistent results.

## Offset pagination for endlessly growing feeds

Degrades and shifts under concurrent changes.

## Cursor without filter identity

Allows invalid continuation across different result sets.

## Exact count on every feed

Adds unnecessary work.

## Raw client sort field in SQL

Creates injection risk.

## N+1 access

Multiplies database queries.

## Shared public and private model

Risks sensitive-field leakage.

## Cache key missing authorization dimension

May expose private data.

## Search result treated as authorized

Allows stale or private content exposure.

## Dependency failure represented as empty result

Misrepresents system state.

## Cross-module repository imports

Breaks module ownership.

## Query reconstructs full aggregate unnecessarily

Adds cost without behaviour.

## Query-specific index added without measurement

Creates write cost and storage without proven benefit.

---

# 7.6.8.103 Implementation Sequence

## Phase 1 — Core read contracts

Implement:

* query and result conventions;
* public and private read-model separation;
* module-specific query ports;
* explicit field projection;
* visibility and authorization;
* stable collection ordering.

## Phase 2 — Pagination and composition

Implement:

* bounded limits;
* cursor envelope;
* keyset pagination for growing feeds;
* homepage composition;
* Journey content composition;
* optional dependency failure policy.

## Phase 3 — Performance discipline

Add:

* query-plan review;
* N+1 detection;
* representative data tests;
* slow-query telemetry;
* purpose-specific indexes;
* count strategy.

## Phase 4 — Derived read systems

Add only when justified:

* Search projections;
* materialized views;
* Analytics summaries;
* recommendation projections;
* cross-domain reporting views.

## Phase 5 — Query governance

Maintain:

* query inventory;
* visibility classification;
* cacheability classification;
* performance budgets;
* read-model compatibility;
* deprecation process.

---

# 7.6.8.104 Architecture Decision Record

**Decision:** TWN uses a dedicated Query Architecture in which read operations are explicit, side-effect free, visibility-aware, and optimized around purpose-specific read models rather than Domain aggregates or generic database entities.

**Supporting decisions:**

* Queries answer questions and do not intentionally change authoritative state.
* Query names express consumer intent and visibility.
* Public and private queries use separate contracts.
* Query handlers may use read-optimized projections directly.
* Domain aggregate reconstruction is not required for ordinary reads.
* Database records, ORM models, and provider responses remain private infrastructure types.
* Read models contain only the fields required by their consumer.
* Sensitive and administrative fields are excluded from public projections.
* Expected query outcomes use typed results.
* Collections require stable deterministic ordering.
* Every potentially large collection is bounded.
* Keyset or cursor pagination is preferred for large, changing feeds.
* Application cursors are stateless opaque tokens, not PostgreSQL session cursors.
* Cursors are versioned and validated against filter and sort context.
* Exact total counts are calculated only where the product requires them.
* Dynamic filters and sort fields use allow-lists and parameterized queries.
* Important query plans are measured rather than assumed.
* Query adapters avoid `SELECT *` and N+1 patterns.
* Composite queries define required and optional dependencies.
* Cross-module reads use public module contracts.
* Explicit read-only cross-domain projections may be introduced when justified.
* Materialized read models are introduced only after measured need.
* Queries declare strong, read-your-writes, eventual, cached, or historical consistency expectations.
* Framework-specific caching remains outside the core Application query contract.
* Search results are treated as read projections, not authorization decisions.
* Object-level and field-level authorization protect private reads.
* Expensive reports and exports move to asynchronous workflows.
* Query tests cover mapping, security, pagination, consistency, and performance.

**Reasons:**

* improves read performance;
* prevents write-side complexity from controlling presentation;
* reduces payload size;
* protects private information;
* supports public caching safely;
* creates scalable pagination;
* preserves module boundaries;
* supports Search, Journeys, Library, and future content types;
* makes query consistency and freshness explicit;
* keeps query behaviour measurable and testable.

**Consequences:**

Positive:

* each interface receives an appropriate model;
* queries can be optimized independently from commands;
* public and private visibility becomes clearer;
* large collections remain bounded;
* future materialized projections can be introduced without rewriting Domain logic;
* Search and Analytics can evolve as specialized read systems.

Costs:

* more query-specific types and mappers;
* some data shapes are intentionally duplicated;
* pagination requires careful sort and cursor design;
* projection freshness must be managed;
* materialized reads add reconciliation requirements;
* cross-module compositions require explicit dependency policies.

**Expected lifetime:** The read/write responsibility separation and purpose-specific read models are permanent. Particular persistence queries, cache implementations, Search providers, and projection strategies may evolve.

---

# Completion Criteria for 7.6.8

This subsection is complete when:

* queries are distinguished from commands, repositories, and Search providers;
* query naming and ownership rules are defined;
* public and private query contracts are separate;
* read models are purpose-specific and minimal;
* Domain aggregate reconstruction is not required for ordinary reads;
* query result and error contracts are defined;
* parameterized persistence access is required;
* dynamic sort and filter allow-lists are established;
* stable ordering is mandatory;
* offset and keyset pagination positions are documented;
* cursor requirements are defined;
* exact and approximate count policies are distinguished;
* index and query-plan measurement responsibilities are recognized;
* N+1 and unbounded parallelism are prohibited;
* cross-module composition and projection rules are defined;
* consistency models are documented;
* caching boundaries are assigned correctly;
* Search visibility and authorization are preserved;
* query security and privacy rules are established;
* performance, integration, pagination, and contract testing are required;
* query anti-patterns and governance are documented.

---

## End of 7.6.8

The next subsection is:

# **7.6.9 — Domain Entities, Value Objects & Aggregate Boundaries**

This will remain within Backend Architecture and refine the concepts introduced in 7.6.4 by defining:

* Entity identity and construction;
* Entity encapsulation;
* Value Object design;
* primitive obsession;
* aggregate roots;
* consistency boundaries;
* aggregate loading and persistence;
* cross-aggregate references;
* aggregate size limits;
* Journey aggregate decisions;
* Article aggregate decisions;
* when an entity should remain a simple record;
* Domain object serialization;
* equality;
* invariant testing;
* avoiding unnecessary DDD ceremony.

[1]: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/architecture/microservices/microservice-ddd-cqrs-patterns/cqrs-microservice-reads?utm_source=chatgpt.com "Implementing reads/queries in a CQRS microservice - .NET"
[2]: https://cheatsheetseries.owasp.org/cheatsheets/SQL_Injection_Prevention_Cheat_Sheet.html?utm_source=chatgpt.com "SQL Injection Prevention Cheat Sheet"
[3]: https://www.postgresql.org/docs/current/btree.html?utm_source=chatgpt.com "Documentation: 18: 65.1. B-Tree Indexes"
[4]: https://www.postgresql.org/docs/current/plpgsql-cursors.html?utm_source=chatgpt.com "Documentation: 18: 41.7. Cursors"
[5]: https://www.postgresql.org/docs/9.5/functions-aggregate.html?utm_source=chatgpt.com "Documentation: 9.5: Aggregate Functions"
[6]: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/architecture/patterns/cqrs?utm_source=chatgpt.com "CQRS Pattern - Azure Architecture Center"
[7]: https://nextjs.org/docs/app/guides/caching-without-cache-components?utm_source=chatgpt.com "Caching and Revalidating (Previous Model)"
[8]: https://cheatsheetseries.owasp.org/cheatsheets/REST_Security_Cheat_Sheet.html?utm_source=chatgpt.com "REST Security - OWASP Cheat Sheet Series"

# 7.6.9 Domain Entities, Value Objects & Aggregate Boundaries

**Status:** Backend Architecture Specification
**Parent chapter:** Volume 7 — Technical Architecture
**Current scope:** Section 7.6 — Backend Architecture
**Applies to:** Editorial, Notebook, Journeys, Community, Identity, Newsletter, Media, Search, Analytics and future TWN domains
**Primary objective:** Define how TWN models business concepts through Entities, Value Objects, and Aggregate boundaries while avoiding unnecessary complexity and preserving long-term maintainability.

---

# Purpose

This subsection defines:

* what a Domain Entity is;
* what a Value Object is;
* aggregate roots;
* aggregate boundaries;
* identity;
* equality;
* invariants;
* encapsulation;
* lifecycle ownership;
* consistency boundaries;
* aggregate references;
* persistence expectations;
* modeling rules;
* when NOT to create Domain objects.

The central rule is:

> Domain objects exist to protect business rules, not to make the codebase look sophisticated.

TWN should avoid both extremes:

```text
Everything is a database row
```

and

```text
Everything is a Domain object
```

The objective is practical modeling.

---

# 7.6.9.1 Domain Model Purpose

The Domain Model exists to protect business meaning.

It answers:

```text
What is this thing?

What rules govern it?

What state transitions are valid?

What must never happen?
```

Examples:

```text
Article
Journey
JourneyChapter
User
MarginNote
NewsletterCampaign
```

The Domain Model is not:

```text
Database schema
API contract
UI state
Provider payload
```

Those belong elsewhere.

---

# 7.6.9.2 Entity Definition

An Entity is a business object that possesses identity.

Examples:

```text
Article
Journey
User
MediaAsset
NewsletterCampaign
```

The identity remains stable even if attributes change.

Example:

```text
Article A
```

Title changes.

Slug changes.

Content changes.

Category changes.

It is still:

```text
The same Article.
```

Identity makes it an Entity.

---

# 7.6.9.3 Entity Identity

Identity should be stable.

Examples:

```text
ArticleId
JourneyId
UserId
MediaId
CampaignId
```

Identity should never depend on mutable business fields.

Bad:

```text
Article title
```

Bad:

```text
Current slug
```

Bad:

```text
Email address
```

These may change.

Identity must survive those changes.

---

# 7.6.9.4 Entity Equality

Entities compare by identity.

Example:

```typescript
articleA.id === articleB.id
```

means:

```text
Same Entity
```

even if loaded at different times.

Example:

```text
Article
id = article_123
title = "Old Title"
```

and

```text
Article
id = article_123
title = "New Title"
```

represent the same Entity.

---

# 7.6.9.5 Entity Lifecycle

Entities possess lifecycle.

Example Article:

```text
Draft
↓
Review
↓
Scheduled
↓
Published
↓
Archived
```

The lifecycle matters.

Rules depend on lifecycle.

Example:

```text
Published Article
```

cannot behave exactly like:

```text
Draft Article
```

The Entity should own these transitions.

---

# 7.6.9.6 Entity State

Entities contain state relevant to business behavior.

Example:

```typescript
Article
```

may contain:

```text
title
slug
status
author
publishedRevision
publicationDate
```

State exists because business rules depend on it.

Do not store data inside an Entity merely because it exists in the database.

---

# 7.6.9.7 Entity Behavior

Entities should own behavior related to themselves.

Example:

```typescript
article.publish()
```

may be valid.

Example:

```typescript
article.archive()
```

may be valid.

Example:

```typescript
article.restore()
```

may be valid.

The Entity protects its own rules.

---

# 7.6.9.8 Anemic Domain Model

An anemic model contains:

```typescript
class Article {
  title;
  status;
}
```

and all behavior lives elsewhere.

Large applications often drift into:

```text
Huge service layer
Tiny entities
```

This increases procedural complexity.

Important business transitions should not be scattered across random services.

---

# 7.6.9.9 Rich Domain Model

A rich model places important rules close to the Entity.

Example:

```typescript
article.publish()
```

may validate:

```text
Current status
Revision readiness
Publication constraints
```

before changing state.

The rule lives beside the state it protects.

---

# 7.6.9.10 Over-Engineering Warning

Not every table needs a rich Domain object.

Example:

```text
FeatureFlag
Country
Language
Tag
```

may remain simple records.

The question is:

```text
Does meaningful business behavior exist?
```

If not:

```text
Simple structure may be enough.
```

---

# 7.6.9.11 Value Object Definition

A Value Object has:

```text
No identity
```

and is defined entirely by its values.

Examples:

```text
EmailAddress
Slug
PublicationSchedule
ReadingTime
DisplayName
ArticleTitle
```

Two Value Objects with identical values are equal.

---

# 7.6.9.12 Value Object Equality

Example:

```typescript
EmailAddress("hello@example.com")
```

equals:

```typescript
EmailAddress("hello@example.com")
```

because:

```text
Values match.
```

Identity does not matter.

---

# 7.6.9.13 Value Objects Are Immutable

Value Objects should be immutable.

Bad:

```typescript
email.value = "new@example.com"
```

Preferred:

```typescript
const updated =
  EmailAddress.create("new@example.com");
```

Immutability reduces accidental state corruption.

---

# 7.6.9.14 Value Object Validation

Validation belongs inside the Value Object.

Example:

```typescript
EmailAddress.create()
```

validates:

```text
Format
Length
Normalization
```

The rest of the system receives a trusted object.

---

# 7.6.9.15 Primitive Obsession

Primitive obsession occurs when:

```text
Everything is string
Everything is number
Everything is boolean
```

Example:

```typescript
title: string
slug: string
email: string
```

The compiler cannot distinguish them.

This increases mistakes.

---

# 7.6.9.16 Useful Value Objects in TWN

High-value candidates include:

```text
ArticleTitle
ArticleSlug
EmailAddress
DisplayName
JourneyTitle
PublicationSchedule
ReadingTime
ImageDimensions
ContentExcerpt
TagName
CategoryName
```

These carry business meaning.

---

# 7.6.9.17 Value Object Overuse

Avoid creating Value Objects for everything.

Bad:

```text
ArticleIdWrapper
PageNumberWrapper
BooleanWrapper
IntegerWrapper
```

without meaningful behavior.

The objective is clarity.

Not ceremony.

---

# 7.6.9.18 Aggregate Definition

An Aggregate is a consistency boundary.

This is one of the most important concepts in TWN.

An Aggregate defines:

```text
What must remain consistent together?
```

Everything inside the Aggregate changes atomically.

Everything outside may change separately.

---

# 7.6.9.19 Aggregate Root

Every Aggregate has one root.

Examples:

```text
Article
Journey
User
NewsletterCampaign
```

The root controls access to internal state.

External code interacts through the root.

---

# 7.6.9.20 Aggregate Purpose

Aggregates exist to:

```text
Protect invariants.
```

Not:

```text
Mirror database tables.
```

The question is:

```text
Which rules must always hold true?
```

The Aggregate boundary protects those rules.

---

# 7.6.9.21 Article Aggregate

Likely Aggregate Root:

```text
Article
```

Owns:

```text
Current state
Published revision reference
Editorial metadata
Lifecycle
```

Does not necessarily own:

```text
Entire revision history
Entire analytics history
Entire media library
```

Those may become separate structures.

---

# 7.6.9.22 Journey Aggregate

Likely Aggregate Root:

```text
Journey
```

Owns:

```text
Journey metadata
Journey structure
Chapter ordering
Content ordering
Publication state
```

Journey rules require structural consistency.

Therefore Journey is a natural Aggregate Root.

---

# 7.6.9.23 User Aggregate

Likely Aggregate Root:

```text
User
```

Owns:

```text
Identity
Status
Roles
Profile state
Preferences
```

User should not directly own:

```text
Articles
Journey content
Margin Notes
Newsletter activity
```

Those belong elsewhere.

---

# 7.6.9.24 Aggregate Invariants

An invariant must always be true.

Example:

```text
Published Article
must reference
published revision.
```

Violation:

```text
Published Article
without published revision.
```

The Aggregate prevents this state.

---

# 7.6.9.25 Aggregate Boundary Rule

Anything requiring atomic consistency belongs inside the Aggregate.

Anything that does not require atomic consistency should probably remain outside.

This rule prevents oversized Aggregates.

---

# 7.6.9.26 Large Aggregate Warning

Large Aggregates create:

```text
Slow loading
Contention
Complex persistence
Concurrency conflicts
```

Bad example:

```text
Journey Aggregate
contains
all Articles
all Comments
all Analytics
all Media
```

This becomes impossible to manage.

---

# 7.6.9.27 Small Aggregate Preference

Modern DDD guidance generally prefers smaller Aggregates with clear consistency boundaries.

Ask:

```text
Can this rule be enforced
without loading everything?
```

If yes:

```text
Keep Aggregates smaller.
```

---

# 7.6.9.28 Cross-Aggregate References

Aggregates should reference each other by identity.

Example:

```text
Journey
contains
ArticleId
```

not:

```text
Journey
contains
entire Article object
```

This reduces coupling.

---

# 7.6.9.29 Aggregate Loading

Loading one Aggregate should not automatically load many others.

Bad:

```text
Load Journey
↓
Load 200 Articles
↓
Load Authors
↓
Load Analytics
```

This causes explosive complexity.

---

# 7.6.9.30 Aggregate Persistence

Repositories persist Aggregate Roots.

Example:

```text
ArticleRepository
JourneyRepository
UserRepository
```

Repositories do not persist arbitrary fragments independently if doing so would violate Aggregate rules.

---

# End of 7.6.9.30

We are still inside **7.6.9 Domain Entities, Value Objects & Aggregate Boundaries**.

Remaining major sections include:

* 7.6.9.31 Aggregate Transactions
* 7.6.9.32 Cross-Aggregate Consistency
* 7.6.9.33 Domain Events & Aggregate Communication
* 7.6.9.34 Entity Construction
* 7.6.9.35 Aggregate Factories
* 7.6.9.36 Encapsulation Rules
* 7.6.9.37 Domain Serialization
* 7.6.9.38 Invariant Testing
* 7.6.9.39 TWN Aggregate Catalog
* 7.6.9.40 Architecture Decision Record
* 7.6.9.41 Completion Criteria

So **7.6.9 is roughly 35–40% complete**. The remaining sections are where we define the actual aggregate boundaries for TWN and avoid the common mistake of turning the modular monolith into a giant object graph.

# 7.6.9 Domain Entities, Value Objects & Aggregate Boundaries — Continued

---

# 7.6.9.31 Aggregate Transactions

An Aggregate defines the state that must remain internally consistent after one business operation.

The normal rule is:

```text
One command
    ↓
Loads one Aggregate
    ↓
Invokes Aggregate behaviour
    ↓
Persists the Aggregate
    ↓
Commits one transaction
```

The Aggregate Root is the approved entry point for modifying the Aggregate and is responsible for preserving its invariants. ([Microsoft Learn][1])

Example:

```text
ReorderJourneyChapterItems
    ↓
Load Journey Aggregate
    ↓
Journey validates new ordering
    ↓
Persist changed ordering
    ↓
Commit
```

At no point should the database contain:

* duplicated positions;
* an item assigned to two positions;
* an item assigned to a nonexistent Chapter;
* a Chapter order inconsistent with its Journey.

The transaction protects the persistence of the valid state produced by the Aggregate.

---

# 7.6.9.32 Aggregate Transaction Principle

Changes within one Aggregate should ordinarily be committed atomically.

Conceptual example:

```typescript
await transactionManager.run(async (transaction) => {
  const journey =
    await journeyRepository.getById(
      command.journeyId,
      transaction,
    );

  if (!journey) {
    return {
      kind: "not-found",
    };
  }

  journey.reorderItems({
    chapterId: command.chapterId,
    orderedMembershipIds:
      command.orderedMembershipIds,
  });

  await journeyRepository.save(
    journey,
    transaction,
  );

  return {
    kind: "reordered",
    version: journey.version,
  };
});
```

The Aggregate validates the requested operation.

The transaction ensures that all persistence changes representing that operation succeed together.

---

# 7.6.9.33 One Aggregate Does Not Mean One Table

An Aggregate may be persisted across several tables.

Example:

```text
Journey Aggregate
├── journeys
├── journey_chapters
└── journey_content_memberships
```

The Aggregate boundary is defined by:

```text
Business consistency
```

not:

```text
Database table count
```

Likewise, several simple Aggregate Roots may share one table in unusual implementations, though that should not be assumed without clear benefit.

The Domain design must not be forced to match storage mechanics exactly.

---

# 7.6.9.34 One Table Does Not Automatically Mean One Aggregate

A single table may store records that are not all part of one Aggregate instance.

Example:

```text
article_revisions
```

may contain revisions belonging to many Articles.

This does not mean:

```text
All Article revisions form one Aggregate.
```

Aggregate identity and ownership determine which records belong together.

---

# 7.6.9.35 Cross-Aggregate Consistency

Not every business rule requires immediate atomic consistency across several Aggregates.

Example:

```text
Article published
    ↓
Search document updated
```

The public Article state may need to be authoritative immediately.

The Search document may be updated asynchronously.

This creates:

```text
Strong consistency
within the Article Aggregate

Eventual consistency
between Editorial and Search
```

Domain events are commonly used to make reactions across Aggregate boundaries explicit, including reactions that may be handled asynchronously. ([Microsoft Learn][2])

---

# 7.6.9.36 Strong Cross-Aggregate Consistency

Some operations may genuinely require several Aggregates to change atomically.

Example:

```text
Transfer ownership of a protected resource
```

may need to ensure:

* old ownership is removed;
* new ownership is assigned;
* the resource never exists without one valid owner.

Before using one cross-Aggregate transaction, ask:

1. Is this truly one business invariant?
2. Is the Aggregate boundary drawn incorrectly?
3. Could one Aggregate own the relationship?
4. Is temporary inconsistency acceptable?
5. Could an event-driven reaction work?
6. Would one transaction create unacceptable coupling?

Cross-Aggregate atomic transactions are permitted in the modular monolith when genuinely required.
