* workflow;
* presentation;
* future evolution;

may differ.

**Owner:** Notebook
**Type:** Domain-definition rule

---

## Notebook Rule N-002 — Public Entry Has Public Content

A publicly visible Notebook Entry must reference an eligible public content representation.

The exact revision model will be confirmed in CMS Architecture and Volume 8.

---

## Notebook Rule N-003 — Archived Entry Cannot Be Actively Featured

An archived or deleted Notebook Entry cannot remain selected as an active public feature such as Today’s Page.

**Owner:** Notebook
**Type:** Cross-entity eligibility rule

---

## Notebook Rule N-004 — Today’s Page Has Separate Selection Semantics

The Today’s Page concept must not be implemented merely as an uncontrolled Boolean field on multiple Entries unless its rules guarantee:

```text
At most one active selection
for the relevant period.
```

The final model depends on whether it is:

* manually pinned;
* date-based;
* scheduled;
* algorithmically rotated.

That product decision remains open.

---

# 7.6.10.102 Community Rules

---

## Community Rule C-001 — Public Community Content Requires Approved State

A Shared Page or moderated Margin Note must not become publicly visible before the required moderation decision.

**Owner:** Community
**Type:** Strong publication rule

---

## Community Rule C-002 — Moderation Transitions Are Explicit

Moderation state changes use approved operations such as:

```text
approve

reject

returnForReview

archive
```

They are not arbitrary status assignments.

---

## Community Rule C-003 — Moderation Decision Is Accountable

A consequential moderation decision should retain:

* decision identity;
* target identity;
* Actor identity;
* outcome;
* timestamp;
* reason code or approved reason;
* operation correlation.

The exact visible explanation and retention period belong to Community, Audit, and Privacy specifications.

---

## Community Rule C-004 — Repeated Moderation Must Follow State Rules

Example:

```text
Approved
→ Approved
```

must not create repeated publication events or duplicate effects accidentally.

The policy should return an explicit:

```text
already-approved
```

or equivalent no-op outcome where appropriate.

---

## Community Rule C-005 — Target Content Must Exist

A Margin Note must reference a valid eligible target.

The target may be:

* Article;
* Notebook Entry;
* another approved content type.

The Community module validates the reference through the owning content module’s public contract.

It must not read private content tables directly.

---

## Community Rule C-006 — Removal Does Not Rewrite Source Content

Archiving or rejecting a Margin Note must not modify the Article or Notebook Entry to which it was attached.

The two concepts are separate Aggregates.

---

# 7.6.10.103 Media Rules

---

## Media Rule M-001 — Declared Upload Is Not Verified Media

A User declaring:

```text
This is an image.
```

does not make it verified Media.

Media must pass the required server-side inspection and lifecycle transitions before activation.

**Owner:** Media
**Type:** Security and lifecycle rule

---

## Media Rule M-002 — Active Media References a Verified Object

A Media Asset cannot become active or publishable unless the authoritative Media process confirms that its required storage object exists and satisfies the required checks.

The exact checks belong to Storage, Media Processing, and Security Architecture.

---

## Media Rule M-003 — Deleted Media Cannot Receive New References

Once Media is in a deleted or permanently unavailable state, new content relationships must be rejected.

Existing references require an explicit broken-reference, archival, or replacement strategy.

---

## Media Rule M-004 — Provider Details Are Not Domain State

The Domain may store a provider-neutral storage reference.

It must not base business policy directly on a Supabase, Cloudinary, or other provider SDK object.

---

## Media Rule M-005 — Processing Transitions Are Controlled

Potential states such as:

```text
pending-upload

uploaded

processing

ready

failed

archived
```

must have explicit transitions.

The final state machine is deferred to Media Processing Architecture.

---

# 7.6.10.104 Newsletter Rules

---

## Newsletter Rule NL-001 — Consent Is Required

A Subscriber must not become actively eligible for email delivery without the consent state required by TWN’s legal and product policy.

**Owner:** Newsletter
**Type:** Strong eligibility rule

---

## Newsletter Rule NL-002 — Unsubscribe Is Authoritative

An unsubscribed recipient must not be reintroduced into an active campaign merely because:

* an old audience list contains the email;
* a campaign was prepared earlier;
* another User imports the address;
* provider state is stale.

The send workflow must consult authoritative subscription state at the appropriate stage.

---

## Newsletter Rule NL-003 — Email Identity Is Normalized

Duplicate handling must use the approved normalized email semantics.

The exact normalization rules require care because email local-part behaviour is not universally equivalent across providers.

TWN should avoid unsafe assumptions such as lowercasing or rewriting every email component without a documented policy.

---

## Newsletter Rule NL-004 — Campaign Dispatch Is Duplicate-Safe

A one-time Campaign must not be dispatched twice because of:

* button double-click;
* network timeout;
* worker retry;
* scheduler overlap.

Dispatch requires strong idempotency.

---

## Newsletter Rule NL-005 — Sent Campaign Is Historical

Once a Campaign has been sent, editing its historical sent representation must not rewrite what recipients previously received.

Corrections should create:

* a new Campaign;
* an amended edition;
* another explicitly modelled follow-up;

according to future Newsletter rules.

---

# 7.6.10.105 Identity and Authorization-Adjacent Rules

These rules are listed only at the Backend Domain boundary.

The complete model belongs to Sections 7.9 and 7.10.

---

## Identity Rule I-001 — Client Does Not Choose Actor Identity

The authenticated Actor is resolved through trusted server-side authentication state.

A browser-submitted `userId` does not establish identity.

---

## Identity Rule I-002 — Authentication Does Not Imply Permission

A valid authenticated session establishes who the Actor is.

It does not automatically authorize:

* publication;
* moderation;
* role assignment;
* User suspension;
* private content access.

Authorization must be evaluated for every protected request. OWASP specifically recommends validating permissions on every request regardless of the request’s source. ([OWASP Cheat Sheet Series][3])

---

## Identity Rule I-003 — System Actor Is Explicit

Workers, schedulers, and integration handlers use an explicit System Actor.

They do not fabricate an ordinary User session.

---

## Identity Rule I-004 — Administrative Authority Does Not Bypass Domain Invariants

An administrator may receive elevated authorization.

That does not mean the system should persist:

* invalid Journey ordering;
* published content without a valid publication state;
* impossible Media lifecycle combinations.

Administration may use special commands.

It does not eliminate structural truth.

---

# 7.6.10.106 Cross-Domain Rules

---

## Cross-Domain Rule X-001 — Ownership Is Singular

Every business rule and mutable business concept has one owning domain.

Other domains may:

* reference it;
* query approved facts;
* request operations through public contracts;
* react to events.

They must not independently redefine its rules.

---

## Cross-Domain Rule X-002 — Cross-Aggregate Rules Receive Facts

A Domain policy spanning several Aggregates receives explicit facts assembled by the Application layer.

It does not query infrastructure secretly.

Domain services are appropriate for rules spanning several entities or Aggregates when the behaviour does not belong naturally to one Entity. ([Microsoft Learn][4])

---

## Cross-Domain Rule X-003 — Secondary Reactions Do Not Rewrite Primary Success

Example:

```text
Article publication succeeds.

Search indexing fails temporarily.
```

The Article remains published.

Search catches up through durable retry and reconciliation unless the approved product policy explicitly makes Search availability a publication prerequisite.

---

## Cross-Domain Rule X-004 — Events Announce Facts

Events such as:

```text
ArticlePublished

JourneyPublished

SubscriberConfirmed
```

are emitted only after the corresponding business transition succeeds.

Domain events are intended to represent completed domain changes and coordinate explicit reactions across Aggregate boundaries. ([Microsoft Learn][5])

---

## Cross-Domain Rule X-005 — UI Rules Are Advisory Until Server-Enforced

The frontend may:

* disable buttons;
* show missing requirements;
* warn editors;
* validate obvious fields.

The authoritative server operation must enforce the same business decision independently.

---

# 7.6.10.107 Rule Storage Position

Most business rules should live in source-controlled Domain code.

Do not store ordinary critical rules as arbitrary editable database expressions such as:

```text
article.wordCount > 500
AND article.imageCount > 0
```

unless TWN intentionally adopts a governed rule engine.

Reasons:

* difficult type safety;
* unclear testing;
* unsafe editing;
* hidden behaviour;
* poor version control;
* complicated migration;
* security risk.

---

# 7.6.10.108 Configurable Thresholds

Some approved numeric thresholds may become configuration.

Examples could include:

* submission maximum length;
* editorial warning threshold;
* campaign batch size;
* future competition deadline.

A configurable threshold should define:

* owning domain;
* allowed range;
* default;
* effective time;
* authorization required to change it;
* Audit requirement;
* whether old records are re-evaluated.

Do not convert every rule into configuration merely to appear flexible.

---

# 7.6.10.109 Rule Engine Position

TWN does not initially require a general-purpose rule engine.

A rule engine may become justified only if TWN develops:

* many externally configured rule sets;
* several jurisdictions;
* customer-specific policy variation;
* complex campaign qualification;
* non-engineer rule authorship;
* frequent effective-dated rules.

Even then, structural invariants should remain protected in Domain code and Database constraints.

A rule engine must not become a way to execute arbitrary untrusted code.

---

# 7.6.10.110 Rule Change Process

A meaningful rule change should follow:

```text
Identify rule owner
    ↓
Document current behaviour
    ↓
Define new behaviour
    ↓
Determine historical impact
    ↓
Review migration requirements
    ↓
Update policy and result codes
    ↓
Update tests
    ↓
Update UI guidance
    ↓
Update Handbook or Blueprint
    ↓
Deploy with monitoring
```

High-impact changes may require an ADR or Product Decision Log entry.

---

# 7.6.10.111 Historical Impact Assessment

Before changing a rule, determine how it affects:

* already published content;
* scheduled content;
* active Journeys;
* existing Subscribers;
* pending moderation;
* cached results;
* queued commands;
* imported records;
* Audit interpretation.

Possible strategies:

```text
Apply only to new operations

Re-evaluate all active records

Grandfather historical records

Migrate records before activation

Temporarily support both versions
```

The strategy must be explicit.

---

# 7.6.10.112 Policy Result Codes

Policy result codes should be:

* stable;
* machine-readable;
* domain-owned;
* presentation-neutral.

Example:

```typescript
type PublicationIssueCode =
  | "ARTICLE_ARCHIVED"
  | "REVISION_NOT_SELECTED"
  | "REVISION_NOT_PUBLISHABLE"
  | "AUTHOR_NOT_PUBLICLY_ELIGIBLE"
  | "CATEGORY_UNAVAILABLE"
  | "MEDIA_NOT_READY";
```

Only codes corresponding to approved rules should be implemented.

The example set must be refined when the Editorial rules are finalized.

---

# 7.6.10.113 Policy Result Details

A result may include safe structured details.

Example:

```typescript
type PolicyIssue = {
  code: PublicationIssueCode;
  severity: "error" | "warning";

  target?: {
    type:
      | "article"
      | "revision"
      | "author"
      | "category"
      | "media";

    id?: string;
  };
};
```

Do not include:

* database queries;
* stack traces;
* hidden security policy;
* private moderation evidence;
* sensitive personal data;

in ordinary policy results.

---

# 7.6.10.114 Localization Boundary

The Domain returns:

```text
MISSING_CATEGORY
```

The interface translates that into reader-facing language.

Example:

```text
Choose a category before publishing.
```

The Domain should not own every translated sentence.

This preserves:

* localization;
* UI tone;
* different messages for CMS and API consumers;
* stable backend contracts.

---

# 7.6.10.115 Rule Analytics

TWN may later measure aggregated rule outcomes.

Examples:

```text
Most common Article publication blockers

Most common Journey validation failures

Moderation rejection-code distribution
```

Analytics must use:

* stable rule codes;
* privacy-conscious aggregation;
* bounded labels;
* approved retention.

Do not place content IDs, User IDs, or free-text moderation explanations into metric labels.

---

# 7.6.10.116 Rule Monitoring

Operational monitoring should distinguish:

```text
Normal policy rejection
```

from:

```text
Unexpected policy failure
```

Example normal result:

```text
ARTICLE_NOT_READY
```

Example defect:

```text
Publication policy threw because
required persisted state was structurally impossible.
```

Normal rejection may support product Analytics.

Unexpected failure requires engineering diagnosis.

---

# 7.6.10.117 Rule Testing Strategy

Rules should be tested at the layer where they are authoritative.

---

## Value Object Tests

Verify:

* accepted values;
* rejected values;
* normalization;
* equality;
* immutability.

---

## Entity and Aggregate Tests

Verify:

* valid transitions;
* forbidden transitions;
* invariant preservation;
* state unchanged after failure;
* event generation;
* no duplicate events from no-op transitions.

---

## Policy Tests

Verify:

* eligible case;
* each individual rejection reason;
* several simultaneous rejection reasons;
* temporal boundaries;
* warning versus error;
* unchanged input;
* deterministic output.

---

## Application Handler Tests

Verify:

* required facts are loaded;
* correct policy is invoked;
* policy rejection prevents mutation;
* policy reasons are returned;
* authorization remains separate;
* successful policy evaluation leads to the command transaction.

---

## Database Integration Tests

Verify supporting constraints such as:

* uniqueness;
* ownership;
* required relationships;
* conditional version updates;
* transaction rollback.

The Aggregate remains responsible for invariant enforcement during Domain state changes, with the database acting as a supporting integrity boundary. ([Microsoft Learn][6])

---

# 7.6.10.118 Boundary-Value Testing

Temporal and numeric rules require exact boundary tests.

Example scheduling rule:

```text
scheduledAt must be later than now
```

Test:

```text
scheduledAt < now

scheduledAt = now

scheduledAt > now
```

Do not test only obviously valid and invalid values.

---

# 7.6.10.119 Policy Composition Tests

A composed policy should verify:

* rules evaluate in the intended order;
* fail-fast rules stop where appropriate;
* independent issues accumulate;
* duplicate issue codes are removed where required;
* warnings do not incorrectly block;
* exceptions are not converted into ordinary rejection.

---

# 7.6.10.120 Historical Rule Tests

When old records remain valid under previous rules, preserve regression tests.

Example:

```text
Article published under Policy Version 1
remains publicly readable after Policy Version 2
becomes active.
```

New publication may require Version 2.

Historical retrieval should not re-run current eligibility policy unless the product explicitly requires revalidation.

---

# 7.6.10.121 Security Tests

Tests should verify that:

* client-side bypass cannot avoid a rule;
* alternate transport invokes the same policy;
* scripts and workers cannot bypass lifecycle sequencing;
* unauthorized Actors are rejected before mutation;
* forged identifiers do not change rule inputs;
* hidden fields cannot set protected state.

Server-side workflow state validation is essential because frontend logic alone cannot enforce operation sequencing securely. ([OWASP Cheat Sheet Series][2])

---

# 7.6.10.122 Rule Anti-Patterns

---

## Anti-Pattern 1 — Boolean-Only Decisions

```typescript
return false;
```

No explanation.

**Consequence:** Poor UX, support difficulty, debugging ambiguity.

**Correction:** Return stable reason codes.

---

## Anti-Pattern 2 — Rules Embedded in Server Actions

```typescript
if (formData.get("category")) {
  publish();
}
```

**Consequence:** APIs, schedulers, and scripts may behave differently.

**Correction:** Invoke one Domain policy.

---

## Anti-Pattern 3 — Rules Embedded in React Components

```tsx
disabled={!article.category}
```

treated as enforcement.

**Consequence:** Client bypass.

**Correction:** Keep UI guidance but enforce on the server.

---

## Anti-Pattern 4 — Repository Makes Business Decisions

```typescript
if (row.status === "draft") {
  UPDATE status = 'published';
}
```

**Consequence:** Persistence layer becomes the Domain.

**Correction:** Domain decides; repository persists.

---

## Anti-Pattern 5 — Database Trigger as Sole Business Workflow

A trigger changes lifecycle and creates hidden side effects.

**Consequence:** Application behaviour becomes difficult to understand and test.

**Correction:** Use explicit commands and Domain behaviour; reserve database triggers for narrowly justified integrity or operational work.

---

## Anti-Pattern 6 — Arbitrary Status Assignment

```typescript
entity.status = input.status;
```

**Consequence:** Invalid transitions.

**Correction:** Expose business operations.

---

## Anti-Pattern 7 — Hidden Infrastructure in Policy

```typescript
publicationPolicy.evaluate()
```

secretly queries several repositories.

**Consequence:** Non-deterministic and difficult-to-test policy.

**Correction:** Application supplies explicit facts.

---

## Anti-Pattern 8 — Authorization Mixed with Eligibility

```text
Publication denied.
```

without identifying whether:

* Actor lacked authority;
* Article lacked readiness.

**Consequence:** Incorrect security and poor recovery.

**Correction:** Keep permission decision and business eligibility distinct.

---

## Anti-Pattern 9 — Every Recommendation Becomes a Blocking Rule

**Consequence:** Editors cannot publish because of subjective or optional guidance.

**Correction:** Classify errors, warnings, and recommendations explicitly.

---

## Anti-Pattern 10 — Arbitrary Thresholds Invented During Coding

Example:

```text
Every Journey needs five Chapters.
```

with no approved product reason.

**Consequence:** Architecture invents product policy.

**Correction:** Leave the threshold undefined until the owning product specification approves it.

---

## Anti-Pattern 11 — Rules Stored as Uncontrolled Database Expressions

**Consequence:** Weak typing, difficult testing, hidden changes, security exposure.

**Correction:** Keep rules in governed Domain code unless a real rule-engine requirement exists.

---

## Anti-Pattern 12 — Current Rules Applied Blindly to History

**Consequence:** Historical content unexpectedly becomes invalid.

**Correction:** Define effective dates, grandfathering, or migration.

---

## Anti-Pattern 13 — Policy Creates Side Effects

A policy sends email or updates state while deciding.

**Consequence:** Evaluation becomes unsafe to repeat.

**Correction:** Policies return decisions; Application handlers perform approved effects.

---

## Anti-Pattern 14 — Duplicate Rules Per Transport

```text
CMS publication policy

API publication policy

Scheduler publication policy
```

**Consequence:** Behaviour divergence.

**Correction:** One authoritative Domain policy.

---

## Anti-Pattern 15 — Rule Failure Logged as System Error

**Consequence:** Alert noise and misleading reliability metrics.

**Correction:** Treat expected rejection as a controlled outcome.

---

## Anti-Pattern 16 — System Error Converted into Rule Failure

Database failure becomes:

```text
Article not eligible.
```

**Consequence:** Hides outages and misleads Users.

**Correction:** Preserve unexpected failure classification.

---

## Anti-Pattern 17 — Policies Receive Entire Application Context

**Consequence:** Hidden dependencies and accidental coupling.

**Correction:** Pass only required Domain facts and authoritative time.

---

## Anti-Pattern 18 — Rule Catalog Becomes Source of Executable Truth

Documentation is updated but code is not, or code is generated blindly from prose.

**Correction:** Domain code and tests remain executable authority; catalog indexes and explains them.

---

# 7.6.10.123 Rule Enforcement Matrix

| Rule type                | Primary authority                          | Supporting layers                      |
| ------------------------ | ------------------------------------------ | -------------------------------------- |
| Input shape              | Transport/Application validation           | UI                                     |
| Value validity           | Value Object or Domain                     | Transport, Database                    |
| Aggregate invariant      | Aggregate Root                             | Database constraints                   |
| Cross-Entity eligibility | Domain Policy                              | Application coordination               |
| Authorization            | Authorization policy                       | UI capability hints                    |
| Visibility               | Owning Domain query/policy                 | Database predicates, cache rules       |
| Uniqueness               | Domain/application intent                  | Database unique constraint             |
| Lifecycle transition     | Entity/Aggregate                           | Application handler, Database checks   |
| Consent                  | Newsletter Domain                          | Database state, transport confirmation |
| Request limits           | Transport/Infrastructure                   | UI guidance                            |
| Provider contract        | Infrastructure adapter                     | Application error mapping              |
| Editorial guidance       | Editorial Handbook/policy where formalized | CMS guidance                           |

The matrix prevents one layer from being expected to solve every concern.

---

# 7.6.10.124 Rule Change Review Checklist

Before approving a rule change, verify:

* Which Domain owns the rule?
* Is it an invariant, policy, authorization rule, validation rule, or recommendation?
* Is the new behaviour already approved by the product specification?
* Does the change affect existing records?
* Does it require migration?
* Does it require effective dating?
* Does it affect scheduled or queued work?
* Are policy codes stable?
* Are User-facing messages updated?
* Are all transports still invoking the same rule?
* Are Database constraints aligned?
* Are tests updated?
* Is Audit required?
* Is Analytics classification affected?
* Does the Decision Log require an entry?
* Can the rule be explained clearly?

---

# 7.6.10.125 Architecture Decision Record

**Decision:** TWN centralizes authoritative business rules in its owning Domain through Entity behaviour, Aggregate invariants, Value Objects, Specifications, and pure Domain Policies. Application handlers assemble authoritative facts and invoke those rules, while transports, interfaces, repositories, and database constraints provide supporting—not competing—enforcement.

**Supporting decisions:**

* Business rules are distinguished from transport and technical rules.
* Input validity is distinct from operation eligibility.
* Aggregate Roots protect invariants during every state transition.
* Cross-Aggregate rules receive explicit facts from the Application layer.
* Policies do not query databases or call providers.
* Policy results contain stable reason codes and distinguish errors, warnings, and guidance.
* Authentication, authorization, and business eligibility remain separate decisions.
* Lifecycle transitions are explicitly modelled and enforced server-side.
* The same Domain rule is reused by Server Actions, APIs, workers, schedulers, and administrative scripts.
* Time-dependent rules receive one authoritative injected time.
* Historical records are not automatically invalidated by future policy changes.
* Rule versions or effective dates are introduced only where actual product requirements justify them.
* Significant decisions may create accountable records, while ordinary policy evaluations do not generate permanent audit noise.
* Rule identifiers provide stable references for CMS feedback, tests, Analytics, and documentation.
* Confirmed architectural rules are separated from potential product rules whose thresholds remain undecided.
* The Rule Catalog documents ownership and enforcement but does not replace executable Domain code and tests.
* Database constraints provide final structural protection without becoming the sole owner of business workflows.
* A general rule engine is not introduced initially.
* Rule changes require impact analysis across persisted state, queued work, caches, interfaces, and historical interpretation.
* Expected policy rejection is distinct from infrastructure or programming failure.
* Rule testing includes boundary, composition, historical, security, Aggregate, and persistence tests.

**Reasons:**

* prevents contradictory business behaviour;
* preserves one source of truth;
* keeps Domain decisions testable;
* gives editors clear explanations;
* protects alternate transports from bypass;
* supports future product evolution;
* prevents infrastructure from controlling business meaning;
* supports accountable moderation and publication;
* avoids inventing arbitrary TWN requirements;
* keeps the modular monolith aligned with business ownership.

**Consequences:**

Positive:

* rules are easier to locate and change;
* publication and moderation decisions become explainable;
* UI guidance can use stable backend codes;
* scheduled and asynchronous operations follow the same rules;
* historical policy changes can be managed deliberately;
* security testing has a clear authoritative target.

Costs:

* policies and rule catalogs require maintenance;
* product teams must decide which guidance is genuinely blocking;
* rule changes may require migrations and historical strategy;
* mapping codes into interface language adds work;
* cross-Aggregate policies require explicit fact gathering;
* duplicate supporting checks must remain aligned without redefining authority.

**Expected lifetime:** The single-authoritative-rule principle is permanent. Individual rules, policies, result codes, and effective-date mechanisms will evolve as TWN’s editorial, community, newsletter, Media, and future product requirements mature.

---

# 7.6.10.126 Completion Criteria

This subsection is complete when:

* business rules are distinguished from technical rules;
* validation and eligibility are separated;
* invariants and policies have explicit ownership;
* lifecycle transitions are modelled;
* Aggregate and cross-Aggregate rules are distinguished;
* temporal rules use authoritative injected time;
* authorization remains separate from business eligibility;
* policies are deterministic and infrastructure-independent;
* policy results provide stable explanatory codes;
* errors, warnings, and guidance are distinguished;
* historical rule evolution is addressed;
* Audit is required only for meaningful decisions;
* the Rule Catalog structure is defined;
* confirmed TWN architectural rules are recorded;
* unconfirmed product thresholds remain explicitly undecided;
* rule-engine adoption is deferred;
* enforcement responsibilities across layers are documented;
* rule testing and security-bypass testing are required;
* rule-change governance is established;
* anti-patterns are documented.

---

## End of 7.6.10

The next planned subsection in the agreed **7.6 Backend Architecture** outline is:

# **7.6.11 — Application Services**

This should not repeat **7.6.3 Application Layer Architecture**.

It will focus specifically on when a reusable Application Service is justified beyond a single command or query handler, including:

* reusable orchestration capabilities;
* Application Services versus Domain Services;
* Application Services versus handlers;
* transaction ownership;
* cross-module coordination;
* context passing;
* avoiding giant `ArticleService` and `ContentService` classes;
* service composition and testing;
* when a service should remain a plain function instead.

[1]: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/architecture/microservices/microservice-ddd-cqrs-patterns/ddd-oriented-microservice?utm_source=chatgpt.com "Designing a DDD-oriented microservice - .NET"
[2]: https://cheatsheetseries.owasp.org/cheatsheets/REST_Security_Cheat_Sheet.html?utm_source=chatgpt.com "REST Security - OWASP Cheat Sheet Series"
[3]: https://cheatsheetseries.owasp.org/cheatsheets/Authorization_Cheat_Sheet.html?utm_source=chatgpt.com "Authorization - OWASP Cheat Sheet Series"
[4]: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/architecture/microservices/model/tactical-domain-driven-design?utm_source=chatgpt.com "Use Tactical DDD to Design Microservices - Azure"
[5]: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/architecture/microservices/microservice-ddd-cqrs-patterns/domain-events-design-implementation?utm_source=chatgpt.com "Domain events: Design and implementation - .NET"
[6]: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/architecture/microservices/microservice-ddd-cqrs-patterns/domain-model-layer-validations?utm_source=chatgpt.com "Designing validations in the domain model layer - .NET"

---

# 7.6.11 Application Services

**Status:** Backend Architecture Specification
**Parent chapter:** Volume 7 — Technical Architecture
**Current scope:** Section 7.6 — Backend Architecture
**Applies to:** Reusable application-level coordination within Editorial, Notebook, Journeys, Community, Media, Newsletter, Identity, Administration, and future TWN modules
**Primary objective:** Define when TWN should introduce reusable Application Services, what those services may coordinate, and how to prevent them from becoming oversized procedural containers

---

# Purpose

This subsection defines:

* what an Application Service is;
* how it differs from a command handler;
* how it differs from a Domain Service;
* how it differs from an infrastructure adapter;
* when reusable application orchestration is justified;
* transaction ownership;
* cross-module coordination;
* service inputs and outputs;
* dependency rules;
* service composition;
* testing;
* naming;
* service growth limits;
* when a plain function remains preferable.

The central rule is:

> An Application Service coordinates application capabilities. It does not become the place where every piece of backend logic is stored.

---

# 7.6.11.1 Application Service Definition

An Application Service is a reusable Application-layer capability that coordinates one or more technical or Domain operations without owning the underlying business rules.

Examples may include:

```text
ContentReferenceResolver

EditorialPublicationCoordinator

PublicContentAvailabilityService

ModerationWorkflowCoordinator

MediaAttachmentCoordinator
```

An Application Service may support several command or query handlers when those handlers need the same stable coordination capability.

---

# 7.6.11.2 Application Service Position

The normal dependency direction is:

```text
Transport
    ↓
Command or Query Handler
    ↓
Application Service
    ↓
Domain Policies and Application Ports
    ↓
Infrastructure Implementations
```

An Application Service belongs inside the Application layer.

It may depend on:

* Domain Entities;
* Domain Policies;
* Application ports;
* other modules’ public Application contracts;
* shared backend primitives.

It must not depend on:

* React;
* browser APIs;
* page components;
* concrete provider SDKs;
* concrete database clients;
* unrestricted framework request objects.

---

# 7.6.11.3 Handler Versus Application Service

A handler owns one explicit use case.

Example:

```text
PublishArticleHandler
```

An Application Service provides reusable coordination used by more than one use case.

Example:

```text
PublicContentReferenceResolver
```

may support:

```text
PublishJourneyHandler

PreviewJourneyHandler

ValidateJourneyHandler

GenerateJourneyExportHandler
```

The distinction is:

```text
Handler
    owns one operation

Application Service
    exposes a reusable application capability
```

---

# 7.6.11.4 Application Service Versus Domain Service

A Domain Service contains business logic that belongs to the Domain but not naturally to one Entity or Value Object.

An Application Service coordinates operations around Domain logic.

Example Domain Service:

```text
JourneyStructurePolicy
```

It evaluates:

```text
Is this Journey structure valid?
```

Example Application Service:

```text
JourneyContentResolutionService
```

It coordinates:

* Editorial public queries;
* Notebook public queries;
* Media availability queries;
* content-reference mapping.

Then it supplies the resulting facts to the Domain policy.

The Domain Service decides.

The Application Service gathers and coordinates.

---

# 7.6.11.5 Application Service Versus Infrastructure Adapter

An Infrastructure adapter communicates with a technical provider.

Example:

```text
PostgresArticleRepository

ResendEmailAdapter

SupabaseStorageAdapter
```

An Application Service coordinates application behaviour.

Example:

```text
TransactionalNotificationCoordinator
```

may decide which approved notification request to create after a command succeeds.

It depends on an application-facing port such as:

```typescript
interface NotificationRequestWriter {
  append(
    request: NotificationRequest,
    transaction: TransactionContext,
  ): Promise<void>;
}
```

It should not import the email provider SDK directly.

---

# 7.6.11.6 When an Application Service Is Justified

An Application Service is justified when a capability:

1. is reused by several handlers;
2. coordinates several Application or Domain dependencies;
3. has a stable application-level meaning;
4. does not naturally belong to one Entity;
5. is not merely a technical provider adapter;
6. would otherwise be duplicated across several use cases.

Example:

```text
Resolve publicly eligible content references
```

may be needed by:

* Journey publication;
* Journey preview;
* Search document creation;
* public Collection generation.

This may justify a reusable Application Service.

---

# 7.6.11.7 When an Application Service Is Not Justified

Do not create an Application Service merely because:

* a handler has several lines;
* a class name sounds architectural;
* a function calls a repository;
* two unrelated operations both use the database;
* code can technically be extracted;
* every Domain concept is expected to have a service.

A simple handler may remain:

```typescript
async function archiveArticle(
  command: ArchiveArticleCommand,
): Promise<ArchiveArticleResult> {
  // One clear use case.
}
```

No `ArticleApplicationService` is required.

---

# 7.6.11.8 Service Extraction Test

Before extracting an Application Service, ask:

```text
What stable capability is being named?

Which handlers use it?

Does it have one coherent responsibility?

Would it still make sense if one caller disappeared?

Does it coordinate application behaviour rather than business policy?

Can its contract remain narrow?
```

If these questions cannot be answered clearly, keep the logic local until a real abstraction emerges.

---

# 7.6.11.9 Avoid Premature Reuse

Two similar blocks of code do not automatically represent one abstraction.

Example:

```text
PublishArticle
```

and:

```text
PublishJourney
```

may both:

* authorize;
* load state;
* validate;
* write an Audit record;
* emit an event.

This does not mean TWN needs:

```text
GenericPublishService<T>
```

The publication rules, Aggregate behaviour, results, and side effects may differ significantly.

Prefer duplicated clarity over false generic reuse.

---

# 7.6.11.10 Good Application Service Characteristics

A good Application Service is:

* cohesive;
* narrowly named;
* transport-independent;
* provider-independent;
* explicit about dependencies;
* explicit about inputs;
* explicit about outputs;
* testable without Next.js;
* free from hidden global state;
* reusable for a real reason.

---

# 7.6.11.11 Poor Application Service Characteristics

Warning signs include:

```text
ArticleService
ContentService
PlatformService
BackendService
CommonService
UtilityService
```

containing dozens of unrelated methods.

Other warning signs:

* accepts `unknown`;
* returns untyped objects;
* imports every repository;
* reads raw environment variables;
* performs redirects;
* sends emails directly;
* starts transactions invisibly;
* mutates several domains without explicit ownership;
* contains large numbers of conditional business rules.

---

# 7.6.11.12 Application Service Naming

Names should describe a capability.

Good:

```text
ContentReferenceResolutionService

PublicationReadinessContextBuilder

JourneyContentAvailabilityService

ModerationDecisionRecorder

PublicMediaReferenceResolver
```

Weak:

```text
HelperService

Manager

Processor

Utility

CommonService
```

The name should explain why the service exists.

---

# 7.6.11.13 Function Versus Class

Application Services may be implemented as functions or classes.

Function-based approach:

```typescript
export function createContentReferenceResolver(
  dependencies: ContentReferenceResolverDependencies,
) {
  return async function resolveContentReferences(
    references: readonly ContentReference[],
  ): Promise<ContentReferenceResolutionResult> {
    // Coordination.
  };
}
```

Class-based approach:

```typescript
export class ContentReferenceResolver {
  constructor(
    private readonly editorial:
      EditorialPublicQueries,
    private readonly notebook:
      NotebookPublicQueries,
    private readonly media:
      MediaPublicQueries,
  ) {}

  async resolve(
    references: readonly ContentReference[],
  ): Promise<ContentReferenceResolutionResult> {
    // Coordination.
  }
}
```

TWN should prefer the simpler representation.

Use a class when it improves:

* cohesion;
* dependency organization;
* interface implementation;
* lifecycle clarity.

Do not use classes merely to imitate enterprise architecture.

---

# 7.6.11.14 Explicit Dependencies

An Application Service should declare only the capabilities it needs.

Example:

```typescript
type ContentReferenceResolverDependencies = {
  editorial: EditorialPublicQueries;
  notebook: NotebookPublicQueries;
  media: MediaPublicQueries;
};
```

Avoid:

```typescript
type Dependencies = {
  application: EntireApplication;
};
```

or:

```typescript
constructor(
  private readonly container: ServiceContainer,
) {}
```

A service locator hides dependency relationships and makes testing more difficult.

---

# 7.6.11.15 Service Inputs

Inputs should be application-oriented.

Example:

```typescript
type ResolveContentReferencesRequest = {
  references: readonly ContentReference[];
  visibility: "public" | "editorial-preview";
  actor?: ActorContext;
};
```

Inputs should not include:

* raw `Request`;
* raw `FormData`;
* database connection;
* provider SDK object;
* React component;
* arbitrary mutable records.

---

# 7.6.11.16 Service Outputs

Outputs should express application meaning.

Example:

```typescript
type ContentReferenceResolutionResult = {
  resolved: ResolvedContentReference[];
  unresolved: Array<{
    reference: ContentReference;
    reason:
      | "not-found"
      | "not-public"
      | "unsupported-type";
  }>;
};
```

The service should not return:

* database rows;
* provider responses;
* framework responses;
* mutable Domain internals.

---

# 7.6.11.17 Application Service and Authorization

An Application Service may need Actor context when the capability itself is permission-sensitive.

Example:

```text
Resolve editorial-preview references
```

may require authorized access to unpublished content.

However, authorization ownership should remain clear.

Possible approaches:

## Handler-owned authorization

The handler authorizes the operation before invoking the service.

Appropriate when the service receives only already-approved facts.

## Service-level resource authorization

The service evaluates access because it coordinates resources across modules.

Appropriate when the service itself performs sensitive cross-module retrieval.

The same permission must not be inconsistently enforced in several places.

Detailed Authorization design remains in Section 7.10.

---

# 7.6.11.18 Application Service and Domain Policies

An Application Service may prepare policy input.

Example:

```text
JourneyPublicationContextBuilder
```

may gather:

* Journey state;
* resolved Article statuses;
* Notebook Entry statuses;
* Media readiness.

It returns:

```typescript
type JourneyPublicationContext = {
  journey: Journey;
  contentStates: ResolvedJourneyContentState[];
  coverMediaState: PublicMediaState | null;
};
```

Then:

```typescript
journeyPublicationPolicy.evaluate(context);
```

The context builder gathers facts.

The policy decides eligibility.

---

# 7.6.11.19 Do Not Put Policy Decisions in Context Builders

Bad:

```typescript
class JourneyPublicationContextBuilder {
  async build(...) {
    if (references.length < 3) {
      throw new JourneyNotPublishable();
    }
  }
}
```

The builder is now deciding policy.

Preferred:

```text
Builder
    gathers facts

Policy
    evaluates facts
```

This preserves rule ownership and testability.

---

# 7.6.11.20 Application Service and Transactions

An Application Service may participate in a transaction.

It should not start a hidden transaction unless transaction ownership is part of its documented contract.

Two acceptable patterns exist.

## Caller-owned transaction

```typescript
await transactionManager.run(async (transaction) => {
  await service.perform(
    request,
    transaction,
  );
});
```

Appropriate when the handler owns the complete business operation.

## Service-owned transaction

```typescript
await service.performAtomically(request);
```

Appropriate only when the service itself represents one complete reusable atomic application capability.

The ownership must be explicit.

---

# 7.6.11.21 Prefer Handler-Owned Transactions

The handler usually understands the complete command boundary.

Therefore:

```text
Command Handler
    owns transaction
```

is normally preferable.

This allows the handler to coordinate:

* Aggregate persistence;
* Audit record;
* outbox event;
* related application work.

An inner service starting its own transaction may create:

* nested transaction confusion;
* partial commits;
* unclear rollback;
* hidden performance costs.

---

# 7.6.11.22 Transaction-Aware Service Contract

When a service must write inside a caller-owned transaction, accept an opaque transaction context.

Example:

```typescript
interface ModerationDecisionRecorder {
  record(
    decision: ModerationDecision,
    transaction: TransactionContext,
  ): Promise<void>;
}
```

The service must not expose the concrete PostgreSQL client.

Infrastructure adapters interpret the transaction context.

---

# 7.6.11.23 Read-Only Application Services

Many Application Services are read-only coordinators.

Examples:

```text
ContentReferenceResolver

PublicationContextBuilder

PublicContentAvailabilityService
```

Read-only services should not:

* create hidden Audit records;
* mutate caches as a business effect;
* update Analytics;
* change Entity state.

Their repeatability makes them easier to use across handlers and queries.

---

# 7.6.11.24 Stateful Application Services

Application Services should ordinarily be stateless between invocations.

Avoid storing:

```text
current Actor

current transaction

currently processed Article

last result
```

as mutable service fields.

Backend processes may serve several concurrent operations.

Request-specific state must be passed into each invocation.

---

# 7.6.11.25 Safe Shared State

A reusable service instance may safely retain immutable dependencies such as:

* query interfaces;
* repository interfaces;
* configuration values;
* Clock;
* policy instances.

It should not retain operation-specific mutable data.

---

# 7.6.11.26 Cross-Module Application Services

Some services coordinate several modules.

Example:

```text
JourneyContentResolutionService
```

may depend on:

```text
Editorial public queries
Notebook public queries
Media public queries
Future Podcast public queries
```

Rules:

* import only public module contracts;
* do not import repositories from other modules;
* do not mutate another module’s state directly;
* use bounded concurrency;
* define partial-failure behaviour;
* maintain type-aware results.

---

# 7.6.11.27 Cross-Module Service Ownership

A cross-module service should live with the module owning the primary use case.

Example:

```text
JourneyContentResolutionService
```

belongs to:

```text
Journeys
```

because Journeys owns the need to resolve content references for Journey behaviour.

It should not live in:

```text
shared/
```

merely because it calls several modules.

Shared ownership should be used only when the capability is genuinely business-neutral.

---

# 7.6.11.28 Orchestration Layer Exception

A separate orchestration or composition module may be justified when:

* no single domain owns the workflow;
* the operation is platform-wide;
* several modules participate equally;
* ownership would be misleading in one module.

Potential example:

```text
AccountDeactivationCoordinator
```

may coordinate:

* Identity;
* Authorization;
* active sessions;
* Newsletter relationship;
* future privacy workflows.

Even then, each module’s mutations must occur through its public commands or ports.

---

# 7.6.11.29 Cross-Module Partial Failure

An Application Service coordinating several reads or secondary effects must define what happens when one dependency fails.

Example:

```text
Resolve 10 Journey references

8 resolve
1 is not public
1 dependency temporarily fails
```

These outcomes are different:

```text
not public
```

is an expected result.

```text
dependency failed
```

is an operational failure.

Do not combine both into:

```text
unresolved
```

without preserving the reason.

---

# 7.6.11.30 Bounded Concurrency

A service resolving many references should avoid unbounded `Promise.all`.

Bad:

```typescript
await Promise.all(
  references.map(resolveOne),
);
```

when `references` may contain thousands of items.

Prefer:

* grouping by content type;
* batch query contracts;
* bounded concurrency;
* maximum input limits;
* efficient projection queries.

Example:

```text
Group Article IDs
    ↓
One Editorial batch query

Group Notebook IDs
    ↓
One Notebook batch query
```

This avoids N+1 calls.

---

# 7.6.11.31 Batch-Oriented Public Contracts

Cross-module resolution should prefer batch contracts where the use case naturally handles collections.

Example:

```typescript
interface EditorialPublicQueries {
  resolvePublishedContentReferences(
    articleIds: readonly ArticleId[],
  ): Promise<
    Map<ArticleId, PublishedContentReference>
  >;
}
```

This is usually more efficient than:

```text
One query per Article ID
```

The maximum batch size must be defined and enforced.

---

# 7.6.11.32 Application Service and Caching

An Application Service should not silently introduce caching unless caching is part of its explicit infrastructure contract.

Preferred:

```text
Application Service
    depends on
ContentReferenceQueries
```

The query implementation or Cache Architecture decides whether results are cached.

The service should not directly import framework cache APIs.

Caching ownership remains in Section 7.13.

---

# 7.6.11.33 Application Service and External Providers

An Application Service may coordinate an application-facing provider port.

Example:

```text
PreviewGenerationCoordinator
```

may call:

* a content query;
* a provider-neutral preview generator;
* a Media storage port.

It should not import provider-specific SDKs.

Provider details remain behind Infrastructure adapters.

---

# 7.6.11.34 Application Service and Background Work

A service may prepare or register background work.

Example:

```text
PublicationFollowUpCoordinator
```

may create requests for:

* Search indexing;
* social image generation;
* newsletter consideration.

However, it should not necessarily execute all work synchronously.

Preferred:

```text
Application Service
    creates durable follow-up requests
```

rather than:

```text
Application Service
    waits for every external system
```

Background-work boundaries remain in 7.6.18.

---

# 7.6.11.35 Service Result Idempotency

If a service coordinates state-changing behaviour that may be retried, its contract must define idempotency.

Example:

```text
ModerationDecisionRecorder
```

should not create duplicate decision records when the same durable operation is replayed.

However, most idempotency should remain owned by the outer command handler because that handler understands the complete command effect.

Do not implement partial idempotency inside one service while allowing the rest of the command to repeat.

---

# 7.6.11.36 Service Error Model

Application Services should distinguish:

* expected application outcomes;
* unavailable dependencies;
* malformed authoritative state;
* unexpected failures.

Example:

```typescript
type ContentReferenceResolutionResult =
  | {
      kind: "resolved";
      references: ResolvedContentReference[];
    }
  | {
      kind: "partially-resolved";
      resolved: ResolvedContentReference[];
      unavailable: UnresolvedContentReference[];
    };
```

Infrastructure failure may still throw or return a controlled dependency failure according to the service contract.

Do not return provider-specific error codes.

---

# 7.6.11.37 Application Service Should Not Own UI Messages

Bad:

```typescript
return {
  error:
    "Oops! One of your Journey articles could not be found.",
};
```

Preferred:

```typescript
return {
  kind: "unresolved-reference",
  referenceId,
  reason: "not-found",
};
```

The frontend maps this to appropriate language.

---

# 7.6.11.38 Application Service Should Not Return HTTP Responses

Avoid:

```typescript
return Response.json(...);
```

or:

```typescript
redirect(...);
```

inside an Application Service.

These are Transport concerns.

The same service may be called by:

* Server Action;
* Route Handler;
* worker;
* scheduler;
* test.

---

# 7.6.11.39 Service Composition

An Application Service may depend on another narrowly scoped Application Service.

Example:

```text
JourneyPublicationContextBuilder
    ↓
ContentReferenceResolver
```

This is acceptable when:

* dependency direction remains acyclic;
* each service has a distinct responsibility;
* the composition is visible;
* no global service graph develops.

Avoid chains such as:

```text
Service A
    ↓
Service B
    ↓
Service C
    ↓
Service D
    ↓
Service E
```

where ownership and transaction behaviour become impossible to trace.

---

# 7.6.11.40 Service Composition Depth

As a practical guideline, repeated deep service chains signal that:

* one service may be too small;
* responsibilities may be incorrectly separated;
* a handler should coordinate directly;
* a Domain model may be missing;
* a workflow may need redesign.

This is a design warning, not a hard numeric prohibition.

---

# 7.6.11.41 Avoid Circular Service Dependencies

Bad:

```text
EditorialPublicationService
    ↓
JourneyReferenceService
    ↓
EditorialPublicationService
```

Resolve cycles by:

* clarifying ownership;
* extracting a narrow read contract;
* moving coordination to the owning handler;
* using a Domain event for a secondary reaction;
* separating fact retrieval from mutation.

Do not hide cycles using dynamic imports or a service locator.

---

# 7.6.11.42 Shared Application Services

A service may belong to shared Application foundations only when it is business-neutral.

Possible examples:

```text
OperationDeadlineCalculator

CursorCodec

ApplicationResultCombiner
```

Most business services do not belong in `shared/`.

Examples that should remain domain-owned:

```text
PublicationReadinessContextBuilder

JourneyContentResolver

SubscriberEligibilityCoordinator
```

---

# 7.6.11.43 Service Interface Position

A service interface is useful when:

* several implementations exist;
* the service is consumed across modules;
* tests require a controlled substitute;
* the implementation is infrastructure-dependent;
* the contract is stable.

Avoid creating an interface for every class automatically.

Example unnecessary pairing:

```text
ContentReferenceResolverInterface
ContentReferenceResolverImpl
```

when only one simple pure implementation exists.

A function type may be sufficient.

---

# 7.6.11.44 Dependency Injection

Application Services should receive dependencies through:

* constructor injection;
* factory parameters;
* explicit function parameters.

Example:

```typescript
const resolveContentReferences =
  createContentReferenceResolver({
    editorialQueries,
    notebookQueries,
    mediaQueries,
  });
```

Avoid retrieving dependencies from global mutable containers during execution.

---

# 7.6.11.45 Composition Root

Application Services are assembled in the backend composition root.

Example:

```typescript
const contentReferenceResolver =
  createContentReferenceResolver({
    editorial:
      editorialModule.publicQueries,
    notebook:
      notebookModule.publicQueries,
    media:
      mediaModule.publicQueries,
  });

const publishJourney =
  createPublishJourneyHandler({
    journeyRepository,
    contentReferenceResolver,
    publicationPolicy,
    transactionManager,
    auditWriter,
    outboxWriter,
    clock,
  });
```

This makes dependency relationships visible.

---

# 7.6.11.46 Application Service Testing

Test Application Services independently when they contain meaningful coordination.

Test:

* successful coordination;
* expected missing data;
* partial resolution;
* dependency failure;
* batching;
* input limits;
* ordering preservation;
* duplicate input handling;
* Actor-sensitive behaviour;
* transaction-context forwarding where applicable.

---

# 7.6.11.47 Avoid Retesting Domain Rules

If the service gathers publication facts, test that it gathers the correct facts.

Do not duplicate every publication-policy test inside the service suite.

Example:

```text
Context Builder Test
    confirms content states are resolved correctly

Policy Test
    confirms those states produce the correct decision
```

Each layer tests its own responsibility.

---

# 7.6.11.48 Use Fakes at Public Contracts

For cross-module service tests, fake the other modules’ public interfaces.

Example:

```typescript
const editorialQueries:
  EditorialPublicQueries = {
  resolvePublishedContentReferences:
    async (ids) => {
      // Controlled results.
    },
};
```

Do not mock the other module’s private repository.

The service consumes only the public contract in production, so tests should preserve that boundary.

---

# 7.6.11.49 Integration Tests

Integration tests are appropriate when the service depends on:

* several real module query implementations;
* transaction propagation;
* batch persistence;
* cross-module read composition.

Integration tests should verify the contracts work together without bypassing module boundaries.

---

# 7.6.11.50 Performance Tests

Application Services that coordinate many resources should be tested with representative volume.

Examples:

* a Journey containing many references;
* publication context with several Media assets;
* moderation dashboard composition;
* large administrative eligibility check.

Measure:

* number of dependency calls;
* batch size;
* memory;
* database connection use;
* latency;
* failure behaviour.

A service that performs one query per reference should be corrected before production growth exposes the problem.

---

# 7.6.11.51 Service Documentation

Every substantial Application Service should document:

```text
Purpose

Owning Module

Consumers

Inputs

Outputs

Dependencies

Authorization Expectations

Transaction Ownership

Failure Semantics

Batch Limits

Consistency Model

Side Effects

Testing Requirements
```

This prevents a reusable service from gradually acquiring undocumented responsibilities.

---

# 7.6.11.52 Service Review Checklist

Before approving an Application Service, verify:

* What reusable capability does it provide?
* Which handlers consume it?
* Why is local handler logic insufficient?
* Is it Application logic rather than Domain policy?
* Is it Application logic rather than infrastructure?
* Does it have one coherent responsibility?
* Are dependencies explicit and narrow?
* Does it import only public module contracts?
* Is transaction ownership explicit?
* Is authorization ownership clear?
* Does it avoid global mutable state?
* Are inputs and outputs transport-independent?
* Are provider-specific types absent?
* Are batch operations bounded?
* Are partial failures represented truthfully?
* Could a plain function be simpler?
* Is the service becoming a generic dumping ground?
* Can it be tested independently?
* Does it create a dependency cycle?
* Is its owner documented?

---

# 7.6.11.53 Application Service Anti-Patterns

## Giant Domain Service Class

```text
ArticleService
```

owns every Article command, query, rule and provider call.

**Correction:** Separate handlers, Domain policies and narrow reusable capabilities.

---

## Service Per Entity

Every Entity receives a service even without coordination needs.

**Correction:** Introduce services only when behaviour justifies them.

---

## Generic Content Service

One service handles Articles, Notebook Entries, Journeys, Podcasts, Books and Courses through conditionals.

**Correction:** Preserve domain-specific modules and use typed cross-content contracts only where the concepts genuinely align.

---

## Hidden Transaction

An inner service commits before the outer command completes.

**Correction:** Make transaction ownership explicit and normally handler-owned.

---

## Infrastructure Leakage

Application Service imports provider SDK.

**Correction:** Depend on Application-facing port.

---

## Business Policy in Service

Service decides publication requirements through procedural checks.

**Correction:** Domain Policy owns the decision.

---

## Raw Framework Input

Service accepts `Request` or `FormData`.

**Correction:** Transport converts input first.

---

## UI Message Result

Service returns reader-facing prose.

**Correction:** Return stable structured outcomes.

---

## Global Mutable Context

Service stores current Actor or transaction in a field.

**Correction:** Pass request-scoped context explicitly.

---

## N+1 Cross-Module Resolution

Service calls one query for every content reference.

**Correction:** Use bounded batch contracts.

---

## Shared Folder Dumping Ground

Business-specific service is placed under `shared/`.

**Correction:** Place it with the owning domain.

---

## Service Chain Maze

Several services delegate through long, unclear chains.

**Correction:** Simplify coordination or return ownership to the handler.

---

## Premature Interface

Interface and implementation are created despite one simple implementation and no substitution need.

**Correction:** Use a function or concrete service until a meaningful abstraction exists.

---

# 7.6.11.54 Proposed TWN Application Services

The following are architectural candidates, not automatic implementation requirements.

---

## Content Reference Resolution Service

**Potential owner:** Journeys or a carefully scoped content-composition capability

**Purpose:** Resolve typed content references through owning modules.

**Potential consumers:**

* Journey validation;
* Journey publication;
* Journey preview;
* future Collection composition.

**Must not:**

* decide Journey publication policy;
* mutate referenced content;
* read other modules’ tables directly.

---

## Publication Context Builder

**Potential owner:** Editorial

**Purpose:** Assemble authoritative facts required by Article publication policy.

**Potential inputs:**

* Article ID;
* revision ID;
* Actor context.

**Potential output:**

* Article;
* selected revision;
* Author state;
* Category state;
* Media state;
* review blockers.

**Must not:**

* publish the Article;
* decide authorization;
* send follow-up events.

---

## Moderation Decision Recorder

**Potential owner:** Community

**Purpose:** Persist accountable moderation-decision information within an approved transaction.

**Must not:**

* decide whether approval or rejection is correct;
* expose internal moderator notes publicly;
* send notifications directly.

---

## Public Media Reference Resolver

**Potential owner:** Media

**Purpose:** Convert approved Media identities into safe public Media references.

**Must not:**

* grant Media visibility;
* trust unverified storage objects;
* expose provider credentials or private object keys.

---

# 7.6.11.55 Candidates Rejected Initially

TWN should not initially create:

```text
UniversalContentService

GenericCrudService

GlobalPublishingService

AllPurposeWorkflowService

BackendManager

CommonBusinessService
```

These names indicate that module ownership is being erased.

---

# 7.6.11.56 Architecture Decision Record

**Decision:** TWN uses narrowly scoped Application Services only for stable, reusable application-level coordination shared across multiple handlers. Single-use-case coordination remains inside command or query handlers, while business decisions remain in Domain Entities and Policies.

**Supporting decisions:**

* Application Services live in the Application layer.
* A handler owns one operation.
* An Application Service provides a reusable coordination capability.
* Domain Services own Domain logic that does not belong to one Entity.
* Infrastructure adapters own technical provider communication.
* Application Services depend on ports and public module contracts.
* Services are stateless between operations.
* Dependencies are explicit and narrow.
* Function-based implementations are preferred when simpler.
* Classes are used only when they improve cohesion or contract implementation.
* Handler-owned transaction boundaries are preferred.
* Service-owned transactions require explicit justification.
* Cross-module services belong to the module owning the primary business need.
* Shared Application Services must be genuinely business-neutral.
* Cross-module collection resolution uses batch interfaces and bounded concurrency.
* Services preserve expected absence, policy rejection and dependency failure as distinct outcomes.
* Application Services do not return HTTP responses, redirects or UI messages.
* Application Services do not directly use framework cache APIs.
* Application Services may register durable follow-up work through approved ports.
* Interfaces are introduced only when substitution or contract value exists.
* Services are tested through their public dependencies rather than another module’s private implementation.
* Giant generic `ArticleService`, `ContentService` and similar classes are prohibited.
* Reusable abstractions are extracted after stable repetition is demonstrated, not before.

**Reasons:**

* prevents duplication across handlers;
* preserves Domain ownership;
* keeps transports thin;
* supports cross-module composition;
* avoids giant procedural service classes;
* improves testing;
* keeps provider and framework details outside application coordination;
* allows future content types without collapsing them into one generic service.

**Consequences:**

Positive:

* reusable coordination receives a clear home;
* handlers remain focused;
* Domain policies remain pure;
* cross-module dependencies remain explicit;
* batch resolution can be optimized centrally;
* testing is simpler.

Costs:

* engineers must judge when reuse is genuine;
* some duplication may remain temporarily;
* transaction ownership must be documented;
* cross-module service contracts add mapping;
* poorly governed services can still become dumping grounds.

**Expected lifetime:** Narrow Application Services will remain part of the architecture. Individual services should be introduced, split or removed according to demonstrated coordination needs rather than theoretical completeness.

---

# Completion Criteria for 7.6.11

This subsection is complete when:

* Application Services are distinguished from handlers;
* Application Services are distinguished from Domain Services;
* Application Services are distinguished from infrastructure adapters;
* service-creation criteria are defined;
* premature abstraction is rejected;
* naming and dependency rules are established;
* transaction ownership is explicit;
* cross-module ownership is defined;
* bounded batching and partial-failure behaviour are addressed;
* service inputs and outputs remain transport-independent;
* service testing expectations are documented;
* candidate TWN services are identified without being falsely declared mandatory;
* giant generic service classes are prohibited.

---

## End of 7.6.11

**Remaining before 7.6 is complete: 13 subsections.**

The next subsection is:

# **7.6.12 — Repository Architecture**

It will define repository ownership, Aggregate persistence, query adapters, purpose-specific repository methods, transaction participation, optimistic concurrency, mapping, error translation, contract testing, and why TWN should avoid universal generic CRUD repositories.


# 7.6 Backend Architecture — Progress

Completed:

