---

# 7.6.13 Provider Adapter Architecture

**Status:** Backend Architecture Specification

**Parent chapter:** Volume 7 — Technical Architecture

**Current scope:** Section 7.6 — Backend Architecture

**Applies to:** Email providers, storage providers, AI providers, search providers, analytics providers, queue providers, payment providers, notification providers, external APIs, and future TWN integrations

**Primary objective:** Ensure TWN can change infrastructure providers without rewriting business logic while preserving clear ownership, reliability, and observability.

---

# Purpose

This section defines:

* external provider boundaries;
* provider-neutral contracts;
* adapter responsibilities;
* provider replacement;
* AI integration architecture;
* storage integration;
* search integration;
* email integration;
* external API integration;
* retries;
* timeouts;
* fallback behaviour;
* contract testing;
* provider migration strategy.

The central rule is:

> TWN business logic must depend on capabilities, not vendors.

The Domain should know:

```text
Send publication email
```

not:

```text
Call Resend API
```

The Domain should know:

```text
Store article cover image
```

not:

```text
Upload to Cloudinary
```

The vendor is implementation.

The capability is architecture.

---

# 7.6.13.1 Why Provider Adapters Exist

Without adapters:

```text
Application
    ↓
Provider SDK
```

becomes:

```text
Editorial
    ↓
Resend

Media
    ↓
Cloudinary

Search
    ↓
Algolia

AI
    ↓
OpenAI
```

Eventually:

```text
Every module
    ↓
Every vendor
```

This creates:

* vendor lock-in;
* duplicated code;
* inconsistent retry behaviour;
* inconsistent error handling;
* migration difficulty;
* security risk.

Provider adapters create:

```text
Application
    ↓
Port
    ↓
Adapter
    ↓
Provider
```

which isolates external dependencies.

---

# 7.6.13.2 Architectural Position

Dependency direction:

```text
Domain
    ↓
Application
    ↓
Provider Port
    ↑
Provider Adapter
    ↓
External Provider
```

Example:

```typescript
interface EmailSender {
  send(
    request: EmailRequest,
  ): Promise<EmailSendResult>;
}
```

Implementation:

```typescript
class ResendEmailAdapter
  implements EmailSender
```

The rest of TWN knows:

```text
EmailSender
```

not:

```text
Resend
```

---

# 7.6.13.3 Port Definition

A port is the capability contract.

Example:

```typescript
interface ObjectStorage {
  upload(
    request: UploadRequest,
  ): Promise<UploadResult>;

  delete(
    objectId: ObjectId,
  ): Promise<void>;
}
```

The port belongs to:

```text
Application Layer
```

The adapter belongs to:

```text
Infrastructure Layer
```

---

# 7.6.13.4 Adapter Definition

An adapter translates between:

```text
TWN capability contract
```

and

```text
Provider-specific API
```

Example:

```text
EmailSender
        ↓
Resend Adapter
        ↓
Resend API
```

The adapter performs:

* request mapping;
* response mapping;
* timeout handling;
* retry coordination;
* provider-specific authentication;
* provider-specific validation;
* provider-specific error translation.

The adapter does not:

* decide business rules;
* authorize users;
* decide publication;
* mutate Aggregates.

---

# 7.6.13.5 Provider-Neutral Language

Ports should describe:

```text
capability
```

not:

```text
vendor feature
```

Good:

```typescript
sendEmail()
```

Bad:

```typescript
sendResendEmail()
```

Good:

```typescript
generateText()
```

Bad:

```typescript
callOpenAI()
```

Good:

```typescript
storeMedia()
```

Bad:

```typescript
uploadCloudinaryAsset()
```

Architecture should survive provider replacement.

---

# 7.6.13.6 TWN Provider Categories

Expected provider categories:

```text
Email
Storage
Search
AI
Analytics
Queues
Push Notifications
Payments
External Data APIs
Monitoring
```

Not all providers exist initially.

The architecture should support them consistently.

---

# 7.6.13.7 Email Provider Port

Example:

```typescript
interface EmailSender {
  send(
    request: EmailRequest,
  ): Promise<EmailSendResult>;
}
```

Possible implementations:

```text
ResendEmailAdapter

PostmarkEmailAdapter

SESAdapter

MailgunAdapter
```

Editorial code should not change when switching providers.

---

# 7.6.13.8 Storage Provider Port

Example:

```typescript
interface MediaStorage {
  upload(
    request: UploadMediaRequest,
  ): Promise<StoredMedia>;
}
```

Possible implementations:

```text
CloudinaryAdapter

SupabaseStorageAdapter

S3StorageAdapter

R2StorageAdapter
```

Media module consumes:

```text
MediaStorage
```

not provider SDKs.

---

# 7.6.13.9 Search Provider Port

Example:

```typescript
interface SearchIndexer {
  index(
    document: SearchDocument,
  ): Promise<void>;

  remove(
    documentId: string,
  ): Promise<void>;
}
```

Possible implementations:

```text
AlgoliaAdapter

TypesenseAdapter

ElasticAdapter

MeilisearchAdapter
```

Search infrastructure remains replaceable.

---

# 7.6.13.10 AI Provider Port

This is especially important for TWN.

TWN is expected to use:

* writing assistance;
* research assistance;
* summarization;
* semantic search;
* recommendation systems;
* future notebook intelligence.

AI architecture must not assume one model vendor.

Example:

```typescript
interface TextGenerationProvider {
  generate(
    request: TextGenerationRequest,
  ): Promise<TextGenerationResult>;
}
```

Possible adapters:

```text
OpenAIAdapter
AnthropicAdapter
GeminiAdapter
NVIDIAAdapter
OpenRouterAdapter
```

---

# 7.6.13.11 Multi-Model Future

Volume 7 already assumes future model routing.

Therefore:

```text
AI Capability
        ↓
AI Gateway
        ↓
Provider Adapter
        ↓
Model
```

not:

```text
Feature
      ↓
Direct OpenAI Call
```

The gateway can later support:

```text
Cost optimization

Fallback models

Quality routing

Model experiments

Regional routing
```

without changing application logic.

---

# 7.6.13.12 Analytics Provider Port

Example:

```typescript
interface AnalyticsRecorder {
  record(
    event: AnalyticsEvent,
  ): Promise<void>;
}
```

Possible adapters:

```text
PostHog

Plausible

Segment

Mixpanel
```

The rest of TWN records:

```text
ArticlePublished
```

not:

```text
Mixpanel.track()
```

---

# 7.6.13.13 Queue Provider Port

Example:

```typescript
interface BackgroundJobDispatcher {
  enqueue(
    job: BackgroundJob,
  ): Promise<void>;
}
```

Potential providers:

```text
BullMQ

SQS

Cloud Tasks

Temporal

Future Queue System
```

Background work remains provider-independent.

---

# 7.6.13.14 External APIs

Some providers are neither infrastructure nor core platform services.

Example:

```text
Wikipedia APIs

Translation APIs

Geolocation APIs

Future Partner APIs
```

These still require adapters.

Never allow:

```typescript
fetch(...)
```

calls scattered throughout modules.

---

# 7.6.13.15 Adapter Ownership

Each adapter belongs to Infrastructure.

Example:

```text
media/
 └─ infrastructure/
     └─ providers/
         └─ cloudinary.adapter.ts
```

The provider implementation should not leak into:

```text
domain/

application/

transport/
```

---

# 7.6.13.16 Provider SDK Isolation

Provider SDK imports should be isolated.

Example:

```typescript
import { Resend } from "resend";
```

belongs only inside:

```text
ResendEmailAdapter
```

Never inside:

```text
ArticleService

JourneyService

PublicationPolicy

React components
```

---

# 7.6.13.17 Adapter Response Validation

External providers are not trusted.

All responses should be validated.

Example:

```text
Provider says upload succeeded.
```

Adapter verifies:

```text
Object identifier exists.

Required fields exist.

Response shape valid.
```

Never trust raw external responses.

---

# 7.6.13.18 Timeout Ownership

Every provider call must have an explicit timeout.

Never:

```typescript
await provider.request(...)
```

without deadline management.

Reasons:

* hung requests;
* resource exhaustion;
* degraded UX;
* cascading failures.

Timeouts should be configurable.

---

# 7.6.13.19 Retry Ownership

Retries should be deliberate.

Safe candidates:

```text
Email submission

Search indexing

Analytics delivery

Webhook notification
```

Unsafe candidates:

```text
Payment charge

Content publication

User creation
```

unless idempotency is guaranteed.

Retries belong to adapter policy or background workers.

Not arbitrary calling code.

---

# 7.6.13.20 Retry Classification

Failures should be classified:

```text
Transient

Permanent

Unknown
```

Transient:

```text
Network timeout

429

Temporary outage
```

Permanent:

```text
Invalid request

Invalid API key

Unsupported capability
```

Only transient failures should be retried automatically.

---

# 7.6.13.21 Provider Error Translation

Provider-specific errors should be translated.

Example:

```text
Resend API Error
```

becomes:

```typescript
EmailProviderUnavailable
```

Application code should not inspect:

```text
Provider SDK exceptions
```

directly.

---

# 7.6.13.22 Fallback Strategy

Not every capability needs fallback.

Fallback candidates:

```text
AI Generation

Search Indexing

Analytics
```

Poor fallback candidates:

```text
Payment Processing

Authentication
```

Fallbacks must be explicitly documented.

Not silently introduced.

---

# 7.6.13.23 AI Fallback Example

Example:

```text
Primary
    Claude

Fallback
    Gemini

Emergency
    OpenRouter
```

The caller requests:

```text
Generate Summary
```

not:

```text
Use Claude
```

Provider choice becomes infrastructure.

---

# 7.6.13.24 Adapter Idempotency

If a provider call can be repeated:

```text
Email send

Webhook

Search index update
```

the adapter must support idempotent handling where possible.

Provider idempotency capabilities should be documented.

---

# 7.6.13.25 Provider Capability Differences

Providers are rarely identical.

Example:

```text
Provider A
    supports streaming

Provider B
    does not
```

Architecture must expose:

```text
Core capability
```

and optionally:

```text
Provider-specific extensions
```

without polluting ordinary callers.

---

# 7.6.13.26 Lowest Common Denominator Trap

Avoid reducing every provider to:

```text
Only capabilities shared by everyone
```

This often weakens architecture.

Instead:

```text
Core capability
```

plus

```text
Optional capability contracts
```

when needed.

---

# 7.6.13.27 Feature Flags and Provider Selection

Provider routing may later use:

```text
Feature flags

Experiments

Canary releases

Cost optimization
```

Therefore:

```text
Provider selection
```

should be centralized.

Not scattered through business code.

---

# 7.6.13.28 Vendor Lock-In Classification

Every provider should be classified.

Example:

```text
Low lock-in
Medium lock-in
High lock-in
```

Factors:

* migration cost;
* proprietary APIs;
* exported data;
* ecosystem dependence.

This helps future technical planning.

---

# 7.6.13.29 Provider Documentation

Every adapter should document:

```text
Provider

Capability

Owner

Timeout

Retry policy

Fallback policy

Idempotency support

Monitoring

Cost considerations

Migration complexity
```

---

# 7.6.13.30 Provider Contract Testing

Every adapter must pass a contract suite.

Example:

```text
EmailSender Contract
```

should work for:

```text
Resend

SES

Postmark
```

without changing application behaviour.

---

# 7.6.13.31 Adapter Integration Tests

Integration tests should verify:

```text
Authentication

Request mapping

Response mapping

Error mapping

Timeout handling
```

against provider environments when feasible.

---

# 7.6.13.32 Fake Providers

Application tests should use fakes.

Example:

```typescript
class FakeEmailSender
```

This allows:

```text
Fast tests

Deterministic behaviour

No provider cost
```

---

# 7.6.13.33 Adapter Observability

Every provider call should record:

```text
Provider

Operation

Duration

Outcome

Retry count
```

without exposing secrets.

---

# 7.6.13.34 Secret Management

Adapters must receive credentials through approved configuration.

Never:

```typescript
const API_KEY = "...";
```

inside code.

Secrets belong to:

```text
Environment management

Secret stores

Deployment configuration
```

---

# 7.6.13.35 Provider Migration Strategy

Provider replacement should require changes only in:

```text
Infrastructure Adapter

Configuration

Composition Root
```

not:

```text
Domain

Application

Frontend
```

This is the primary success metric of adapter architecture.

---

# 7.6.13.36 Architecture Decision Record

**Decision:** TWN isolates all third-party systems behind provider-neutral Application ports implemented by Infrastructure adapters.

**Supporting decisions:**

* providers never appear in Domain logic;
* providers never appear in business rules;
* adapters own request/response translation;
* provider SDKs remain isolated;
* retries, timeouts and error translation are centralized;
* AI systems use capability-based routing;
* future provider replacement is expected;
* contract testing is mandatory.

**Reasons:**

* prevents vendor lock-in;
* improves testability;
* supports multi-provider AI architecture;
* centralizes resilience policies;
* protects business logic from provider changes.

**Consequences:**

Positive:

* provider replacement becomes realistic;
* AI architecture remains flexible;
* testing improves;
* resilience improves.

Costs:

* additional abstraction layer;
* adapter maintenance;
* contract-test maintenance.

---

# Completion Criteria

This subsection is complete when:

* ports and adapters are distinguished;
* provider SDK isolation is enforced;
* AI, storage, search and email providers are covered;
* retry and timeout ownership is defined;
* fallback policies are defined;
* error translation is defined;
* contract testing is required;
* migration strategy is documented.

---

## End of 7.6.13

**Remaining before 7.6 is complete: 11 subsections.**

Next:

# **7.6.14 — Transaction Management**

This will define:

* transaction boundaries;
* transaction ownership;
* unit of work;
* PostgreSQL transaction strategy;
* outbox pattern;
* commit and rollback behaviour;
* nested transaction policy;
* long-running workflow boundaries;
* consistency guarantees;
* eventual consistency inside the TWN modular monolith.

# 7.6 Backend Architecture — Progress

Completed:

```text
7.6.1  Purpose, Scope & Backend Philosophy
7.6.2  Backend Module Structure, Boundaries & Dependency Rules
7.6.3  Application Layer Architecture
7.6.4  Domain Layer Architecture
7.6.5  Infrastructure Layer Architecture
7.6.6  Transport Layer Architecture
7.6.7  Command Architecture
7.6.8  Query Architecture
7.6.9  Domain Entities, Value Objects & Aggregate Boundaries
7.6.10 Domain Policies, Business Rules & Invariant Enforcement
7.6.11 Application Services
7.6.12 Repository Architecture
7.6.13 Provider Adapter Architecture
```

After this subsection, **10 subsections will remain** before 7.6 is complete.

---

# 7.6.14 Transaction Management

**Status:** Backend Architecture Specification
**Parent chapter:** Volume 7 — Technical Architecture
**Current scope:** Section 7.6 — Backend Architecture
**Applies to:** All commands that modify authoritative TWN state, including editorial publication, Journey structure, moderation, Media lifecycle, newsletter consent, identity administration, Audit recording and durable event registration
**Primary objective:** Define how TWN groups state changes atomically, controls transaction ownership, handles rollback, coordinates Domain events, avoids long-running transactions and manages consistency across modules and external systems

---

# Purpose

This subsection defines:

* what constitutes a transaction;
* transaction ownership;
* transaction boundaries;
* PostgreSQL transaction behaviour;
* isolation-level selection;
* transaction context propagation;
* nested operations;
* savepoints;
* Aggregate and cross-module transactions;
* external calls;
* Domain events;
* the transactional outbox;
* commit and rollback;
* after-commit behaviour;
* transaction retries;
* deadlocks and serialization failures;
* transaction timeouts;
* observability;
* testing;
* long-running workflows;
* eventual consistency;
* compensating actions;
* anti-patterns.

The central rule is:

> A transaction protects one authoritative consistency boundary. It must not become a container for every action triggered by a business operation.

---

# 7.6.14.1 Transaction Definition

A database transaction groups related persistence operations so they either:

```text
Commit together
```

or:

```text
Roll back together
```

Example:

```text
Publish Article

- update Article lifecycle
- select published revision
- create publication record
- create required Audit record
- append ArticlePublished outbox record
```

The operation must not leave TWN in a state where only some of these required changes were committed.

PostgreSQL transactions provide an all-or-nothing execution boundary: statements inside a transaction become visible together on commit, or their effects are discarded on rollback. ([PostgreSQL][1])

---

# 7.6.14.2 Transaction Is a Consistency Tool

A transaction exists to protect:

* Domain invariants;
* Aggregate integrity;
* ownership relationships;
* lifecycle transitions;
* version updates;
* Audit requirements;
* durable event registration.

A transaction does not exist merely because several functions happen to execute in sequence.

Example:

```text
Publish Article
+
Send announcement email
+
Update Search
+
Record Analytics
```

does not mean all four operations belong inside one database transaction.

The Article state and its durable outbox event may require atomicity.

Email, Search and Analytics can usually occur afterward.

---

# 7.6.14.3 Transaction Ownership

The outer command handler normally owns the business transaction.

Example:

```typescript
export function createPublishArticleHandler(
  dependencies: PublishArticleDependencies,
) {
  return async function publishArticle(
    command: PublishArticleCommand,
  ): Promise<PublishArticleResult> {
    return dependencies.transactionManager.run(
      async (transaction) => {
        // Load, change and persist authoritative state.
      },
    );
  };
}
```

The command handler understands:

* the complete state change;
* required repositories;
* mandatory Audit entries;
* required outbox records;
* success and rollback conditions.

A Repository should participate in the transaction.

It should not secretly define the overall business transaction.

---

# 7.6.14.4 Transaction Boundary Rule

A transaction boundary should include only operations that must commit atomically.

Include:

```text
Authoritative database writes
Required Audit records
Required outbox records
Required relationship changes
Required concurrency checks
```

Exclude:

```text
Email delivery
Search indexing
AI generation
Media transcoding
Webhook delivery
Remote Analytics
External storage processing
Long-running file conversion
```

External systems cannot ordinarily participate safely in the same local PostgreSQL transaction.

---

# 7.6.14.5 Atomicity Question

Before placing two operations in one transaction, ask:

```text
Would committing one without the other
create invalid authoritative TWN state?
```

If yes:

```text
They likely belong in one transaction.
```

If no:

```text
The secondary action may belong after commit
or in durable background work.
```

Example:

```text
Article lifecycle update
without its required published revision pointer
```

would be invalid.

Therefore both belong together.

Example:

```text
Published Article
without immediate Search indexing
```

is temporarily incomplete as a discovery experience, but the Article itself remains valid.

Search may catch up asynchronously.

---

# 7.6.14.6 Aggregate Transaction Rule

A command modifying one Aggregate normally uses one transaction.

```text
Load Aggregate
    ↓
Validate expected version
    ↓
Execute Domain behaviour
    ↓
Persist Aggregate
    ↓
Write Audit and outbox
    ↓
Commit
```

The transaction protects the Aggregate state resulting from one approved Domain transition.

---

# 7.6.14.7 One Transaction May Span Several Tables

A Journey Aggregate may be stored in:

```text
journeys
journey_chapters
journey_content_memberships
```

A reorder command may modify records across all three tables.

That remains one Aggregate transaction because:

* Chapter ownership;
* ordering;
* memberships;
* Journey version;

must remain consistent together.

The number of tables does not determine the number of transactions.

---

# 7.6.14.8 One Transaction Should Not Automatically Span Several Aggregates

The modular monolith allows a shared PostgreSQL transaction across multiple module-owned records.

That capability must not erase Domain boundaries.

Before changing several Aggregates atomically, determine:

1. whether the operation is truly one invariant;
2. whether one Aggregate should own the relationship;
3. whether a secondary change can follow through an event;
4. whether temporary inconsistency is acceptable;
5. whether the proposed transaction creates unnecessary coupling.

Cross-Aggregate transactions remain possible, but exceptional.

---

# 7.6.14.9 Cross-Module Transaction Position

A cross-module transaction may be justified when all of the following are true:

* modules share the same PostgreSQL database;
* the operation represents one business commitment;
* partial completion would create invalid authoritative state;
* each module retains ownership of its own writes;
* transaction propagation is explicit;
* future extraction cost is accepted.

Example candidate:

```text
Create local User
+
accept one-time invitation
```

may require atomicity if an accepted invitation must always correspond to a created User.

The final Identity design will determine the actual boundary.

---

# 7.6.14.10 Avoid Shared Transactions for Secondary Reactions

Bad:

```text
Editorial transaction
    ↓
Directly updates Search tables
    ↓
Directly updates Analytics tables
    ↓
Directly creates newsletter campaign entries
```

This creates a large cross-domain transaction.

Preferred:

```text
Editorial transaction
    ├── Article state
    ├── Audit record
    └── ArticlePublished outbox event

After commit
    ├── Search reacts
    ├── Analytics reacts
    └── Newsletter evaluates
```

The primary Domain transaction remains small and authoritative.

---

# 7.6.14.11 Transaction Manager Port

The Application layer should depend on an abstract transaction capability.

Conceptual contract:

```typescript
export interface TransactionManager {
  run<TResult>(
    operation: (
      transaction: TransactionContext,
    ) => Promise<TResult>,
    options?: TransactionOptions,
  ): Promise<TResult>;
}
```

Possible options:

```typescript
export type TransactionOptions = {
  isolation?:
    | "read-committed"
    | "repeatable-read"
    | "serializable";

  mode?:
    | "read-write"
    | "read-only";

  timeoutMs?: number;
};
```

Only options actually supported and required by TWN should be exposed.

Do not create a universal database transaction configuration API prematurely.

---

# 7.6.14.12 Opaque Transaction Context

Application code may carry an opaque transaction context.

```typescript
declare const transactionContextBrand:
  unique symbol;

export type TransactionContext = {
  readonly [transactionContextBrand]: true;
};
```

Repositories and transactional writers accept it.

Application code must not access:

```typescript
transaction.client.query(...)
```

The concrete PostgreSQL client remains private to Infrastructure.

---

# 7.6.14.13 Infrastructure Implementation

Conceptual PostgreSQL implementation:

```typescript
export class PostgresTransactionManager
  implements TransactionManager {
  constructor(
    private readonly pool: DatabasePool,
  ) {}

  async run<TResult>(
    operation: (
      transaction: TransactionContext,
    ) => Promise<TResult>,
    options?: TransactionOptions,
  ): Promise<TResult> {
    const client =
      await this.pool.connect();

    try {
      await client.query("BEGIN");

      await applyTransactionOptions(
        client,
        options,
      );

      const transaction =
        createPostgresTransactionContext(
          client,
        );

      const result =
        await operation(transaction);

      await client.query("COMMIT");

      return result;
    } catch (error) {
      await rollbackSafely(client);

      throw translateTransactionError(
        error,
      );
    } finally {
      client.release();
    }
  }
}
```

The final implementation must use the selected PostgreSQL library correctly.

This code is architectural illustration, not a finished production implementation.

---

# 7.6.14.14 No Transaction at Module Import

Transactions must begin only inside an explicit operation.

Never:

```typescript
const transaction =
  await database.begin();
```

at module initialization.

Module imports must not:

* acquire database connections;
* begin transactions;
* create locks;
* execute business writes.

---

# 7.6.14.15 Transaction Scope Must Be Visible

The transaction boundary should be visible in the command handler or clearly named service.

Good:

```typescript
return transactionManager.run(
  async (transaction) => {
    // Atomic work.
  },
);
```

Weak:

```typescript
return publicationService.publish(
  command,
);
```

when `publicationService` silently starts several nested transactions.

Hidden transaction boundaries make:

* rollback;
* performance;
* retries;
* testing;
* failure analysis;

difficult to understand.

---

# 7.6.14.16 Default Isolation Level

PostgreSQL’s default isolation level is `READ COMMITTED`. Under this level, each statement sees rows committed before that statement begins, so two statements in the same transaction may observe different committed states when concurrent transactions complete between them. ([PostgreSQL][2])

TWN should use `READ COMMITTED` for ordinary commands unless a specific invariant requires stronger isolation.

Reasons:

* it is PostgreSQL’s standard default;
* it supports normal concurrent workloads;
* many TWN operations can use explicit version checks;
* stronger isolation has additional conflict and retry implications.

---

# 7.6.14.17 Isolation Is Not a Substitute for Domain Design

Increasing isolation does not repair:

* missing uniqueness constraints;
* incorrect Aggregate boundaries;
* absent version checks;
* duplicated commands;
* hidden side effects;
* invalid lifecycle logic.

Isolation controls how concurrent transactions observe and conflict with one another.

It does not decide TWN business meaning.

---

# 7.6.14.18 Read Committed with Optimistic Concurrency

Ordinary editorial editing should normally use:

```text
READ COMMITTED
+
explicit Aggregate version
+
conditional update
```

Example:

```sql
UPDATE articles
SET
  status = $1,
  version = version + 1,
  updated_at = $2
WHERE id = $3
  AND version = $4
RETURNING version;
```

This avoids holding a lock while a human edits a draft and prevents silent overwrites when two commands use the same expected version.

---

# 7.6.14.19 Repeatable Read Position

`REPEATABLE READ` may be considered when one transaction needs a stable snapshot across several reads.

Potential case:

```text
Build a consistency-sensitive report
from several related authoritative reads
```

However:

* the transaction may still fail under concurrency;
* long transactions retain older snapshots;
* the application must retry serialization-related failures where appropriate.

PostgreSQL documents that `REPEATABLE READ` and `SERIALIZABLE` transactions may produce serialization failures that require the complete transaction to be retried. ([PostgreSQL][3])

TWN should not use `REPEATABLE READ` globally.

---

# 7.6.14.20 Serializable Position

`SERIALIZABLE` may be justified for rare operations whose correctness depends on a complex predicate that cannot be protected safely through:

* unique constraints;
* conditional updates;
* row locking;
* Aggregate versioning.

Potential example:

```text
Allocate one resource only if
no conflicting active allocation exists
across a complex range.
```

PostgreSQL’s Serializable isolation can abort transactions to prevent serialization anomalies; applications using it must be prepared to retry the entire transaction. ([PostgreSQL][2])

TWN should not default every transaction to Serializable.

---

# 7.6.14.21 Isolation Selection Rule

Use the weakest isolation level that correctly protects the documented invariant.

Selection order:

```text
1. Database constraint
2. Conditional atomic update
3. Explicit row lock
4. Repeatable Read
5. Serializable
```

This is a design sequence, not an absolute universal hierarchy.

The correct mechanism depends on:

* invariant;
* query shape;
* contention;
* transaction length;
* retry cost.

---

# 7.6.14.22 Read-Only Transactions

A read-only transaction may be useful for:

* consistency-sensitive administrative reporting;
* exports;
* migration verification;
* reconciliation.

Ordinary public page queries do not automatically require explicit read-only transactions.

A single SQL statement already executes atomically from the database’s perspective.

Do not wrap every read query in a manual transaction without need.

---

# 7.6.14.23 Locking Position

PostgreSQL provides explicit table- and row-locking mechanisms for cases where ordinary MVCC behaviour does not provide the required application semantics. ([PostgreSQL][4])

TWN should prefer:

* optimistic concurrency for human editing;
* conditional claims for jobs;
* narrow row locks for short exclusive transitions.

Avoid long-held pessimistic locks around interactive work.

---

# 7.6.14.24 `SELECT ... FOR UPDATE`

A purpose-specific Repository operation may use a row lock when a command must serialize access to one current record.

Possible case:

```text
Claim one due scheduled publication
```

Conceptual flow:

```sql
SELECT id, version
FROM scheduled_publications
WHERE id = $1
FOR UPDATE;
```

Then verify and update within the same short transaction.

This must remain inside Infrastructure.

Application code should request:

```text
claim due publication
```

not:

```text
execute FOR UPDATE
```

---

# 7.6.14.25 Lock Scope

Locks should be:

* as narrow as possible;
* held for as little time as possible;
* acquired in consistent order;
* released through commit or rollback;
* used only for a documented invariant.

Do not lock an entire table when one row or conditional update is sufficient.

---

# 7.6.14.26 Deadlock Prevention

Deadlocks may occur when concurrent transactions acquire locks in incompatible order.

TWN should reduce risk through:

* consistent lock ordering;
* short transactions;
* small write sets;
* avoiding unnecessary cross-module transactions;
* bounded batch sizes;
* avoiding remote calls while holding locks.

A deadlock may still occur.

The infrastructure must classify it as a retryable transaction failure when the complete command can be retried safely.

---

# 7.6.14.27 Transaction Retry Rule

A transaction may be retried only when:

1. the failure category is retryable;
2. the entire transaction callback can safely run again;
3. side effects have not escaped the transaction;
4. idempotency and command semantics permit retry;
5. the retry count is bounded.

Retry candidates may include:

* serialization failure;
* selected deadlock cases;
* transient connection interruption before commit is known.

Do not automatically retry every database error.

---

# 7.6.14.28 Retry the Whole Transaction

When PostgreSQL reports a serialization failure, retry the complete transaction logic, including the reads that informed its decisions, rather than replaying only the final SQL statement. PostgreSQL’s guidance for serialization failures is based on rerunning the full transaction because the prior reads may no longer support the same decision. ([PostgreSQL][3])

Bad:

```text
Retry only the failed UPDATE
using stale earlier reads.
```

Preferred:

```text
Reload authoritative state
re-evaluate policies
attempt the complete transaction again.
```

---

# 7.6.14.29 Retry Must Use Stable Command Input

A transaction retry uses the same command intent.

It may reload different authoritative state.

Example:

```text
PublishArticleCommand
expects version 10.
```

After a concurrent change:

* the next attempt may detect version 11;
* the command should return conflict;
* it should not silently publish against the new version.

Retrying the database transaction does not override optimistic concurrency semantics.

---

# 7.6.14.30 Commit Uncertainty

A connection failure around commit can produce uncertainty:

```text
Did the database commit,
or was the transaction rolled back?
```

The caller must not assume:

```text
No response means no commit.
```

For commands exposed to retries, TWN should use:

* idempotency records;
* stable operation IDs;
* status reconciliation;
* unique business identities.

This is especially important when a command can be submitted again after a timeout.

Detailed command idempotency belongs to 7.6.15.

---

# 7.6.14.31 Rollback Behaviour

Any unexpected failure before successful commit should cause the transaction to roll back.

Rollback includes:

* Aggregate writes;
* relationship writes;
* required Audit entries;
* outbox entries;
* idempotency result updates;

that occurred inside that transaction.

The transaction manager must attempt rollback before releasing the connection.

---

# 7.6.14.32 Rollback Failure

Rollback itself may fail because:

* the connection is already broken;
* the database terminated the session;
* infrastructure is unavailable.

The original failure must not be hidden.

The transaction manager should:

* preserve the original cause;
* record rollback failure as additional diagnostic context;
* discard the connection appropriately;
* return no false success.

---

# 7.6.14.33 Domain State After Rollback

An in-memory Aggregate may already contain the proposed transition before database persistence fails.

Example:

```typescript
article.publish(...);

await articleRepository.save(...);
// Database failure.
```

After rollback, the in-memory object must not be reused as though it represents authoritative state.

The handler should:

* terminate with failure;
* discard the modified instance;
* reload authoritative state for any later attempt.

Do not attempt to continue using a failed transaction’s Domain objects.

---

# 7.6.14.34 Savepoints

PostgreSQL savepoints mark a point inside a transaction and allow work performed after that point to be rolled back without necessarily aborting the entire outer transaction. ([PostgreSQL][1])

Conceptual SQL:

```sql
BEGIN;

-- Work A

SAVEPOINT optional_step;

-- Work B

ROLLBACK TO SAVEPOINT optional_step;

-- Work C

COMMIT;
```

TWN should use savepoints sparingly.

---

# 7.6.14.35 Savepoints Are Not Nested Business Transactions

PostgreSQL does not create independent top-level nested transactions through ordinary `BEGIN` calls; savepoints or subtransactions provide partial rollback within one outer transaction. ([PostgreSQL][5])

Therefore:

```text
Inner operation commits
```

is misleading when the outer transaction can still roll it back.

A savepoint does not create an independently durable inner business operation.

---

# 7.6.14.36 Savepoint Use Cases

Potential valid uses:

* optional import record inside one bounded batch;
* recoverable database operation where the outer work remains valid;
* controlled migration or administrative script;
* infrastructure compatibility with libraries that use savepoints.

Ordinary command handlers should generally avoid savepoint-driven partial success unless the product workflow explicitly requires it.

---

# 7.6.14.37 Savepoint Anti-Pattern

Bad:

```text
Publish Article transaction

Savepoint:
try Search indexing

If Search fails,
roll back only Search
and continue.
```

Search should not be inside the database transaction at all.

Savepoints must not be used to justify placing secondary external effects inside a transaction.

---

# 7.6.14.38 Nested Transaction Policy

TWN’s default policy:

```text
The outer command handler owns one transaction.

Inner Repositories and Application Services
join the provided transaction.

They do not begin independent transactions.
```

An inner operation requiring a transaction must either:

* require a transaction context;
* remain non-transactional;
* be invoked through a separately documented workflow.

---

# 7.6.14.39 Transaction Propagation

Recommended pattern:

```typescript
await transactionManager.run(
  async (transaction) => {
    await articleRepository.save(
      article,
      expectedVersion,
      transaction,
    );

    await moderationRecorder.record(
      decision,
      transaction,
    );

    await outboxWriter.append(
      events,
      transaction,
    );
  },
);
```

Each participant uses the same opaque transaction context.

No participant commits independently.

---

# 7.6.14.40 Transaction Context Validation

Infrastructure should reject:

* transaction context from another connection type;
* already-completed transaction;
* missing required transaction;
* transaction context used after callback completion.

Exact enforcement depends on the database library.

The architecture should not allow a transaction object to escape and be reused later.

---

# 7.6.14.41 Transaction Lifetime

A transaction context is valid only during its callback.

Bad:

```typescript
let storedTransaction;

await transactionManager.run(
  async (transaction) => {
    storedTransaction = transaction;
  },
);

await repository.save(
  entity,
  storedTransaction,
);
```

The transaction has already completed.

Transaction contexts must not be stored:

* globally;
* in caches;
* in long-lived service state;
* in queued jobs;
* in Domain objects.

---

# 7.6.14.42 Long Transaction Risks

Long-running transactions may:

* hold locks longer;
* increase contention;
* retain older database snapshots;
* delay cleanup;
* increase deadlock risk;
* consume scarce connections;
* make rollback more expensive.

Therefore the transaction callback should contain only the required database work and in-memory Domain evaluation.

---

# 7.6.14.43 No User Interaction During Transaction

Never:

```text
Begin transaction
    ↓
Wait for editor confirmation
    ↓
Commit later
```

Human interaction can take seconds, minutes or hours.

Instead:

```text
Load current state
    ↓
Show interface
    ↓
User submits command
    ↓
Begin short transaction
    ↓
Check expected version
    ↓
Apply and commit
```

Optimistic concurrency protects against changes that occurred during the editing period.

---

# 7.6.14.44 No AI Calls Inside Transaction

An AI provider call may:

* take seconds;
* time out;
* be rate-limited;
* return malformed output;
* incur cost;
* succeed remotely while the response is lost.

Therefore AI generation must not occur while holding the primary database transaction open.

Possible flow:

```text
Create AI task record
+
outbox job
    ↓
Commit
    ↓
Worker invokes AI
    ↓
Validate result
    ↓
Persist result in a new transaction
```

The final AI architecture may support other controlled workflows, but the transaction boundary remains short.

---

# 7.6.14.45 No Email Delivery Inside Transaction

Bad:

```text
Update Subscriber
    ↓
Send email
    ↓
Commit
```

Possible failure:

```text
Email is sent
but database commit fails.
```

The User receives a message describing a state that did not commit.

Preferred:

```text
Update Subscriber
+
write WelcomeEmailRequested outbox record
    ↓
Commit
    ↓
Worker sends email
```

---

# 7.6.14.46 No Search Indexing Inside Transaction

Search is a derived read system.

Preferred:

```text
Publish Article
+
append ArticlePublished outbox event
    ↓
Commit
    ↓
Search consumer indexes Article
```

If indexing fails, the outbox event remains available for retry and reconciliation.

---

# 7.6.14.47 Transactional Outbox Problem

A command may need to:

```text
Update the database
```

and:

```text
Publish an event or job
```

A direct approach creates two independent writes:

```text
1. Commit database
2. Publish message
```

Failure between the two can lose the message.

Reversing the order can publish a message for a database change that later fails.

The transactional outbox pattern solves this by storing the business state and the event record within one database transaction, then relaying the event after commit. ([Microsoft Learn][6])

---

# 7.6.14.48 TWN Outbox Decision

TWN should use a transactional outbox for durable reactions that must not be lost.

Examples:

```text
ArticlePublished
JourneyPublished
MediaProcessingRequested
SubscriberConfirmed
CampaignDispatchRequested
UserSuspended
```

The authoritative transaction writes:

* business state;
* Audit record where required;
* outbox message.

A separate relay or worker publishes or processes the outbox entry.

---

# 7.6.14.49 Outbox Record Shape

Conceptual model:

```typescript
export type OutboxRecord = {
  eventId: string;
  eventType: string;
  eventVersion: number;

  aggregateType: string;
  aggregateId: string;

  occurredAt: string;
  recordedAt: string;

  payload: unknown;

  operationId: string;
};
```

Operational state may include:

```text
pending
processing
published
failed
```

The exact schema belongs to Volume 8.

---

# 7.6.14.50 Outbox Payload Rule

An outbox payload should contain enough information for durable processing.

Avoid embedding unrestricted Domain Aggregates.

Prefer:

* stable event identity;
* event type and version;
* Aggregate identity;
* required immutable facts;
* correlation metadata.

Consumers may retrieve additional approved data through the owning module’s public query contract.

---

# 7.6.14.51 Outbox Atomicity

The business write and outbox insert must use the same PostgreSQL transaction.

```typescript
await transactionManager.run(
  async (transaction) => {
    await articleRepository.save(
      article,
      command.expectedVersion,
      transaction,
    );

    await auditWriter.write(
      auditEntry,
      transaction,
    );

    await outboxWriter.append(
      articlePublishedEvent,
      transaction,
    );
  },
);
```

If any required write fails:

```text
All roll back.
```

This is the core reliability guarantee of the pattern. ([Microsoft Learn][6])

---

# 7.6.14.52 Outbox Does Not Guarantee Exactly-Once Consumption

A relay may publish the same event more than once because:

* processing succeeds but acknowledgement fails;
* a worker crashes after delivery;
* a lease expires;
* the broker redelivers.

Outbox consumers must be idempotent.

The outbox provides durable at-least-once-compatible delivery, not magical end-to-end exactly-once behaviour. ([Microsoft Learn][7])

Detailed idempotency belongs to 7.6.15.

---

# 7.6.14.53 Outbox Relay Options

Potential relay strategies include:

```text
Polling publisher
Transaction-log tailing
Managed change-feed mechanism
```

A polling publisher periodically claims pending outbox rows and publishes them. Transaction-log tailing observes committed database changes. Each approach has different ordering, operational and provider implications. ([microservices.io][8])

TWN should begin with the simplest reliable strategy supported by its selected deployment and queue infrastructure.

The final mechanism belongs to Background Jobs and Queue Architecture.

---

# 7.6.14.54 Outbox Ordering

Some events require ordering per Aggregate.

Example:

```text
ArticlePublished version 12
ArticleArchived version 13
```

Consumers should not permanently apply:

```text
Archived
then Published
```

as though the older event were newer.

Useful ordering metadata may include:

* Aggregate version;
* occurred time;
* event sequence;
* event ID.

Global ordering across unrelated Aggregates is usually unnecessary and expensive.

---

# 7.6.14.55 Outbox Cleanup

Published outbox records should not grow indefinitely without policy.

Cleanup options may include:

* retention period;
* archive table;
* partitioning;
* completed-record deletion;
* external event archive.

Retention must account for:

* reconciliation;
* operational investigation;
* replay needs;
* Audit separation;
* privacy.

The exact policy belongs to Database, Queue and Audit Architecture.

---

# 7.6.14.56 After-Commit Hooks

Framework or in-process after-commit hooks may be used for:

* low-risk best-effort cache hints;
* immediate local wake-up of an outbox relay;
* non-authoritative diagnostics.

They must not replace durable outbox records when work cannot safely be lost.

Bad:

```text
After commit callback sends the only copy
of ArticlePublished.
```

If the process crashes before or during the callback, the event is lost.

---

# 7.6.14.57 Commit Before Secondary Dispatch

Correct ordering:

```text
Persist state and outbox
    ↓
Commit
    ↓
Dispatch or wake relay
```

Do not publish the event before commit.

A consumer must not observe:

```text
ArticlePublished
```

while the Article transaction later rolls back.

---

# 7.6.14.58 Audit Atomicity

An Audit record should be included in the same transaction when the operation is not validly complete without accountability.

Examples:

* role assignment;
* account suspension;
* irreversible purge approval;
* moderation decision;
* publication by an editor.

If the Audit record is optional diagnostic information, it should not block the business operation.

The Audit chapter must classify each audited action.

---

# 7.6.14.59 Idempotency Record Atomicity

For commands using durable idempotency, the transaction may need to include:

* claim or validate idempotency key;
* business mutation;
* Audit entry;
* outbox entry;
* completed result record.

This ensures a retry cannot observe:

```text
Business state committed
but idempotency still says unprocessed.
```

The detailed design belongs to 7.6.15.

---

# 7.6.14.60 Transaction Result

A transaction callback should return application data only after all required internal operations have succeeded.

Example:

```typescript
const committed =
  await transactionManager.run(
    async (transaction) => {
      const saveOutcome =
        await articleRepository.save(
          article,
          command.expectedVersion,
          transaction,
        );

      if (
        saveOutcome.kind !== "saved"
      ) {
        return saveOutcome;
      }

      await auditWriter.write(
        auditEntry,
        transaction,
      );

      await outboxWriter.append(
        events,
        transaction,
      );

      return {
        kind: "ready-to-commit",
        newVersion:
          saveOutcome.newVersion,
      };
    },
  );
```

The caller should describe success as committed only after `run` returns successfully.

---

# 7.6.14.61 Early Business Result Inside Transaction

Expected outcomes may be detected inside a transaction.

Example:

```text
Version conflict
```

The handler may return a controlled result without writing anything.

The transaction manager may still issue a normal commit for a read-only path or roll back according to implementation.

No partial mutation must occur before the expected result is returned.

---

# 7.6.14.62 Exception Versus Controlled Outcome

Controlled outcome:

```text
version-conflict
not-found
already-published
invalid-transition
```

Unexpected transaction failure:

```text
deadlock after retries
database unavailable
commit failure
corrupted state
```

Do not throw generic exceptions for ordinary conflicts.

Do not convert infrastructure failure into a business rejection.

---

# 7.6.14.63 Transaction Timeout

Every write transaction should have a bounded execution budget.

Possible controls:

* request deadline;
* database statement timeout;
* transaction timeout in the selected library;
* lock timeout;
* worker deadline.

Exact values must be measured and configured by operation class.

Do not set one arbitrary timeout for:

* draft save;
* Journey reorder;
* batch import;
* migration;
* reporting.

---

# 7.6.14.64 Lock Timeout

A lock timeout can prevent one transaction from waiting indefinitely for another.

The application must classify the result appropriately:

```text
temporary contention
```

not:

```text
resource not found
```

A retry may be appropriate when:

* the command is idempotent;
* deadline allows;
* retries are bounded.

---

# 7.6.14.65 Statement Timeout

A statement timeout protects the database and application from unexpectedly long SQL execution.

A timeout may indicate:

* missing index;
* excessive result set;
* lock contention;
* poor query plan;
* infrastructure degradation.

It should generate operational visibility.

It must not be silently retried indefinitely.

---

# 7.6.14.66 Batch Transactions

A batch transaction must be bounded.

Bad:

```text
One transaction updates
every Article ever created.
```

Risks:

* long locks;
* large rollback;
* connection exhaustion;
* deployment timeout;
* replication pressure;
* operational recovery difficulty.

Preferred:

```text
Bounded batches
+
progress checkpoint
+
idempotent continuation
```

---

# 7.6.14.67 Atomic Versus Batched Operation

Some operations require one atomic unit:

```text
Replace complete order of
a bounded Journey structure.
```

Others should be batched:

```text
Rebuild Search documents
for 500,000 records.
```

The first protects one Domain structure.

The second is derived operational work and should be resumable.

---

# 7.6.14.68 Long-Running Business Workflows

A workflow spanning minutes, hours or days cannot remain one database transaction.

Examples:

* Book export;
* Video processing;
* newsletter campaign delivery;
* multi-stage AI research;
* account data export;
* large import;
* external approval workflow.

Use:

```text
Short local transactions
+
durable workflow state
+
idempotent steps
+
retries
+
explicit completion
```

---

# 7.6.14.69 Workflow State

A long-running workflow may persist:

```text
workflow ID
type
current state
current step
attempt count
input reference
result reference
last error category
created time
updated time
completed time
```

Each step commits independently.

The workflow does not hold one database transaction open.

Detailed orchestration belongs to Background Jobs and Queue Architecture.

---

# 7.6.14.70 Compensating Actions

When a workflow has already committed one step and a later step fails, database rollback cannot undo prior committed transactions.

A compensating action may be needed.

Example:

```text
Reserved campaign audience
    ↓
Campaign approval later revoked
    ↓
Release reservation
```

Compensation is a new business operation.

It is not time travel.

Azure’s compensating-transaction guidance recommends using compensation only where committed work genuinely needs to be undone and avoiding the complexity when a design can safely proceed without it. ([Microsoft Learn][9])

---

# 7.6.14.71 Compensation Is Not Blind Reversal

A compensation must consider current state.

Example:

```text
Publish Article
```

cannot always be compensated by deleting it.

A safer compensating action may be:

```text
Archive Article
```

because:

* readers may already have accessed it;
* links may exist;
* newsletter messages may have been sent;
* Audit history must remain.

Compensation follows Domain semantics.

---

# 7.6.14.72 Saga Position

A Saga coordinates a sequence of local transactions with compensating actions when a workflow spans independent systems or services. ([microservices.io][10])

TWN does not initially require a general Saga framework.

A Saga may become justified when TWN introduces workflows such as:

* payments;
* external publishing;
* complex account deletion;
* multi-provider commerce;
* partner integrations.

Do not call every background job a Saga.

---

# 7.6.14.73 Eventual Consistency

Eventual consistency means secondary state may temporarily lag behind authoritative state.

Example:

```text
Article published at 10:00:00

Article page available at 10:00:01

Search result updated at 10:00:05

Analytics projection updated later
```

This is acceptable when:

* the authoritative source is clear;
* expected freshness is documented;
* retries exist;
* reconciliation exists;
* the UI does not falsely claim immediate synchronization.

---

# 7.6.14.74 Authoritative State

Every eventually consistent workflow must identify the source of truth.

Examples:

| Derived state           | Authoritative source                            |
| ----------------------- | ----------------------------------------------- |
| Search document         | Published Editorial content                     |
| Homepage projection     | Owning content modules                          |
| Analytics summary       | Raw approved Analytics events                   |
| Email-delivery status   | Newsletter record plus verified provider events |
| Media public projection | Media Asset lifecycle                           |

Derived systems must not become accidental authorities.

---

# 7.6.14.75 Reconciliation

Retries alone are insufficient.

TWN should eventually support reconciliation processes that detect:

* published Articles missing from Search;
* ready Media missing variants;
* outbox records stuck in processing;
* Campaign records inconsistent with provider receipts;
* projections behind their source version.

A reconciliation operation compares:

```text
Authoritative state
```

with:

```text
Derived state
```

and repairs or reports divergence.

---

# 7.6.14.76 Transaction and Cache Invalidation

Framework cache invalidation does not belong inside the database transaction.

Possible sequence:

```text
Commit authoritative state and outbox
    ↓
Revalidate cache immediately as best effort
and/or
consume durable content-changed event
```

The Cache Architecture will define whether invalidation is:

* synchronous after commit;
* event-driven;
* tag-based;
* TTL-assisted;
* reconciled.

A database rollback must never leave a successful cache invalidation representing nonexistent state as though publication succeeded.

---

# 7.6.14.77 Transaction and File Storage

Database state and object storage cannot usually share one local atomic transaction.

Possible Media flow:

```text
1. Create bounded upload capability
2. Browser uploads object
3. Confirm upload command
4. Verify object exists
5. Transaction marks Media uploaded
   and requests processing
6. Worker processes Media
```

If the object exists but database confirmation fails, the object may become orphaned.

TWN needs:

* temporary upload lifecycle;
* expiration;
* orphan cleanup;
* idempotent confirmation.

Do not attempt to pretend storage and PostgreSQL committed atomically.

---

# 7.6.14.78 Transaction and Provider Idempotency

When a remote operation follows commit, use provider idempotency where available.

Example:

```text
EmailDeliveryJob ID
```

may become the provider submission identity.

This reduces duplicate remote effects when:

* worker retries;
* provider response is lost;
* acknowledgement fails.

Provider idempotency complements TWN’s local idempotency.

It does not replace the outbox.

---

# 7.6.14.79 Read-Your-Writes After Commit

After a successful command, the caller may need to read committed state immediately.

Options:

* command returns required committed data;
* caller performs an authoritative query;
* related cache is invalidated;
* a query bypasses stale derived projection;
* result includes new Aggregate version.

Do not require Search or Analytics projections to update before the initiating User can see the command result.

---

# 7.6.14.80 Transaction Logging

Transaction-level logs may include:

* operation name;
* transaction outcome;
* duration;
* retry count;
* isolation level when non-default;
* rollback category;
* deadlock or serialization category;
* number of affected Aggregate roots;
* operation ID.

Do not log:

* complete SQL parameters;
* Article bodies;
* subscriber lists;
* secrets;
* raw transaction objects.

---

# 7.6.14.81 Transaction Metrics

Useful bounded metrics include:

```text
transaction operation
outcome
isolation class
retry count bucket
duration bucket
rollback category
```

Avoid metric labels containing:

* User ID;
* Article ID;
* transaction ID;
* operation ID;
* email address.

High-cardinality identifiers belong in controlled logs or traces.

---

# 7.6.14.82 Slow Transaction Monitoring

TWN should identify transactions that:

* exceed expected duration;
* wait excessively for locks;
* retry repeatedly;
* touch unexpectedly many rows;
* hold connections during provider calls;
* create deadlock patterns.

A slow transaction is often more dangerous than one slow read because it may block other writers.

Detailed thresholds belong to Observability Architecture.

---

# 7.6.14.83 Transaction Tracing

A transaction trace may include spans for:

```text
Acquire connection
Begin transaction
Load Aggregate
Evaluate policy
Persist Aggregate
Write Audit
Write outbox
Commit
```

The trace should make it clear whether time was spent:

* waiting for a connection;
* waiting for a lock;
* executing SQL;
* running Domain logic;
* committing.

---

# 7.6.14.84 Testing Transaction Commit

Integration test:

```text
Publish Article
```

Expected after commit:

* Article status updated;
* published revision selected;
* version incremented;
* Audit record present;
* outbox record present.

All must be visible together.

---

# 7.6.14.85 Testing Transaction Rollback

Force failure after one or more writes but before commit.

Expected:

```text
No Article mutation
No Audit record
No outbox record
No idempotency completion
```

This must be tested against real PostgreSQL.

An in-memory fake does not prove database rollback semantics.

---

# 7.6.14.86 Testing Concurrency

Run overlapping transactions.

Examples:

* two Article saves expecting the same version;
* two workers claiming one scheduled publication;
* two commands creating the same slug;
* two webhook deliveries claiming one event identity.

Verify:

* one valid winner where required;
* controlled conflict for the other;
* no silent overwrite;
* no duplicate outbox event.

---

# 7.6.14.87 Testing Serialization Retry

Where a command uses `REPEATABLE READ` or `SERIALIZABLE`, simulate a serialization failure.

Verify:

* entire transaction callback reruns;
* retry count is bounded;
* Domain rules are re-evaluated;
* no external side effect occurs between attempts;
* final failure is classified correctly.

---

# 7.6.14.88 Testing Deadlock Handling

For operations where deadlocks are plausible:

* produce opposing lock order in an integration test;
* verify one transaction is aborted;
* verify retry behaviour;
* verify no partial state remains;
* confirm lock order is corrected where possible.

Retry should not become a substitute for fixing consistently reproducible lock-order problems.

---

# 7.6.14.89 Testing Outbox Atomicity

Test:

1. business state save succeeds;
2. outbox insert is forced to fail;
3. transaction rolls back;
4. business state remains unchanged.

Then test:

1. both writes succeed;
2. transaction commits;
3. relay later observes the outbox record.

---

# 7.6.14.90 Testing Duplicate Relay

Simulate:

```text
Relay publishes event
    ↓
crashes before marking it complete
    ↓
publishes again
```

The consumer must process the duplicate safely.

This test crosses into 7.6.15 and Background Jobs Architecture but is essential to validate the outbox system end to end.

---

# 7.6.14.91 Transaction Test Database

Transaction tests must use an actual PostgreSQL environment because correctness depends on:

* isolation;
* locking;
* constraints;
* savepoints;
* commit;
* rollback;
* deadlocks;
* serialization failures;
* connection behaviour.

Mocks can verify that a handler requests a transaction.

They cannot prove PostgreSQL transaction behaviour.

---

# 7.6.14.92 Transaction Review Checklist

Before approving a transactional command, verify:

* What invariant does the transaction protect?
* Which Aggregate owns the primary mutation?
* Which writes must commit atomically?
* Is Audit mandatory?
* Is an outbox record mandatory?
* Is transaction ownership visible?
* Do all repositories use the same context?
* Does any inner service start a hidden transaction?
* Are external network calls excluded?
* Is the isolation level justified?
* Is optimistic concurrency sufficient?
* Are locks narrow and ordered?
* Is retry safe?
* Is the retry count bounded?
* Could commit become uncertain?
* Is idempotency required?
* Is the transaction short?
* Is batch size bounded?
* Is eventual consistency acceptable for secondary work?
* Is reconciliation defined?
* Are commit, rollback and concurrency tested with PostgreSQL?

---

# 7.6.14.93 Transaction Anti-Patterns

## Transaction in Repository

Repository secretly begins and commits its own business operation.

## Transaction Around Entire Request

Parsing, authorization, provider calls and rendering occur while a connection is held.

## Remote Call Inside Transaction

Email, AI, Search or storage delays database commit.

## Publish Event Before Commit

Consumer observes a state change that later rolls back.

## Direct Publish After Commit Without Outbox

Process crash loses required event.

## Hidden Nested Transaction

Inner service commits independently.

## Savepoint as Independent Commit

Misunderstands partial rollback semantics.

## One Global Serializable Default

Creates unnecessary conflicts and retry cost.

## Isolation Used Instead of Constraints

Attempts to replace uniqueness and version checks.

## Long Human Transaction

Locks state while an editor thinks.

## Unbounded Batch Transaction

Creates large rollback and contention.

## Retry Only Final Statement

Uses stale decisions after serialization failure.

## Retry Every Database Error

Repeats permanent failures.

## Reuse Aggregate After Rollback

Treats uncommitted in-memory state as authoritative.

## No Reconciliation

Assumes retry alone guarantees derived-system consistency.

## Cache Mutation Treated as Atomic with Database

Pretends two independent systems commit together.

## Storage Upload Treated as Database Transaction

Ignores orphaned-object scenarios.

---

# 7.6.14.94 Initial TWN Transaction Catalog

The initial likely transaction boundaries include:

---

## Save Article Draft

Atomic:

* append or update working revision according to CMS model;
* update Article working revision reference;
* increment Aggregate version;
* write required autosave metadata where authoritative.

Not atomic with:

* Search;
* AI suggestions;
* preview image generation.

---

## Publish Article

Atomic:

* validate current expected version;
* update Article lifecycle;
* select immutable published revision;
* create publication metadata;
* increment version;
* required Audit entry;
* `ArticlePublished` outbox event.

---

## Reorder Journey

Atomic:

* update affected Chapter or membership ordering;
* validate complete reorder set;
* increment Journey version;
* required Audit where policy demands it;
* `JourneyStructureChanged` outbox event where needed.

---

## Approve Shared Page

Atomic:

* update moderation state;
* identify approved content version;
* create moderation decision record;
* create required Audit entry;
* append publication or notification event.

---

## Confirm Media Upload

Atomic:

* mark verified upload state;
* store approved object metadata;
* request Media-processing work through outbox;
* increment Media version.

Not atomic with:

* transcoding;
* image optimization;
* virus scanning provider;
* AI-generated alt-text.

---

## Confirm Newsletter Subscriber

Atomic:

* confirm consent state;
* update Subscriber lifecycle;
* record confirmation evidence required by policy;
* append welcome-email request.

Not atomic with actual email delivery.

---

# 7.6.14.95 Architecture Decision Record

**Decision:** TWN uses short, explicit PostgreSQL transactions owned primarily by command handlers. Each transaction protects one authoritative consistency boundary and includes required business state, Audit records and durable outbox events, while external and derived-system work executes after commit.

**Supporting decisions:**

* Transactions protect Domain invariants rather than arbitrary call sequences.
* The outer command handler normally owns the transaction.
* Repositories and Application Services join an explicit transaction context.
* Hidden transactions and hidden commits are prohibited.
* Transaction contexts are opaque, callback-scoped and server-only.
* `READ COMMITTED` is the ordinary default.
* Stronger isolation is selected only for a documented invariant.
* Human editing uses optimistic concurrency rather than long-held locks.
* Explicit locks are narrow, short and purpose-specific.
* Retryable transaction failures rerun the complete transaction.
* Retry counts are bounded.
* Expected version conflicts are not automatically retried against newer state.
* Savepoints are used sparingly and are not treated as independent commits.
* External calls do not occur inside database transactions.
* Domain state, required Audit records and outbox entries commit atomically.
* Required asynchronous reactions use the transactional outbox.
* Outbox processing assumes at-least-once delivery and idempotent consumers.
* Derived systems may be eventually consistent.
* Reconciliation processes repair divergence from authoritative state.
* Long-running workflows use several short local transactions.
* Compensating actions are explicit Domain operations rather than database rollback across time.
* A general Saga framework is deferred until a real multi-system business workflow requires it.
* Transaction correctness is tested against real PostgreSQL.

**Reasons:**

* preserves Aggregate consistency;
* avoids partial publication and moderation state;
* prevents event loss;
* keeps database locks short;
* supports reliable background work;
* avoids pretending PostgreSQL and external providers share one atomic commit;
* provides a clear path for Search, Analytics, email, Media and AI reactions;
* supports modular-monolith boundaries without unnecessary distributed transactions.

**Consequences:**

Positive:

* critical state changes commit safely;
* Audit and event registration remain reliable;
* external provider failures do not hold database locks;
* secondary systems can recover through durable events;
* concurrency failures become explicit;
* long-running workflows can be resumed and reconciled.

Costs:

* outbox infrastructure is required;
* consumers must be idempotent;
* derived state may temporarily lag;
* transaction retries require careful implementation;
* cross-module atomicity requires architectural review;
* reconciliation and outbox cleanup require operational ownership.

**Expected lifetime:** The short-transaction, explicit-ownership and transactional-outbox principles are intended to remain permanent. Isolation choices, queue providers, relay mechanisms and transaction-library implementation may evolve.

---

# Completion Criteria for 7.6.14

This subsection is complete when:

* transaction purpose and ownership are defined;
* Aggregate and cross-module transaction boundaries are distinguished;
* transaction context propagation is explicit;
* hidden transactions are prohibited;
* isolation-level selection is documented;
* optimistic concurrency and explicit locking have clear roles;
* retries rerun the complete transaction;
* commit uncertainty is acknowledged;
* savepoint and nested-operation policy is established;
* external calls are excluded from transactions;
* Audit and idempotency atomicity are recognized;
* the transactional outbox is selected;
* outbox delivery and consumer idempotency expectations are defined;
* long-running workflows use short local transactions;
* compensation and eventual consistency are addressed;
* reconciliation is required;
* PostgreSQL transaction, rollback, concurrency and outbox tests are required;
* initial TWN transaction candidates are documented without inventing unresolved product rules.

---

## End of 7.6.14

**Remaining before 7.6 is complete: 10 subsections.**

Next:

# **7.6.15 — Idempotency Architecture**

It will define:

* idempotent command semantics;
* idempotency keys;
* operation fingerprints;
* key scope;
* duplicate browser submissions;
* webhook duplication;
* queue redelivery;
* scheduled-task overlap;
* concurrent claims;
* stored results;
* processing leases;
* provider idempotency;
* idempotent consumers;
* expiry and cleanup;
* unknown outcomes;
* testing and recovery.
